challenge_prompt: A 31-year-old Chinese man, teetotaler with no past medical history, presented with one day of acute respiratory tract symptoms. He had travel history to London a month prior and to Batam, Indonesia, a week prior, no sick contacts, no illicit drug use. On Day 1, he felt unwell with generalized lethargy and flu symptoms without fever. On Day 2, he developed fever with chills and headache. A GP diagnosed influenza B serologically and prescribed baloxavir. That evening, he became delirious and unresponsive. En route to hospital, he had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting two minutes, self-aborted. In the ward, he was febrile (38.9°C), tachycardic (110 bpm), confused, disoriented. No meningism. Motor power: 5/5 upper limbs, 4+/5 lower limbs due to proximal muscle pain. Tone, reflexes, gait, sensory normal. Initial labs: lymphopenia, CRP 168.6 mg/L, Na 125 mmol/L, mixed metabolic acidosis on ABG. Chest X-ray: basal lung infiltrates. CT brain: negative for mass, stroke, hemorrhage. Serum and urine toxicology negative. A nasopharyngeal swab PCR confirmed Influenza B. MRI brain showed a focus of diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) signal abnormality in the splenium of the corpus callosum. EEG was normal. Lumbar puncture was declined by family.

aliases: Clinically Mild Encephalitis/Encephalopathy with a Reversible Splenial Lesion
answer_key.diagnosis: Mild Encephalopathy with Reversible Splenial Lesion (MERS) associated with Influenza B
answer_key.etiology: Influenza B infection triggering a systemic inflammatory response (cytokine storm) with associated hyponatremia leading to reversible splenial vasogenic edema.
answer_key.next_management_step: Supportive care (monitoring, hydration, seizure control), consider antiviral therapy (oseltamivir 150 mg BID per ID consult), and repeat MRI brain in 3 months to confirm resolution of splenial lesion.
required_findings: Acute encephalopathy with delirium and seizure
Influenza B detected by nasopharyngeal PCR
Hyponatremia (Na 125 mmol/L)
DWI hyperintensity in the splenium of corpus callosum on MRI
Lymphopenia and elevated CRP (168.6 mg/L)
Viral prodrome (fever, respiratory symptoms)
No meningism
Normal CT brain

title: Influenza B-Associated Mild Encephalopathy with Reversible Splenial Lesion in an Adult: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 40-year-old right-handed female with a master’s degree, employed as a manager in a nursing home, with no significant medical history and unvaccinated for COVID-19, had SARS-CoV-2 infection in December 2020 (mild symptoms: headache, fatigue, anosmia, ageusia, dyspnea, fever; no hospitalization). She returned to work after 10 days but noticed cognitive symptoms: brain fog, difficulty concentrating, forgetfulness, word retrieval problems. She also had disrupted sleep and hair loss. Ten months after infection, at a COVID-19 clinic, persistent symptoms included olfactory/gustatory disorders, tension headache, sleep disturbances, cognitive deficits, and fatigue. No dyspnea, chest pain, cough, anxiety, or depression. Neurologic exam normal. Routine labs (thyroid function, ferritin, folate, vitamin B12, Borrelia, Treponema, HIV antibodies) normal except vitamin D deficiency. Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score 28/30. Detailed neuropsychology: clinically relevant fatigue on Chalder Fatigue Scale, deficit in D2 Sustained-Attention Test; other domains normal. Brain MRI (3T with contrast, volumetric T1) was unremarkable. She had a second mild COVID-19 infection in December 2021. At follow-up 18 months after initial infection, sleep improved but fatigue and attention deficits persisted. Neurologic exam normal. EEG normal. Neuropsychology: MoCA 26/30, mild cognitive impairment on Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test (AVLT) with progressive decline in delayed recall but preserved recognition, and preserved primacy/recency effect. No anxiety/depression (Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale normal). Her father had early-onset Alzheimer's disease (EOAD) diagnosed at age 50 with bilateral temporo-parietal atrophy on MRI, temporal/posterior parietal hypometabolism on FDG-PET, and low CSF amyloid-beta and elevated tau. At a Memory Clinic visit, headache increased (tension-type, no associated symptoms), no benefit from amitriptyline or topiramate. Repeat neuropsychology showed worse long verbal memory; fatigue, anxiety, depression unchanged. 18F-FDG PET/CT (July 2022, 18 months after initial infection) showed hypometabolism in inferior frontal lobe (olfactory gyrus), temporal lobe (limbic/paralimbic regions), and cerebellum. CSF analysis: normal cell count, protein, glucose; negative oligoclonal bands; normal total tau, phospho-tau, amyloid-beta 42, amyloid-beta 42/40 ratio; negative paraneoplastic antibodies (comprehensive panel). Based on this presentation and workup, what is the most likely etiology of this patient's cognitive symptoms and what further step is most appropriate?

answer_key.diagnosis: Post-COVID-19 syndrome (Long COVID, PASC)
answer_key.etiology: SARS-CoV-2 infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Reassurance, multidisciplinary rehabilitation, symptomatic management, and longitudinal follow-up; no specific disease-modifying therapy indicated
optional_findings: Family history of early-onset Alzheimer's disease
AVLT showing progressive decline in delayed recall with preserved recognition and preserved primacy/recency effect
Second mild COVID-19 infection in December 2021

Normal neurologic examination

Normal EEG

title: Persistent 18F-FDG Brain PET Fronto-Temporal Hypometabolism and Cognitive Symptoms Two Years after SARS-CoV-2 Infection: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: An 89-year-old woman with a history of hypertension presents with progressive cognitive decline over 6 months. Her Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score decreased from 26 to 22, with particular deficits in orientation, attention (serial-7), three-stage command, delayed recall, and visuospatial construction (copy of double pentagon). She also developed depressive symptoms (Geriatric Depression Scale score 8) and parkinsonism including resting tremor, muscle rigidity, and bradykinesia (Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale part 3 score 45). Prior to the cognitive decline, she experienced oral cenesthopathy (feeling of filaments in her mouth), which evolved into a fixed delusional belief of filaria infection in her nose and eyes. Blood tests and electroencephalogram were normal. Brain magnetic resonance imaging showed mild atrophy of the prefrontal cortex and bilateral hippocampus. Single photon emission computed tomography revealed reduced cerebral blood flow in the bilateral thalamus, prefrontal, temporal, occipital, and parietal association areas. 123I-metaiodobenzylguanidine myocardial uptake was reduced. She was treated with mirtazapine 22.5 mg/day for depression without improvement of delusions. Based on the presentation and investigations, what is the most likely diagnosis? Additionally, what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Probable dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB)
answer_key.etiology: Neurodegenerative (alpha-synucleinopathy)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate a cholinesterase inhibitor (e.g., donepezil) and consider an atypical antipsychotic with low extrapyramidal side effect risk (e.g., aripiprazole) for delusions, with careful monitoring for neuroleptic sensitivity.

MMSE score 22 with specific subdomain deficits
Oral cenesthopathy evolving to delusional parasitosis
Lack of response to mirtazapine alone

title: Delusional parasitosis in dementia with Lewy bodies: a case report

challenge_prompt:
A 33-year-old female with systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosed one month prior (presented with photosensitive rash, arthritis, anemia, lymphocytopenia, albuminuria, hematuria, positive ANA) was admitted for a tonic seizure. She was on prednisone 1 mg/kg/day, hydroxychloroquine, and antihypertensives. No prior headache, cognitive changes, or seizures. Poor sleep for past week. On exam: BP 160/100, facial acneiform lesions, moon facies, supraclavicular fullness, grade 1 bipedal edema, left shoulder tenderness. Neurologic exam normal. Labs: microcytic hypochromic anemia (Hb 99 g/L), thrombocytopenia (100x10^9/L), positive direct and indirect Coombs, normal creatinine and electrolytes, urinalysis with +3 proteinuria, mild pyuria, coarse granular casts, 24h urine protein 4.47g. She was started on IV hydrocortisone and mycophenolate mofetil. On hospital day 2, developed sudden holocranial headache, confusion, bilateral total vision loss (preceded by peripheral blurring). BP 190/110. Disoriented, bilateral vision loss, otherwise normal cranial nerves and motor/sensory exam. CT head: acute parenchymal hemorrhage right occipital lobe (18.4 cc) extending to right parietal lobe, surrounding cerebral edema, subfalcine and uncal herniation; possible acute infarcts bilateral occipital and parietal lobes. Four-vessel angiogram showed no arteriovenous malformation, only poor parenchymal staining right parietal region from hematoma. Cranial MRI (day 7) revealed 4.6x4.3x2.5 cm parenchymal hematoma right parietooccipital, compressing occipital horn of right lateral ventricle, peripherally hyperintense and centrally hypointense on T1, with surrounding encephalomalacia. Patchy T2 FLAIR hyperintensities in cortical/subcortical occipital and posterior parietal lobes bilaterally.

Based on the presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) with intraparenchymal hemorrhage
answer_key.etiology: Hypertension, active lupus nephritis, corticosteroid use
answer_key.next_management_step: Aggressive blood pressure control (target systolic <140 mmHg), seizure management with antiepileptics, intracranial pressure reduction (mannitol), continuation of immunosuppression for lupus, and close neurological monitoring.

optic atrophy and pathologic myopia on ophthalmology exam

challenge_prompt: A 35-year-old pregnant woman with no significant past medical history was admitted for management of severe hyperemesis gravidarum during early pregnancy. Her initial clinical course was characterized by persistent vomiting, markedly reduced oral intake, and progressive dehydration. During hospitalization, she developed progressive alteration of mental status, which rapidly evolved into a severe disorder of consciousness, prompting transfer to the intensive care unit. On ICU admission, her Glasgow Coma Scale score was 6, with preserved brainstem reflexes. Biochemical investigations: serum sodium increased from 146 mmol/L to a peak of 180 mmol/L over a short period, followed by partial correction to values ranging between 156 and 163 mmol/L. Concomitantly, persistent hypokalemia with potassium levels as low as 2.7 mmol/L. Her course was further complicated by febrile episodes, elevated inflammatory markers, and nutritional deficiency requiring enteral feeding. An initial brain MRI performed approximately 48 hours after admission, prior to the onset of impaired consciousness, was unremarkable with no abnormal signal intensity on T2-weighted or FLAIR sequences, no diffusion restriction, and no pathological enhancement. A follow-up MRI performed 14 days later demonstrated extensive and symmetric signal abnormalities involving both pontine and extrapontine structures. At the level of the pons, a central lesion appeared hyperintense on T2-weighted and FLAIR images and hypointense on T1-weighted images, with faint patchy enhancement following contrast administration, without associated diffusion restriction. Additionally, multiple extrapontine regions were affected including the caudate nuclei, putamina, thalami, internal and external capsules, hippocampi, and bilateral cerebellar hemispheres. These lesions were symmetrically distributed and hyperintense on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences. Diffusion-weighted imaging revealed mild diffusion restriction limited to the internal and external capsules. A focal lesion was identified within the corpus callosum, characterized by marked diffusion restriction, hyperintensity on DWI, and corresponding low ADC values. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Osmotic demyelination syndrome (extensive central pontine and extrapontine myelinolysis) with concurrent cytotoxic lesion of the corpus callosum (CLOCC)
answer_key.etiology: Hypernatremia and rapid osmotic shifts due to severe hyperemesis gravidarum
answer_key.next_management_step: Supportive care: avoid rapid correction of sodium, maintain electrolyte stability, provide nutritional support, and monitor for neurological complications; no specific therapy reverses established demyelination.
optional_findings: Febrile episodes and elevated inflammatory markers
required_findings: Severe hyperemesis gravidarum
Serum sodium peak of 180 mmol/L with fluctuation
Hypokalemia (2.7 mmol/L)
GCS 6 on ICU admission
Initial MRI normal
Follow-up MRI: symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in pons, basal ganglia, thalami, internal/external capsules, hippocampi, and cerebellar hemispheres
Diffusion restriction limited to internal and external capsules
Focal lesion in corpus callosum with marked diffusion restriction (CLOCC)

title: Extensive Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome With a Concomitant Cytotoxic Lesion of the Corpus Callosum in Hyperemesis Gravidarum: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 22-year-old Latvian Caucasian man presents with 6-month history of fatigue, weakness, chest heaviness, and episodic breathlessness, plus 1-week of right lower leg swelling. Initial ECG shows ST elevations and PR depressions in V2-V6 consistent with pericarditis. Labs: CPK 1755 U/L, CK-MB 148 U/L, hs-TnT 86.24 ng/L, ASAT 218 U/L, ALAT 67 U/L, direct bilirubin 9.3 µmol/L, total bilirubin 33 µmol/L. Urogenital chlamydiosis and ascariasis treated. Negative for viral hepatitis, HIV, and autoantibodies (anti-Jo-1, anti-U1-RNP, anti-Ro52, anti-SSB, anti-PM-Scl). Echocardiogram: left ventricular hypertrophy. Chest CT: hepatomegaly. Chest X-ray, USG, and cardiac MRI: normal. EMG: myogenic injury in all observed muscles with leg predominance; no neuropathy. Muscle biopsy (right shin): interstitial edema, mild fibrosis, CD8 T-lymphocyte perivascular and intravascular infiltration consistent with inflammatory myopathy. Diagnosed with unspecified myositis and myopericarditis, treated with methylprednisolone. Initial improvement, but 5 months later returns with edema in left forearm, wrist, and right shin; generalized muscle pain and weakness; difficulty climbing stairs; breathlessness. Echo now shows symmetrical left ventricular hypertrophy and NT-proBNP 1172 pg/mL (up from 337 pg/mL), class II chronic heart failure, preserved EF 60%. Hepatomegaly, small ascites, right leg lymphostasis. No malignancy. Suspected Fabry disease, but alpha-galactosidase activity normal. Steroid pulse therapy followed by oral methylprednisolone; CPK decreased then rose again. Muscle weakness slightly improved. Family history: patient's maternal grandmother had two sisters who died suddenly around age 45. Patient had exercise intolerance since school years. On third admission: fatigue, very low physical tolerance, back pain, breathlessness, hyperesthesia in limbs, asymmetrical edema. Labs: LDH 1706 U/L, CPK 836 U/L, NT-proBNP 2738 pg/mL, metabolic acidosis. EF 55%. EEG shows signs of encephalopathy. Physical exam: severe muscle atrophy and elbow contractures. Repeat Doppler USG: no vasculitis or DVT. Abdominal and thyroid USG normal. No vision impairment, ophthalmoplegia, ptosis, hearing loss, or diabetes.

Genetic testing revealed the m.3260A>G variant in the MT-TL1 gene.

answer_key.diagnosis: Mitochondrial cardiomyopathy with skeletal muscle myopathy
answer_key.etiology: m.3260A>G mutation in MT-TL1 gene (mitochondrial DNA)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate mitochondrial cocktail (L-carnitine, thiamine, coenzyme Q10, riboflavin) and perform genetic counseling/family screening
optional_findings: Absence of ophthalmoplegia, ptosis, hearing loss, diabetes
History of exercise intolerance since childhood
required_findings: Young adult with exercise intolerance and fatigue
Elevated CPK (1755 U/L) and troponin (86.24 ng/L)

EMG showing myogenic injury without neuropathy
Muscle biopsy with CD8 T-cell infiltration (inflammatory myopathy) but poor response to steroids

challenge_prompt: A 48-year-old woman presents to the emergency room with sudden-onset bilateral lower extremity weakness that began immediately after defecating. She has a history of alcohol use disorder and alcoholic liver disease, but has not consumed alcohol for the past year and takes anti-alcoholic medication. She is not on anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents. Physical examination reveals bilateral lower extremity muscle strength 0/5 (manual muscle test), hypesthesia below the level of the lesion, loss of patellar and Achilles tendon reflexes, and negative Babinski reflexes. Blood tests show normal coagulation panel, platelet count, electrolytes, and liver and kidney function. No imaging studies are performed initially. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Thoracic spinal epidural hematoma (SCEH)
answer_key.etiology: Spontaneous; likely precipitated by Valsalva maneuver (straining during defecation) in the setting of alcoholism-related hemostatic disturbances (even with normal coagulation labs)
answer_key.next_management_step: Emergent thoracolumbar MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) of the spine and immediate neurosurgical consultation

No prior history of conversion paralysis, hypertension, or stroke

challenge_prompt: A 65-year-old male farmer presents with fever, headache, and mental status changes of 3 days duration. On examination, he is confused, babbling, neck stiffness (4 transverse fingers), and positive Kernig sign. challenge_prompt: A 64-year-old woman with metastatic papillary renal cell carcinoma presents with acute onset of pulsatile headache, nausea, vomiting, impaired consciousness, and lower extremity weakness. She has been on sunitinib for approximately 2 months, initially at 50 mg daily (2-weeks-on/1-week-off) but reduced to 37.5 mg due to toxicity. On admission, blood pressure is 170/100 mmHg; she has no prior history of hypertension or neurological disorders. Brain MRI shows bilateral vasogenic edema involving cerebellar and cerebral hemispheres with confluent hyperintensities on FLAIR/T2, elevated ADC, and no diffusion restriction. EEG shows generalized background slowing with diffuse theta-delta activity. Neurological consultation and additional evaluations for metastases, infection, and metabolic causes are unremarkable. Based on the clinical presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)
answer_key.etiology: Sunitinib-induced (VEGF receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor)
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue sunitinib, initiate antihypertensive therapy, and provide supportive care; consider corticosteroid for cerebral edema if severe.

challenge_prompt: A 57-year-old male presented with acute onset dizziness and diplopia, followed within an hour by incoherent speech and drowsiness over 6 hours. No fever, headache, vomiting, or seizures. On examination: drowsy, GCS 9 (E2V3M4), pulse 65 bpm, BP 120/70 mmHg, afebrile. Pupils 3.5 mm bilaterally, sluggishly reactive. Other cranial nerves intact. challenge_prompt: A 14-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a two-week history of unsteadiness, recurrent fainting, frequent falls, ear pain, progressive vision deterioration, and repeated episodes of right-sided focal seizures. He underwent resection of a posterior fossa pilocytic astrocytoma at age 4, with gross total resection confirmed on postoperative CT, though compensated communicating hydrocephalus was noted. He was discharged but lost to follow-up for 10 years. On admission, he is alert and oriented. Physical exam shows bilateral horizontal nystagmus, fluent speech, a broad-based gait, dysmetria, positive finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin tests, and truncal ataxia. Visual assessment: right eye can only detect hand movements, left eye can count fingers only when held close; both pupils are sluggish but reactive. CT reveals a 4.9 cm AP × 4.7 cm TVS × 4.8 cm CC heterogenous mass in the posterior fossa occupying the fourth ventricle, causing moderate supratentorial hydrocephalus. The mass is confined to the posterior fossa with no supratentorial extension. MRI confirms the mass (4.3 × 4.7 × 4.5 cm) is limited to the fourth ventricle and superior cerebellar cistern, with tumoral hemorrhage, and no involvement of the optic nerves, chiasm, or optic tracts. An external ventricular drain (EVD) is placed. Three days later, subtotal resection is attempted via a transtentorial approach (4-hour procedure). Intraoperative blood loss occurs (INR 1.6), requiring transfusion, but the patient remains hemodynamically stable with no hypotension. No immediate postoperative change in vision is noted. Postoperatively, the EVD is accidentally dislodged, requiring emergent reinsertion. The patient is suspected to have meningitis and started on vancomycin and meropenem. One week after resection, a second visual assessment shows no light perception in either eye. Dilated fundal exam reveals severe bilateral optic disc atrophy with hard exudates. The patient then undergoes ventriculoperitoneal shunt insertion. One month later, prior to any radiotherapy, a third visual assessment confirms persistent bilateral no light perception. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely cause of this patient's progressive vision loss and blindness, and what is the most appropriate immediate management step?

aliases: Chronic papilledema leading to secondary optic atrophy
answer_key.diagnosis: Prolonged elevated intracranial pressure (ICP) from obstructive hydrocephalus secondary to recurrent posterior fossa tumor
answer_key.etiology: Obstructive hydrocephalus due to recurrent pilocytic astrocytoma in the fourth ventricle
answer_key.next_management_step: Urgent cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) diversion (e.g., ventriculoperitoneal shunt or external ventricular drain)

challenge_prompt:
A 13-year-old male presented with cognitive decline over one month, worsened by headaches for 10 days after a minor head fall. Physical exam showed alertness but impaired fluency, orientation, memory, and calculation. Labs revealed homocysteine 48.1 µmol/L; other tests normal. MRI showed mild cerebral atrophy. He was treated with vitamin B12, B6, and vinpocetine, with significant improvement. At age 18, he was readmitted with decreased memory, emotional disturbances, sluggishness, and headaches. Exam showed reduced mental vitality, impaired memory/calculation. MRI/MRV demonstrated cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (right transverse, sigmoid sinus, internal jugular vein). Labs: homocysteine 100.00 µmol/L, mild anemia (Hb 117 g/L), ESR 48 mm/h, CSF normal. He improved with low molecular weight heparin, antibiotics, folic acid, methylcobalamin, and vitamin B6. A third admission at age 18 for recurrent emotional disturbances, sluggishness, leg weakness, and gait difficulty showed similar findings: homocysteine 99.90 µmol/L, mild cerebral atrophy, and residual venous sinus abnormalities. Based on the overall presentation, what is the most likely underlying diagnosis, and what diagnostic test should be performed to confirm?

Plasma methylmalonic acid level was markedly elevated.

answer_key.diagnosis: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocysteinemia (cobalamin C deficiency, cblC type)

challenge_prompt:
A 21-year-old man presented with blurred vision in his right eye starting in April 2022, progressing to involve the entire visual field over one month. One month later, he developed bilateral lower limb weakness, particularly foot drop, and subsequently his left eye became affected. He lost the ability to walk independently and had difficulty maintaining a grip on objects. He was treated with intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g/day for 3 days, followed by 500 mg/day for 3 days) then oral prednisolone 60 mg/day tapered by 5 mg weekly. At the time of referral to our neurology department three months after symptom onset, he was on prednisolone 45 mg/day. There was no history of recent travel, new medications, or toxic exposure. Family history was unremarkable for neurological or autoimmune disorders.

Neurological examination: isocoric pupils (3 mm) with prompt light reflexes, no relative afferent pupillary defect, intact extraocular movements, mild distal limb weakness, diffusely hyporeflexic tendon reflexes, ataxic gait, intention tremor, dysmetria on heel-to-shin and finger-to-nose tests.

Best-corrected visual acuity: 0.5 in both eyes. Funduscopy: normal optic discs. Humphrey visual field perimetry: normal. Visual evoked potentials (VEP): bilaterally prolonged P100 latencies. Optical coherence tomography (OCT): normal retinal nerve fiber layer thickness.

Cerebral MRI: multiple scattered demyelinating lesions. Optic nerve MRI: possible optic neuritis.

Electrophysiological studies: demyelinating sensorimotor polyneuropathy with prolonged distal motor latencies, reduced motor and sensory nerve conduction velocities, prolonged F-wave latencies in the right ulnar nerve, and absent F-waves in multiple nerves of upper and lower extremities.

Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis: protein 344 mg/dL (normal <45 mg/dL), white cell count 0 cells/mm³, negative oligoclonal IgG bands.

Serological testing by fixed cell-based immunofluorescence assay: positive for IgG4 anti-NF155 antibodies.

Right sural nerve biopsy: semi-thin sections showed mildly reduced density of small-diameter myelinated fibers in individual nerve fascicles, demyelination, and occasional axonal degeneration.

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis (specific disease entity) and what is the most appropriate next management step?

aliases: Anti-NF155 antibody-positive combined central and peripheral demyelination
answer_key.diagnosis: Combined central and peripheral demyelination (CCPD) associated with IgG4 anti-NF155 antibodies (IgG4 anti-NF155-positive CCPD)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune, IgG4 anti-neurofascin 155 antibodies disrupting paranodal axo-glial junctions
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate rituximab therapy (B-cell depletion) combined with corticosteroid pulse therapy (e.g., intravenous methylprednisolone followed by oral taper)

Normal optic disc appearance and OCT
Poor response to initial corticosteroid taper
Persistent anti-NF155 antibody titer despite clinical improvement

Demyelinating sensorimotor polyneuropathy on electrophysiological studies

Negative CSF oligoclonal IgG bands
Seropositivity for IgG4 anti-NF155 antibodies
Sural nerve biopsy showing demyelination and mild axonal loss

challenge_prompt: A 35-year-old previously healthy male farmer was brought to the emergency department after being struck by lightning while working in an open field during a thunderstorm. According to eyewitnesses, the patient collapsed immediately and remained unresponsive for approximately 2–3 minutes before being transported to the hospital. On arrival, he had regained consciousness and was alert and oriented, complaining of severe burning pain in the right thigh and reporting acute bilateral lower limb and left upper limb weakness. Vital signs: blood pressure 130/80 mmHg, heart rate 102 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, axillary temperature 36.8°C. Pulses in bilateral lower limbs were diminished, and the limbs were swollen, cool, and pale. Neurological examination: motor strength was reduced (left lower limb 3/5, right lower limb 1/5, left upper limb 3/5, right upper limb normal, using MRC scale); tone was decreased in bilateral lower limbs and left upper limb; plantar reflexes were bilaterally mute; deep tendon reflexes were diminished; sensory modalities and cranial nerve function were intact. The left upper limb weakness improved within 6 hours, and bilateral lower limb power gradually improved within 12 hours. Cutaneous examination revealed a large, irregular partial-thickness burn over the anterior right thigh extending to the proximal right leg, with erythema, epidermal sloughing, and moist pink dermis, as well as well-demarcated hyperpigmentation and dry eschar at the periphery; no charring, deep tissue necrosis, or Lichtenberg figures were present. Laboratory tests on admission: serum potassium 2.8 mmol/L; subsequent labs showed serum creatine kinase peaking at 10,612 U/L on day 2, with normal renal function, negative urine myoglobin, and otherwise unremarkable electrolytes, complete blood count, cardiac enzymes, and urinalysis. The patient was admitted for observation and supportive management. No neuroimaging or neurophysiological studies were performed.

aliases: Lightning strike with transient paralysis and rhabdomyolysis
answer_key.diagnosis: Lightning-induced keraunoparalysis and rhabdomyolysis
answer_key.etiology: Lightning strike causing temporary autonomic nervous system overstimulation (keraunoparalysis) and direct muscle injury (rhabdomyolysis)
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue supportive care: aggressive intravenous fluid hydration to maintain urine output and prevent acute kidney injury; monitor serum creatine kinase, renal function, and electrolytes; provide analgesia; assess and treat burns with wound care; correct hypokalemia; monitor for arrhythmias; avoid unnecessary invasive interventions given the transient nature of the paralysis
optional_findings: Severe burning pain in right thigh
Intact sensory modalities and cranial nerves
No evidence of fractures, spinal cord injury, or intracranial pathology

Transient bilateral lower limb and left upper limb weakness improving within 12 hours
Diminished pulses in bilateral lower limbs with cool, pale extremities
Hypokalemia (2.8 mmol/L)
Elevated serum creatine kinase peaking at 10,612 U/L
Negative urine myoglobin
Normal renal function
Partial-thickness burn on right thigh without Lichtenberg figures

challenge_prompt:
A 61-year-old male from Guangxi presents with a 3-day history of fever and a 2-day history of bilateral lower limb weakness and urinary retention, following moxibustion-induced burns on the lower back. He has a 1-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus with poor glycemic control recently, and a history of chronic low back pain treated with moxibustion. No recent surgery, trauma, indwelling devices, or injections. On exam: temperature 38.9°C, pulse 103, BP 120/71, oxygen saturation 99%. Coarse breath sounds with scattered wet rales. On the lower back, a 3×3 cm burn wound with redness, swelling, and elevated skin temperature. Neurological exam: reduced light touch and pinprick sensation at T8-T12, absent below L1, motor strength 0-1/5 in lower extremities, decreased reflexes. Labs: WBC 23.02×10^9/L, CRP 147.9 mg/L, procalcitonin 0.11 ng/mL, IL-6 704.90 pg/mL, ESR 61 mm/h, fasting glucose 9.3 mmol/L, HbA1c 8.2%. Pus and blood cultures grew MRSA. Transesophageal echocardiography normal. Thoracic spine MRI shows T8-T9 vertebral osteomyelitis and epidural abscess with spinal cord compression. PET-CT shows increased FDG uptake in T8-T9 vertebrae, right 7th-8th intercostal space, liver segment S2, right upper lobe, mediastinal and bilateral hilar lymph nodes.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

answer_key.diagnosis: Disseminated methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infection secondary to moxibustion-induced burns

challenge_prompt: A 75-year-old right-handed woman presents with a 2-year history of progressive anomia and amnesia. She reports word-finding difficulty and trouble spelling. For 2 months before admission, she has had repeated visual hallucinations of an unfamiliar person approximately once a week. At age 60, she had an acute episode of amnesia and headaches due to a venous infarction in the left anterior temporal lobe, from which she fully recovered. On examination, she has word-finding difficulty and impaired word comprehension, but fluent speech without distortion or agrammatism. There is constipation, orthostatic hypotension, and hyposomnia; no REM sleep behavior disorder. Neurological exam shows no motor or sensory deficits, no parkinsonism. On the odor stick identification test, she scores 2/12 (hyposmia). No behavioral or personality changes. Neuropsychological testing: Western Aphasia Battery reveals anomia, poor single-word comprehension, superficial dyslexia, with preserved repetition and speech fluency; Test of Lexical Processing in Aphasia shows two-way anomia for 23 words; Montreal Cognitive Assessment score 12/30; Mini-Mental State Examination 21/30; Raven's Colored Progressive Matrices show mild visuospatial dysfunction. MRI shows diffuse mild atrophy and an old venous infarction in the left anterior temporal lobe. 123I-IMP SPECT shows hypoperfusion in the left anterior temporal lobe and bilateral parieto-occipital regions. 123I-ioflupane DaTscan shows reduced striatal uptake bilaterally. 123I-MIBG myocardial scintigraphy shows significantly reduced cardiac uptake. Based on these findings, what is the most likely underlying etiology of her language syndrome, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) as the underlying etiology, presenting as semantic variant primary progressive aphasia (svPPA)

answer_key.next_management_step: Start treatment with a cholinesterase inhibitor such as donepezil

challenge_prompt: A 12-year-old male presents with sudden onset of aphasia and left-sided limb weakness for half a day. He had a runny nose one week prior and a fall five days prior without seeking medical attention. Physical exam: unable to speak, left limb strength 3/5, right limb strength 4/5, normal tone, left knee jerk and Achilles tendon reflex slightly diminished, left Babinski sign positive. challenge_prompt:
A 10-year-old girl, born at term after uncomplicated pregnancy, with no family history of neuromuscular disorders, had normal psychomotor development. From early childhood, she had difficulty lifting her feet during gait (bilateral steppage gait). At age 9, progressive gait instability and increased falls led to referral. Neurological examination of the lower limbs revealed areflexia, Achilles tendon contracture, atrophy of the anterior compartment muscles, bilateral pes cavus, and reduced ankle dorsiflexion strength. Upper limb exam was normal. Serum creatine kinase was normal. Nerve conduction studies showed bilaterally reduced compound muscle action potential amplitudes of the deep peroneal nerve (below 3 mV) with preserved distal latencies and conduction velocities. All other motor and sensory nerves (median, posterior tibial, sural) were normal. Needle EMG of the tibialis anterior revealed myotonic-like discharges on the left, with low-amplitude, short-duration motor unit action potentials and reduced recruitment. MLPA testing for spinal muscular atrophy (SMN1 deletion) was negative. ECG and echocardiogram were normal. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what diagnostic test would best confirm it?

Genetic testing revealed a heterozygous truncating variant in the TTN gene, confirmed by Sanger sequencing.

aliases: Hereditary distal myopathy with peripheral nerve involvement due to TTN mutation
answer_key.diagnosis: Titinopathy (TTN-related distal myopathy with axonal peripheral neuropathy)
answer_key.etiology: Heterozygous truncating mutation in TTN gene
answer_key.next_management_step: Whole-exome sequencing or targeted genetic panel for inherited neuromuscular disorders, with specific analysis of TTN
optional_findings: No family history of neuromuscular disorders

challenge_prompt:
A 45-year-old Caucasian man presented with progressively worsening occipital headache, dizziness, and gait instability. He had a history of liver cirrhosis (abstinent for 8 years). On exam, he was oriented but dysarthric, with horizontal nystagmus, dysdiadochokinesis, dysmetria, and intention tremor, and was drowsy. Vital signs: temperature 37.1°C, BP 110/78, RR 18, HR 112. Blood tests showed no leukocytosis but elevated CRP (128 mg/L). CT head showed a hypodense lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere, repeat CT showed progression with mass effect on the fourth ventricle and obstructive hydrocephalus. MRI brain demonstrated a gadolinium-enhancing lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere with restricted diffusion and T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, suspicious for cerebellar abscess. Chest radiograph showed bilateral atypical opacities; chest CT revealed ground-glass opacities. Flow cytometry of peripheral blood showed marked T-lymphocytopenia: CD3+ 44.6% (223/µL), CD4+ 20.2% (101/µL), CD8+ 23.0% (115/µL), CD4/CD8 ratio 0.88, NK cells 5.0%, B cells 46.9%. Extensive microbiological testing (serum and CSF) for TB, HIV, EBV, CMV, HSV, Cryptococcus, Coccidioides, Pneumocystis jirovecii, T. pallidum, and Toxoplasma was negative except bronchoalveolar lavage was positive for P. jirovecii DNA (52,250 copies/mL). Blood and urine cultures were negative. What is the most likely diagnosis (including both the underlying condition and the causative organism of the abscess)?

Culture of the cerebellar abscess material grew Streptococcus intermedius. Biopsy of the lung lesion revealed non-caseating granulomas consistent with sarcoidosis.

answer_key.diagnosis: Sarcoidosis presenting with cerebellar abscess due to Streptococcus intermedius
answer_key.etiology: Sarcoidosis with secondary immunodeficiency leading to opportunistic infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Transbronchial biopsy to confirm sarcoidosis; targeted antibiotic therapy (ceftriaxone + metronidazole) for S. intermedius abscess

CD4+ T-cell lymphocytopenia (101/µL)

Negative HIV serology
Negative TB testing

title: Streptococcus intermedius cerebellar abscess as first presentation of sarcoidosis: a case report

challenge_prompt:
A 52-year-old woman and a 46-year-old man underwent orthotopic liver transplantation for decompensated cirrhosis secondary to alcoholic liver disease. Pre-transplant neurological assessment showed only fine postural tremor, mild psychomotor slowing, and minimal attentional deficit; EEG was normal. Postoperative recovery was uncomplicated; they regained full consciousness. Within 24 hours, triple immunosuppression was started: cyclosporine A 8 mg/kg/day, mycophenolate mofetil, and prednisone. On postoperative day 3, both patients became confused with psychomotor agitation, then mute. Over the next two days, they developed altered consciousness with intermittent alertness, but no spontaneous motor or verbal responses, unresponsive to noxious stimuli; sleep-wake cycle was conserved. Neurological exam showed oculogyric upward deviation of gaze, intact oculocephalic and corneal reflexes, sporadic myoclonic-like movements without EEG correlate. EEG showed mild background slowing. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was normal; fungal, bacterial, and viral cultures negative. Blood pressure normal. Serum sodium, potassium, magnesium, phosphate, cholesterol, renal and liver function tests normal. Daily cyclosporine blood levels were therapeutic. MRI at clinical onset: bilateral symmetrical hyperintensities on T2-FLAIR and DWI involving temporo-parieto-occipital cortex. In one patient, ADC values were normal (mean ± SD 0.830 ± 0.097 × 10^3 mm²/s). In the other patient, ADC values were decreased (mean ± SD 0.604 ± 0.116 × 10^3 mm²/s) with additional hyperintensities in basal ganglia and thalami.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) induced by cyclosporine A (calcineurin inhibitor neurotoxicity)
answer_key.etiology: Cyclosporine A neurotoxicity
answer_key.next_management_step: Withdrawal or dose reduction of cyclosporine; consider switching to alternative immunosuppressant (e.g., mycophenolate mofetil, sirolimus); supportive care with close neurological monitoring.

normal liver and renal function tests
additional hyperintensities in basal ganglia and thalami (in the patient with decreased ADC)
pre-transplant mild psychomotor slowing and minimal attentional deficit

normal ADC values (0.830 ± 0.097 × 10^3 mm²/s) in one patient
decreased ADC values (0.604 ± 0.116 × 10^3 mm²/s) in the other patient
normal CSF analysis
normal blood pressure

title: Diffusion MRI Findings in Encephalopathy Induced by Immunosuppressive Therapy after Liver Transplantation

challenge_prompt: A 37-year-old immunocompetent man presented with new-onset generalized seizures, progressive memory impairment, episodic headaches, and generalized weakness. Neurological examination showed mild cognitive deficits, GCS 15/15. Brain MRI revealed a giant left frontal AVM measuring 63×51 mm, classified as Spetzler-Martin grade V and Buffalo grade 5, supplied by bilateral internal and common carotid arteries and vertebral arteries, with superficial and deep venous drainage into sagittal and transverse sinuses and a 13.1×13.6 mm deep venous aneurysm. He underwent three-stage endovascular embolization with Onyx in September 2022, December 2022, and April 2023, all technically successful. One month after the final embolization, he was readmitted with fever (39°C), motor aphasia, right facial asymmetry, and right-sided hemiparesis. Brain MRI showed a 53×40×30 mm abscess in the left frontal lobe surrounding the embolized nidus, with a 14 mm midline shift. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (16.65×10^9/L) and elevated CRP (59.8 mg/L). Blood and urine cultures were negative. Transthoracic echocardiography, full-body imaging, and oral/ENT evaluations were unremarkable. No gastrointestinal or urinary symptoms. Lumbar puncture was refused. Emergency left frontal craniotomy was performed; approximately 40 mL of purulent material was aspirated, and a continuous drainage system placed. Cultures of the abscess grew hemolytic Escherichia coli (108 CFU/mL), sensitive to ceftriaxone, meropenem, ciprofloxacin, and amikacin. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next management step?

aliases: Post-embolization cerebral abscess due to E. coli
E. coli brain abscess associated with intracranial foreign body
answer_key.diagnosis: Hemolytic Escherichia coli brain abscess secondary to infected embolic material (Onyx) following AVM embolization
answer_key.etiology: Infection of retained Onyx embolic material with hemolytic E. coli, likely introduced during endovascular procedure or via transient bacteremia, with biofilm formation on synthetic material
answer_key.next_management_step: Complete surgical excision of the infected AVM nidus and retained embolic material, combined with culture-directed intravenous antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone) for at least 4 weeks, followed by oral antibiotics

title: Unexpected infectious complication following AVM embolization: E. coli brain abscess

challenge_prompt:
A 17-year-old Caucasian female with no past medical history and no previous steroid use presented to the emergency department with a 6-day history of progressive, erythematous, vesicular rash, accompanied by neck pain and stiffness. She denied similar prior symptoms and initially attributed the rash to a tick bite, though no tick or punctate lesion was identified. She reported primary varicella infection at 6 months of age following contact from an infected sibling, although no confirmatory testing was performed at the time. She received all her childhood immunizations in the United States, including 2 doses of the varicella vaccine at ages 12 months and 5 years old.

She was initially evaluated at an outside hospital 2 days prior where she was diagnosed with herpes zoster and was prescribed oral valacyclovir (dose and frequency unknown), lidocaine, and oxycodone. Despite treatment, new vesicles appeared, and existing lesions worsened. Two days later, she developed a unilateral facial droop, hearing loss, tinnitus, and blurry vision, prompting presentation to the emergency department.

On admission, her vital signs were within normal limits. Physical examination revealed a female of stated age in moderate distress due to neck pain. Her head was normocephalic and atraumatic. Her pupils were equally round and reactive to light, and extraocular movements were intact. No relative afferent pupillary defect was noted. There was normal external ear anatomy with right ear erythema and several 1 to 2 mm painful and pruritic vesicles with crusting on an erythematous base along the helix. No lesions were present in the concha bowl or external acoustic meatus. The tympanic membranes were intact without effusion. Weber test lateralized to the left with air conduction greater than bone bilaterally. Cardiac exam revealed a regular rate and rhythm, with a soft, low-pitched, I/VI systolic murmur at the left upper sternal border. No rubs or gallops were present. The lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally without wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. On neurologic examination, she was alert and oriented to person, place, and time. There was hypoesthesia along the right V2, V3, C3, and C4 sensory dermatomes, while the motor function of cranial nerve V and the remaining cranial nerves II-VI and IX-XII were intact. She had mild facial drooping along the right nasolabial fold and asymmetric smile. However, eyebrow lift was normal bilaterally. Unilateral hearing loss to finger rub and tinnitus were also present. Dermatologic exam demonstrated multiple grouped, ruptured, pruritic, and painful vesicles (2-5 mm) coalescing into bullae on an erythematous base with a hemorrhagic crust and minimal scale located on the lateral face, neck, chest, shoulder, back, and preauricular region spanning the V3-C5 dermatomes, without crossing midline. There was no oral or ocular involvement.

Laboratory evaluation revealed a positive VZV polymerase chain reaction (PCR) from both the lesion and serum, confirming active infection. VZV IgG titer was positive. The complete metabolic panel showed transaminitis with ALT 483 U/l (reference range 0-33 U/l), AST 214 U/l (reference range 0-32 U/l), and alkaline phosphatase of 242 U/l (reference range 45-87 U/l). Complete blood count revealed mild thrombocytopenia (platelet count 138 × 10^9/l, reference range: 150-450 × 10^9/l) with normal leukocyte and hemoglobin levels. Immunologic workup demonstrated elevated IgM (375 mg/dl, reference range: 40-230 mg/dl), normal IgG, modestly elevated CD3 and CD8 counts, slightly reduced CD4 count, and normal B and NK cell populations. Hepatitis serologies demonstrated nonreactive hepatitis B surface antigen (HbsAg), hepatitis B surface antibody (Anti-HBs), and hepatitis B core antibody (anti-HBc) as well as negative hepatitis A IgM antibody (IgM anti-HAV) indicating no evidence of prior hepatitis B infection or immunity despite the patient receiving both hepatitis A and B immunizations. The patient reported no sexual history or history of sexually transmitted infections. HIV serology obtained during hospitalization was negative.

Based on the presentation and initial workup, what is the most likely diagnosis and the next appropriate management step?

aliases: Herpes zoster with Ramsay Hunt Syndrome
Reactivated VZV with cranial nerve involvement
Shingles complicated by Ramsay Hunt Syndrome
answer_key.diagnosis: Disseminated varicella-zoster virus infection with Ramsay Hunt Syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Reactivation of varicella-zoster virus
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate intravenous acyclovir, consider adding corticosteroids, and implement aggressive pain management
optional_findings: Mildly elevated CD3 and CD8 counts
required_findings: Positive VZV PCR from lesion and serum
Transaminitis (ALT 483, AST 214)

Vesicular rash spanning multiple dermatomes (V3-C5)

title: Severe Disseminated Varicella Zoster in an Immunocompetent Adolescent: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 73-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with sudden onset of posterior neck and back pain. She had no history of trauma or hypertension and was not taking anticoagulant or antiplatelet agents. On arrival, her vital signs were stable. She initially reported mild and transient paresthesia in the right upper limb involving the C6 and C7 dermatomes, but this symptom resolved spontaneously upon arrival. A subsequent thorough neurological examination revealed no objective motor weakness, persistent sensory loss, or sphincter dysfunction. Laboratory investigations, including platelet counts and coagulation studies, were within normal limits. Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck and chest, evaluated using soft-tissue windows, revealed no vascular abnormalities but demonstrated a hyperattenuating lesion in the epidural space surrounding the cervical and upper thoracic spinal cord on axial images. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Spontaneous spinal epidural hematoma (SSEH)
answer_key.etiology: Likely venous origin (rupture of internal vertebral venous plexus)
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform urgent MRI to confirm diagnosis and then initiate conservative management with analgesia, blood pressure control, and close neurological monitoring
optional_findings: CT performed to exclude aortic dissection

challenge_prompt:
A 46-year-old female with mild asthma was admitted with SARS-CoV-2 pneumonia. During admission, incidental hepatic cystic lesions were identified on CT. challenge_prompt:
A 25-year-old primigravida at 37 weeks and 1 day presents with a 2-day headache and 1-month bilateral leg swelling. She reports a preceding upper respiratory infection but no nausea, vomiting, visual disturbances, or right upper quadrant pain. Past medical history is unremarkable. Vital signs: BP 161/120 mmHg, pulse 80 bpm, temperature 36.8°C, RR 20/min. Physical exam: bilateral pitting edema (++), normal cardiopulmonary exam. Obstetric exam: fetus cephalic, LOA, FHR 145 bpm, intact membranes with scant light red discharge. Labs: proteinuria (2+), hypoalbuminemia (32.1 g/L), hyperuricemia (422 μmol/L), LDH 315 U/L, normal ALT/AST/platelets/Cr, fibrinogen 4.13 g/L, D-dimer 1.26 mg/L. Obstetric ultrasound: estimated fetal weight ~2200 g, high uterine artery PI, left early diastolic notch. Fundoscopy: no papilledema. Severe preeclampsia is diagnosed. Initial treatment with labetalol 100 mg TID and magnesium sulfate. Blood pressure fluctuates (120-150/80-100 mmHg) and headache persists. Emergency cesarean section is performed due to severe preeclampsia with neurological symptoms. Postpartum, CT head shows enlargement of lateral ventricles (left 3.7 cm, right 4.6 cm) and third ventricle (3.4 cm) with periventricular hypodensity; fourth ventricle and aqueduct are normal. MRI confirms supratentorial ventriculomegaly (left lateral ventricle 5.1 cm, right 4.4 cm, third ventricle 3.0 cm) with no abnormal cerebellar signals. Neurological exam: alert, oriented, normal strength, positive right Babinski sign. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 105 mmH2O, slow flow, clear CSF.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

Phase-contrast MRI demonstrated absent CSF flow through the cerebral aqueduct, confirming obstructive hydrocephalus.

answer_key.diagnosis: Obstructive (non-communicating) hydrocephalus
answer_key.etiology: Uncertain; possibly congenital CSF outflow restriction decompensated by pregnancy-related hemodynamic and osmotic changes
answer_key.next_management_step: Neurosurgical consultation, close monitoring, and conservative management with osmotherapy (e.g., mannitol) as needed; no immediate CSF shunting warranted given stable neurological status

challenge_prompt: A 56-year-old African American man presents with progressive disequilibrium, lower extremity weakness, difficulty walking, and hearing loss over 2 years. On examination, he has mildly unsteady tandem gait, slightly decreased hearing bilaterally, mildly decreased pinprick sensation in bilateral distal lower extremities, hip flexion/extension 4/5 bilaterally, otherwise 5/5 strength, and symmetric reflexes 3/4. Initial MRI brain and CT head are normal. Lumbar puncture shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, hypoglycorrhachia, and hyperproteinorrachia; infectious workup is negative. Symptoms progress. Repeat brain MRI 2 months later shows leptomeningeal enhancement. Subsequent brain MRI shows nodular focal leptomeningeal enhancement overlying the superior cerebellum, interpeduncular cistern, frontal region, superior temporal lobe, periventricular region, near the obex, and in the left internal auditory canal. PET shows hypermetabolic right upper and lower paratracheal, right hilar, and subcarinal lymph nodes. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis now shows leukocytosis, glucose <30 mg/dL, proteins >600 mg, and ACE 8.6 U/L. Endobronchial ultrasound reveals enlarged lymph nodes. challenge_prompt: A woman in her mid-70s with a history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, Hashimoto’s thyroiditis (status post partial thyroidectomy), and COPD presented with progressively worsening shortness of breath and chest tightness for one week, worsening over one day. In the ED, she was tachycardic (atrial fibrillation, 140s) and hypoxic (70s). Exam: diffuse wheeze. Labs: leukocytosis with bandemia, negative cardiac enzymes, respiratory acidosis with CO2 retention on ABG. CXR: multifocal pneumonia. Started on BiPAP, IV methylprednisolone, IV fluids, and antibiotics (ceftriaxone, azithromycin). A CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPE) with 100 cc of Isovue 370 (iopamidol 76%) was performed. Within minutes of contrast administration, she developed acute confusion and agitation, with blood pressure 180/120 mmHg. She was drowsy and disoriented. CTPE was negative. Neurological exam: no focal motor or cranial nerve deficits, bilateral 2+ reflexes, mute plantar response, no neck stiffness, no seizure activity. Stat labs (CBC, CMP) unchanged. TSH and thyroid profile normal. ABG no hypercapnia. Blood cultures negative, procalcitonin normal. CT brain (non-contrast) within 2 hours of symptom onset: no intracranial abnormality, only chronic microangiopathic changes. She was started on nicardipine drip and IV fluids. challenge_prompt:
A previously healthy 2-year-old boy presented with a 3-day history of high-grade fever (up to 39°C axillary) and nonbloody diarrhea, seven to eight episodes daily. He was treated for presumed acute gastroenteritis with oral rehydration, antibiotics (cefdinir and metronidazole), and antipyretics, but symptoms persisted and oral intake declined. On the fourth day of illness, he was found unresponsive with teeth clenching and frothy oral secretions. At the referring hospital, he was febrile with GCS 4/15. Ophthalmologic exam showed slightly pale optic discs and sluggish pupillary reflexes. He developed persistent generalized dystonia. EEG demonstrated diffuse encephalopathy with later prominence of slow waves over the left hemisphere. Noncontrast head CT and CSF analysis (2 cells/μL) were unremarkable. Seizures were refractory to benzodiazepines and phenytoin. He was transferred to a tertiary center, where he remained encephalopathic (GCS 3-4), requiring intubation and ventilation. Comprehensive septic workup (CSF, blood, urine cultures; HSV and enterovirus PCR) was negative. Empiric antibiotics and acyclovir were discontinued. Laboratory investigations revealed: venous blood gas pH 6.9, bicarbonate 3.4 mmol/L, pCO2 11 mmHg, anion gap 24; lactate 16.6 mmol/L; glucose 1.8 mmol/L (nonketotic hypoglycemia); triglycerides 2900 mg/dL; cholesterol 375 mg/dL; AST 417 U/L, ALT 219 U/L; CPK 590 U/L. MRI brain showed bilateral symmetrical necrosis of the caudate and putamen with increased T2/FLAIR signal, diffusion restriction, low ADC, no blooming or enhancement. During hospitalization, he had a secondary febrile episode with CRP 193 mg/L; urine culture grew Klebsiella species, treated with piperacillin-tazobactam. He was extubated after 2 days. Persistent oropharyngeal dysphagia required gastrostomy tube insertion. Dystonia was managed with baclofen and trihexyphenidyl; seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam. Follow-up EEG showed persistent encephalopathic features. Biochemical markers (liver enzymes, lipid profile, lactate) normalized, but generalized dystonia persisted. Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and the next immediate management step?

Genetic analysis revealed a homozygous missense variant in the HMGCS2 gene (c.167 T>A, p.Ile56Asn).

answer_key.diagnosis: Mitochondrial 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA synthase deficiency (HMGCS2 deficiency; ketogenesis disorder)

challenge_prompt: A 74-year-old male with a history of right medullary stroke (June 2024) on aspirin 325 mg daily; poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus on insulin; hypertension on hydrochlorothiazide, hydralazine, amlodipine; hyperlipidemia on rosuvastatin 20 mg daily; coronary artery disease on isosorbide dinitrate; chronic kidney disease; and prostate cancer presented to the emergency department for evaluation of new neurological symptoms. challenge_prompt:
A 42-year-old multiparous woman (gravida 4, para 4) delivered a female infant via spontaneous vaginal delivery at 40 weeks and 5 days of gestation. The newborn had a birth weight of 3010 g and a head circumference of 34 cm (median to +1Z score). The pregnancy was non-consanguineous and regularly monitored. Third-trimester ultrasonography revealed fetal hydrocephalus with hypertension complicating pregnancy. The mother had chronic alcohol intake, consuming approximately 300 mL of locally brewed homemade alcohol (approximately 20% alcohol) daily before and throughout pregnancy. There was no history of maternal fever, rash, or exposure to teratogenic medications. At birth, the neonate exhibited a boggy swelling in the parietal region, deviation of the mouth to the right side, and an absent nasolabial fold on the left side. The infant was active with normal muscle tone, intact newborn reflexes, APGAR scores of 7 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes, and no respiratory distress. Blood investigations (complete blood count, liver function tests, renal function tests) were within normal limits. TORCH screening was negative. Echocardiography was normal. Cranial ultrasonography demonstrated near-complete replacement of the brain parenchyma by fluid with significantly dilated ventricles; the frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital brain regions could not be visualized.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?

challenge_prompt: An 18-year-old Azerbaijani female, 2 months postpartum, presents with constant chest pain for several hours (non-exertional, no radiation) and pain/swelling in the lower extremities. She denies cough, dyspnea, weight loss, night sweats, constipation, or nephrolithiasis. No prior medical history or medication use. Family history unremarkable. Vital signs: HR 115 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, RR 19/min. Exam: dry tongue/oral mucosa (volume depletion); normal heart and lung auscultation; tenderness and edema in the right thigh with a large anterior lump; normal muscle tone and range of motion; normal neurological and abdominal exams. Labs: WBC 9800/μL, Hb 11.7 g/dL, platelets 344,000/μL; creatinine 0.8 mg/dL; TSH 3.3 mIU/L; ESR 24 mm/h; calcium 13.7 mg/dL (normal 8.5-10.5); albumin 4 g/dL; CK 250 U/L (normal <300). ECG: no ischemic changes, conduction blocks, or pericarditis. hs-cTnI <14 ng/L. Echocardiography: no regional wall motion abnormalities. Further hypercalcemia workup: calcium 12.5 mg/dL, PTH 5.6 pg/mL (low), 25-OH vitamin D 27.4 ng/mL (not deficient), phosphorus 2.9 mg/dL. Malignancy workup (multiple myeloma screen, etc.) negative. MRI: mild bilateral soft-tissue edema in vastus lateralis muscles; after contrast, bilateral peripheral enhancement in pes anserine muscle groups. Ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy of the right thigh mass: fibroconnective and muscle tissue with patchy infiltration of lymphocytes and histiocytes, associated with some giant cells, without caseous necrosis. Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) level: 119.5 IU/L (normal 13-63). CT chest: no pulmonary parenchymal abnormality, no interlobular septal thickness or nodules, no hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Based on the above findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

challenge_prompt: An 11-year-old female presents to the pediatric emergency department with a one-week history of blurred vision, gait instability, nausea, vomiting, and persistent headache. She has a past medical history of congenital ectopia lentis requiring intraocular lens implantation surgery five years ago, premature graying of hair noted two years ago, and a left radial fracture sustained five months ago after a fall. There is no history of head trauma, febrile illness, or known allergies, and no family history of venous thromboembolism. On physical examination, left-sided hemiparesis with decreased muscle strength in the upper and lower limbs is noted, along with premature graying of the hair. High-resolution 3D-T1W MRI of the brain confirms extensive cerebral venous thrombosis with prominent collateral circulation. challenge_prompt: A 47-year-old right-handed Japanese woman with a medical history of a right arm injury in childhood (resulting in retained hand contracture) was admitted to the emergency room with impaired consciousness and high fever since the previous day. She was diagnosed with septic shock caused by right lithiasis pyelonephritis and required mechanical ventilatory support. A fever exceeding 40°C was sustained for four days after admission. After resolution of the infection, she developed tremors in the neck and both upper limbs and difficulty walking due to ataxia. Daily doses of clonazepam (1.5 mg) and zonisamide (100 mg) were minimally effective. Four months later, she was referred for further evaluation. On examination, she had bilateral horizontal gaze-directional nystagmus, hypermetric eye movements, highly ataxic speech, no palatal tremors, severe left-predominant bilateral motor decomposition and dysmetria on finger-to-nose test, terminal oscillation, and postural and action tremors with a frequency of 3–5 Hz in both upper limbs. No rest tremor was present. She could rise from a chair with light assistance but had severe difficulty walking due to truncal ataxia. Brain MRI showed atrophy and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) high-intensity signal changes in the bilateral cerebellum and superior cerebellar peduncles, progressing over time. 123I-IMP SPECT revealed marked hypoperfusion in the bilateral cerebellum and frontal lobes, with normal basal ganglia and brainstem perfusion. Levodopa was ineffective; beta-blockers were not prescribed due to hypotension; primidone was not tried due to ineffectiveness of zonisamide; clonazepam 6 mg and valproic acid 600 mg daily were effective for postural tremor but induced intolerable drowsiness. The patient remained motivated for treatment due to severe hand tremors interfering with daily life. What is the most likely etiology of this patient's cerebellar dysfunction, and what next management step is most appropriate?

answer_key.diagnosis: Hyperthermia-induced cerebellar dysfunction
answer_key.etiology: Prolonged hyperthermia (fever >40°C for 4 days) secondary to septic shock due to lithiasis pyelonephritis.
answer_key.next_management_step: Unilateral thalamic (ventralis intermedius nucleus) deep brain stimulation (DBS) for medication-refractory postural tremor.

challenge_prompt:
A 4-month-old boy, born at 38 weeks via normal vaginal delivery (birth weight 3.2 kg), presented with fever, lethargy, poor oral intake, and loose stools. He had a history of suspected neonatal sepsis at 5 days of life. On examination, he appeared pale and malnourished, tachycardic, tachypneic, with significant lethargy and poor feeding. Pulses were strong, precordium soft, heart sounds normal. Investigations: Arterial blood gas showed pH 7.65, pCO2 14 mmHg, HCO3- 15.5 mmol/L (primary respiratory alkalosis with partial metabolic compensation). Serum urea was elevated at 77.4 mg/dL; creatinine normal. Liver function tests slightly abnormal. Plasma ammonia 78.1 μmol/L (reference 11-32). Quantitative plasma amino acids: glutamate 263 μmol/L (18-98), citrulline 6 μmol/L (16-32), arginine and ornithine normal. Contrast-enhanced CT brain showed diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement with mild dilation of bifrontotemporal subarachnoid spaces (BESS pattern), no focal lesions or mass effect. Echocardiography revealed a small secundum ASD and tiny PDA with left-to-right shunts. Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?

Urine orotic acid was markedly elevated, and genetic testing revealed a hemizygous pathogenic variant in the OTC gene.

challenge_prompt:
A 53-year-old female presents with acute onset of dysarthria, dysmetria, lower facial weakness on the right side, and right hemiparesis (4/5 strength in arm and leg). NIHSS score is 5. Her medical history includes surgically treated neuroblastoma with left pyramidal spastic hemiparesis, thyroidectomy, no vascular risk factors, on levetiracetam and thyrohormone. She is a non-smoker, non-alcohol abuser, no family history of stroke. Previous mRS is 2. Emergency brain CT shows chronic subdural hematomas in the right hemisphere curvature, right temporal lobe glial area, and left frontoparietal calcified meningioma; no acute hemorrhage or infarct. On day 2, she worsens: right upper limb becomes hemiplegic (strength 0/5), while right lower limb strength remains 4/5. NIHSS increases to 7. Over the next 48 hours, her clinical status fluctuates, especially upper limb strength (1/5 at worst). Repeat CT shows no change. Subsequent ultrasound of extracranial carotid arteries shows no hemodynamic stenoses; echocardiogram normal; 24-hour Holter no atrial fibrillation. During hospitalization, high blood pressure is observed.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis/etiology and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or treatment step?

The brain MRI revealed an infarction in the distribution of the left anterior choroidal artery.

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute anterior choroidal artery territory infarction
answer_key.etiology: likely atherothrombotic or cryptogenic, with hypertension as a risk factor
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform brain MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) to confirm acute ischemic stroke localization; start antiplatelet therapy (aspirin) and manage blood pressure; consider prolonged cardiac monitoring if cause remains unclear.

challenge_prompt: An 8-year-old boy with pre-B cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) has been undergoing chemotherapy for 1 year, including intravenous vincristine weekly and intrathecal methotrexate (MTX) 12.5 mg monthly. Two weeks after completing his 12th intrathecal session, he developed paresthesia of the feet, followed by limb pain, limb weakness, persistent urinary retention, and fecal incontinence. He received IVIG (2 g/kg) at another center without improvement. On admission, examination revealed bilateral symmetric lower limb weakness (MMT 1/5 proximally and distally), upper limb strength 4/5, absent deep tendon reflexes in knees and ankles, no Babinski sign or clonus, and urinary retention. No sensory level, cerebellar signs, or spine tenderness were noted. Pre-chemotherapy lumbar puncture showed normal CSF glucose (56 mg/dL), protein (20 mg/dL), negative gram stain and culture, and no white blood cells or malignant cells. Laboratory tests: hemoglobin 14 g/L, ESR 26 mm/h, AST 40 IU/L, ALT 71 IU/L, ALKP 46.3 IU/L, CRP normal. Lumbosacral MRI revealed a 42×10 mm Tarlov cyst within the sacral canal with increased enhancement in the cauda equina region, particularly the ventral roots. Brain, neck, and thoracic MRI were normal. Nerve conduction study and EMG showed low-amplitude or absent compound muscle action potentials (CMAPs) in the lower limbs, with other sensory potentials and CMAPs at the lower limit of normal, consistent with bilateral lumbosacral polyradiculopathy from L2 to S1 (most severe at L5). Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next management step?

aliases: Methotrexate-induced neurotoxicity causing cauda equina syndrome
answer_key.diagnosis: Drug-induced cauda equina syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Intrathecal methotrexate chemotherapy
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue intrathecal methotrexate, administer high-dose methylprednisolone (e.g., 500 mg daily for 5 days), and provide supportive care including bladder management (Foley catheter) and rehabilitation.

challenge_prompt: A 37-year-old male military pilot, F-15, presents with an acute tingling sensation in his left lower extremity. Two days prior, he performed basic fighter maneuvers (dogfighting) with high G-force, applying excessive force to his lower extremities. He has a history of lumbar nucleus pulposus (L-HNP) diagnosed during aviation cadet training, with neuropathic pain controlled for 10 years via self-exercise and transforaminal epidural steroid injections. One month ago, he had low back pain and right lower extremity radiating pain; physical exam at that time showed negative Lasègue sign, Freiberg sign, Pace sign, and FAIR test, but positive straight leg raise (SLR) test on the right. He was treated with a transforaminal epidural injection and maintained on pregabalin and NSAIDs. Now, physical examination reveals: no sensory impairment; normal knee and ankle deep tendon reflexes; Lasègue sign, Freiberg sign, and Pace sign are positive in the left lower extremity; SLR and femoral nerve stretch tests are negative bilaterally; direct tenderness of the left piriformis muscle is noted. Plain radiography of the hip and lumbar spine shows no abnormalities. Resting and motion visual analog scale scores are 5 and 6, respectively. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute piriformis syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Acute piriformis syndrome secondary to high-G-force strain in a pilot with chronic lumbar radiculopathy

challenge_prompt:
A 40-year-old man presented with persistent visual disturbance 6 years after traumatic brain injury. He had undergone emergent decompressive craniectomy for a large right frontotemporal epidural hemorrhage, small contusional hemorrhage, and left frontotemporoparietal subdural hemorrhage caused by a fall. During recovery, he developed a new right centrum semiovale infarction. After the accident, he experienced blurred peripheral vision in both eyes. He underwent intensive rehabilitation for 4 months. Six years after discharge, he had left hemiparesis (Medical Research Council grade 3+ to 4) and intact cognitive function (30 points on the Mini Mental State Examination). Brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed the known prior infarction in the right centrum semiovale and encephalomalacia. Humphrey visual fields test revealed right upper and left lower quadrantanopsia in both eyes. Visual-evoked potentials demonstrated no response in the right upper quadrant, and prolonged P100 latency in the left upper quadrant (146 ms for left eye, 160 ms for right eye stimulation). Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what next diagnostic step is most appropriate?

Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) and tractography (DTT) revealed discontinuation of the right optic radiation in the upper and lower regions, consistent with traumatic optic radiation injury.

aliases: Quadrantanopsia due to optic radiation lesion

answer_key.etiology: Traumatic brain injury with ischemic and hemorrhagic lesions involving the optic radiation
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) and diffusion tensor tractography (DTT) of the optic radiation for objective assessment

challenge_prompt:
A 30-year-old bisexual man with a history of two prior treated syphilitic infections (RPR titers decreased appropriately after each) and well-controlled HIV (undetectable viral load, on dolutegravir/TAF/emtricitabine) presents with worsening chest pain. One month prior, he had unprotected vaginal intercourse with a female sex worker. On exam, tenderness over sternum and right sternoclavicular joint. Initially diagnosed with sternoclavicular arthritis, but 2 months later he develops limited range of motion and pain in right upper arm. Chest CT and MRI show erosive lesions on sternum and left ninth rib. Sternal biopsy reveals granulomas with caseous necrosis and bacillus-like organisms on Ziehl-Neelsen staining. PCR for M. tuberculosis, M. avium complex, and M. intracellulare are negative. Immunohistochemistry for Treponema pallidum on the biopsy is negative. He is started on antitubercular therapy. One month later, a painless erosive cutaneous lesion appears under the jaw, diagnosed as pyoderma gangrenosum and treated with prednisolone 5 mg/day. He develops periarticular pain in right wrist; MRI shows high intensity in radius, ulna, and second metacarpal. The submandibular lesion enlarges, and multiple erosive lesions appear on face and chest. Prednisolone increased to 15 mg/day. Routine RPR screening reveals a titer of 390 RU (baseline <0.99). Repeated MRI shows additional abnormal signals in ulna, radius, metacarpals, and proximal phalanx of thumb. Sternal and mandibular lesion biopsies are negative for syphilis on immunohistochemistry and Warthin-Starry staining.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Syphilitic osteomyelitis (gumma)
answer_key.etiology: Treponema pallidum
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g/day (or intravenous penicillin if no allergy) for at least 5 weeks

Granulomas with caseous necrosis on biopsy

challenge_prompt:
A 22-year-old female was brought to the hospital with vomiting, generalized weakness, and two episodes of witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizures 24 hours prior to admission. She had about 5 episodes of nonbloody nonbilious vomiting. She was nonverbal at baseline but was more lethargic than usual, had poor oral intake for 2 days, and appeared to be in pain. Review of systems negative for prior seizures, fever, diarrhea, abdominal pain, history of diuretic or laxative abuse, periorbital puffiness, or extremity swelling. She received lorazepam with resolution of seizures.

On physical exam: BP 105/56 (borderline low, close to baseline), HR 80, RR 18, O2 sat 100% on room air. Systemic exam unremarkable without overt signs of dehydration.

EKG showed U waves and nonspecific T wave changes. Labs: BUN 16, Cr 0.77, Na 150 mEq/L, K 1.4 mEq/L, Mg 2.8 mg/dL, HCO3 35 mEq/L. Urine electrolytes: K 22 mEq/L, Na 121 mEq/L, Cl 146 mEq/L. TTKG 6.82. CBC and LFTs normal. PRA 0.33 ng/ml/hr, serum aldosterone/K ratio 1/1.4, aldosterone/plasma renin ratio 3. EEG showed abnormal epileptiform activity consistent with seizure. Differential included primary hyperaldosteronism, vomiting, and Bartter/Gitelman syndrome.

Based only on the presentation above, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

Urine calcium was low.

answer_key.diagnosis: Gitelman syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Biallelic inactivating mutations in SLC12A3 gene
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for SLC12A3 mutations; initiate electrolyte replacement (potassium and magnesium supplementation) and consider antiepileptics if seizures persist; refer to nephrology.

challenge_prompt: A 22-year-old healthy man with no significant past medical history presented to the emergency department with lower extremity swelling and was found to have a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in his right popliteal, tibial, and peroneal veins. He was discharged home on rivaroxaban. He presented again two days later with right groin pain and ataxia. Ataxia started one week prior, along with frequent falls, bilateral foot numbness, and urinary retention. He endorsed daily nitrous oxide 'whippets' use for the last two years, using multiple canisters per day. On examination, heart rate 120 bpm. Neurologic exam showed proximal lower extremity weakness (3/5 bilaterally) and 3+ hyperreflexia of patellar and plantar tendons. Laboratory studies: hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL (normal 13.7–17.5), MCV 100 fL (normal 79–92), homocysteine 67.9 micromol/L (normal <16), methylmalonic acid 2.417 micromol/L, vitamin B12 187 pg/mL (normal 213–816). Peripheral smear showed hypersegmented neutrophils. CT angiogram showed extensive bilateral pulmonary embolism involving right middle, right lower, left upper, and left lower lobar arteries. MRI thoracic spine with contrast suggested areas of cord signal abnormality on T2-weighted images (likely artifact). Hypercoagulability workup: negative for prothrombin mutation, Factor V Leiden, RF, ANA, anti-dsDNA, anticardiolipin antibodies, anti-B2gp1 antibody; normal antithrombin III, protein C, protein S. Heterozygous MTHFR C677T mutation. What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?

aliases: Nitrous oxide-induced myeloneuropathy and hypercoagulable state
Nitrous oxide-associated combined subacute combined degeneration of the cord and thromboembolism
answer_key.diagnosis: Functional vitamin B12 deficiency and hyperhomocysteinemia due to chronic nitrous oxide abuse
answer_key.etiology: Chronic nitrous oxide abuse causing inactivation of vitamin B12, leading to functional B12 deficiency, elevated homocysteine, and secondary hypercoagulable state
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue nitrous oxide use, initiate vitamin B12 supplementation (intramuscular 1000 mcg weekly and oral 500 mcg daily), continue or start anticoagulation (e.g., rivaroxaban 20 mg daily) for pulmonary embolism and DVT, and monitor homocysteine and B12 levels
