challenge_prompt: A 44-year-old man presented with a sudden onset of lower extremity paresthesias, with an urge to move his legs when he rests in bed or sits for a long time. The patient met all IRLSSG criteria for restless legs syndrome: unpleasant sensation in the legs when resting, urge to move, worsening in the evening, relief with walking. Examination was normal except brisk lower extremity deep tendon reflexes. MRI of the brain revealed periventricular and pons plaques. Cervical and lumbar MRI were normal. Laboratory studies: vitamin B12, E, D levels; serum iron, iron-binding capacity, and ferritin within normal limits; autoantibody tests including ANA, anti-ds DNA, ANCA, anti-SSA, anti-SSB, and antiphospholipid antibodies were negative. No history of drug intake associated with RLS (dopamine antagonists, antidepressants, lithium). Posterior tibial somatosensory evoked potentials showed prolonged P1 and P2 latencies and central conduction time on the left side. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely underlying etiology for this patient's restless legs syndrome?

answer_key.diagnosis: Multiple sclerosis
answer_key.etiology: inflammatory demyelination of the central nervous system
answer_key.next_management_step: initiate disease-modifying therapy (e.g., interferon beta) and consider further workup for clinically definite MS

challenge_prompt:
A 20-year-old male with a history of recent head trauma presents to the emergency department with headache, fever, vomiting, and disorganized speech for 2 days. Head trauma occurred about three weeks prior during a motor vehicle accident, resulting in bilateral subdural hematoma managed by burr hole evacuation without craniotomy. He also developed cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) otorrhea managed conservatively. On physical examination, signs of meningeal irritation are present without focal neurological deficits. Brain MRI with contrast shows bilateral subdural effusion and diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement. Lumbar puncture yields turbid fluid. CSF analysis reveals: white blood cell count 2250/μL with predominant polymorphonuclear cells, protein 285 mg/dL, glucose 20 mg/dL. Blood and CSF cultures for rapid-growing bacteria, mycobacteria, fungi, Nocardia species, and Brucella species are negative. PCR for Mycobacterium tuberculosis and cryptococcal antigen are negative. CSF cytology shows no atypical cells. Broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy (meropenem and vancomycin) does not improve clinical symptoms or CSF parameters. Brain MR venography, CT cisternography, and spinal MRI are normal. Echocardiography is unremarkable. Over several weeks, repeat lumbar punctures reveal an increasing trend of CSF leukocyte count and protein, while glucose remains below 20 mg/dL. Repeated evaluations for infectious pathogens and connective tissue disorders are negative. Serum immunoglobulins and complement levels are normal. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and next management step?

After initiating methylprednisolone 1 g/day, the patient's fever resolved and CSF leukocyte count decreased to 200/μL with normalization of glucose.

answer_key.diagnosis: Steroid-responsive post-traumatic persistent neutrophilic meningitis (SPNM)
answer_key.etiology: Brain injury-induced immune dysregulation with exaggerated inflammatory response
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider a trial of corticosteroids (e.g., dexamethasone) after excluding infectious causes

challenge_prompt:
A 63-year-old right-handed female presents to the neurological emergency department with acute headache and vomiting, followed by rapid decline in consciousness (GCS 3). Her past medical history includes a severe traumatic brain injury (TBI) at age 21, after which she had no known chronic conditions and a premorbid modified Rankin score of 0. On arrival, she is sedated, intubated, and mechanically ventilated. Neurological exam reveals a non-reactive dilated right pupil; corneal and vestibulo-ocular reflexes are intact. Blood pressure is 160/90 mmHg, ECG shows sinus rhythm. Laboratory tests, including coagulation studies and tumor markers, are normal. Chest X-ray is unremarkable. Non-contrast head CT shows a 6 × 5 × 6 cm intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) in the right hemisphere, with surrounding subdural hematoma encircling the right cerebral hemisphere and tentorium, and transtentorial herniation. CT angiography is unremarkable; cerebral venous sinuses and major veins are patent. Based solely on this information, what is the most likely underlying etiology of this patient's ICH?

A cortical biopsy identified β-amyloid (Aβ) deposits in the blood vessel walls, confirming a definitive diagnosis of CAA.

answer_key.diagnosis: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA) related to remote severe traumatic brain injury
answer_key.etiology: Remote severe TBI with subsequent amyloid deposition
answer_key.next_management_step: Surgical evacuation of the ICH (already indicated), consideration of brain biopsy during surgery for definitive diagnosis, and close monitoring for recurrent hemorrhage

challenge_prompt: A 51-year-old man with Parkinson's disease (PD) diagnosed 6 years ago presents with gradually worsening left-sided neck pain for 1 week, following a mild COVID-19 infection (fever, cough, sneezing). He had transient motor aphasia lasting 2-3 hours the day before. He has levodopa-induced neck dyskinesia during on-states and chronic neck pain due to cervical spondylosis. Medications: levodopa/carbidopa, entacapone, selegiline, ropinirole tape. No vascular risk factors (smoking, hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia) or family history of arterial dissection or connective tissue disease. Neurological exam normal except for parkinsonism. MMSE 26 (lost 4 points on serial 7s). CRP mildly elevated at 0.46 mg/dL (normal <0.14). Liver and renal function normal. Brain CT shows a low-density area in the left parietal lobe. MRI reveals acute infarctions in the left hemisphere. An intramural hematoma is seen in an area of stenosis in the left internal carotid artery approximately 2 cm proximal to the skull base. No atrial fibrillation, intracardiac thrombus, or carotid plaque. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate management step?

answer_key.etiology: Likely mechanical stress from levodopa-induced neck dyskinesia and COVID-19 infection causing endothelial dysfunction

challenge_prompt: A 50-year-old man presented with complaints of abdominal (left lower and left upper quadrant) and left lower limb pain, as well as weakness, from 27 days ago that worsened gradually until the day of attendance. There were no past medical diseases, past surgical histories, or drug histories. He and his family had a history of caring for animals such as sheep, goats, and dogs, and resided in Bushehr city in southwestern Iran, an area endemic for hydatid cysts. On physical examination, he had a fever (temperature: 38.5°C), pain on active and passive flexion of the left lower extremity, and left lower quadrant abdominal tenderness. Routine laboratory tests were normal. Ultrasound revealed a hyperechoic solid cystic structure measuring 86 × 36 mm in the left lower quadrant of the abdomen, suggesting a collection. CT scan showed a cystic mass measuring 71 × 43 × 128 mm on the left side of the abdominal pelvic cavity, extending to the left lumbar muscle. No cystic lesions were seen in the liver or lungs. Based on this presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

challenge_prompt:
A 51-year-old Chinese female was admitted with cough, sputum, and chest tightness for 5 days, worsening over 1 day. On hospital day 2, she suffered sudden cardiac and respiratory arrest, underwent CPR and intubation. After restoration of spontaneous circulation, she was transferred to the ICU. Two extubation attempts failed due to poor cough and expectoration. She underwent tracheostomy and mechanical ventilation. Ventilator weaning was attempted 5 days later with high-flow oxygen via tracheostomy. She was transferred to rehabilitation for further care.

Physical exam: Conscious but weak mental state. Bilateral hearing impairment. Pupils: right ~3.5 cm, left ~3 cm (unequal), light reflexes brisk. Palpebral fissures narrowed bilaterally. Chest symmetrical, coarse breath sounds, no significant chest rise and fall. Proximal limb muscle strength 4/5, distal 3/5. Diminished grip strength. Tendon reflexes absent. Babinski negative.

Labs (day 1): PaCO2 69.5 mmHg, PaO2 78.4 mmHg, A-a gradient 91 mmHg, WBC 15.3×10^9/L (88.7% neutrophils), CRP 121.8 mg/L. Repeat labs (day ~64): PaCO2 51.5 mmHg, PaO2 79.4 mmHg, A-a gradient 74 mmHg, WBC 6.6×10^9/L, CRP 41.7 mg/L.

Chest CT: Bilateral lung inflammation; atrophy of chest wall muscles. Abdominal CT: Atrophy of abdominal wall muscles. EMG: Myogenic damage in upper and lower limb muscles. Muscle biopsy: Myopathic pattern of damage without dystrophic features.

Based only on the presentation above, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

Genetic testing revealed a mutation in the RYR1 gene.

answer_key.diagnosis: RYR1-related myopathy (RYR1-RM)
answer_key.etiology: RYR1 gene mutation
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform whole exome sequencing or targeted RYR1 genetic testing; optimize respiratory support and weaning strategies.

absent tendon reflexes

myogenic damage on EMG
myopathic pattern on muscle biopsy without dystrophic features
CT showing atrophy of chest and abdominal wall muscles

title: Respiratory Failure Associated With Mutations in the RYR1 Gene: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 70-year-old man presents with a progressively enlarging scalp skin tumor over six months. Physical examination shows erythematous skin lesions with yellow crust and erosion on the scalp. Differential diagnoses include cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, metastatic tumor, angiosarcoma, and lymphoid malignancies. A skin biopsy shows diffuse proliferation of medium-to-large atypical lymphocytes extending from dermis to subcutaneous tissue. Immunohistochemistry: positive for CD20, BCL2, BCL6, and MUM1; negative for CD3, CD5, CD10, and cyclin D1. Ki-67 labeling index is approximately 80%. Laboratory findings: lactate dehydrogenase 260 U/L, soluble interleukin-2 receptor 5237 U/mL, serum calcium 11.7 mg/dL. Computed tomography shows a large soft tissue mass (56 × 133 × 124 mm) in the posterior scalp with osteolytic skull destruction. Magnetic resonance imaging shows tumor extension into the superior sagittal sinus and contrast enhancement in the leptomeninges. Positron emission tomography-computed tomography reveals increased uptake in the scalp skin tumor, left palatine tonsil, right seminal vesicle, and ascending colon. Bone marrow examination shows infiltration of atypical lymphocytes. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate next management step?

aliases: Systemic DLBCL with secondary cutaneous involvement
answer_key.diagnosis: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), non-germinal center B-cell-like (non-GCB) subtype (ABC-like), stage IV with suspected CNS involvement
answer_key.etiology: Not definitively determined; presumptive systemic DLBCL
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate systemic chemotherapy with Pola-R-CHP (polatuzumab vedotin, rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, prednisone) combined with high-dose methotrexate (1.6 g/m²) and intrathecal chemotherapy (methotrexate, prednisolone, cytarabine)
optional_findings: Extension into superior sagittal sinus on MRI
Increased FDG uptake in left palatine tonsil, right seminal vesicle, ascending colon on PET
required_findings: CD20 positivity
CD10 negativity
BCL6 positivity
MUM1 positivity
Osteolytic skull destruction
Leptomeningeal enhancement
Bone marrow infiltration

title: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presenting as a scalp tumor with skull destruction and neurological symptoms: a case report

challenge_prompt:
A 24-year-old Caucasian male graduate student presents to the emergency department after a brief loss of consciousness at home. Paramedics measured blood glucose at 47 mg/dL. He reports a 3-month history of gradual loss of appetite, persistent diarrhea, and 7-kg weight loss (from 80 to 73 kg), along with weakness, dizziness, and general malaise. He has no prior medical history. His older sister has celiac disease. Initial labs show positive antinuclear antibody (1:160X, reference negative <1:40X). Subsequent serologies: anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA negative (1 U/mL, positive ≥15 U/mL), anti-deamidated gliadin peptide IgA positive (118 U/mL, positive ≥15 U/mL). He is nondiabetic. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with small intestinal biopsy reveals Marsh classification type I histopathological changes (increased intraepithelial lymphocytes). What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

Genetic testing for HLA-DQ2 and DQ8 was performed, which was positive.

answer_key.diagnosis: Celiac disease
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune reaction to gluten in genetically predisposed individual
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate strict gluten-free diet

chronic diarrhea and weight loss
older sister with celiac disease
positive anti-deamidated gliadin peptide IgA (118 U/mL)
negative anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA
small intestinal biopsy Marsh type I

title: A rare case report of celiac disease: Hypoglycemic coma in a nondiabetic patient

challenge_prompt: A 65-year-old male heavy smoker (45 pack-year) underwent radical left upper lobectomy for a 7.0 cm × 5.0 cm × 5.0 cm invasive adenocarcinoma of the lung. Pathologic staging was pT3N0M0 (stage IIB), with clear margins. Immunohistochemistry: TTF-1 diffuse+, napsin A+, P53 mutant pattern+, Ki-67 70%. Molecular testing by ARMS-PCR: EGFR wild-type (exons 18-21), ALK rearrangement-negative. He received 3 cycles of adjuvant docetaxel plus cisplatin. Five days after the third cycle, he developed grade 2 neurological symptoms. Brain MRI revealed a 2.4 cm right parietal metastasis with significant perilesional edema. He was treated with hippocampal-sparing whole-brain radiotherapy (30 Gy/15 fractions with boost) and systemic pemetrexed (500 mg/m²) plus bevacizumab (15 mg/kg) for 6 cycles, followed by maintenance bevacizumab. Surveillance imaging over the next 2.5 years showed no intracranial recurrence and stable pulmonary lesions. However, follow-up chest CT in October 2019 demonstrated slight enlargement of a non-peripheral right lower lobe lung metastasis (from 12 mm × 10 mm to 15.2 mm × 14.6 mm over 7 months) with increased solid components. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) remained normal, and no other metastatic sites were detected. Percutaneous biopsy of the lung mass confirmed recurrent adenocarcinoma. The patient has poor postoperative pulmonary function. Based on this presentation, what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Recurrent lung adenocarcinoma (EGFR/ALK wild-type, previously treated)
answer_key.next_management_step: CT-guided iodine-125 brachytherapy for the oligoprogressive lung metastasis

BEV maintenance for 2.5 years

Biopsy confirming recurrent adenocarcinoma

title: Sustained complete remission in chemotherapy-refractory advanced lung adenocarcinoma with bevacizumab and iodine-125 seed implantation: A long-term case report

challenge_prompt: A 34-year-old right-handed carpenter presents with a 1-year history of right forearm pain aggravated by elbow flexion and pronation, and sensory disturbances over the entire palm. Pain is 9/10 on VAS. The pronator compression test is positive; Tinel and Phalen tests are negative; Adson test normal. Exam shows thenar muscle atrophy, index and middle finger flexion weakness (4/5), thumb abduction weakness (4/5), ring and little finger flexion normal (5/5), and hypoesthesia in the median nerve territory. Deep tendon reflexes normal; can make 'OK sign'. Autoimmune and metabolic tests (including HIV, VDRL, CK, borreliosis) normal. X-ray shows no cervical ribs; Doppler ultrasound of neck and upper limb vessels normal. MRI of right elbow and cervical spine normal. Nerve conduction study shows only prolonged sensory median latency; EMG of affected limb normal. Conservative treatments (steroid injections, physical therapy, gabapentin 1200 mg/d, pregabalin 600 mg/d, ultrasound-guided hydrodissection with corticosteroid) provide no improvement. Muscle ultrasound reveals enlarged pronator teres. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Pronator teres syndrome (proximal median neuropathy at pronator teres)
answer_key.etiology: Compression of the median nerve by the pronator teres muscle, often due to repetitive forearm pronation and elbow flexion
answer_key.next_management_step: Surgical decompression of the median nerve, including release of the lacertus fibrosus, pronator teres, and any other compressive structures (e.g., Struthers ligament)
optional_findings: Normal nerve conduction study except prolonged sensory median latency
Failure of conservative therapy (steroids, physical therapy, gabapentin, pregabalin, hydrodissection)

challenge_prompt: A 49-year-old African American female patient with a history of untreated HIV infection presents to the emergency room with a two-week history of left ear pain, headache, fever, and blurred vision. Physical exam reveals crusted vesicles in the left ear and a vesicular skin rash on the left side of the face consistent with herpes zoster. Ophthalmic examination shows visual acuity 20/100 in the right eye, an afferent pupillary defect in the right eye, and a normal fundoscopic examination bilaterally. CSF analysis demonstrates lymphocytosis; VZV polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is positive. MRI of the brain shows enhancement of the right optic nerve and chiasm. Laboratory assessment reveals a low CD4 count and high HIV viral load consistent with uncontrolled AIDS.

challenge_prompt:
A 2-month-old female presented with poor appetite for 2 weeks, failure to thrive (weight 3.8 kg, <3rd percentile), distended abdomen, poor activity, and decreased urine output. She was born at term, previously healthy. On exam, distended abdomen, normoactive bowel sounds, tachyarrhythmia (HR 165). Abdominal x-ray and ultrasound showed flatulence without obstruction; EKG showed sinus tachycardia. Labs: WBC 12900/μL, platelets 720k/μL, AST 111 U/L, ALT 63 U/L, CRP, electrolytes, troponin I normal. Neurologic exam: significant hypotonia, muscle weakness, absent deep tendon reflexes, negative Babinski. NG tube placed for feeding. Further labs: AST 159 U/L, ALT 67 U/L, ammonia 116 μg/dL, lactate 65 mg/dL, CK 738 U/L, metabolic acidosis (pH 7.296, HCO3 19.2, BE -7.3). Serum Cu 79.4 μg/dL (mildly decreased), ceruloplasmin normal. Protein intake adjusted. Ketone, zinc, SMA screening normal. Brain echo: bilateral subependymal cysts (left 6.5x5.0 mm, right 8.7x2.5 mm). Cardiac echo: borderline interventricular septal hypertrophy, patent foramen ovale 1.2 mm. For elevated CK, borderline septal hypertrophy, and lactic acidosis, mitochondrial DNA screening was arranged for suspected mitochondrial myopathy. The family history includes maternal hypotonia before age 1 and an older brother with leg pains on exertion. Based on the presentation and available workup, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step?

Mitochondrial DNA analysis revealed a homoplasmic 14674T>C missense mutation in the MTTE gene.

answer_key.diagnosis: Infantile reversible cytochrome c oxidase (COX) deficiency myopathy (benign COX deficiency myopathy)
answer_key.etiology: Homoplasmic m.14674T>C mutation in mitochondrial MT-TE gene
answer_key.next_management_step: Mitochondrial DNA sequencing to confirm MT-TE gene mutation; start ubidecarenone and vitamin B6
optional_findings: Bilateral subependymal cysts on brain echo
Family history of maternal hypotonia and brother with leg pains

title: A Case of Infantile Reversible Cytochrome C Oxidase Deficiency Myopathy in Taiwan: A 4-Year Follow-Up

challenge_prompt: A 28-year-old male with no significant cardiovascular history but with a family history of heart failure in his maternal grandfather presented to the emergency department with symptoms of an ischemic stroke: aphasia, facial paralysis, right hemiparesis, and sixth cranial nerve involvement. Initial NIHSS score was 13. Thrombolysis led to improvement (NIHSS 4). Post-thrombolysis cranial CT revealed a left frontal cortical-subcortical hypodensity. Workup for secondary causes: infectious panels (hepatitis, HIV, syphilis), thrombophilia, and rheumatologic studies returned negative. 24-hour Holter monitoring showed no atrial fibrillation and no ventricular ectopic beats. Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) found no embolic source but revealed severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction with LVEF 20%. After discharge, the patient experienced two episodes of acute pulmonary edema. Cardiac MRI confirmed severe biventricular dilated cardiomyopathy: LVEF 15%, RVEF 16%, left atrial enlargement, moderate mitral regurgitation. There was no evidence of myocardial edema, fibrosis, or inflammatory/infiltrative processes. ECG showed left bundle branch block with QRS duration 200 ms. The patient was started on heart failure therapy. Genetic testing for cardiomyopathy-related genes and Fabry disease was pursued.

aliases: Dilated cardiomyopathy due to FLNC gene mutation
answer_key.diagnosis: Dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM)
answer_key.etiology: Genetic mutation in the FLNC gene (c.1156G>T; p.Glu386*)
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for cardiomyopathy panel, ICD implantation for primary prevention of sudden cardiac death, and cascade screening of first-degree relatives

Left atrial enlargement and moderate mitral regurgitation on MRI
Initial treatment with beta-blocker, SGLT2i, ACEi, ivabradine, statin, diuretic

No evidence of hypertension, coronary artery disease, or valvular disease
New York Heart Association class II at first follow-up

challenge_prompt: A 75-year-old African American woman was admitted for severe progressive headaches in the setting of hypertensive emergency. Her past medical history included coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) 20 years ago and multiple prior percutaneous coronary interventions (PCIs) to the right coronary artery (RCA) and left circumflex artery (LCx), most recently 10 months before with a 2.75 × 14 mm drug-eluting stent (DES) for an in-stent restenosis of the mid-RCA. An echocardiogram in 2015 showed reduced ejection fraction of 40–45% and regional wall motion abnormalities. She had no cardiac symptoms on admission. Initial ECG showed sinus rhythm, left ventricular hypertrophy by Cornell criteria, and inferior Q waves. Troponin I was 0.04 ng/mL (normal <0.05). Noncontrast head CT showed no acute changes. She was admitted for blood pressure management. A routine second ECG 13 hours later was similar. Serial troponins were 0.05 ng/mL and 0.05 ng/mL six hours apart. The next morning at 07:35 AM, she suffered a tonic-clonic seizure lasting 5 minutes, treated with lorazepam. An ECG at 08:03 AM showed 2.5 to 3 mm acute ST elevations in leads II, III, and aVF. She was loaded with aspirin and ticagrelor and started on heparin. Blood pressure was 187/80 mmHg. A bedside limited echocardiogram showed EF 50% with hypokinesis in the basal and mid inferior and inferolateral walls. The ST elevations persisted for 40 minutes, and she was transferred for primary PCI. Coronary angiography showed a chronically occluded mid-LAD, a mid-LCx with a patent stent, and an 80% in-stent restenosis in the mid-RCA at the site of prior intervention with TIMI 3 flow before intervention. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) of the mid-RCA revealed malapposition of the prior overlapping stent and no thrombus or plaque rupture. Based on the presentation and findings, what is the most likely etiology of the acute ST elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and what next diagnostic step would best confirm this mechanism?

answer_key.diagnosis: Seizure-induced hypercatecholaminergic coronary vasospasm at the site of prior stent malapposition
answer_key.etiology: Acute catecholamine surge from seizure causing coronary vasospasm, likely at the site of prior stent malapposition
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider acetylcholine provocation test during angiography to confirm vasospasm; optimize medical therapy with calcium channel blockers and beta-blockers
optional_findings: Initial ECG with LVH and inferior Q waves
Hypokinesis on echo in inferior and inferolateral walls
required_findings: 75-year-old African American woman
Hypertensive emergency at presentation
Prior CABG and multiple PCIs, most recent DES for in-stent restenosis of mid-RCA 10 months ago
Tonic-clonic seizure lasting 5 minutes followed by inferior ST elevations on ECG
Angiography showing 80% in-stent restenosis with TIMI 3 flow
OCT showing stent malapposition with no plaque rupture or thrombus

title: Seizure-Associated ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction in Absence of Plaque Rupture

challenge_prompt: A 36-year-old male with a history of post-traumatic hydrocephalus status post ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt placement presents to the emergency department with a complaint of a swelling in the left supraclavicular region, noted for an unspecified duration. The swelling measures 3×4 cm. He reports no fever, pain, or other symptoms. He has a history of multiple shunt revisions, most recently a year ago. An ultrasound of the swelling confirms a collection of air. On thorough physical examination, a yellowish tube is observed protruding from the anus. The patient has no gastrointestinal symptoms, including no abdominal pain, distension, or peritonitis signs. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate step in management?

aliases: Bowel perforation due to VP shunt
answer_key.diagnosis: Transanal protrusion of ventriculoperitoneal shunt (bowel perforation with shunt migration)
answer_key.etiology: Bowel perforation from chronic migration of the VP shunt catheter through the colonic wall
answer_key.next_management_step: Urgent surgical removal of the distal shunt catheter via the anus, with proximal shunt revision; broad-spectrum antibiotics; CSF analysis for infection.
optional_findings: No symptoms of meningitis or ventriculitis

Left supraclavicular swelling (3×4 cm)
Ultrasound confirmed air collection in the swelling
Yellowish tube protruding from the anus on examination
No fever or pain
No gastrointestinal symptoms (no peritonitis, no abdominal pain)

title: A case of transanal protrusion of ventriculoperitoneal shunt in an adult patient without any classic symptoms of bowel perforation

challenge_prompt: A 20-year-old previously healthy man developed three episodes of generalized non-thunderclap headache in a self-remitting fashion over one week. The last episode was accompanied by acute quadriparesis progressing to respiratory discomfort. He reported no sensory complaints, band-like sensations, or sphincter impairment. No substance use, infection, or recent vaccination. On examination, he was alert and oriented. Vital signs: temperature 36.5°C, BP 115/80, RR 26, HR 85, O2 sat 96%. Neurological exam: quadriplegia (MRC grade 1), generalized hyperreflexia, bilateral Hoffmann sign, upgoing plantar reflexes, no sensory level. Initial investigations: brain CT normal, routine biochemistry normal. Brain and cervical spine MRI: few ovoid T2 periventricular and right internal capsule hyperintense lesions; a short central T2 hyperintense lesion at the cervicomedullary junction. CSF: normal glucose, cells, protein; 6 oligoclonal bands restricted to CSF. Comprehensive autoimmune antibody panel (cell-surface/synaptic and paraneoplastic) negative. Serology: AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG negative (fixed cell-based assay); vasculitis/autoimmune panel (ANA, anti-dsDNA, lupus anticoagulant, anti-β2-glycoprotein I, anti-cardiolipin, anti-Sm, anti-ribosomal P, p-ANCA, c-ANCA, HLA-B51) negative; infectious serologies (hepatitis B/C, HIV, TB interferon-gamma release assay, brucellosis, enterovirus PCR) negative. All diagnostic investigations completed before treatment.

answer_key.diagnosis: Atypical multiple sclerosis (relapsing-remitting)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune demyelinating
answer_key.next_management_step: High-dose methylprednisolone and plasma exchange acutely; then high-dose oral prednisolone taper and rituximab for relapse prevention

challenge_prompt:
A 34-year-old female smoker with no significant past medical history presents after discharge from an admission for an ischemic stroke. The stroke manifested as right-sided weakness, left facial droop, and central vision loss. On further questioning, she reports joint pain in the right elbow and shoulder, and dryness of the mouth and eyes. Past obstetric history includes multiple abortions and irregular menstrual cycles. She denies contraceptive use, recent pregnancy, or long-term medications.

Physical exam shows right-sided weakness and left facial palsy with intact sensation and coordination. Skin and musculoskeletal exam are unremarkable except for facial hyperpigmentation. No joint deformities or ulcers.

Blood work: unremarkable basic metabolic panel aside from low potassium and high LDL. Immunological workup: positive for B2-glycoprotein, anticardiolipin, and lupus anticoagulant; ANA profile borderline for PCNA. Thrombophilia workup: low free protein S level, normal protein C.

Brain CT: unremarkable. Brain MRI: left fronto-parieto-temporal cortical and subcortical area of cytotoxic edema with internal foci of T1 hyperintensity (hemorrhagic transformation) and diffusion restriction, consistent with venous infarction. MR venography: transverse sinus thrombosis with attenuation of the sigmoid and internal jugular veins.

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis? What is the next appropriate management step?

aliases: Antiphospholipid syndrome with protein S deficiency
Acquired thrombophilia due to APS and protein S deficiency
answer_key.diagnosis: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) with associated protein S deficiency
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune (APS) and acquired protein S deficiency
answer_key.next_management_step: Anticoagulation (e.g., rivaroxaban) and initiation of immunomodulatory therapy for APS (e.g., hydroxychloroquine, azathioprine)

Low free protein S level
MRV showing transverse sinus thrombosis
MRI showing venous infarction with hemorrhagic transformation and diffusion restriction
Ischemic stroke in a young patient with multiple abortions
Normal protein C level
Unremarkable brain CT
Absence of other thrombophilia risk factors (e.g., contraceptive use, pregnancy)

title: Coexistence Between Antiphospholipid Syndrome and Protein S Deficiency in a Patient With Transverse Sinus Thrombosis: A Rare Association

challenge_prompt:
A 17-year-old female with no prior psychiatric history presented 5 days after exposure to tablets and a chloride-smelling spray (suspected 'bath salts' and ethyl chloride). Initially she had confusion and hallucinations, then within 24 hours developed anxiety and depression. She then developed confusion, dissociations, insomnia, mydriasis, behavioral changes, speech latency of 20-30 seconds, dysarthria, impaired balance, slowness of motion, and emotional distancing. Over the next 2 weeks, while treated with risperidone (1 mg BID) and midazolam (up to 7.5 mg TID), she developed mutism, negativism, waxy flexibility, posturing, and required a feeding tube. Catatonia was recognized using the Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale (positive on procedures 1-4 and 7). Risperidone was stopped and lorazepam started. She briefly oriented but lorazepam up to 20 mg daily did not achieve remission. Olanzapine 2.5 mg and aripiprazole 7.5 mg were tried but worsened symptoms, so all antipsychotics were discontinued. She developed DVT due to immobilization, and ECT was not considered due to anticoagulation. Family history: mother with bipolar I disorder. Diagnostic workup: urine toxicology (negative for common drugs, but not tested for bath salts/ethyl chloride), serum ethanol, methanol, salicylates, creatinine, hepatic panel, arterial blood gas all normal. CSF normal. Brain MRI normal. EEG normal. Infectious workup negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Drug-induced catatonia
answer_key.etiology: Exposure to mephedrone (bath salts) and ethyl chloride
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate lithium and memantine with continued lorazepam therapy

Feeling of living in a dream (derealization)
Intermediate response to lithium+memantine (not included in prompt)

title: Delayed drug-induced catatonia in an adolescent girl—clinical implications: a case report

challenge_prompt: A case series describes six patients (mean age 61.7 years, 2 female, 4 male) who had severe SARS-CoV-2 pneumonia requiring intubation and mechanical ventilation in the ICU between March-April 2020. Exclusion criteria: preexisting neurocognitive impairment, history of traumatic brain injury, psychiatric, oncological, or neurological disease, and stroke or cardiac arrest as a complication of COVID-19. All had ≥12 years of education and were socially well-inserted. At 12 months post-COVID-19 onset, they completed cognitive assessment. Key findings: Five patients had severe ARDS, one had Guillain-Barré syndrome; two had ICU delirium. All had ventilator-associated pneumonia. Structural 3T MRI during acute stage was normal in all five scanned (one had endothelial microlesions in splenium of corpus callosum). At 12 months, all had returned to work but with reduced efficiency due to fatigue and weakness. Self-reported fatigue scales: Fatigue Scale for Motor and Cognitive Functions (FSMC) motor scores 22-34 (max 50), cognitive scores 22-33; Brugmann Fatigue Scale physical 3-5, mental 2-5; Visual analogue scale for situational mental fatigue (VAS-SMF) before cognitive testing 0.2-2.5, after 1.2-9.7 (increased in all). Perceived mental effort (VAS-PME) 2.1-9.2. HAD anxiety 5-9, depression 1-9; f-DAS apathy subscales; PCL-5 PTSD 3-18; ESS sleepiness 0-10; ISI insomnia 2-13. Cognitive testing: MoCA, FAB, and additional tests (BECLA naming, digit spans, Zoo Map, Color Trails, TAP subtests). Four of six patients (P1-P3, P6) showed deficient performance or at lower limit of norm in some attentional, executive, and/or working memory tests; mental fatigability (decline in sustained attention task) was present in five (P1-P5). Episodic memory and visuospatial attention were preserved. No patient had prior fatigue or cognitive complaints. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely clinical syndrome affecting these patients, and what is the next appropriate step in management?

aliases: Post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 (PASC) with cognitive and fatigue symptoms
answer_key.diagnosis: Post-COVID-19 syndrome with chronic fatigue and cognitive impairment (post-intensive care syndrome-like, without structural brain damage)
answer_key.etiology: SARS-CoV-2 infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Multidisciplinary rehabilitation including cognitive remediation, graded physical reconditioning, psychological support, and occupational therapy; further neuropsychological assessment to guide tailored interventions
optional_findings: Somatic complaints (dyspnea, muscle weakness, olfactory disorders)
Reduced quality of life (SF-36 physical role, vitality)
Post-COVID functional status scale increase from before to after
required_findings: Severe COVID-19 requiring intubation and mechanical ventilation
No preexisting neurocognitive impairment or neurological disease
No stroke or cardiac arrest as complication of COVID-19
Normal structural brain MRI (except minor corpus callosum lesion in one)
Elevated FSMC motor and cognitive scores (22-34 and 22-33)
Increased VAS-SMF after cognitive testing (mental fatigability)
Deficits in attention, executive, and/or working memory tests in 4 of 6 patients
Preserved episodic memory and visuospatial attention

title: Outcome of severe COVID-19: spotlight on fatigue, fatigability, multidomain complaints and pattern of cognitive deficits in a case series without prior brain dysfunction and without COVID-19-related stroke and/or cardiac arrest

challenge_prompt:
A 16-year-old Iranian female presents with progressive speech impairment over 2 weeks, including difficulty pronouncing letters, intermittent stuttering, and impaired reading and writing. She also has emotional instability, atypical behaviors, disorientation to time and place, and delusional thoughts. Eight years ago, she had a similar episode with a seizure, was diagnosed with anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (NMDAR) encephalitis, and fully recovered after treatment. On examination, she has altered mental status, agitation, paranoia, hallucinations, mood swings, facial twitching, involuntary writhing, abnormal postures, generalized hyperreflexia, increased muscle tone, fluctuating consciousness, impaired executive function, and limb dystonia. Laboratory tests: CBC, coagulation, electrolytes, renal function, thyroid function, and paraneoplastic antibodies are normal; anti-dsDNA, ANCA, anti-phospholipid, anti-NMO, anti-SSA/Ro, anti-SSB/La are negative; serum anti-NMDAR antibodies are positive. CSF analysis: clear, colorless, Gram stain negative, WBC 8 cells/µL (mild pleocytosis), glucose 48 mg/dL, protein 54 mg/dL (elevated), LDH 3.9 U/L (elevated), oligoclonal bands positive (multiple bands), IgG index 0.9 (elevated), IgM index 0.3, anti-NMDAR antibodies positive (titer 1000). Brain MRI shows several diffuse cortical hyperintensities but otherwise normal; brain CT, chest X-ray, thyroid/abdominal/pelvic ultrasound, and EEG are normal.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Late relapse of anti-NMDAR (anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor) encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate immunotherapy with intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g/day for 5 days) and intravenous immunoglobulin (2 g/kg over 5 days), and consider plasmapheresis

challenge_prompt:
A 10-year-old Sri Lankan boy presents with fever and headache for 5 days and difficulty in walking for 2 days. The fever was low grade and intermittent; he was well between spikes. He did not have photophobia or vomiting. After the fever subsided, he developed imbalance in walking, worsening over 2 days, and was unable to stand unaided on admission. His parents report repetitive, involuntary extension movements of the right upper limb. No slurred speech, dysphagia, or incontinence.

Past medical history: Diagnosed with acute lymphoblastic leukemia at age 3, treated with chemotherapy, achieved remission. At age 6, developed CNS relapse. Current medications: daily oral dexamethasone and mercaptopurine, weekly oral methotrexate and folinic acid, and intrathecal vincristine every two weeks.

On examination: Afebrile, GCS 15/15, no neck stiffness, Kernig sign negative. Neurologic exam shows dystonic movements of right upper limb, hypotonia and reduced power (3/5) of left upper and lower limbs proximally and distally, right side power 4/5. Tendon reflexes diminished in all limbs, plantar flexor bilaterally. Dysmetria and intention tremors bilaterally. Cranial nerves, pupils, fundi normal. Vital signs: BP 124/80, HR 88. Respiratory and abdominal exams normal.

Laboratory: Hb 11.9 g/dL, WBC 4200/μL, platelets 156,000/μL, ALT 32 IU/L, creatinine 48 μmol/L, Na 140 mmol/L, K 4.5 mmol/L, CRP 11 mg/L, LDH 279 IU/L. Non-contrast CT head: no cerebral edema, hemorrhage, or infarction.

Urgent brain MRI (within 12 hours of admission): T2-weighted images show symmetrical high signal intensities with diffusion restriction involving bilateral putamen, subcortical areas, and deep white matter.

Based on the above information, what is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Methotrexate-induced leukoencephalopathy
answer_key.etiology: Methotrexate toxicity
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue oral methotrexate and initiate supportive care with intravenous folinic acid, aminophylline, dexamethasone, and oral dextromethorphan; consider ICU monitoring for neurologic deterioration.

Hypotonia and reduced power (3/5) on left side

Over the past 6 months, he has developed progressive dysarthria, dysphagia, and fasciculations of the tongue. Examination shows wasting and weakness of bulbar muscles, with mild lower limb weakness. challenge_prompt: A 36-year-old woman presents with a 4-week history of severe left-sided unilateral headaches that last 4–5 days, are non-throbbing, without nausea or vomiting, and not relieved by standard analgesics. Neurological examination, including fundoscopy, cranial nerves, and motor/sensory systems, is normal. Family history of headache in mother. Laboratory workup is negative for systemic inflammation (normal CRP, ESR), sarcoidosis (negative ACE), and vasculitis (negative anti-dsDNA, ANA, anti-phospholipid, anticardiolipin, beta2-glycoprotein). CSF analysis shows positive oligoclonal bands (>8 bands compared with serum), elevated IgG index (>3/4), and normal glucose and protein. MRI shows periventricular plaques, a tumefactive juxtacortical plaque with large edema, and demyelinated plaques in the cervical region, meeting dissemination in space criteria for MS. No gadolinium enhancement is reported. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Radiologically isolated syndrome (RIS) with high risk of conversion to multiple sclerosis
answer_key.etiology: Demyelinating disease, likely early multiple sclerosis
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate disease-modifying therapy (e.g., dimethyl fumarate) and monitor for clinical or radiological conversion to multiple sclerosis

challenge_prompt: A patient was diagnosed in his early 60s with amnestic mild cognitive impairment (MCI) related to microvascular encephalopathy, based on neuropsychological testing and MRI. In his late 60s, he experienced transient cognitive worsening due to neuroborreliosis, with cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) findings not indicative of Alzheimer's pathology. His cognitive status returned to baseline after antibiotic treatment and remained stable for several years. In his mid-70s, he exhibited cognitive and behavioural decline that necessitated hospitalisation. He displayed Diogenes syndrome and delusional speech, claiming to live with about 20 squatters. These squatters were actually individuals physically depicted in photographs whom he believed to be alive. He exhibited caregiving behaviour towards them, such as providing blankets. When questioned, he acknowledged that they were photographs and described his actions as logical and benevolent, reflecting impaired insight. Following this decline, repeat neuropsychological evaluation showed global worsening, with impaired episodic memory, temporal disorientation and emerging language, executive and attentional deficits. MRI demonstrated progression of vascular lesions and new left frontoparietal and hippocampal atrophy. 18F-FDG PET revealed bilateral temporoparietal, frontal and precuneus hypometabolism, left-predominant. Revised CSF thresholds reclassified amyloid levels as abnormal.

aliases: Delusional misidentification syndrome: animated picture variant

answer_key.etiology: Mixed dementia (vascular and probable Alzheimer's disease)
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider antipsychotic medication and environmental modifications (e.g., removal of photographs) to alleviate distress; if safety concerns, consider institutionalization.

title: When images come to life: a case series

challenge_prompt:
A 22-year-old woman of North African origin was brought to the emergency department for confusional syndrome and ataxia after being found at home in this condition and surrounded by nitrous oxide cylinders. She had no relevant medical history or familial history. She did not take any medication or contraceptives. The initial heteroanamnesis reported a depressive state that had been evolving for a few weeks. The patient's main complaints were headache, dyspnoea and pain in both knees following a fall. She had no chest pain, no cough, no expectoration, no abdominal pain, and no urinary complaints.

On clinical examination, the patient presented with an increased heart rate of 100 beats/min, and pyrexia at 38.4 °C. The other parameters were normal. Her hygiene was poor, and she was clinically dehydrated. She was eupnoeic at rest. Cardiopulmonary auscultation was unremarkable. No signs of acute abdomen or visceral mass were noted. There were no impairments in the lower limbs. Neurologically, she had L1-level paraparesis with hypoesthesia interesting the epicritic modality. Protopathic sensitivity, nociception and proprioception were preserved. The deep tendon reflexes were average and symmetrical. There were no pyramidal or extra-pyramidal signs. There were no meningeal signs. The cerebellar tests were correctly performed. The examination of the cranial nerves was normal. She had no dysarthria or phasic disorder. No bladder or sphincters disorders were found.

Her initial biology showed an inflammatory syndrome with non-megaloblastic pancytopenia (Table 1). Vitamin B12 was under 148 ng/l. Search for alcohol or benzodiazepine was negative. Urine analysis was negative for any illicit drug but revealed leukocyturia with bacteriuria. The urine culture was later positive for E. coli. The biological assessment was completed by the search for pro-thrombotic factors, which showed hyperhomocysteinemia (207.1 µmol/l [nl < 10]). Methylmalonic acid was also elevated to 919.00 µg/l. Biermer's disease was ruled out based on a normal gastroscopy performed a few months earlier and the absence of intrinsic factor and parietal cell antibodies in the blood. Finally, a myelogram was performed and excluded any sign of acute leukemia or macrophage activation syndrome (MAS). The marrow was of normal richness, without excess blasts whose cell morphology was compatible with a vitamin B12 deficiency.

A cranial CT-scan did not reveal any acute phenomenon or suspicious parenchymal or meningeal signs. A thoraco-abdominal CT-scan revealed a right lower lobar and a posterobasal segment of the left lower lobe pulmonary embolism, with no lesions at the abdominal level. The iconographic work-up was then completed by an MRI of the brain and spine, which confirmed the absence of brain or spinal cord injury. Electroneuromyography (ENMG) performed 2 weeks after the onset of the symptoms showed slowing of the motor conductions of the lower limbs with lengthening of the distal motor latencies, amplitude decease of the compound muscle action potential and conduction blocks. No abnormality of the sensory conductions was found in the lower limbs and the EMG of the upper limbs was normal. To exclude a partial form of Guillain-Barré syndrome, a lumbar puncture was carried out to search an albumin-cytological dissociation, which was negative.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely etiology and what is the most appropriate next management step?

aliases: Nitrous oxide-induced polyneuropathy, pancytopenia, and pulmonary embolism
answer_key.diagnosis: Nitrous oxide toxicity causing functional vitamin B12 deficiency leading to polyneuropathy, pancytopenia, and pulmonary embolism
answer_key.etiology: Recreational nitrous oxide use causing irreversible oxidation of vitamin B12, leading to functional B12 deficiency, hyperhomocysteinemia, and elevated methylmalonic acid
answer_key.next_management_step: Cessation of nitrous oxide exposure, vitamin B12 supplementation (intramuscular or intravenous), anticoagulation for pulmonary embolism (e.g., enoxaparin followed by direct oral anticoagulant), transfusion for pancytopenia if needed, and supportive care including rehabilitation for neuropathy
optional_findings: Negative lumbar puncture for albumin-cytologic dissociation
required_findings: Surrounded by nitrous oxide cylinders
Vitamin B12 < 148 ng/L
Hyperhomocysteinemia 207.1 µmol/L
Elevated methylmalonic acid 919 µg/L
Non-megaloblastic pancytopenia
Bilateral pulmonary embolism on CT
Motor polyneuropathy of lower limbs with conduction blocks on ENMG
Normal gastroscopy and negative intrinsic factor/parietal cell antibodies

title: Nitrous oxide-induced polyneuropathy, pancytopenia and pulmonary embolism: a case report

challenge_prompt: A 22-year-old previously healthy female tested positive for COVID-19 in January 2023 with mild symptoms. She was not on any chronic treatments and had no relevant medical history. Ten days after infection, she developed neurocognitive symptoms without respiratory manifestations and was admitted to the emergency unit without hospitalization. Symptoms included alexithymia (inability to describe emotions), pseudobulbar affect (sudden uncontrollable inappropriate laughing/crying), dementia with severe disorientation and instability affecting daily tasks, aggressive behavior with self-harm intentions, anxiety, fatigue, and sleep problems. She also had mild cough and fever. Brain MRI and CT scans were normal. What is the most likely diagnosis or etiology of these symptoms, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

answer_key.etiology: COVID-19 infection

challenge_prompt: Two 7-year-old children (one male, one female) of Turkic ethnicity attend a school for exceptional children. The male child was born via cesarean section at 37 weeks to a 28-year-old mother and 42-year-old father. He was diagnosed after a general assessment before primary education and a specialized intelligence test, which led to enrollment in the special school. He has a 13-year-old brother with a similar condition, hyperactivity, and attention deficit; a maternal grandmother also had a similar condition. His first words emerged after 3 years of age. challenge_prompt: A 39-year-old male with known hypertension (non-adherent) presents with acute thunderclap headache, disorientation, and agitation. He has no history of trauma, fever, illicit drugs, autoimmune disease, or immunosuppression. Blood pressure is 200/110 mmHg. GCS 13/15, left-sided hemiparesis, no meningeal signs. Initial non-contrast head CT shows extensive acute intraventricular hemorrhage (IVH) throughout the ventricular system with associated hydrocephalus. No parenchymal hematoma. CT angiography not performed due to kidney injury. Laboratory studies: creatinine 159 µmol/L, coagulation studies normal, HIV negative. MRI brain (performed after stabilization) shows extensive vasogenic edema predominantly in the posterior left hemisphere (occipital and parietal lobes) on T2/FLAIR, with increased ADC signal and no diffusion restriction, consistent with reversible vasogenic edema. There is no evidence of aneurysm, arteriovenous malformation, or other vascular lesion on available imaging. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome (PRES) with intraventricular hemorrhage
answer_key.etiology: uncontrolled chronic hypertension
answer_key.next_management_step: Cautious blood pressure reduction (target systolic 140-160 mmHg), external ventricular drainage for hydrocephalus, seizure prophylaxis, ICU monitoring

challenge_prompt:
A 49-year-old previously healthy Sri Lankan hotel chef presented with subacute painful weakness and reduced muscle bulk of left upper arm for 3 months, without contralateral upper limb or lower limb involvement. No neck pain, trauma, or autonomic symptoms. Weight loss of 7 kg over 3 months without appetite change. No systemic symptoms. Clinically euthyroid. No history of diabetes or hyperglycemic symptoms. No family history of similar disorders, diabetes, or malignancies. Thin built, BMI 19 kg/m2. Neurological exam: muscle wasting of shoulder girdle and biceps left, no fasciculations, proximal muscle strength 4/5 MRC, diminished bicep and triceps jerks. Sensory normal. Other limbs, cranial nerves, fundi, higher functions normal. Systemic exam unremarkable.

Investigations: ESR 60 mm/hr, CRP normal. CSF: protein 70 mg/dl, acellular, negative high volume cytospin. MRI cervical and thoracic spine with brachial plexus normal. Fasting plasma glucose 198 mg/dl, HbA1c 9%. C-peptide normal, negative type 1 diabetes antibodies. Negative vasculitis screening. No evidence of malignancy. NCS: reduced amplitude left upper limb sensory and motor, normal conduction velocity. EMG: positive sharp waves, frequent fibrillations, high amplitude polyphasic motor unit potentials, reduced recruitment in proximal muscles left upper limb.

What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Diabetic cervical radiculoplexus neuropathy (Bruns Garland syndrome) as first presentation of type 2 diabetes
answer_key.etiology: Type 2 diabetes mellitus; underlying dysimmune microvasculitis
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate oral antidiabetic medications and symptomatic pain management with neuropathic agents and physical therapy

challenge_prompt:
A 76-year-old Indian woman with poorly controlled diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 11.2%) and dementia presents with worsening confusion over 4 weeks and left-sided falls for 1 week. She has a dry cough for 1 month. Exam: oriented only to self, left-sided neglect, left hemiparesis (left upper 2/5, left lower 3/5). Labs: glucose 268 mg/dL, WBC 6200/µL (87% neutrophils). CT head: right subdural hematoma and multiple right-sided ring-enhancing lesions. MRI brain: multiple right parietal abscesses (largest 1.4×1.9×2.1 cm). Lumbar puncture: opening pressure low, glucose 79 mg/dL, protein 105 mg/dL, WBC 225/µL (neutrophilic). Chest CT: necrotic mediastinal mass and bilateral upper lobe pulmonary nodules. Started on vancomycin, ceftriaxone, metronidazole. TB workup: bronchoscopy with lavage and FNA – bacterial/fungal cultures negative, cytology negative, AFB smears negative, TB PCR negative. Blood cultures, HIV, Histoplasma antigen, cryptococcal antigen, Echinococcus antibody negative. CSF cultures negative. Neurosurgery: right parietal craniotomy and partial resection of largest abscess. Intraoperative specimens: AFB smear negative, TB PCR negative. All above infectious workup negative. Based on this information, what is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next step in management?

Intraoperative specimens were sent for modified AFB smear and culture, which were positive for Nocardia.

answer_key.diagnosis: Nocardia brain abscess
answer_key.etiology: Nocardia farcinica
answer_key.next_management_step: Start empiric combination antibiotics with CNS penetration (e.g., trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole plus meropenem or linezolid) and await culture and susceptibility results; consider extending treatment to at least 12 months.

challenge_prompt:
A 73-year-old Caucasian female presented to the emergency department with sudden onset slurring of speech. She reported a brief episode of blurred vision the day prior that spontaneously resolved. Past medical history: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, and uneventful right total hip replacement 5 weeks ago. Medications: atorvastatin 10 mg daily, omeprazole 20 mg daily, candesartan 16 mg daily. She was independent and lived alone. No smoking or alcohol. On examination: vital signs: BP 147/87, pulse 88, temp 36.8°C, O2 sat 96% on room air. Neurologic exam: alert, GCS 15, normal extraocular movements, intact visual fields, symmetric face, 5/5 strength in all limbs, normal sensation. Only finding was dysarthria (mild to moderate slurring), NIHSS=1. Swallow test passed. CT head showed no acute hemorrhage but moderate small vessel disease. She was admitted for suspected lacunar stroke and started on aspirin 300 mg and atorvastatin 80 mg.

Over the next 48 hours, dysarthria persisted. On day 3, she reported feeling exhausted and a new sensation of a lump in her throat. Speech and language therapy recommended liquid medications. She was unable to tolerate MRI due to shortness of breath when lying flat. A repeat CT head on day 4 showed no acute intracranial abnormality. That morning, she developed new mild bilateral ptosis, a prominent nasal voice, general fatigue, and required 1 L oxygen. Muscle power remained normal. The medical team suspected possible myasthenia gravis based on the evolving symptoms and sent an urgent sample for acetylcholine receptor antibodies. A neurology consultation was sought. The patient's respiratory status worsened, and she could not lie flat.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Myasthenia gravis with myasthenic crisis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune acetylcholine receptor antibody positive
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) or plasma exchange, and admit to intensive care unit for close monitoring and possible elective intubation

challenge_prompt: A 59-year-old Moroccan man presents to the emergency department after a transient ischemic attack with right hemiparesis and left central facial paralysis that resolved spontaneously. He has a history of rheumatic fever at age 14 and stage II NYHA dyspnea on moderate exertion for 2 years. There is no history of recent cardiac intervention or aortic/carotid manipulation. On examination, he has an irregular heart rhythm of 90 bpm, blood pressure 124/79 mmHg, a mid-diastolic rumble at the apex, and no signs of heart congestion or neurological impairment. ECG shows atrial fibrillation. Transthoracic echocardiography reveals important mitral valve leaflets calcifications (likely rheumatic), severe mitral stenosis with a mean gradient of 15 mmHg and continuity equation surface of 1 cm², a thickened but non-calcified aortic valve with moderate aortic regurgitation, normal left ventricular function (LVEF 55%), right ventricular longitudinal systolic dysfunction (TAPSE 11 mm, S'VD 6 cm/s), mild tricuspid regurgitation, and estimated systolic pulmonary artery pressure of 69 mmHg. Cardiac CT confirms mitral leaflet calcification. Unenhanced cerebral CT shows calcified emboli in both middle cerebral arteries (M3 and M4 segments). Susceptibility-weighted cerebral MRI shows absence of signal in both middle cerebral arteries. Cervical artery duplex is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis/etiology, and what is the next appropriate management step?

challenge_prompt:
A 58-year-old woman presents with insidious onset and progressively worsening dysphagia associated with persistent pain localized to the right side of the neck. She has hypothyroidism (levothyroxine 62.5 μg daily), hypertension for 15 years (atenolol 50 mg twice daily), and anxiety disorder (sertraline 50 mg at bedtime). Neurological and otolaryngological examination reveals: reduced elevation of the right side of the soft palate with diminished gag reflex (CN IX palsy), immobility of the right vocal cord on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy (CN X palsy), weakness of right shoulder elevation with impaired contralateral head rotation due to sternocleidomastoid weakness (CN XI palsy), and deviation of the tongue to the right with ipsilateral atrophy and fasciculations (CN XII palsy). These findings indicate combined dysfunction of cranial nerves IX, X, XI, and XII. MRI of the brain with contrast shows a lobulated, avidly enhancing soft tissue mass centered in the right hypoglossal canal with extension into the right jugular foramen and adjacent carotid space. The lesion is iso- to hypointense on T1-weighted sequences and hyperintense on T2/FLAIR images, without diffusion restriction. Additional findings include features of right vocal cord palsy and significant thickening of the right lateral glossoepiglottic fold with mild post-contrast enhancement. Scattered discrete T2/FLAIR hyperintense foci in bilateral frontal and parietal deep white matter are consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic changes.

Based on this clinical and imaging presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and etiology, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

Histopathological examination of the biopsy specimen confirmed a paraganglioma.

answer_key.diagnosis: Collet-Sicard syndrome secondary to skull base paraganglioma
answer_key.etiology: Skull base paraganglioma arising from paraganglionic tissue
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate multidisciplinary supportive care including swallowing rehabilitation, speech and vocal therapy, optimization of blood pressure control, and counseling regarding persistent lower cranial nerve deficits and aspiration risk; no biopsy recommended due to characteristic imaging and clinical presentation; consider definitive treatment (radiation therapy or surgery) based on tumor size/symptoms.

challenge_prompt: A woman in her early 30s with no prior comorbidities presented with a two-month history of progressive left leg pain and palpable swelling, associated with low-grade intermittent fever and unintentional weight loss. Over the preceding week, she had developed generalized weakness along with urinary and fecal incontinence. On admission, her hemoglobin was 4 g/dL, for which she received three units of packed red blood cells. X-ray of the left foot revealed a pathological talus fracture. Ultrasound of the left leg showed large, heterogeneous, vascular structures in the left inguinal region and popliteal fossa. CT angiogram demonstrated widespread soft tissue lesions and extensive lymphadenopathy in the abdomen, pelvis, and thorax, along with lytic lesions in the vertebrae, sacrum, and right iliac bone, multiple pulmonary nodules in the left lung, compression of the left common femoral vein, and soft tissue edema of the left leg. MRI of the brain and whole spine with contrast exhibited severe metastatic disease involving both the brain (dural-based enhancing metastasis in the left middle cranial fossa with temporal edema) and the vertebral column (diffuse vertebral metastatic infiltration and large sacral lesion). CT chest confirmed multiple bilateral pulmonary nodular infiltrates. An ultrasound-guided biopsy of the presacral mass showed cores of fibro-muscular tissue infiltrated by a neoplastic lesion arranged in sheets, composed of small, round, blue cells with scant cytoplasm, round nuclei, and dispersed chromatin. Immunohistochemistry revealed strong diffuse membranous positivity for CD99, patchy positivity for NKX2.2, positivity for Cyclin D1, and negative for cytokeratin AE1/AE3, desmin, CD20, CD3, TdT, PAX5. The Ki-67 index was raised. Confirmatory molecular testing (FISH or RT-PCR for EWSR1 rearrangement) was not performed due to financial constraints. A PET-CT scan could not be performed.

answer_key.diagnosis: Small round blue cell tumor consistent with Ewing sarcoma (Ewing sarcoma)
answer_key.etiology: EWSR1 rearrangement (most commonly EWSR1-FLI1 fusion) highly suspected but not molecularly confirmed
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate systemic chemotherapy with interval-compressed VDC/IE regimen; pursue molecular confirmation via FISH or RT-PCR for EWSR1 rearrangement if feasible; consider radiotherapy for local control and CNS-directed therapy given intracranial metastases.
optional_findings: Vascular soft tissue masses in inguinal and popliteal regions

Negative for cytokeratin AE1/AE3, desmin, CD20, CD3, TdT, PAX5
Raised Ki-67 index
Lytic bone lesions (vertebrae, sacrum, right iliac)
Dural-based intracranial metastasis

title: No Identifiable Primary, Only Metastases: Case Report of a Small Round Blue Cell Tumor Consistent With Ewing Sarcoma Presenting With Multisystem Spread in a Young Woman

challenge_prompt: A 54-year-old South Asian man presents with left-sided hemiparesis 3 months after a sudden loss of consciousness while driving, leading to a fall from his motorcycle. He was diagnosed with a hemorrhagic stroke at a tertiary center. He has a 3-year history of hypertension with inconsistent medication adherence. On examination, he is conscious and oriented (MMSE 28/30). Cranial nerves intact. Left upper limb shows increased tone: shoulder flexors 1+, extensors 0, abductors 1+, adductors 0; elbow flexors 0, extensors 0; wrist flexors 0, extensors 0 (Modified Ashworth Scale). Reflexes: biceps +, triceps +, supinator +, knee +, ankle +, plantar flexor bilaterally. Left shoulder depressed, adducted, internally rotated; elbow flexed, forearm pronated. Palpation meter shows scapular height discrepancy of 1.3 cm. Fugl-Meyer Assessment for Upper Extremity (FMA-UE) score 24/66. Functional Independence Measure (FIM) score 99/126. CT brain reveals a moderate-sized bleed in the right gangliocapsular region with involvement of the internal capsule, abutting the anterior pole of the right lateral ventricle.

challenge_prompt: A 21-year-old primipara underwent spinal anesthesia (SA) in the right lateral position for an emergency caesarian section. The injection was initially attempted at L1–L2 using a 25-gauge spinal needle. Since there was no free flow of cerebrospinal fluid, a second attempt was made at T12–L1 interspace. A bolus dose of 1.6 mL 0.5% bupivacaine was injected into the subarachnoid space. The patient experienced radiating pain down the left lower extremity during the procedure, but did not report it immediately. The next day she noted persistent numbness and weakness of her left lower limb with normal bladder and bowel sensations. At presentation, vitals and general examination were unremarkable. Neurological examination revealed moderate weakness of the left lower limb: proximal 3/5 and distal 2/5 (MRC grade). All muscle stretch reflexes in the lower limbs were absent except the right knee reflex which was easily elicitable. There was dissociative sensory loss on the left side of the abdomen and lower limb below T8 with absent pain and temperature sensations, but preserved touch and kinesthetic sensations. Routine investigations were normal. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine performed one week later revealed lower thoracic cord expansion with extensive signal changes extending from T7 to T12, which were isointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, without contrast enhancement. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or treatment step?

aliases: Iatrogenic spinal cord injury due to spinal anesthesia
Direct needle injury of spinal cord
Spinal cord trauma from lumbar puncture
answer_key.diagnosis: Traumatic spinal cord injury (direct needle trauma during spinal anesthesia)
answer_key.etiology: Direct needle injury of the spinal cord during spinal anesthesia, with possible intraparenchymal injection of local anesthetic
answer_key.next_management_step: Administer high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g/day for 5 days) followed by a short course of oral steroids, and provide supportive care

No free flow of CSF on first attempt

MRI: lower thoracic cord expansion with T2 hyperintensity from T7 to T12, no contrast enhancement

title: Accidental spinal cord injury during spinal anesthesia: A report

challenge_prompt: A 66-year-old male presented with a two-week history of acutely worsening back pain and two days of altered mental status. He was intubated for airway protection. Initial exam showed equally reactive pupils and withdrawal to pain in all extremities. Labs: WBC 12.1 × 10^9/L, ESR 128 mm/h, CRP 53.6 mg/L, procalcitonin 26.20 ng/mL. Empiric vancomycin and cefepime were started. CT head showed moderate ventricular prominence. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis were unremarkable aside from mild atelectasis. MRI spine showed diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement along cervical and thoracic spine, L4-L5 facet joint effusions, and small paraspinal muscle abscesses. Brain MRI showed leptomeningeal enhancement, communicating hydrocephalus, ventriculitis, and punctate infarcts in inferior frontal lobes and right paramedian frontal lobe. Blood cultures grew Streptococcus pneumoniae. CSF analysis: WBC 113 cells/μL, protein 319 mg/dL, glucose <10 mg/dL; CSF cultures also positive for S. pneumoniae. Antibiotics de-escalated to ceftriaxone. Dexamethasone was not given as meningitis was diagnosed after antibiotic initiation. On day 6, the patient developed an asymmetric pupil; repeat brain MRI showed acute infarcts in right cerebellum and left basal ganglia. Transthoracic and transesophageal echocardiogram were negative for endocarditis. On day 24, left hemiplegia developed; repeat brain MRI showed additional acute infarcts in right thalamus and left basal ganglia. CT angiography (CTA) of head and neck was unremarkable. Repeat CSF and blood cultures were negative. On day 30, repeat CTA showed multifocal intracranial stenosis and a new left cerebellar hypoattenuation. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis (or etiology) and what is the next most appropriate management step?

aliases: cerebral vasculopathy secondary to bacterial meningitis
answer_key.diagnosis: Delayed cerebral vasculopathy (DCV) following pneumococcal meningitis
answer_key.etiology: Inflammatory and immune-mediated vascular injury after pneumococcal meningitis, possibly exacerbated by delayed corticosteroid administration
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider cerebral angiography or CT angiography to confirm vasculopathy, and initiate vasodilatory therapy (e.g., milrinone) or intra-arterial vasodilators

challenge_prompt:
A 72-year-old Caucasian male presented with subacute onset of painful tongue weakness and dysphagia over three days. He had a history of papillary urothelial cancer treated with transurethral resection three years ago, followed by cystectomy for recurrence with local lymph node metastases two years and three months prior. He declined adjuvant cytostatic treatment. Three days after tongue weakness, he developed increasing dysphagia, dysarthria, lockjaw, and painful facial spasms. Neurological examination revealed moderate dysarthria, dysphagia, dysconjugate eye movements, and an inverted plantar reflex on the left. MRI brain showed mild central atrophy but was otherwise normal. CSF analysis showed mild pleocytosis (8 mononuclear cells/µL); CSF antibodies for anti-NMDA, anti-AMPA receptors 1 and 2, GABA-B receptors 1 and 2, anti-LGI1, anti-CASPR2, and anti-DPPX IgG were negative. Serum paraneoplastic antibodies including anti-GAD65 and anti-amphiphysin were negative. Tetanus was suspected due to lockjaw and stimulus-sensitive spasms, and he was treated with intravenous penicillin, tetanus immunoglobulin, and diazepam, with significant improvement. He was discharged after 20 days. One month later, he was readmitted with deterioration: increasing painful spasms in extremities aggravated by noise and touch, lockjaw, swallowing difficulties, slurred speech, and diplopia. Symptoms were attributed to tetanus and treated with diazepam, baclofen, onabotulinum toxin, and physiotherapy without improvement. Three weeks after discharge from rehabilitation, he was readmitted with sudden bouts of whole-body stiffness accompanied by perioral cyanosis and brief episodes of unconsciousness responsive to diazepam. He had hallucinations and delusions. Neurological exam showed myoclonic jerks, painful spasms triggered by passive movement, and bilateral pes equinus. EEG on three occasions showed no epileptiform activity. Tetanus was considered unlikely due to progression despite adequate treatment. EMG revealed simultaneous activation of motor units in agonist and antagonist muscles in upper and lower extremities. CT thorax showed a nodule in the lower lobe of the right lung; CT abdomen showed enlarged paraaortic lymph nodes. Immunofluorescence assay for paraneoplastic antibodies showed strong positivity for anti-GlyR IgG in serum (1:3200) and CSF (1:100). Routine CSF examination and assays for other antibodies (amphiphysin, GAD65, titin, NMDAR, AMPAR1/2, DPPX, GABRB1, CASPR2, LGI1) were negative.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.aliases: PERM, stiff-person syndrome spectrum disorder, paraneoplastic PERM
answer_key.diagnosis: Progressive encephalomyelitis with rigidity and myoclonus (PERM) associated with anti-glycine receptor antibodies
answer_key.etiology: Paraneoplastic syndrome secondary to urothelial carcinoma with anti-glycine receptor antibodies
answer_key.next_management_step: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) and chemotherapy (e.g., gemcitabine/cisplatin) for underlying cancer
optional_findings: Mild central atrophy on brain MRI
required_findings: anti-GlyR IgG positive in serum (1:3200) and CSF (1:100)
EMG showing simultaneous activation of agonist and antagonist muscles
CT thorax showing lung nodule
CT abdomen showing enlarged paraaortic lymph nodes
History of urothelial carcinoma (recurrence with lymph node metastases)
Clinical features: dysphagia, dysarthria, lockjaw, stimulus-sensitive spasms, myoclonus
Progression despite adequate tetanus treatment
Negative other autoimmune and paraneoplastic antibodies

title: Progressive encephalomyelitis with rigidity and myoclonus (PERM) associated with anti-glycine receptor antibodies and urothelial carcinoma: a case report

challenge_prompt: A 70-year-old male with type 2 diabetes and myasthenia gravis presents with acute onset of vertigo, ataxia, and slurred speech over two days. He is a known case of skull base osteomyelitis caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa. He received IV metronidazole 1500 mg/day for 5 days followed by oral metronidazole for 5 weeks. At presentation, cumulative metronidazole dose is 60 g. Clinical exam reveals dysdiadochokinesia, positive Romberg’s sign, restricted lateral gaze bilaterally, and staccato speech. Brain MRI shows symmetrical hyperintensities on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences in the bilateral dentate nuclei and tectum. What is the most likely diagnosis/etiology and the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.etiology: Metronidazole toxicity
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue metronidazole and initiate alternative antibiotic therapy for osteomyelitis (e.g., meropenem based on antibiogram) and supportive care.

challenge_prompt:
An 11-month-old Iranian Turk boy was referred for progressive limb weakness and loss of previously attained developmental milestones for the past 2 months. He also had persistent fever and loss of consciousness for about 14 to 21 days. He was born at 35 weeks by cesarean section with normal birth weight (2650 g), length (50 cm), and head circumference (35 cm). He received BCG and other routine immunizations. Neonatal period, development up to 9 months, and family history were unremarkable. At 11 months, parents noted progressive weakness of all four limbs, loss of head control, inability to creep or crawl, constant crying and irritability. Subsequently, he became unresponsive and lost consciousness. He was taken to a hospital where CT and MRI of the brain showed hydrocephalus, possibly due to acute bacterial meningitis, and he was treated with acetazolamide, ceftriaxone, corticosteroid, and antipyretic for 11 days. He was referred due to clinical deterioration and new symptoms: convergence deviation of the eyes and flexion of lower limbs when held upright. On examination at our center: ill, febrile (38.6°C axillary), lethargic, continuously crying, lost head control, unable to crawl. Anterior fontanel bulging, neck stiffness. Left 6th cranial nerve paresis and quadriparesis (3/5 for both upper and lower limbs). Hyperreflexia (DTR 3/4) and extensor plantar reflexes bilaterally. Brain CT without contrast: right basal ganglia hypodensity consistent with infarct-induced ischemia along with four-ventricular hydrocephalus. Brain MRI with contrast: severe four-ventricular hydrocephalus with peri-ependymal edema, significant leptomeningeal enhancement of intracranial cisterns and sulci, and enhancing foci at bilateral thalami and pons. During endoscopic third ventriculostomy, diffuse thickening of the floor and lateral walls of the 3rd ventricle and cobblestone appearance with multiple white patchy lesions were noted. CSF obtained during the procedure: protein 183 mg/dL (normal 15-45), glucose 56 mg/dL (normal 45-80), blood glucose 109 mg/dL (normal 70-179), WBC 55/mm3 (normal 0-25), RBC 0/mm3, PMN 10%, MN 90%.

CSF culture grew Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

answer_key.diagnosis: Tuberculous meningitis
answer_key.etiology: Mycobacterium tuberculosis
answer_key.next_management_step: Start empiric antitubercular therapy (isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide) and consider endoscopic third ventriculostomy with biopsy if not already performed.

hypodensity in right basal ganglia on CT
enhancing foci in thalami and pons on MRI
cobblestone appearance of third ventricle floor during endoscopy

bulging anterior fontanel and neck stiffness
left 6th cranial nerve paresis
quadriparesis with hyperreflexia and extensor plantars
hydrocephalus (four-ventricular) on CT/MRI
right basal ganglia infarct on CT
leptomeningeal enhancement on MRI
CSF: protein 183 mg/dL, glucose 56 mg/dL (blood glucose 109), WBC 55/mm3 with 90% mononuclear cells

title: An 11-month-old boy with tuberculous meningitis presenting as progressive limb weakness, fever, developmental retardation, and loss of consciousness: a case report

challenge_prompt:
A 22-year-old black African woman, primigravida at 14 weeks gestation, presents with a 1-month history of unilateral left-sided throbbing headache unresponsive to paracetamol, ibuprofen, and anti-migraine medications. Two weeks prior, she developed severe headache and a one-day history of serial seizures: jerking of the right arm progressing to generalized tonic-clonic seizures with eye rolling, no loss of consciousness, each lasting 2-3 minutes, followed by confusion and persistent right arm weakness. She reports two episodes of epistaxis. On examination, she is afebrile, BP 110/60, pulse 101, O2 sat 99%. Neurologic exam: alert, oriented, cranial nerves intact, power grade 3/5 in right upper limb, normal tone, reflexes, sensation, coordination, and gait. ENT examination reveals an actively bleeding small tuft of blood vessels in the right nasal mucosa. Brain MRA shows multiple foci of low signal on post-central sulcus of both parietal lobes (AVMs) with associated hyperintensity on T2WI and intra-lesional hyperintensity on T1WI, consistent with bilateral parietal lobe bleeding AVMs with vasogenic edema. No deep venous drainage is identified.

Based on the presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Ruptured bilateral brain arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) likely associated with hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT)
answer_key.etiology: Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome)
answer_key.next_management_step: Conservative management with anticonvulsants (e.g., lamotrigine) and antifibrinolytics (e.g., tranexamic acid), close neurological and obstetric monitoring, and genetic counseling for HHT.
