Power grade 3/5 in right upper limb
Normal sensation, reflexes, coordination, and gait
No skin rashes or other systemic abnormalities

challenge_prompt: A 29-year-old Sri Lankan female doctor presents with a 23-day history of on-and-off low-grade fever without chills or rigors, associated with arthralgia and myalgia. For the past 5 days, she has had a gradually worsening headache with phonophobia, but no photophobia, nausea, or vomiting. She has a background history of migraine. She was diagnosed with asymptomatic COVID-19 in October 2020 and received the Oxford AstraZeneca (AZD-1222) vaccine. Two weeks after the second dose, she had fever and left axillary pain with self-detected axillary lymphadenopathy, which resolved within two days. She was well for six weeks until this episode. On examination, temperature 39.3°C, no neck stiffness or focal neurological signs, no skin rash, no hepatosplenomegaly, and no lymphadenopathy initially. Laboratory: Hb 10.1 g/dL, WBC 5.31×10^3/μL, platelets 140×10^3/μL, CRP 49.7 mg/L, ESR 42 mm/hr, LDH 620 U/L, ferritin 1704 ng/mL, AST 32 U/L, ALT 62 U/L, albumin 30 g/L. CSF: total cells 48/mm³ (75% lymphocytes), protein 50.6 mg/dL, glucose 2.5 mmol/L (serum glucose 6.4 mmol/L), adenosine deaminase negative, TB culture negative. Blood and CSF cultures negative. ANA negative, anti-dsDNA negative, C3 133.9 mg/dL, C4 51.3 mg/dL, HIV 1/2 antibodies negative, anti-EBV IgM negative, VDRL non-reactive, Mantoux negative, sputum AFB negative. Initially treated with IV ceftriaxone 2 g bid with mild improvement, but then developed spiking fever with painful axillary (mainly left) and cervical lymphadenopathy. Ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT showed enlarged lymph nodes with preserved architecture, thickened cortex, and peripherally enhancing cervical nodes at level II B. Axillary lymph node biopsy: nodal tissue with preserved architecture, necrotizing regions with histiocytes and numerous karyorrhexis in paracortical distribution, hyperplastic follicles, no granulomas, no evidence of lymphoma. Bone marrow biopsy: reactive marrow. Vasculitic screen negative. Based on this presentation and workup, what is the most likely diagnosis?

answer_key.diagnosis: Kikuchi disease (Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease) complicated with aseptic meningitis
answer_key.etiology: Post-viral or post-vaccination immune trigger, likely COVID-19 vaccine
answer_key.next_management_step: Start oral corticosteroids after excluding tuberculosis (e.g., prednisolone 45 mg daily)

Transient axillary lymphadenopathy after second vaccine dose
Response to corticosteroids after failure of antibiotics

challenge_prompt: An African-American female in her fifties with past medical history of hypertension presents to outpatient clinic with complaints of "blurred vision" for two weeks. Symptoms are associated with facial discomfort and generalized headache. She denies fever, problems with speech or swallowing, weakness or numbness, abnormal gait, or preceding infections or vaccinations. On physical examination, there is swelling of the left upper eyelid. Extraocular movements reveal defects: the right eye fails to move in the horizontal plane (abduction and adduction), while the left eye fails to adduct (move inwards) but abduction is normal. Left upper eyelid droops (ptosis), and the left eye fails to lift/elevate completely when looking upwards. A positive Cogan lid twitch is noted. Vision, detailed fundoscopic exam, and complete neurological examination are normal. Imaging studies including CT orbits, MRI brain, MRA, and MRV of the brain are unremarkable. Blood work shows positive anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies, negative anti-MUSK antibodies, negative GQ1b antibodies, negative rheumatoid factors, negative anti-smooth muscle antibody, negative ANA, negative anti-thyroid antibody, and normal thyroid profile.

answer_key.diagnosis: Ocular myasthenia gravis (OMG) with positive acetylcholine receptor antibodies
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune disorder of neuromuscular junction
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate symptomatic treatment with pyridostigmine and consider corticosteroid therapy (e.g., prednisone). Also, evaluate for thymoma with chest CT.
optional_findings: Negative autoimmune serologies (rheumatoid factors, anti-smooth muscle, ANA, anti-thyroid)

challenge_prompt: A 71-year-old man with epithelioid neoplasm of the lung presents with confusion, personality changes, and loss of appetite within 2 weeks after cycle 6 of pembrolizumab. Admission labs show acute kidney injury, hypernatremia (149 mmol/L), mild hypokalemia (3.1 mmol/L), and low cortisol and ACTH levels. Initial exam: inattention, lethargy, follows simple commands, intact motor strength and reflexes. He is diagnosed with immune checkpoint inhibitor-mediated central adrenal insufficiency and treated with steroids and thiamine, with correction of metabolic derangements. Despite this, he becomes more delirious and lethargic. Repeat exam: only intermittently follows 1-step commands, recognizes few objects, unable to calculate change, moves extremities spontaneously. Routine EEG shows mild diffuse slowing without epileptiform discharges. Brain MRI with and without contrast shows no signal abnormalities. Lumbar puncture: protein 44 mg/dL, glucose 86 mg/dL, WBC 1, RBC 1; infectious panel and cytology negative. CSF and serum autoantibody panels negative. challenge_prompt: A 53-year-old man presents with a 3-year history of progressive muscle cramps, choreiform movements of all four limbs and orolingual muscles, slurred speech, and tongue and lip biting. He has asymmetrical weakness (L>R) of all limbs, proximal muscle wasting, occasional fasciculations in deltoids and triceps, areflexia, flexor plantar responses, no sensory deficits, normal cognition (MMSE 30/30). Family history: two older brothers with similar but variable phenotypes (one had weakness without movement disorder, the other had chorea and seizures). No family history of inherited neurologic disease in prior generations. Investigations: serum CK mildly elevated (400 U/L; normal <180), normal ceruloplasmin, normal lipid profile. MRI brain shows mild caudate atrophy. EMG shows chronic partial denervation and reinnervation (fibrillations, fasciculations, positive sharp waves, high-amplitude long-duration potentials) consistent with anterior horn cell disease. Nerve conduction studies show mild slowing and reduced CMAP amplitudes, normal sensory conductions. Peripheral blood smear (Leishman stain) shows 75% acanthocytes. Extended RBC phenotyping: O Rh(D) positive, C+, c+, E-, e+, K-, k weak by adsorption/elution (weaker expression of k antigen). Direct antiglobulin test negative. ECG, Holter, and echocardiogram normal. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

answer_key.diagnosis: McLeod syndrome (neuroacanthocytosis, X-linked recessive)
answer_key.etiology: Mutation in XK gene (deletion of exon 2) leading to absence of Kx RBC antigen and weak expression of Kell antigens
answer_key.next_management_step: Confirm by molecular genetic testing of XK gene; consider treatment with tetrabenazine for chorea; screen for cardiomyopathy and seizures; counsel regarding X-linked inheritance

challenge_prompt: A 34-year-old Chinese man presents with a 3-day history of progressive left lower limb weakness and lumbar pain. He has difficulty walking independently and requires assistance. He also reports dysuria. Neurological examination reveals grade 2 muscle strength in the left lower limb, diminished tendon reflexes in the left lower limb, and reduced pain and temperature perception in the right lower limb. Routine blood work is normal. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis, including metagenomic next-generation sequencing and demyelinating antibody titers, shows no abnormalities. Brain MRI is unremarkable. Lumbar spine MRI demonstrates sagittal short segments of T1 and T2 hypointensity and axial T2 hypointensity with a spindle-shaped hematoma sign along the spinal cord tract extending from T12 to L1. There is also swelling in the annular region surrounding the fringe of T2 hypointensity between T9 and T11. No contrast enhancement is seen. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate step in management?

challenge_prompt: A 47-year-old male police officer presents with a 1-month history of persistent, dull headaches associated with ocular discomfort, intermittent nausea, and visual strain that worsens during prolonged near tasks and smartphone use. Prior neurological evaluation including a CT scan of the brain was normal. Ocular history: mild refractive error, no prior surgery. Examination: best-corrected visual acuity 20/25 OD, 20/30 OS; intraocular pressure 14.0 mmHg OD, 19.7 mmHg OS; slit-lamp exam normal; fundus normal; orthoptic evaluation shows normal alignment, full motility, no esodeviation at near, no abduction limitation, and no pupillary miosis. Pre-cycloplegic autorefraction: -0.75 -1.75 x 9° OD and -0.75 -0.75 x 159° OS. At Visit 1, unilateral cycloplegic refraction (1% cyclopentolate) of the left eye shows post-cycloplegic refraction +1.25 -1.00 x 165°, a +1.87 D hyperopic shift. At Visit 2 (14 days later), the patient reports mild improvement but persistent discomfort. Pre-cycloplegic refraction of right eye: plano -1.50 x 9°; post-cycloplegic refraction: +1.75 -1.50 x 8°, a +1.75 D hyperopic shift. The spherical equivalent of the non-cycloplegic right eye improved from -1.625 D at Visit 1 to -0.75 D at Visit 2 after two weeks of near-addition wear and reduced near-work. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?

aliases: Spasm of the near reflex (isolated accommodative spasm)
answer_key.diagnosis: Accommodative spasm presenting as pseudomyopia
answer_key.etiology: Prolonged ciliary muscle contraction from sustained near-work/digital device use

challenge_prompt: 70-year-old Sri Lankan man with hypertension and stroke presents with 3-month history of burning dysesthesia in distal extremities and scalp. He has a 4-year history of progressive exertional dyspnea and 6-month history of bilateral lower limb edema. No fever, weight loss, cough. CT chest over 4 years showed evolving reticulonodular opacities, horizontal fissure thickening, and paratracheal/hilar lymphadenopathy. CECT abdomen revealed bilateral renal cortical hypodense lesions (left 3x4x5 cm, right 3.5x3.5x4 cm) without arterial enhancement. Renal biopsy showed dense lymphoplasmocytic infiltration and storiform fibrosis consistent with tubulointerstitial nephritis; IgG4 immunostaining showed >25 positive cells per high power field. Nerve conduction studies demonstrated mononeuritis multiplex. Labs: Hb 8.7 g/dL, macrocytes, ESR persistently >100 mm/hr over 4 years (range 96-125), normal creatinine, low C3 77 mg/dL (83-177) and C4 9 mg/dL (12-36). Extensive TB workup (sputum GeneXpert x4, Mantoux, EBUS node biopsy, BAL) negative. No lymphadenopathy on exam, no skin lesions. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and the next appropriate management step?

answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune fibroinflammatory disorder of unknown trigger
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone 0.6 mg/kg/day) and consider rituximab if inadequate response or contraindications to steroids.

challenge_prompt:
A 16-year-old Moroccan girl of Arab origin with a history of pain and swelling in the right lower limb presented to the emergency department with acute onset of headache, followed by continuous generalized tonic–clonic seizures lasting approximately 30 minutes without regaining consciousness between episodes. Urgent management with intravenous benzodiazepines was initiated. Following stabilization, contrast-enhanced brain CT revealed a right parietal ovoid lesion with well-defined margins, heterogeneously hyperdense, and mild post-contrast enhancement. Brain MRI showed a well-circumscribed right parietal mass with intermediate signal on T2 and FLAIR, diffusion restriction, areas of intrinsic high signal on T1 and T2, and low signal on T2*, consistent with hemorrhagic components. The lesion exhibited ring enhancement on post-contrast subtraction sequences and was surrounded by perilesional edema. Multivoxel MRS showed a lactate and lipid peak on short TE, decreased NAA, and an inverted choline/creatine ratio on long TE, with normal peritumoral spectrum, suggestive of a secondary brain lesion. Contrast-enhanced CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules with soft-tissue density and smooth margins, along with an aggressive lytic lesion centered on the right femoral neck breaking through the cortex and extending into adjacent soft tissues. MRI of the thigh confirmed a large tumor centered on the right femoral neck, hyperintense on T2, with diffusion restriction and heterogeneous enhancement. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or management step?

A bone biopsy was performed, and histopathology confirmed a conventional osteosarcoma of the right femur.

answer_key.diagnosis: Osteosarcoma with hemorrhagic brain metastasis and pulmonary metastases
answer_key.etiology: Primary malignant bone tumor, likely osteosarcoma, with hematogenous spread to lungs and brain
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform biopsy of the right femoral lesion to confirm histologic diagnosis, followed by chemotherapy and whole-brain radiotherapy

challenge_prompt:
A 24-year-old male presents with sudden onset of left-sided weakness, found fallen in the morning. He complains of intense holocranial headache and fever, followed by drowsiness and hypo-responsiveness. He has been attending outpatient follow-ups for epigastric pain diagnosed as gastritis for two months. No smoking, alcohol, or chronic diseases. Not on corticosteroids or immunosuppressants. Examination: drowsy, clear speech, gaze deviated to right, mydriatic pupils reactive to light, left facial droop, left hemiparesis (muscle strength 1/5), neck stiffness, preserved sensation. Temperature 38.9°C, BP 90/55 mmHg, HR 90 bpm. Cardiac exam: 5/6 murmur at 2nd and 5th intercostal spaces left mid-clavicular line with thrill. Labs: leukocytosis with neutrophilic predominance, moderate anemia, mild hyponatremia and hypochloremia. Malaria, HIV, and COVID-19 tests negative. Blood culture positive for Staphylococcus aureus, sensitive to ceftriaxone. Cardiac ultrasound reveals carditis with vegetations on mitral and aortic valves. Brain MRI shows an extensive lesion in the right middle cerebral artery territory and occipital region with extensive perilesional edema.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next most appropriate management step?

answer_key.etiology: Infective endocarditis due to Staphylococcus aureus on native valves (mitral and aortic), likely associated with underlying rheumatic heart disease

challenge_prompt: A 12-year-old Russian boy, born to non-consanguineous parents, presented at age 2 weeks with hypoglycemia, metabolic acidosis, and neonatal cholestasis rapidly progressing to liver failure and encephalopathy. A liver transplant was performed at age 11 months, with etiology of liver failure undetermined at that time. Post-transplant follow-up was complicated by portal vein stenosis treated by mesorex shunt surgery. At age 8 years, genetic testing revealed a homozygous mutation in the first exon of DGUOK (c.3G>A, p.Met1Ile). At age 12 years, the patient developed important amyotrophy and an occasional cough that became chronic. Radiological assessment showed bilateral pneumothorax. Over 4 months, nearly a dozen spontaneous pneumothoraces occurred, requiring multiple pleural drainages and an unsuccessful left surgical pleurodesis. Lung biopsy revealed emphysema and extensive fibrous pleurisy. Microbiological workup of pleural fluid was negative.

challenge_prompt:
A 40-year-old unmarried man presents with a 7-year history of progressive ataxia, clumsiness in fine activities, and 2-year history of slurred speech. He also reports reduced facial, axillary, and pubic hair, and impotence with absence of early morning erections. Past history includes bouts of diarrhea since his early twenties.

On examination: BMI 21, arm span 182 cm, height 170 cm. Reduced facial, axillary, and pubic hair. Gynecomastia. MMSE 30/30. Scanning dysarthria. Bilateral gaze-evoked nystagmus. No pyramidal or sensory deficits. Romberg negative. Bilateral hypoactive ankle reflexes. Bilaterally symmetric dysmetria, dyssynergia, incoordination, truncal ataxia. Broad-based gait ataxia.

Investigations: Hb 11.3 g/dL (normocytic normochromic). Normal metabolic panel. HIV negative. Stool shows minimal fat globules. Vitamin B12 795.7 pg/mL, vitamin E 7.60 mg/L. Serum FSH 1.11 mIU/mL (1.4–18.1), LH 0.30 mIU/mL (1.5–9.3), testosterone 0.33 ng/mL (2.7–10.7). T3, T4, TSH normal. Testicular volume right 9 cc, left 10.5 cc. Nerve conduction: attenuated H reflexes, normal SNAPs and CMAPs. MRI brain: gross cerebellar atrophy. Gastroscopy: mild gastric atrophy, mild duodenitis D1. Duodenal biopsy: short flattened villi, intra-epithelial lymphocytic infiltrates, increased villous-crypt ratio (consistent with celiac sprue). Serum IgA anti-gliadin antibodies 31.2 U/mL (≤11), IgG anti-gliadin 17.4 U/mL (11–17), IgA anti-tissue transglutaminase 168.90 U/mL (0–20), IgA anti-endomysial antibodies 243.40 U/mL (0–20).

Based on this presentation and workup, what is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next management step?

challenge_prompt: NS, a 40-year-old male diver, worked for a bridge-construction firm diving to 20–30 meters depth. He was a chronic smoker. On April 8, 2008, he surfaced rapidly from a dive within seconds. Immediately after surfacing, he developed severe back and neck pain, bilateral lower limb weakness, and generalized myalgias. He was treated with NSAIDs and IV fluids but symptoms persisted. He also developed hearing difficulty, abdominal pain, giddiness, and vomiting. He presented to a hospital 10–12 hours after the incident. On examination: BP 80/60, HR 140, RR 30, mild confusion. No respiratory findings. Neurologic exam revealed hypotonia in both lower limbs, patchy sensory loss to T10 level, areflexia, and urinary retention. Cranial nerves and cognition were normal. Labs: Hb 14.2 g/dL, WBC 14,900/mm3, platelets 92,000/mm3, CPK 1,192 U/L (elevated), normal electrolytes and ABG. HIV negative. ECG and chest X-ray normal. challenge_prompt: A previously healthy Caucasian man in his fifties presents with recurrent acute episodes, each starting shortly after meals. Episodes begin with perioral paresthesias and numbness, followed by abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. He also experiences marked dizziness and fatigue. Family members report brief loss of consciousness during some episodes. On examination during episodes, severe hypotension (70/40 mmHg) is noted, along with frequent ventricular ectopic complexes. He has experienced two documented cardiac arrests with polymorphic ventricular tachycardia degenerating to ventricular fibrillation. Between episodes, clinical examination, including neurological examination, is normal. Extensive cardiac workup between episodes shows: normal resting ECG with normal QTc interval; normal echocardiography; normal cardiac MRI with no structural, valvular, or vascular abnormalities and no signs of inflammation or scarring; normal coronary angiography; no genetic variants associated with channelopathies or cardiomyopathies; and negative invasive electrophysiology testing with no induced arrhythmias. He has a history of two prior episodes of acute renal tubular injury (10 and 6 years ago) that recovered after short-term dialysis, and one episode of severe acute hepatic failure (2 years ago) that recovered spontaneously, with no etiology found. He has monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS). Antiepileptic medication was started for suspected epilepsy but did not prevent episodes; episodes have included convulsive movements, tongue biting, and shoulder dislocation. A loop recorder and later an ICD have been implanted. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely etiology of these episodes? What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

answer_key.etiology: Intentional or accidental ingestion of aconitine, a toxin from Aconitum plants
answer_key.next_management_step: Obtain serum and urine toxicology screening for aconitine during an acute episode; consider hair analysis for retrospective confirmation

challenge_prompt:
A 49-year-old female with a history of left breast cancer treated with partial mastectomy and paclitaxel-based chemotherapy developed numbness and hypesthesia in the periphery of her extremities during chemotherapy. She was treated with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, mecobalamin, pregabalin, and amitriptyline without sufficient improvement. Four years after completing chemotherapy, she presented with chief complaint of numbness in the extremities. Examination revealed reduced sensations to temperature, touch, and vibration in the bilateral toes and soles. Manual muscle testing of the flexor digitorum longus muscles was 4-5/5, with no marked toe motion impairment. Numbness was more severe in the toes than in the fingers. Numerical rating scale (NRS) score for both fingers and toes was 8, and the pain DETECT questionnaire (PD-Q) score was 30. Cold stimulation aggravated pain, causing severe pain necessitating absence from work. The main symptom was bilateral symmetrical numbness, with no findings suggesting peripheral neuropathy of spinal origin. Spinal magnetic resonance imaging showed no abnormal signals in the spinal cord. Workup for autoimmune diseases, diabetes mellitus, and other peripheral neuropathies was negative. Duloxetine was initiated at 20 mg/day and increased to 60 mg/day. After duloxetine, NRS improved to 6, but hand pain and numbness persisted.

What is the most likely etiology of this neuropathy, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

Her past medical history includes bilateral renal artery stenosis (RAS) diagnosed 10–15 years prior, managed with a stent in the left renal artery which later became nonfunctional, leading to left kidney atrophy. She is on aspirin, statin, and oral antihypertensive medications. Three years ago, she developed symptoms of dry mouth, dry eyes, dizziness, and on-and-off headache. Workup at that time revealed Schirmer’s test with 0 mm on bilateral eyes, low complement factor III, positive antinuclear antibody (ANA), and positive anti-Sjögren’s syndrome-related antigen A (anti-SSA) 60 kDa and 52 kDa antibodies. She was started on hydroxychloroquine but discontinued it 7–8 months ago. On admission, she is afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and neurologically intact apart from the seizures. Brain MRI shows mild bifrontal atrophy without acute pathology. Lumbar puncture yields normal cerebrospinal fluid. Obstetric ultrasound shows a single live intrauterine fetus at 16 weeks and 2 days with normal parameters.

challenge_prompt: A 75-year-old patient with severe left internal carotid artery stenosis undergoes elective carotid artery stenting under local anesthesia. Approximately 2 minutes after stent deployment, the patient develops abrupt hypotensive shock (systolic blood pressure falls from approximately 140 mmHg to approximately 80 mmHg within seconds). This is immediately followed by reduced consciousness, global aphasia, and right-sided weakness. The patient cannot follow simple commands. Intravenous vasopressor is administered, and blood pressure normalizes within 3–4 minutes. Neurological deficits begin to improve shortly thereafter, with consciousness recovering first and mild aphasia persisting the longest. Functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) monitoring shows a progressive rise in the standard deviation (STD) of oxygenated hemoglobin concentration (Δ[OxyHb]) beginning shortly after angioplasty and peaking around the onset of shock. The fNIRS signal pattern mirrors the temporal evolution of the clinical event. Based only on the presentation above, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology of this acute neurological event, and what is the next appropriate management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Carotid sinus reflex-induced hypotension leading to acute cerebral hypoperfusion
answer_key.etiology: Carotid sinus reflex activation during carotid stenting causing profound hypotension and cerebral hypoperfusion
answer_key.next_management_step: Administer intravenous vasopressors (e.g., phenylephrine or norepinephrine) and consider atropine if bradycardic; continue hemodynamic support and monitor neurological status.

challenge_prompt: A 29-year-old primigravida woman with a known bicornuate uterus presented with preterm premature rupture of membranes at 35 weeks' gestation. She received tocolysis with ritodrine hydrochloride and antibiotics. Ultrasound showed reduced amniotic fluid and a fetal vertex position. Tocolysis was stopped to promote vaginal delivery. One hour before birth, cardiotocography showed non-reassuring patterns. Pelvic examination confirmed a fixed cephalic position. Half an hour before birth, fetal bradycardia was sustained, and pelvic examination revealed an unexpectedly floating fetal head. An emergency cesarean section was performed, revealing a breech presentation, very little amniotic fluid, no meconium staining, and no cord coiling. The neonate (male, 2304 g, 35 weeks 5 days) had severe asphyxia with APGAR scores of 1 at 1 minute and 3 at 5 minutes. Umbilical cord blood gas was not obtained. At 15 minutes after birth, venous blood gas showed pH 6.85, pvCO2 77 mmHg, HCO3- 12.6 mmol/L. The neonate required intubation, mechanical ventilation, and phenobarbital. He was transferred for therapeutic hypothermia. No congenital anomalies were identified. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely cause of the severe asphyxia, and what preventive management step could have been considered?

aliases: Acute intrapartum asphyxia from fetal position inversion
Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy due to fetal malpresentation
answer_key.diagnosis: Severe neonatal asphyxia due to acute fetal distress from unexpected fetal malpresentation (breech) in the setting of bicornuate uterus and oligohydramnios
answer_key.etiology: Unexpected inversion of fetal position from vertex to breech during labor, likely causing umbilical cord compression or twisting, exacerbated by oligohydramnios and lack of amniotic fluid cushion
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider planned elective cesarean delivery for pregnant women with known uterine anomalies and preterm premature rupture of membranes, especially if tocolysis is used, or perform frequent ultrasound assessments during labor to detect malpresentation

challenge_prompt:
A 28-year-old male presented with a large reddish-purple, soft tumor on the left upper arm, specifically above the deltoid muscle, which had been present for over 10 years. The lesion appeared shortly after he received a meningitis vaccination at that site. He had no other symptoms or relevant medical history. On physical examination, the lesion resembled a keloid but was soft upon palpation. Laboratory tests were negative for hepatitis B and C; liver enzymes and other parameters were within normal ranges. challenge_prompt: A 74-year-old female with a history of bipolar disorder diagnosed at age 16, with a family history of bipolar disorder (mother died by suicide), experienced repeated manic and depressive episodes but never received lithium treatment. In old age, depressive episodes became predominant, leading to a catatonic state and a suicide attempt by jumping from a second floor, resulting in a lumbar vertebral burst fracture. After surgical repair, she presented with severe depressive symptoms, suicidal ideation, and delusions of guilt (Hamilton Depression Rating Scale = 29). Brain CT showed slight diffuse atrophy; blood tests and EEG were normal. Mini-Mental State Examination score was 28 with points lost on calculation. She was initially diagnosed with severe psychotic bipolar depression and started on quetiapine extended-release, but due to swallowing dysfunction, lurasidone was administered via nasogastric tube. Over the following weeks, cognitive fluctuations and parkinsonism (rigidity, bradykinesia) gradually emerged. Facility staff reported progressive attention and executive dysfunction starting one year prior. Single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) revealed reduced cerebral blood flow in bilateral occipital and temporal lobes. Dopamine transporter imaging (123I-ioflupane SPECT) showed markedly decreased accumulation in the bilateral striatum. Based on the above presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB)
answer_key.etiology: Synucleinopathy
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate a cholinesterase inhibitor (e.g., donepezil) for cognitive symptoms, consider electroconvulsive therapy for refractory depression, and avoid antipsychotics due to risk of severe hypersensitivity reactions.

challenge_prompt:
A 24-year-old woman of Chinese descent with no prior medical or psychiatric history was brought to the emergency department for a 5-day history of acute behavioral change. There were no known stressors, fever, constitutional symptoms, or infective symptoms. She became socially withdrawn with restlessness, agitation, insomnia, and irrelevant speech. She exhibited classic Cotard's syndrome: nihilistic delusions (e.g., "my body is not real, filled with grave dirt as I am already dead", "I am already dead and buried in the grave", "I am in the afterlife, everyone here is already dead"), delusion of control, auditory hallucinations, and disorganized behavior (spitting out saliva believing it was grave dirt). Echolalia was present. Orientation fluctuated, recent memory was impaired, and affect was inappropriate. Physical exam and vital signs were normal. Full blood count showed raised total white cell count; ESR and CRP were not raised. CT brain was normal. She was admitted to the psychiatric ward and started on oral olanzapine 10 mg at night.

About one week later, she developed a complex seizure with tonic movements of all limbs and up-rolling of eyeballs lasting 1 minute. She then developed low-grade fever, sweating, and tachycardia. Olanzapine was withdrawn due to suspicion of neuroleptic malignant syndrome. MRI brain with contrast was normal. EEG was normal. A lumbar puncture was performed.

Over the next days, she progressed to a catatonic state with resistance to passive movements, no response to commands, no eye opening, and fluctuating consciousness. Physical exam revealed hypertonicity of all four limbs and neck muscles, and oro-buccal dyskinesia (clenching of teeth, repetitive chewing movements). Autonomic instability with hypertension, tachycardia, and fever was present.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

CSF analysis showed positive anti-NMDA receptor antibodies.

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (anti-NMDAR) encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune
answer_key.next_management_step: Test cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) for anti-NMDAR (NR1) IgG antibodies

challenge_prompt:
Ben, a 16-year-old male, was admitted with logorrhea, restlessness, severe anxiety, and psychotic symptoms including thought broadcasting, imperative auditory hallucinations, and delusions of restoring 'magical energy' by showering. He also exhibited catatonic features (reduced mimics, motor slowing, response latency, thought blocking, rigidity, ambitendency) and compulsive rituals. Past medical history: ADHD (methylphenidate), asthma, conjunctivitis, neurodermatitis, mild autistic traits. Family history: uncle with dysthymia, father with asthma. Four and a half years prior, he had an episode of extreme hypernatremia (176 mmol/L) with absent thirst, muscle weakness, fatigue, hypersomnia, weight gain (14 kg in 3 months), autonomic dysregulation (sweating, decreased cold sensitivity), and behavioral changes (disinhibition, perseveration, tics). At that time, workup ruled out salt intoxication, central/nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, limbic encephalitis (including anti-NMDA receptor, anti-GABA receptor, SLE), mucopolysaccharidosis, adrenoleukodystrophy, and Wilson's disease. CSF analysis was normal; EEG normal; IQ normal (101); attention deficits noted. MRI showed a small T2-hyperintensity in the circumventricular region of the hypothalamus. Vasopressin release was not adjusted to serum osmolality, and prolactin levels were persistently elevated. He was prescribed desmopressin and daily fluid intake. Over the next years, he had recurrent hypernatremia and required behavioral support. At current admission, multiple antipsychotics (risperidone, levomepromazine, haloperidol, zuclopenthixol) and benzodiazepines failed to improve psychosis/catatonia; clozapine partially effective. Longitudinal MRIs showed progressive bilateral basal ganglia volume loss. Blood tests for autoimmune encephalitis (including anti-NMDA receptor antibodies) were negative. No findings in CSF or EEG indicated inflammation. Based on the available information, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

Antibodies against the subfornical organ (SFO) were detected.

answer_key.next_management_step: Plasmapheresis and intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy; consider rituximab if refractory

challenge_prompt:
A 16-year-old fully vaccinated female with no past medical history presents with acute onset auditory and visual hallucinations. She reports seeing a flying green dinosaur and hearing voices telling her to hurt herself. Her parents report a one-month decline in grades and social withdrawal, with worsening bizarre behavior and active hallucination interaction over the past two days. There is no prior psychiatric history or family history of schizophrenia. Travel to the Philippines three weeks prior and a cough during that time are noted. In the emergency department, she is afebrile, normotensive, tachycardic (140s bpm), tachypneic (20s bpm), and saturating well on room air. She is tremulous and diaphoretic. Neurologic exam is non-focal including normal gait. Initial workup from the transferring hospital: normal CBC, electrolytes, TSH; negative urine drug screen; normal urinalysis; normal chest radiograph. Non-contrast CT head is unremarkable. Lumbar puncture attempted in ED is unsuccessful. Upon admission, EEG shows no abnormal findings. On day 2, she develops complete catatonia, which improves with lorazepam. MRI brain with and without contrast is normal. Further workup including respiratory viral panel, HIV, hepatitis panel, HSV PCR, and CSF studies (cell count, glucose, protein, meningitis panel, culture) are all normal. On day 4, a GI panel is positive for Cryptosporidium, treated with nitazoxanide. Despite treatment, she continues to have psychosis and difficulty feeding. Psychiatrically, she is deemed not stable for transfer due to medical instability. On day 8, the psychiatry team recommends inpatient psychiatry transfer, but she develops difficulty with independent feeding. On day 11, olanzapine is started for persistent psychosis. Serum and CSF autoimmune encephalitis panels are pending. Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or treatment step?

Serum and CSF autoimmune encephalitis panels revealed anti-neuronal intermediate filament antibodies.

challenge_prompt: A 25-year-old male with a family history significant for psychiatric disorders and a medical history of cavernous vein malformation (confirmed CCM1 gene), status post 3 craniotomies, seizure disorder, major depressive disorder, unspecified anxiety disorder, and prior suicide attempts presents to the Emergency Department with homicidal ideation toward his father. He reports intrusive homicidal thoughts for approximately two years, worsening over time. Days after being kicked out of his parents’ home, he experienced a dissociative episode with visual hallucinations of blood and body parts and a strong desire to harm his father. He voluntarily sought evaluation. He notes a temporal relationship between symptom onset and initiation of zonisamide approximately two years prior, with a daily reproducible pattern: homicidal ideation emerges after medication at ~09:00, peaks around 12:00 (approximately 3 hours post-dose), and gradually subsides. He denies current suicidal ideation, active intoxication, or withdrawal. Alcohol use is intermittent, with no recent heavy use. Physical exam: afebrile, hemodynamically stable, no acute distress. Mental status exam: awake, alert, oriented, coherent speech, intact cognition, linear thought process with prior intrusive violent ideation, good insight and intact judgment. Neurologic exam: chronic left-sided upper motor neuron deficits (left limb strength 4/5, spasticity, hyperreflexia, positive Babinski and Hoffman, left hemibody sensory loss), consistent with prior CCM-related injury. Labs (CBC, CMP, TSH, acetaminophen level, salicylate level, ethanol level, urine drug screen): all within normal limits. Additional investigations: serum zonisamide level 31.3 mcg/mL (therapeutic), magnesium, vitamin B12, folate, vitamin D, RPR, HbA1c normal; ECG normal; spot EEG: right hemispheric cortical dysfunction, no epileptiform discharges or electrographic seizures. Prior brain MRI reviewed: no acute changes. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely etiology of the homicidal ideation, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Probable zonisamide-induced psychiatric adverse effect (homicidal ideation)
answer_key.etiology: Zonisamide
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue zonisamide and cross-titrate to an alternative antiseizure medication with favorable psychiatric profile (e.g., lacosamide, lamotrigine, valproate). Continue psychiatric monitoring and supportive care.

challenge_prompt: A 68-year-old left-handed woman (high school graduate) presents with a 4-month history of persecutory delusions: she believes neighbors enter her house during her absence and follow her everywhere; she finds objects moved and thinks someone moved them; she reports sensitivity to lights and says someone flashes at her. She has extreme anxiety and a delusional mood (feeling something scary will happen) and delusion of observation. No memory deficits, cognitive fluctuation, hallucinations, or mood symptoms. Neurological exam, blood tests, and EEG normal. CSF: cell count 0/μL, protein 35 mg/dL. MMSE 28 (lost 2 points on serial 7's). WMS-R Logical Memory: immediate recall 11, delayed recall 1. WAIS-III subtests (Digit Symbol, Block Design, Digit Span, Information) and ADAS-J cog normal. NPI-12: anxiety, apathy, difficulty falling asleep, appetite loss, delusions. Brain MRI: no atrophy, no cerebrovascular lesions, no white matter T2 hyperintensities. 123I-IMP SPECT and cardiac 123I-MIBG scintigraphy normal. CSF biomarkers: phosphorylated tau (p-tau) 86 pg/mL, total tau (t-tau) 697 pg/mL. APOE genotyping: ε4/ε3. 123I-FP-CIT SPECT: specific binding ratio 6.53 right, 6.04 left (negative). Florbetapir F18 PET: positive. Based on these findings, what is the most likely underlying etiology of the patient's psychotic symptoms?

answer_key.diagnosis: Alzheimer's disease (prodromal AD) presenting as very-late-onset schizophrenia-like psychosis
answer_key.etiology: Alzheimer's disease pathology (amyloid and tau accumulation)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (e.g., rivastigmine) and consider symptomatic management of psychosis with antipsychotics if needed, while monitoring for dementia progression.

No family history of psychiatric or neurological disorders

title: Case Report: Usefulness of Biomarkers for Alzheimer's Disease in Two Cases With Very-Late-Onset Schizophrenia-Like Psychosis

challenge_prompt: A 39-year-old man of European ancestry presents with acute-onset vertigo, balance problems, and nightly confusion. He has a history of pulmonary sarcoidosis diagnosed seven years ago, for which he was prescribed hydroxychloroquine 200 mg/day, but he stopped taking it a few months prior. He also has diabetes mellitus with poor glycemic control. No personal or family history of psychosis; however, three years ago he was seen for anxiety symptoms related to childhood trauma. On neurological examination, there are fluctuating attention problems and confabulations, but no focal deficits. Urine drug screen and serum alcohol are negative. Labs are normal except for CRP 35.6 mg/L (rising to 69.2) and HbA1c 7.4%. During psychiatric evaluation, he exhibits paranoid delusions, auditory and visual hallucinations, and catatonic features scored on the Bush Francis Catatonia Rating Scale: stupor (1), catalepsy (2), psychomotor agitation (4), rigidity (1), gegenhalten (3), and autonomic instability (2) with tachycardia (110 bpm) and tachypnea. He had a paradoxical reaction to lorazepam 2.5 mg, worsening delirium, and olanzapine 5-10 mg aggravated catatonic symptoms. A chest X-ray shows bilateral parenchymatous infiltrates and pleural fluid, consistent with pneumonia. Bronchoalveolar lavage and endotracheal aspirate grow Haemophilus influenzae and coronavirus. He is intubated and transferred for further workup. Lumbar puncture reveals elevated opening pressure (37 cm H2O, 45 with Valsalva); CSF shows WBC 5/μL, protein 100 mg/dL, normal glucose, negative gram stain, cultures (bacterial and mycobacterial), PCR for HSV, EBV, CMV, Toxoplasma, and hepatitis B/C, cytology, flow cytometry, and negative for paraneoplastic and autoimmune encephalitis antibodies (including NMDA-R, LGI1, CASPR2, etc.) and ANA, ANCA, anti-Ro, anti-La, anti-Scl-70, etc. Serum and CSF ACE are normal (23 U/L and 9 U/L). PET-CT shows diffuse lung abnormalities and enlarged mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes consistent with pulmonary sarcoidosis; no masses. EEG shows normal alpha rhythm without epileptiform activity. Contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain demonstrates infratentorial leptomeningeal enhancement, most notably around the brain stem and in the fourth ventricle walls. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

answer_key.diagnosis: Probable neurosarcoidosis
answer_key.etiology: Granulomatous inflammation of the central nervous system secondary to sarcoidosis
answer_key.next_management_step: High-dose corticosteroids (e.g., intravenous methylprednisolone 1g/day for 3-5 days followed by oral prednisone taper) and initiation of a corticosteroid-sparing agent such as azathioprine or methotrexate.
optional_findings: Bilateral pneumonia with H. influenzae and coronavirus
Absence of personal or family psychiatric history

title: Case report: Psychosis with catatonia in an adult man: a presentation of neurosarcoidosis

challenge_prompt:
A 18-year-old male presented with excessive daytime sleepiness, sleeping >20 hours/day, increased appetite with compulsive eating and weight gain, loss of interest in social activities, and occasional visual hallucinations. Symptoms began after starting high school. There was no sleep paralysis. Workup including fluid tests, brain MRI, dopamine transporter (DaTSCAN) scan, and EEG was normal. IQ was excellent. Polysomnography followed by multiple sleep latency test (MSLT) was performed. Based on the presentation, he was diagnosed with a sleep disorder and treated with modafinil 200 mg daily and risperidone. After 15 months, his sleep decreased to 10 h/day, eating normalized, and hallucinations ceased. Therapy was gradually discontinued after 18 months, and he remained asymptomatic for over 2 years, finishing college, getting a job, and starting a family.

In January 2021, he developed respiratory symptoms and loss of smell and taste, diagnosed with moderate SARS-CoV-2 infection by RT-PCR. He developed bilateral pneumonia and was hospitalized for 10 days, treated with antibiotics, corticosteroids, and oxygen.

One month after infection, he experienced recurrence of sleep and eating disturbances identical to his initial episode: sleeping >18 h/day, constant fatigue, overeating with weight gain, loss of interest in work and family, social withdrawal, and occasional outbursts of anger. Neurological exam was normal except for somnolence. Brain MRI showed numerous white matter changes (hyperintense nonconfluent lesions on FLAIR). EEG was normal. Transcranial color Doppler (TCCD) with breath-holding test indicated a decreased breath-holding index and impaired cerebral vasoreactivity.

What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's current condition, and what is the most likely precipitating factor? What is the next appropriate step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Kleine-Levin syndrome (relapse)
answer_key.etiology: SARS-CoV-2 infection (moderate COVID-19)
answer_key.next_management_step: Reintroduce modafinil (200 mg daily) and add a mood stabilizer such as lithium (300 mg daily) for symptom control; provide supportive care and monitor for side effects.
required_findings: Initial episode with hypersomnia >20 h/day, hyperphagia, weight gain, social withdrawal, visual hallucinations
Normal brain MRI, DaTSCAN, EEG, and MSLT initially
Full remission with modafinil/risperidone, off therapy for >2 years
Moderate SARS-CoV-2 infection with pneumonia confirmed by RT-PCR
Recurrence of identical symptoms one month after COVID: hypersomnia >18 h/day, hyperphagia, weight gain, social withdrawal, irritability
New white matter changes on brain MRI after relapse
Impaired cerebral vasoreactivity on TCCD with breath-holding test

title: SARS-CoV-2 Infection Causes Relapse of Kleine-Levin Syndrome: Case Report and Review of Literature

challenge_prompt:
A 74-year-old man presented to the emergency department with gradual back pain, myalgia, and memory impairment. Two days prior, he experienced vivid dreams, behavioral and language changes. Balance impairment started five days prior. He also had abdominal pain, constipation, and sleep disturbances. One day before admission, his speech changed and he became confused. Weight loss was reported. Medical history included treated prostate cancer three years ago with no evidence of metastasis; he was vaccinated against tick-borne encephalitis.

On examination: confused, agitated, partially oriented, GCS 14, BP 154/87, HR 75, SpO2 99%, afebrile. Neurological exam: full extraocular movements, no nystagmus, symmetrical pupils; no dysphonia, dysarthria, dysphagia, or paresis. Mild left facial droop, left hemihypesthesia. Periosteal tendon reflexes symmetrical and preserved, bilateral Babinski sign, mild nuchal rigidity (3 cm), urinary retention.

CT head normal. Labs: slightly elevated inflammatory markers, severe hyponatremia (124 mmol/L). CSF: pleocytosis 79 cells/μL (85% agranulocytes), protein 1.924 g/L, glucose 2.6 mmol/L (serum glucose 5.9). Working diagnosis: electrolyte imbalance and unspecified meningoencephalitis. Empirical acyclovir and ceftriaxone started.

Day 2: MRI head normal except incidental meningioma. New ataxia of right lower extremity, dysmetria upper extremities, positive Romberg.

Day 3: Repeated LP showed slightly improved CSF parameters; sodium normalized. Serum and CSF sent for autoimmune encephalitis and paraneoplastic antibody panels. Syphilis and HIV serologies negative.

Day 6: Infection workup results:
- Blood serum: positive Borrelia burgdorferi IgM (OspC P41, sum points 9) and IgG (169 AU/mL, VIsE p41 OspC sum points 12).
- CSF: positive B. burgdorferi IgM (8.9 AU/mL) and IgG (172.6 AU/mL); IgG antibody index 3.58 (positive >1.5).
- Borrelia burgdorferi sensu lato DNA PCR negative.
- Negative for tick-borne encephalitis, Ehrlichia, Anaplasma, meningitic bacteria, CMV, HSV 1/2, VZV, EBV, enterovirus.
- CSF albumin 1772.4 mg/L, IgG 282.5 mg/L; serum albumin 32.2 mg/L, IgG 9.9 mg/L.

Despite ongoing antibiotics, no marked clinical improvement by day 14.

Based on this presentation and available workup, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Lyme neuroborreliosis (acute neuroborreliosis) with suspicion of secondary autoimmune encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Borrelia burgdorferi infection leading to central nervous system involvement and possible autoimmune response
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue intravenous ceftriaxone for 28 days and urgently test CSF and serum for anti-NMDAR antibodies and other autoimmune encephalitis markers; consider starting high-dose corticosteroids if autoimmune encephalitis confirmed or strongly suspected.
required_findings: Positive Borrelia burgdorferi IgG antibodies in CSF (172.6 AU/mL) with elevated IgG antibody index (3.58)
Positive Borrelia burgdorferi IgM in CSF (8.9 AU/mL)
Positive Borrelia burgdorferi IgG in serum (169 AU/mL) with positive immunoblot
CSF pleocytosis (79 cells/μL, 85% agranulocytes)
Elevated CSF protein (1.924 g/L)
Negative CSF PCR for Borrelia burgdorferi sensu lato
Lack of improvement after empiric antibiotics
Negative serology for syphilis, HIV, and other meningitis pathogens

title: Lyme Disease and Associated NMDAR Encephalitis: A Case Report and Literature Review

challenge_prompt: A 23-year-old obese woman (BMI 30 kg/m²) with no prior psychiatric history presents with a three-month history of new-onset intrusive thoughts and compulsions. The obsessions center on contamination fears and causing harm through carelessness, with rituals involving elaborate finger-tapping sequences repeated in multiples of four, occupying 2–3 hours daily. She has good insight but significant distress. Onset was acute over 2–3 weeks. Initial psychiatric evaluation: Y-BOCS score 22 (moderate OCD), TMAS 33. She received escitalopram 40 mg/day for 12 weeks, then fluvoxamine 300 mg/day for 8 weeks, plus aripiprazole 15 mg/day for 8 weeks, and 20 sessions of exposure and response prevention therapy, all without clinically significant improvement (Y-BOCS remained 20–22). During follow-up, she reports a severe bitemporal headache (10/10) that is worse in the morning, exacerbated by Valsalva maneuvers, associated with unilateral tinnitus and transient visual obscurations. Fundoscopic examination reveals bilateral papilledema. MRI brain shows normal parenchyma, a partially empty sella, tortuous optic nerves with increased perioptic CSF signal, and focal stenosis of the right transverse sinus on MRV; venous sinus thrombosis is excluded. Lumbar puncture shows opening pressure of 30 cm H₂O with normal CSF composition.

answer_key.diagnosis: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) with venous sinus stenosis
answer_key.etiology: Idiopathic, likely related to venous sinus stenosis and impaired CSF absorption
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate medical therapy with acetazolamide; if refractory, consider venous sinus stenting or CSF diversion surgery

Treatment-refractory OCD despite adequate trials of SSRIs, antipsychotic augmentation, and ERP
Severe bitemporal headache worsened by morning and Valsalva, associated with tinnitus and visual obscurations
Bilateral papilledema on fundoscopy
MRI: partially empty sella, tortuous optic nerves, increased perioptic CSF signal
MRV: focal stenosis of right transverse sinus
Lumbar puncture opening pressure 30 cm H₂O with normal CSF composition

title: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension presenting with obsessive thoughts: a rare neuropsychiatric manifestation “case report”

challenge_prompt: A 16-year-old girl with a history of multiple psychiatric hospitalizations over the past 2 years, diagnosed in the past with major depressive disorder, PTSD, and later bipolar I disorder with psychotic features (based on prior manic episode with expansive mood, grandiosity, increased goal-directed activity), presents after a suicide attempt by overdose. Initial ED workup (metabolic panel, CBC, urine toxicology) is unremarkable. On psychiatric admission, she has flat affect, depressed mood, psychomotor retardation, and is minimally verbal. She is started on fluoxetine and risperidone. Over the first 3 days, she becomes increasingly withdrawn. On day 4, she becomes mute, non-responsive, stops eating/drinking, and exhibits posturing (e.g., holding a cup in a fixed position without bringing it to her mouth). A lorazepam challenge (1 mg IM) yields partial response. She is started on scheduled lorazepam 1 mg PO TID. She improves slightly (begins responding minimally, eating with prompting). Fluoxetine is switched to bupropion. On day 10, a rapid response is called for seizure-like activity with preserved awareness. Workup including MRI, EEG, CBC, metabolic panel, heavy metal study is unremarkable. Bupropion is stopped. Catatonic features return (echopraxia, mutism, negativism). Lorazepam is titrated back to 1 mg PO TID, and over 8 days she stabilizes. She is discharged. Three days later, she returns worse: tearful, mute, not eating/drinking, limited purposeful movement. Per father, after discharge she acted 'odd,' then at a family dinner made 'bizarre' statements. At home she was paranoid, pacing, saying electronics were talking to her, refusing food. During this hospitalization, catatonic features relapse and remit over weeks. She approaches nursing station in distress, pushes on doors, staff observe her responding to internal stimuli. She requires aspiration precautions for saliva. ECT is not available due to institutional policy. She is placed on lorazepam 2 mg PO TID with IM PRN, and started on long-acting injectable paliperidone 234 mg. On day 4 of this course, her behavior changes: she spends prolonged periods washing hands and taking showers, requests staff change gloves before touching her, asks for food containers opened in front of her. She alternates between daytime catatonia and evening obsessive/intrusive thoughts (e.g., feeling the environment is unclean, ego-dystonic thoughts of harming others). She reports feeling pregnant (urine hCG negative) and expresses concerns that staff are trying to harm her. During remissions, she tends to basic ADLs. Her paranoia is noted but its nature (psychotic vs. obsessive) is unclear. Risperidone is adjusted, and after paliperidone reaches steady state, catatonia continues to wax and wane, prompting addition of valproate 250 mg PO BID for augmentation. Based on the presented information, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate management step?

aliases: Catatonia secondary to comorbid OCD and bipolar I disorder with psychotic features
Catatonia in the context of obsessive-compulsive and psychotic disorder
Co-occurring OCD and affective psychosis with catatonia
answer_key.diagnosis: Co-occurring obsessive-compulsive disorder and affective psychotic disorder (likely bipolar I disorder with psychotic features) complicated by catatonia
answer_key.etiology: Complex interaction of trauma (mother's suicide), mood disorder, and anxiety disorder leading to catatonia
answer_key.next_management_step: Prioritize treatment of catatonia with lorazepam (up to 20 mg daily if tolerated); then address comorbid OCD with SSRIs (e.g., sertraline) and mood stabilization (e.g., valproate, paliperidone); consider ECT if available and catatonia is refractory
optional_findings: Previous diagnosis of bipolar I disorder with psychotic features
required_findings: Catatonic features including mutism, posturing, negativism, echopraxia

Insight into irrationality of contamination fears (reported as ego-dystonic)
History of prior manic episode (expansive mood, grandiosity, increased goal-directed activity)
Paranoid ideation (electronics talking, staff trying to harm) but nature unclear
Negative workup (MRI, EEG, labs, toxicology) for acute causes

title: Case report: Co-occurring obsessive-compulsive disorder and affective psychotic disorder complicated by catatonia in an adolescent female patient

challenge_prompt: A 13-year-old previously healthy girl presented with subacute neuropsychiatric symptoms. She had no past medical conditions, no medications, no substance use, normal development, and no family history of autoimmune or psychiatric disorders. Symptoms began 1 month before presentation with withdrawal, reduced speech, and crying episodes without triggers. Over the following 2 weeks, she developed increasing restlessness, inappropriate laughter, excessive talkativeness, and markedly decreased need for sleep. Three days before admission, she experienced a sudden worsening with incoherent messaging to strangers, fixed delusional beliefs, and auditory hallucinations. Upon mental status examination, the patient was conscious and alert, oriented to time and person but not to place, with a thin body build and average hygiene. Eye contact was maintained. She was calm and cooperative, with normal attention and activity levels. No abnormal movements were observed. Speech was coherent and goal-directed with normal tone, rate, and volume. Affect was labile, with episodes of inappropriate laughing and crying. Thought processes were disorganized with frequent shifts. Thought content was notable for delusional beliefs, including the conviction that she was a trainee in medicine, along with other false beliefs. Judgment and insight were poor. The remainder of physical and neurological examinations was unremarkable. Investigations: Thyroid hormones (TSH, free T4) were within normal limits. Thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies were markedly elevated. Thyroglobulin antibodies were markedly elevated. A neuronal autoantibody panel (including anti-NMDA receptor, anti-AMPAR, anti-GABABR, anti-LGI1, anti-CASPR2, anti-Hu, anti-Yo, anti-Ri, anti-Ma2, anti-CV2, anti-amphiphysin) was negative. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) was mildly elevated. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed normal cell count, protein, and glucose, with no oligoclonal bands. Brain MRI (with and without contrast) was normal except for an incidental left temporal fossa arachnoid cyst (2.4 × 1.1 × 3 cm). Awake and sleep electroencephalogram (EEG) was normal, without epileptiform discharges. Thyroid ultrasound revealed mild gland enlargement, heterogeneous echogenicity, and increased vascularity. Based only on the information above, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

answer_key.aliases: Steroid-responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis (SREAT)
answer_key.diagnosis: Hashimoto's encephalopathy (autoimmune encephalopathy associated with thyroid antibodies)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (e.g., 1 g/day for 3-5 days) or equivalent corticosteroid therapy; consider IVIG as second-line or maintenance therapy.
optional_findings: Subacute onset of psychiatric symptoms (depressive then manic/psychotic)
No family history of autoimmune or psychiatric disorders

Markedly elevated thyroglobulin antibodies
Normal thyroid function tests
Negative neuronal autoantibody panel
Normal CSF analysis
Normal brain MRI (except incidental arachnoid cyst)
Normal EEG

title: Hashimoto’s encephalopathy presenting with manic and psychotic features in a 13-year-old girl: a case report

challenge_prompt:
A 59-year-old man presents with fever (peak 39.5°C), headache, and slow, unclear speech. He denies recent infections, travel, or other symptoms. Past medical history: gout for 20 years, no medications. Initial workup: neurological exam normal; cranial CT and MRI normal; EEG shows minor irregularities (5-20 μV, 14-20 Hz β waves); CBC, CRP, vitamin B12, folic acid, ANA, ANCA, rheumatoid factors all unremarkable; CSF shows WBC 104×10^6/L (ref 0-8), protein 1.68 g/L (ref 0.15-0.45); CSF culture, smear, and bacterial, fungal, viral, and tubercle bacillus antibodies negative. He is diagnosed with viral encephalitis and treated with antivirals; symptoms resolve within a week. Five months later, he returns with fever (38.5°C) and occasional sentence confusion. Repeat CSF: WBC 39×10^6/L, protein 1.32 g/L. Cranial MRI again normal. Thyroid function: TSH 43.39 uIU/ml (ref 0.27-4.2), FT3 2.89 pmol/ml (ref 3.1-6.8), FT4 7.18 pmol/ml (ref 12.0-22.0), TT3 1.17 pmol/ml (ref 1.3-3.1), TT4 44.26 pmol/ml (ref 66-181). Serum anti-thyroglobulin antibodies (TgAb) 1274 IU/ml (ref <115), anti-thyroperoxidase antibodies (TPOAb) 600 IU/ml (ref <35). CSF: TPOAb 17.06 IU/ml, TgAb 16.02 IU/ml. Ultrasound shows diffuse thyroid lesions. Antibodies for anti-NMDAR, AMPA1, AMPA2, LGI1, CASPR2, GABA, GAD, anti-Hu, Yo, Ri, MAI, MA2, CV2, Amphiphysin, SOX-1, Tr, Zic4, and GAD65 are all negative in serum and CSF.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate step in management?

aliases: SREAT (steroid-responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis)
answer_key.diagnosis: Hashimoto's encephalopathy
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune thyroiditis-associated encephalopathy
answer_key.next_management_step: Start high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone) and thyroid hormone replacement if hypothyroid.

No response to antiviral therapy (only temporary improvement)
Age 59, male (though HE more common in females)

Normal brain MRI

title: Hashimoto’s Encephalopathy Mimicking Viral Encephalitis: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 42-year-old Chinese woman presented with a 6-month history of progressive apathy, hypoactivity, slowed responsiveness, and memory impairment affecting both recent and remote recall. Over subsequent months, she developed urinary and fecal incontinence and mood symptoms (mild anxiety and depression). She received donepezil, nimodipine, butylphthalide, aspirin, rosuvastatin, and sertraline without improvement. Family history: her mother, maternal aunt, and younger sister had similar onset in midlife (emotional blunting, hypoactivity, cognitive decline, loss of ambulation, dysphagia) and died within 5 years; her younger brother (age 37) recently showed mild memory decline and disorganized speech. Neurological exam: normal cranial nerves, normal tone/strength (5/5), negative Babinski, mildly broad-based gait. Cognitive testing: MMSE 20/30, MoCA 13/30; impairments in recall, orientation, attention, calculation, visuospatial ability. Brain MRI (T2WI/FLAIR) showed multiple punctate and patchy hyperintensities in bilateral periventricular regions, corona radiata, centrum semiovale, and corpus callosum; ventricular enlargement and widened sulci; DTI showed sparse but continuous trajectories in frontal lobes, periventricular superior longitudinal fasciculus, corticospinal tract, and corpus callosum fibers. CSF analysis: normal pressure, cell count, protein; elevated Aβ1-42 (1,048 pg/mL); negative for oligoclonal bands and antibodies against AQP1, AQP4, MOG, MBP, GFAP, Flotillin1/2 IgG. Blood tests: total cholesterol 6.23 mmol/L, LDL 3.71 mmol/L, fasting glucose 3.71 mmol/L, calcium 2.09 mmol/L, CA125 52.00 U/L, CA724 52.1 U/mL, prolactin 2108.2 mIU/L, T3 1.27 nmol/L, CD4/CD8 ratio 2.81, NK cells 3.73%; other routine labs, ANA, ANCA, antiphospholipid, syphilis, HIV normal/negative.

Genetic testing revealed a previously reported CSF1R p.Ala781Val variant.

aliases: adult-onset leukoencephalopathy with axonal spheroids and pigmented glia (ALSP)

answer_key.etiology: CSF1R gene mutation (autosomal dominant)
answer_key.next_management_step: CSF1R genetic testing (whole exome sequencing or targeted mutation analysis) and genetic counseling
optional_findings: Sparse DTI trajectories in frontal lobes and corpus callosum

title: CSF1R-related leukoencephalopathy presenting with early apathy, hypoactivity, and cognitive flattening: a case report of a diagnostic challenge

challenge_prompt:
A 34-year-old male presents with a two-month history of worsening low mood, decreased interest, poor sleep, and forgetfulness. He has a 16-year history of depressive symptoms and a right subscalp shunt device placed in 2003 for hydrocephalus. He has untreated hypothyroidism for three years. In 2008, he was diagnosed with depressive disorder and treated with venlafaxine, zopiclone, and piracetam, with initial improvement. Since 2023, his work performance declined, and in March 2024, he experienced increasing fatigue, resigned after a conflict, and reported persistent drowsiness, difficulty keeping eyes open, forgetfulness, poor concentration, headaches, dizziness, and blurred vision. Craniocerebral MRI and blood tests in March 2024 were normal. He was readmitted to a psychiatric hospital for recurrent depressive disorder and treated with venlafaxine 150 mg/day, zopiclone 7.5 mg/night, and piracetam 2.4 g/day, but his condition did not improve. He became agitated, disoriented, had difficulty navigating with eyes closed, and was discharged after five days. At presentation to our hospital, he reports insomnia, excessive daytime sleepiness, inability to keep eyes open, blurred vision, frequent urination, and significant memory impairment (unable to recall phone password or differentiate time of day). He denies toxic exposure or family history of mental illness. On admission, cranial CT shows: drainage tube extending from right frontal region to lateral ventricle level; supratentorial ventricular system notably widened with blunt margins and altered tension; symmetrical low-density blurred area surrounding lateral ventricles; transparent septum in midline; bilateral lateral cisterns and sulci significantly narrowed or absent; midline structures central. Mental exam: alert, oriented but impaired time/place orientation; diminished concentration; low tone; no hallucinations; reports dizziness, headache, blurred vision; psychomotor retardation; delusional thinking (ideas of possession); anhedonia, fatigue, anxiety, restlessness; social withdrawal; cognitive decline with preserved long-term memory but impaired recent/immediate memory; confabulation; reduced computational ability; limited insight. Physical exam: indifferent, slow responses; reduced time/space orientation; intact memory/calculation. Neurological exam: clear consciousness; gait unsteady; finger-to-nose and knee-heel test normal; Romberg test positive; tendon reflexes normal; bilateral Babinski, Chaddock, Oppenheim, Gordon, and Hoffman reflexes positive; no neck stiffness; Kernig and Brudzinski negative. HAMD-17 score 18 (moderate depressive symptoms); PANSS score 49 (low mood, social withdrawal). Based only on this information, what is the most likely etiology of this patient's symptoms, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or treatment step?

Shunt series showed disconnection of the distal catheter, confirming blockage.

answer_key.diagnosis: Shunt blockage causing hydrocephalus (normal pressure hydrocephalus)
answer_key.etiology: Obstruction of ventriculoperitoneal shunt
answer_key.next_management_step: Confirm shunt blockage with CT or shunt series, then perform shunt revision surgery

title: A case report of mental disorder caused by shunt blockage after hydrocephalus surgery

challenge_prompt:
14-year-old female presents with acute behavioral changes. Fifteen days prior, she developed irritability and sad mood. Five days ago, she was sexually assaulted. Since then, she has exhibited bizarre behavior (showering with clothes on, leaving house without shoes) and mutism. On exam, afebrile, normal neurological exam, but silent or minimally responsive. Initial labs: CBC, chemistry, CRP 0.5 mg/L, toxicology screen normal. She is hospitalized and becomes agitated with incoherent speech. EEG shows focal slow wave activity in left anterior region. She develops catatonic symptoms: excitement alternating with stupor, mutism, staring, grimacing, echolalia, stereotypy, verbigeration, negativism, refusal to eat/drink, withdrawal. Bush Francis Catatonia Rating Scale (BFCRS) score 26. Diazepam IV gives partial response (8-point improvement at 1 hour), then lorazepam 10 mg/day continued. She develops vivid visual hallucinations (complex scenes involving people and animals trying to hurt her), insomnia. Brain MRI normal. On day 5, she becomes dehydrated with hypernatremia (Na 148 mmol/L). On day 10, she has seizures, treated with levetiracetam. Lumbar puncture shows moderate pleocytosis (59 cells/mm3), negative gram stain, sterile culture, negative multiplex PCR for viruses/bacteria, normal protein, glucose, and ions. Contrast MRI shows possible left frontal-insular hyperintensity, but metallic braces cause artifact. She has no prior psychiatric history except dyslexia; family history unremarkable for autoimmune or neurological disorders. What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate diagnostic or treatment step?

CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies were positive.

challenge_prompt:
A previously healthy, independent, right-handed 72-year-old high-functioning Sinhalese Sri Lankan teetotaler man presented following a low-impact car accident. He did not have evidence of any physical injury, including any head injury or impact. He had driven into a wall on the side of the road and was found by bystanders. He was conscious, talking but unable to identify where he was and what happened for about 15–30 minutes following the event, until he became fully oriented after about 30 minutes. He was unable to remember the event but was able to remember getting into the car and driving toward home. Clinical examination and extensive investigation, including metabolic screening of blood (random and fasting blood sugars, lipid profile, serum electrolytes, serum calcium, serum phosphate, serum magnesium), MRI of the brain, and routine EEG did not reveal a secondary cause for the presentation. He had been complaining of brief episodes of anterograde memory loss, which were attributed to age, anxiety, and stress during the previous 2 years before this accident. He also admitted to having a recent worsening of memory. On suspicion, he was subjected to a sleep EEG, which showed occasional bilateral sharpened theta transients, suggesting a reduced seizure threshold.

Based only on the presentation above, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or treatment step?

aliases: temporal lobe epilepsy presenting as amnesia

answer_key.etiology: Temporal lobe epilepsy (likely mesial temporal lobe epilepsy)

challenge_prompt: A 59-year-old male presents with a 1-year history of recurrent insomnia (difficulty falling asleep, frequent nocturnal awakenings, inability to return to sleep), persistent low mood, occasional staring spells with delayed reactions, intermittent dysphagia, and choking while drinking water. He was initially misdiagnosed with a gastrointestinal disorder and then with mixed anxiety-depressive disorder, but symptoms worsened despite treatment. One week prior to admission, he developed delirious speech and hallucinations. On examination, neuropsychological testing showed MoCA 13/30, MMSE 20/30, ADL 23/56, and Water Swallow Test indicated Grade 2 dysphagia. Cranial MRI showed mild cerebral atrophy, bilateral hippocampal volume reduction (left > right), widened choroidal fissures, and mild ventricular dilation. MRS showed decreased NAA/(Cho+Cr) ratio (right 0.28, left 0.29, normal ≥0.72). SWI showed paramagnetic deposits in bilateral basal ganglia. Polysomnography revealed moderate sleep apnea-hypopnea syndrome (AHI 24 events/hour), with prolonged sleep latency, total sleep time 15 minutes, absent stages 2, 3, and REM sleep, and 16 arousals. 24-hour video EEG was normal. Lumbar puncture showed CSF protein 573.7 mg/L, WBC 3×10^6/L, pressure 100 mmH2O. Anti-IgLON5 antibodies were detected in CSF (titer 1:100, threshold >1:3.2) and serum (titer 1:3200, threshold >1:10), with a serum/CSF ratio of 32:1. 18F-FDG PET-CT showed reduced FDG uptake in the left temporal lobe and increased uptake in the medulla oblongata. Serum p-Tau 181 and p-Tau 217 levels were elevated. HLA typing revealed HLA-DRB1*10:01 and HLA-DQB1*05:01 alleles. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate management step?

challenge_prompt:
A 70-year-old retired man, previously self-sufficient with no psychiatric or neurological history, presented with progressive behavioral and cognitive symptoms over 1.5 years. Onset at age 67 with hyperactivity, euphoria, disinhibition, accelerated speech, severe insomnia, and cognitive decline (inability to recognize his home, remember dates). Within months, he developed hallucinations (tactile, visual, auditory) and persecutory delusions, with confusion and temporal disorientation. Despite treatment with risperidone (2 mg, causing confusion and hypothermia) and sodium valproate (1 g), symptoms worsened and he was discharged with a dementia diagnosis to a nursing home. At age 70, he was assessed at a memory clinic. Neuropsychiatric exam showed mood fluctuations, apathy, delusions (including Capgras syndrome), insomnia, severe attention deficit, disinhibition, episodic memory impairment, severe temporal/spatial disorientation, and illogical speech. Neurological exam was normal: no pyramidal/extrapyramidal signs, no sensorimotor deficits, no dysarthria, no faciobrachial dystonic seizures, no dysautonomia, no ataxia. Laboratory testing: serum sodium 132 mmol/L (normal 136-145). MRI brain: aspecific white matter hyperintensities. EEG (multiple, including sleep-deprived): no epileptiform discharges or encephalopathic patterns. CSF analysis: leukocytes 9/uL (normal 0-8), protein 49 mg/dL (normal 20-40). Serum and CSF were sent for autoantibody testing. FDG-PET brain: frontal hypometabolism with temporal and striatal hypermetabolism. Whole-body FDG-PET/CT: no structural or metabolic abnormalities. Neuropsychological testing: MMSE 24/30, FAB 12/18, severe episodic memory deficits. He had no improvement with initial psychiatric medications and cognitive symptoms progressed. Autoantibody testing revealed positive anti-leucine-rich glioma-inactivated 1 (LGI1) antibodies in serum and CSF.

challenge_prompt: A 10-year-old boy presents with recurrent episodes of blood-spitting beginning 5 months ago. Episodes consist of scant (<5 mL) pale red, watery blood mixed with saliva, no clot formation, occurring 5-6 times daily, both daytime and occasionally at night during restlessness. No cough, dyspnea, chest pain, or fever. Serial hemoglobin levels stable at 12.8-13.2 g/dL. Caregivers report clear temporal association with psychosocial stressors: parental conflicts and school transition (new school, declining grades). Symptoms are minimal or absent when emotionally stable. During periods of heightened stress, the patient also experiences episodic convulsive-appearing motor events with asynchronous limb movements. During these events, awareness and responsiveness are preserved, they occur only during wakefulness, and there is no postictal confusion. Extensive multidisciplinary evaluation over one year, including repeated otolaryngologic exams, bronchoscopy, chest and abdominal CT, upper GI endoscopy (showing only mild gastritis without hemorrhage), echocardiography, and hematologic/coagulation studies, all normal. Cytology of expectorated material shows inflammatory cells and scant erythrocytes. Video-EEG monitoring shows no epileptiform activity; brain MRI normal. There is no evidence of intentional symptom production or secondary gain. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (FND) presenting as pseudohemoptysis with concurrent psychogenic nonepileptic seizures (PNES)
answer_key.etiology: Functional/psychogenic, stress-related, with psychosocial reinforcement

challenge_prompt: A male in his 50s with history of cervical disc degeneration presented with acute tetraparesis after maintaining a flexed neck posture for over an hour and using a percussive massage device. MRI showed ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament, spinal stenosis, and acute spinal cord injury (SCI). Posterior cervical decompression (C3-C6) was performed on day 2. He was transferred to rehabilitation 17 days post-injury. At admission, he received antibiotics for pulmonary and urinary tract infections (UTI). Prior hypertension medication was discontinued as blood pressure normalized. Neurological examination: motor and sensory levels bilaterally at C2, American Spinal Injury Association Impairment Scale (AIS)-D, left muscle strength grade 4, right ranging 0-3. Initial Spinal Cord Injury Independence Measure (SCIM) score was 10. Structured rehabilitation was initiated. At 10 weeks post-injury, he experienced syncope lasting ~10 seconds during a wheelchair-to-bed transfer. Syncope recurred frequently (1-5 times/day), often with mild right limb tremors, occurring during transfers or seated forward head tilt. Orthostatic blood pressure (BP) and heart rate (HR) measurements during syncopal episodes showed HR increase of 31 bpm without significant BP drop (e.g., HR from 80-95 bpm to 110-125 bpm, lasting 3-10 seconds, returning to baseline). Asymptomatic orthostatic tachycardia (HR rise ≥25 bpm) occurred during routine transfers. Resting HR <100 bpm. 24-hour Holter monitoring showed reduced heart rate variability (SDNN 48 ms, reference 141±39 ms). Sympathetic Skin Response (SSR) showed mildly abnormal left upper limb and absent reproducible response in right upper limb, normal lower limbs. Follow-up cervical MRI showed no SCI progression. Vascular ultrasound, magnetic resonance angiography, echocardiography, electroencephalography, complete blood count, renal/liver function, electrolytes, BNP, cardiac enzymes, thyroid function, supine renin/aldosterone were normal. Carotid sinus massage and Valsalva maneuver were negative. Syncope was not triggered by emotional stress. He remained syncope-free during early admission when emotional fluctuations were intense. No bradycardia or hypotension during sustained upright posture. No structural heart disease. No prior cardiac history.

answer_key.diagnosis: Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS)
answer_key.etiology: Cervical spinal cord injury (C2 AIS-D) causing autonomic dysregulation, likely neuropathic POTS
answer_key.next_management_step: Implement graded postural transition protocol and consider low-dose beta-blocker (e.g., metoprolol 12.5 mg twice daily) to control tachycardia and syncope.
