challenge_prompt: A 7-year-old male child, previously healthy, presented with an 11-day history of fever (max 101°F) without chills/rigor. On day 2, he developed tonic-clonic seizures involving the right limbs, without loss of consciousness or incontinence. Postictally, he had right-sided weakness impairing walking and fine motor tasks. He was diagnosed with ischemic stroke at a local hospital and started on aspirin and levetiracetam, with partial improvement. After discharge, symptoms worsened: increased sleepiness, high-grade fever (103°F) unresponsive to medications, and inability to walk or move the right limb. He was readmitted and referred to our center. On examination: alert, GCS 15/15, temperature 98.6°F, pulse 102/min, RR 26/min, BP 100/60 mmHg. Multiple small petechiae on abdomen, mild pallor. Neurological exam: decreased tone and power (3/5) on right side, right plantar upgoing, sensation intact, no meningeal signs. Baseline labs unremarkable. MRI brain showed multiple acute infarcts in bilateral frontal and left parietal lobes. MR angiography revealed attenuated caliber with tapering of the supraclinoid and communicating segments of the bilateral carotid arteries (left > right), attenuated A1 segment of right anterior cerebral artery and M1 segment of right middle cerebral artery, and multiple collaterals in the region of the distal internal carotid artery and right middle cerebral artery. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

aliases: spontaneous occlusion of the circle of Willis
answer_key.diagnosis: Moyamoya disease (MMD)
answer_key.etiology: Unknown; likely genetic and/or inflammatory triggers leading to progressive stenosis of terminal internal carotid arteries and compensatory collateral formation.
answer_key.next_management_step: Conservative management with antiplatelet therapy (aspirin), anticonvulsants (levetiracetam), and supportive care; neurosurgical consultation for consideration of revascularization surgery if symptoms recur or progress.

challenge_prompt:
A 13-year-old male presents with irregular sleep disturbances, hypersomnolence, and progressively aggressive behavior. He has a history of autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), combined type, for which he takes lisdexamfetamine 30 mg and clonidine 0.1 mg. Developmental history includes severe malnutrition at two months, delayed walking (14 months) with frequent tripping and balance problems, and atypical sensory development (did not perceive pain until age 4). From early childhood, he had persistent deficits in social communication, reduced social reciprocity, and repetitive behaviors (rocking, head-banging). He was bullied and highly suggestible. Family history includes ADHD and cocaine addiction in his biological mother; potential prenatal cocaine exposure is reported. Neurological examination reveals obesity (BMI 27-28), bilateral positive Marinescu's sign, poor eye contact, reduced verbal reciprocity, and mild difficulty with tandem gait. He reports visual hallucinations ("seeing green lights"). An MRI of the brain performed for episodic mood disorder shows congenital absence of the splenium of the corpus callosum with no residual splenial tissue, a CSF collection approximately 2 cm wide behind the third ventricle (unchanged, likely a cavum velum interpositum cyst), and nonspecific diffuse T2/FLAIR signal abnormalities in the frontal deep white matter. A polysomnogram confirms obstructive sleep apnea (OSA). EEG shows no epileptiform activity. Based on the presentation and workup, what is the most likely underlying neuroanatomical abnormality?

MRI also confirmed that the genu and body of the corpus callosum were present and normal, consistent with isolated splenial agenesis.

aliases: Partial agenesis of the corpus callosum
answer_key.diagnosis: Isolated agenesis of the splenium of the corpus callosum
answer_key.etiology: Congenital; possible association with prenatal cocaine exposure
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate CPAP therapy for OSA, continue lisdexamfetamine and clonidine, and consider neuropsychological evaluation

CSF collection behind third ventricle (cavum velum interpositum cyst)
History of prenatal cocaine exposure (caregiver report)

title: Isolated Agenesis of the Splenium of the Corpus Callosum Associated With Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Rare and Clinically Relevant Association

challenge_prompt: A 39-year-old man with no prior medical comorbidities presented with subacute, progressive cognitive decline, behavioral changes, hallucinations, and headache evolving over three to four months. He became increasingly withdrawn with marked reduction in spontaneous speech and social interaction. He was noted to remain in fixed, awkward postures for prolonged durations and did not change position despite encouragement. He also reported complex auditory hallucinations with a religious theme. Initial evaluation at another center included CSF autoimmune antibody testing using a cell-based immunofluorescence assay on undiluted CSF (including NMDA, AMPA, GluR1, GluR2, GABA-B, LGI1, and CASPR2 antibodies), which was negative. He received a five-day course of intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g/day), with partial clinical improvement followed by symptom recurrence. On examination, he was alert and oriented; higher mental functions were normal. Neurological examination revealed grade I spasticity with brisk deep tendon reflexes (3+), while cerebellar function was preserved. A trial of risperidone showed no improvement. Baseline labs were normal: ESR 2 mm/hr, CRP 0.2 mg/dL, negative HIV/HBV/HCV. Vasculitis workup showed weakly positive ANA and mildly elevated c-ANCA (17.7). Tumor markers (AFP, CA 15-3, CA 19-9, CEA) were normal. Paraneoplastic antibody testing revealed borderline positivity for SOX-1 antibodies. MRI brain (with MR angiography and venography) was normal. EEG showed normal awake background with no epileptiform activity. CT angiography and perfusion were normal. Transthoracic echocardiography was unremarkable. CSF analysis: protein 43.25 mg/dL, glucose 79.4 mg/dL, ADA 6 U/L, no cells, negative cultures, no oligoclonal bands. Extended autoimmune antibody panel (including NMDAR, CASPR2, IgLON5, GABA-A, GABA-B, LGI1, AMPA, and VGCC) on CSF and serum showed positive VGCC antibodies at 270 pg/mL (reference <149 pg/mL). Whole-body PET scan showed no abnormal hypermetabolic lesions. Based on the presentation and workup, what is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Non-paraneoplastic voltage-gated calcium channel (VGCC) antibody-associated autoimmune encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune (anti-VGCC antibodies)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate immunotherapy with intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 2 g/kg over 5 days and consider rituximab for relapsing course

title: Neuropsychiatric Presentation of Non-paraneoplastic Voltage-Gated Calcium Channel Autoimmune Encephalitis: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 68-year-old Han Chinese male with no significant past medical history underwent right hemihepatectomy, cholecystectomy, and Roux-en-Y anastomosis for a liver neoplasm with malignant obstructive jaundice. Around postoperative day 10, he developed progressive abdominal distension, hypoproteinemia, hyponatremia, anemia, and intra-abdominal infections. Digestive tract radiography revealed a biliary anastomotic leak. He was started on parenteral nutrition, electrolyte fluids, and antibiotics. On postoperative day 25, his condition deteriorated with poor mental health, somnolence, and fever. By postoperative day 35, he developed progressive visual deterioration accompanied by nystagmus. Best-corrected visual acuity declined to 20/500 and further to light perception only within 2 days. Cognitive impairment became evident with difficulty in verbal communication. Neurological examination revealed delayed responses, dysarthria, spatial disorientation, uncooperative behavior, and bilateral Babinski signs. Funduscopy showed peripapillary intraretinal hemorrhages on the left optic disc. Cranial computed tomography was normal. Laboratory studies around the time of neurological deterioration showed hypomagnesemia. A re-operation was needed for the biliary leak. In the operating room, the patient was drowsy and ataxic. Sedline Brain Function monitoring showed a patient state index (PSI) of approximately 50 at baseline. After induction with sufentanil 25 µg, etomidate 20 mg, and cisatracurium 12 mg, EEG revealed burst suppression with a burst suppression ratio of 12% and PSI of 17. To maintain anesthesia, only 0.4 minimum alveolar concentration sevoflurane and 0.05-0.1 µg/kg/min remifentanil were needed. Intermittent asymmetric burst suppression between hemispheres was noted. Postoperative infection and metabolic disturbances were addressed but neurological symptoms persisted. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

answer_key.diagnosis: Wernicke's encephalopathy
answer_key.etiology: Thiamine deficiency due to prolonged parenteral nutrition and fasting after gastrointestinal surgery
answer_key.next_management_step: Empiric high-dose intravenous thiamine (e.g., 500 mg three times daily) and correction of hypomagnesemia

Rapid improvement after thiamine therapy (to be used as confirmatory, not in prompt)

title: Perioperative Wernicke’s encephalopathy associated with thiamine deficiency: Intraoperative EEG findings and anesthetic implications: a case report

challenge_prompt: A 55-year-old woman with history of hypertension (uncontrolled, blood pressure often > 180/110 mmHg) and chronic kidney disease (stage 3, eGFR 35 mL/min/1.73 m²) presents with acute onset of generalized tonic-clonic seizure, followed by confusion. She also reports a throbbing headache and blurred vision over the past 2 days. On examination, blood pressure is 185/115 mmHg, heart rate 90 bpm. Neurologic exam: somnolent, oriented to person only, no focal deficits. Fundoscopy shows mild hypertensive retinopathy. Labs: creatinine 2.7 mg/dL, BUN 31 mg/dL, hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL, platelets 200 × 10^3/μL. Urinalysis: proteinuria, no casts. EEG shows diffuse background slowing without epileptiform discharges. MRI brain T2/FLAIR sequences reveal bilateral subcortical hyperintensities in the parieto-occipital white matter, without corresponding diffusion restriction on DWI/ADC maps, and no post-contrast enhancement. MR venography is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)
answer_key.etiology: Hypertensive crisis in the setting of chronic kidney disease and hypertension
answer_key.next_management_step: Control blood pressure (target reduction of 20-25% in mean arterial pressure within first few hours, e.g., IV labetalol or nicardipine), remove triggering factors, and provide supportive care with seizure management (e.g., levetiracetam).

challenge_prompt:
A 32-year-old woman presented with an 18-month history of progressive neurological decline. Onset in August 2005 with attention deficits and progressive memory loss. By June 2006, she exhibited anisocoria and bizarre behavior, talking incoherently to herself, and was referred to psychiatry. Mini-mental state examination showed abnormally low attention/calculation and reduced ability to repeat three unrelated objects. Later in 2006, she was in constant motion, had unfocused hand gestures, and difficulty with ambulation. She was alert but confused, sad, and had thought process difficulties. By February 2007, she was bed-bound, unable to follow commands, with unintelligible speech. She expired in June 2007, 22 months after onset. History: travel to Puerto Rico (1995-1996), two back surgeries (2000 with cadaver bone, donor pre-screened; 2001 with autologous bone), no family history of neurodegenerative disease. EEG (July 2006): generalized slowing with bilateral periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges. MRI (April 2006): negative; (February 2007): supratentorial parenchymal atrophy, no other acute findings. CSF (March 2007): normal, including 14-3-3 protein. After preliminary autopsy results, the family was re-interviewed and reported that the patient had disturbed sleep at disease onset, progressively worsening. Some nights she did not sleep; other nights sleep was intermittent; she would roam the house. By August 2006, maximum sleep was 4 hours in one stretch; she would go 2-3 days without sleep. Sleep medications were not beneficial. Based on this information, what is the most likely diagnosis?

PRNP gene sequencing revealed no D178N mutation.

answer_key.diagnosis: Sporadic fatal insomnia (sFI)
answer_key.etiology: Prion disease (prion protein misfolding)
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for PRNP mutation and codon 129 genotyping; consider polysomnography and nuclear imaging (thalamic hypometabolism)

Unsuccessful second EEG due to excessive movements
Constant motion and unfocused hand gestures

EEG with generalized slowing and bilateral periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges
MRI showing supratentorial parenchymal atrophy

title: Sporadic fatal insomnia in a young woman: A diagnostic challenge: Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 28-year-old male presented with a 2-day history of restlessness, bizarre behavior, confusion, and hallucinations. His past medical history was significant for schizophrenia, seizure disorder, and stroke with residual left-sided facial droop. There was no family history of psychiatric disorders. His family reported no recent fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, runny nose, sore throat, cough, headaches, urinary symptoms, loss of appetite, or loss of weight. On physical examination, he was afebrile and hemodynamically stable. He was alert, oriented to person and place, but not to time. He was admitted to the psychiatry department for acute psychosis, where he was unsuccessfully managed with valproic acid, risperidone, and benztropine, with haloperidol and diphenhydramine as needed. A review of his chart revealed two past admissions for a similar presentation of acute psychosis. His hospital course was complicated by worsening mental status, and he continued to be unresponsive to medications. A thorough evaluation for organic causes was performed. MRI of the brain with contrast showed multiple areas of bright signal intensity throughout the brain parenchyma, most notably in the medial temporal lobes and bilateral insular cortices, compatible with encephalitis. Lumbar puncture yielded CSF with white blood cell count 8/mm3, neutrophils 1/mm3, lymphocytes 95%, protein 35 mg/dL, glucose 56 mg/dL. CSF testing for herpes simplex virus 1 and 2 DNA, varicella zoster PCR, VDRL, FTA ABS, enterovirus, and viral cultures were negative. Blood and CSF were positive for oligoclonal bands. A mild elevation of antinuclear antibody (1:40 titer) was noted. Initially, the patient was started on vancomycin, ceftriaxone, and acyclovir for possible infectious causes of encephalitis. Based on this presentation and workup, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or treatment step?

Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies were detected in the CSF and serum.

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune
answer_key.next_management_step: Obtain CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing and consider treatment with IVIG and methylprednisolone after excluding infection.
optional_findings: Prior history of schizophrenia, seizure disorder, stroke

Failure to respond to antipsychotic medications

title: Finding the Cause of Psychosis: A Challenging Case of Anti-NMDAR Encephalitis

challenge_prompt: A 7-year-old boy, second child of consanguineous parents (first-cousin marriage), presents with developmental delay in gross motor and verbal milestones. He walked independently and made simple sentences at age 2 after occupational and speech therapy. There is no history of seizures or perinatal complications. His older sister (14 years) has normal development. On examination, he has a wide-based ataxic gait and speech disfluency. No facial dysmorphism is noted. Behavioral abnormalities include hyperactivity, talkativeness, and inattention, suggestive of ADHD. Comprehensive laboratory tests (CBC, thyroid and liver function, serum immunoglobulins, amino acids, alpha-fetoprotein, creatine kinase) and electromyography/nerve conduction studies are normal. Brain MRI shows inferior vermis hypoplasia and elongation of the superior cerebellar peduncles. Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children (WISC-IV) yields a full-scale IQ of 76 (borderline intellectual functioning). The mother, who terminated education at elementary school but performs daily activities independently, has a Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale (WAIS) IQ of 72 (borderline). Based on this presentation and workup, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

challenge_prompt:
A 67-year-old man with a history of generalized lichen planus diagnosed one year ago presents with 20 days of progressive symptoms. The earliest symptom was mild bilateral ptosis. Gradually, he developed dysphagia (initially to solids, progressing to liquids 3 days before admission) and dysarthria (nasal speech, unintelligible words, worsening with prolonged talking). Symptoms are milder in the morning and exacerbate in the afternoon. No diplopia, blurred vision, vertigo, dizziness, ataxia, gastrointestinal symptoms, or respiratory difficulty. Medical history includes hypertension and diabetes mellitus type 2. No recent travel, infection, or unusual food intake. Family history is unremarkable.

Physical exam: alert, oriented, no respiratory distress. Vital signs: T 37.1°C, BP 124/85 mmHg, HR 87 bpm, RR 14/min, O2 sat 98% on room air. Skin: flat-topped, polygonal, violaceous papules all over; hyperpigmented macules and cutaneous eruptions on flexural surfaces of lower extremities. No mucous membrane, genital, nail, hair, or scalp involvement. Neurologic exam: asymmetric bilateral fatigable ptosis, no ophthalmoplegia. No facial asymmetry or sensory deficit. Gag reflex severely decreased. Uvula midline, soft palate elevates symmetrically. No tongue deviation or fasciculations. Motor: fatigability and weakness in proximal muscles of upper and lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes, superficial abdominal reflex, and plantar responses normal. Sensory and cerebellar exams normal.

Initial labs (Table 2 in source): normal. Comprehensive autoimmune workup: rheumatologic tests and thyroid function tests normal. MG-specific tests: anti-acetylcholine receptor antibody (anti-AChR Ab) positive at 5.43 nmol/L; anti-MuSK antibody negative. Chest X-ray normal. Chest CT without contrast (due to patient instability) shows no thymoma or other abnormalities. MRI of brain and orbit normal. Routine nerve conduction studies and electromyography normal. Repetitive nerve stimulation at 3 Hz shows decremental response in anconeus, trapezius, and orbicularis oculi muscles with reduced excitatory postsynaptic potentials.

answer_key.diagnosis: Myasthenia gravis, late-onset seropositive class IVb
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune, non-thymomatous
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate pyridostigmine 60 mg three times daily, intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 25 g daily for 5 days, and oral prednisolone 50 mg daily. Consider monitoring for thymoma and further immunosuppression as needed.

challenge_prompt: A 9-year-old boy presents with new-onset focal motor seizures and encephalopathy. He had a prodromal respiratory infection one week ago. He has no prior history of seizures or neurological disorders. Serum and CSF are positive for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies. He has not received any immunotherapy. According to the ILAE Autoimmune and Inflammation Working Taskforce classification, acute symptomatic seizures secondary to autoimmune encephalitis (ASSAE) occur during active encephalitis, while autoimmune-associated epilepsy (AAE) refers to recurrent unprovoked seizures after adequate immunotherapy without active inflammation. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely classification, and what is the next appropriate treatment step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute symptomatic seizures secondary to autoimmune encephalitis (ASSAE) due to anti-NMDAR encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune (anti-NMDA receptor antibody-mediated)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate first-line immunotherapy with IVIG and intravenous methylprednisolone, and add perampanel as an adjunctive antiseizure medication.

title: Perampanel use in pediatric autoimmune encephalitis-related seizures and epilepsy: a retrospective case series

challenge_prompt: A 27-year-old female patient with a 3-year history of clonic convulsions and subsequent recurrent focal seizures (nausea, convulsions, retrograde amnesia) presents with worsening seizures over the last 2 months despite levetiracetam 1500 mg and lamotrigine 250 mg. Medical history: ulcerative colitis, ovarian endometriotic cysts, Meniere's disease, insomnia, depression. Medications: flunitrazepam 2 mg, trazodone 150 mg, vonoprazan 10 mg, eszopiclone 3 mg, ramelteon 8 mg, risperidone 2 mg, lemborexant 10 mg, alprazolam 0.8 mg, quetiapine 200 mg, prednisolone 12.5 mg, methylcobalamin 1500 μg. Family history: mother with panic disorder and type 1 diabetes mellitus with high anti-GAD antibody titers; father with lung cancer. Blood testing reveals hypoglycemia (49 mg/dL). Extensive evaluation for hypoglycemia cause is negative (CSF normal, oligoclonal bands absent, anti-neuronal antibodies panel negative including anti-GAD, NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2, AMPAR, GABABR, DPPX, glycine receptor; brain MRI normal). EEG shows intermittent irregular delta waves and epileptic spikes localized to the left temporal region. To assess the contribution of hypoglycemia to seizure exacerbation, simultaneous long-term video-EEG and flash glucose monitoring is performed during nighttime (23:00-05:00) for 6 hours. The EEG shows focal slow activities, epileptic spikes, and Brief Potentially Ictal Rhythmic Discharges (BIRDs) in the left temporal area; no clinical or electrographic seizures are observed. Analysis of 40 definite BIRDs (11 evolving, 29 non-evolving) reveals that the number of evolving BIRDs per 30-minute epoch is significantly higher when glucose levels are below 60 mg/dL (2.00 ± 0.71 vs. 0.38 ± 0.70, p<0.05) and below 50 mg/dL (2.33 ± 0.47 vs. 0.44 ± 0.68, p<0.05). The duration of total definite BIRDs per epoch is significantly longer when glucose <50 mg/dL (3.15 ± 1.82 vs. 2.10 ± 1.00 s, p<0.05). No significant difference is found with a cutoff of 70 mg/dL. Based on the presentation and monitoring results, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next step in management is most appropriate?

aliases: Focal epilepsy with hypoglycemia-induced seizure worsening
answer_key.diagnosis: Focal epilepsy exacerbated by idiopathic hypoglycemia
answer_key.etiology: Idiopathic hypoglycemia (non-diabetic) causing increased epileptic activity via BIRDs when glucose falls below 60 mg/dL
answer_key.next_management_step: Maintain glucose levels above 60 mg/dL (e.g., dietary measures, monitoring) alongside continued antiseizure medications

Current medications include multiple psychotropics and prednisolone
No response to standard antiseizure medication optimization

challenge_prompt:
A 75-year-old Saudi male farmer with type 2 diabetes mellitus presents with a 3-month progressive neurological decline. Initial symptoms: impaired hand coordination, visual and auditory hallucinations. Then febrile illness with vomiting, followed by cachexia, postural instability, frequent falls, bradykinesia, bilateral tremors, hypophonia, incontinence, cognitive deterioration. No recent travel or unpasteurized dairy, but >20 years close contact with cattle, goats, sheep. Exam: inattention, non-fluent aphasia, impaired recall (MoCA 9/26), bilateral resting and kinetic tremors, lower limb rigidity, bradykinesia, reduced strength (MRC 3/5 right, 2/5 left), hyperreflexia. Labs: HbA1c elevated. Brucella serology strongly positive (>1:1280). Thyroid peroxidase antibodies elevated. CSF: protein 2.97 g/L, WBC 12/mm3 (75% lymphocytes). Repeat CSF (2 weeks later): turbid, protein 3.37 g/L, WBC 14/mm3 (70% neutrophils), glucose 4.6 mmol/L. CSF Brucella PCR positive. Brain MRI: initial: right temporal cortical lesions, acute frontoparietal lacunar infarcts, microangiopathy, atrophy. Repeat MRI (2 weeks): diffuse pachymeningeal and leptomeningeal enhancement. Third MRI (2 months): persistent pachymeningeal enhancement, new left temporal convexity lesion, no acute ischemia.

Based only on the presentation above, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

challenge_prompt: A 17-year-old primigravida woman (BMI 31.2 kg/m²) was admitted to Neurology on postpartum day 1 with an 8-day history of lethargy, weakness in both lower limbs, numbness, slurred speech, and cognitive difficulties. During pregnancy, mild anemia was noted at 31 weeks (Hb 106 g/L) and progressed to Hb 99 g/L pre-delivery with macrocytosis (MCV 96.7 fL), elevated RDW (SD 57.4 fL, CV 17.0%), and leukopenia (WBC 2.32×10⁹/L). She received oral folate 0.8 mg/day for presumed megaloblastic anemia. On postpartum day 1, she had delayed responses, communication problems, limb weakness, and headache. Vital signs: BP 132/82, HR 93, RR 20, SpO2 99%. Five days later, fever (38.5°C) and severe anemia (Hb 66 g/L) required transfusion. Labs: severe hyperhomocysteinemia (>150 μmol/L), elevated D-dimer (4.42 µg/mL), liver dysfunction (TBil 51.7 µmol/L, DBil 26.3 µmol/L, ALP 270.9 U/L), hypoalbuminemia (TP 59.4 g/L, Alb 32.1 g/L), CRP 52.5 mg/L, negative blood cultures, normal renal function, uric acid 406.5 µmol/L, urinalysis with leukocyte esterase 3+, occult blood 3+, protein 1+ (Candida glabrata on culture). CSF: mild hypoglycorrhachia, increased LDH. EEG: diffuse slowing with sharp waves. MRI: mild swelling of right parietotemporal cortex. Nerve conduction: polyneuropathy with facial nerve involvement. On postpartum day 6, left-limb myoclonus, unresponsiveness, tachycardia, hypoglycemia, and hyperammonemia (39.7 μmol/L) developed. Valproate sodium was started for suspected seizures, leading to rapid decline in consciousness and metabolic worsening. Metabolic screening of her newborn showed severe carnitine deficiency. Valproate was stopped, and L-carnitine (100 mg/kg/day IM) started, with improvement in consciousness. Confirmatory metabolic tests: severely decreased free carnitine, elevated methylmalonic acid (81.2 μmol/L), elevated 3-hydroxybutyric acid. Whole-exome sequencing revealed compound heterozygous pathogenic variants in the MMACHC gene (c.217C>T and c.482G>A). Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Methylmalonic acidemia with homocystinuria (cblC type)
answer_key.etiology: Autosomal recessive MMACHC gene mutations causing impaired cobalamin metabolism
answer_key.next_management_step: Start hydroxocobalamin (1 mg/week), L-carnitine, and folate; discontinue valproate; use levetiracetam for seizure control

challenge_prompt:
A two-day-old male infant, born late preterm at 36+4 weeks, presents with coffee-ground vomiting, lethargy, and feeding difficulties. Neurological assessment shows reduced light reflex (3.0 mm bilaterally) and decreased activity. Birth weight 2775 g, delivered via cesarean section to healthy non-consanguineous parents. No family history of hematological, neurological, vascular, metabolic, or immune diseases. Initial brain CT reveals acute ischemic stroke with hemorrhagic transformation in the left middle cerebral artery (MCA) territory, acute subdural hematoma in the left frontal-temporal-parietal region, and intraventricular hemorrhage with hydrocephalus. Echocardiogram shows patent foramen ovale (0.3–0.4 cm) with left-to-right shunting and trivial tricuspid regurgitation; no structural defects or vegetations. Hematological evaluation shows prolonged prothrombin time (PT) of 53.4 seconds, normal activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT), normal factor VIII and factor IX levels. Antibiotics, vitamin K, fresh frozen plasma, and cryoprecipitate are administered. Neurosurgery discusses decompression craniectomy, but family defers due to bleeding risk. By day 4, the infant develops tonic-clonic seizures followed by coma. MRI shows additional left posterior cerebral hemorrhagic infarction in the parietal-occipital lobes. A broad coagulopathy workup is initiated.

Assays revealed a FVII level of <1%.

answer_key.diagnosis: Congenital factor VII deficiency
answer_key.etiology: Genetic deficiency of coagulation factor VII due to F7 gene mutation (homozygous F7:c.681+1G>T)
answer_key.next_management_step: Administer recombinant activated factor VII (eptacog alfa) and, after correction of coagulopathy, consider ventriculoperitoneal shunt for hydrocephalus
optional_findings: Additional posterior cerebral hemorrhagic infarction on MRI

title: Perinatal ischemic stroke in an infant with factor VII deficiency: A CARE-compliant case report

challenge_prompt: A 30-year-old Chinese female was transferred to our hospital from a local emergency department where she presented with slurred speech and sudden numbness and weakness of the right upper and lower extremities while resting. Coughing while drinking water and dysphagia were also present. Cerebral MRI revealed acute infarction in the left cerebellar hemisphere and left temporo-occipital lobe. Thrombolysis was not performed due to a low platelet count. The patient had a previous stroke in 2011 (right temporo-occipital infarction) with left-sided weakness and facial paralysis, and paroxysmal seizures 3-4 times per year. She was diagnosed with immune thrombocytopenia in 2012 and treated with prednisone. At ages 19 and 29, she had spontaneous abortions at 16 weeks gestation due to fetal asphyxia. On exam: temperature 36.5°C, pulse 89/min, BP 119/72 mmHg, clear consciousness but depressed mood. Ecchymosis on left lower limb. Left nasolabial groove shallow, muscle strength left upper limb III+, left lower limb IV, right limbs IV, left pathological sign positive. Labs: hemoglobin 60.0 g/L, platelet count 32 × 10^9/L, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (fragmented RBCs 10% on bone marrow), renal dysfunction, urinalysis occult blood 2+ and protein 3+. ADAMTS13 activity < 5%, no inhibitors detected.

answer_key.diagnosis: Hereditary thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (Upshaw-Schulman syndrome)
answer_key.etiology: Compound heterozygous ADAMTS13 mutations (p.Arg349Cys and p.Cys804Tyr) resulting in severe ADAMTS13 deficiency
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate plasma exchange or fresh frozen plasma infusion

challenge_prompt: A 26-year-old Asian woman presents with a 5-year history of progressive walking difficulties. Onset at age 21 with sudden backward tilt of lower back while bathing, followed by stroke-like gait leaning rightward and backward. Within months, developed involuntary tremors in right upper limb at rest and with movement. Over the next 3 years, symptoms worsened: head tilting back, episodes of convulsions without loss of consciousness, elevated serum CK, electromyography showing repeated involuntary muscle contractions, EEG normal. Later developed muscle stiffness in lower back and upper body, arched posture when lying flat, difficulty chewing, then difficulty opening mouth, tight jaw closure, swallowing difficulties with coughing. Symptoms asymmetrically worse on left side. No response to flupentixol, benztropine, levodopa, or botulinum toxin injections. Physical exam: facial muscle tension, slow movements, dysarthria, clear consciousness, pupils equal and round, corneal Kayser-Fleischer rings negative, eye movements normal, no nystagmus, nasolabial folds symmetrical, tongue protrusion central, elevated muscle tone in neck and all limbs (left > right), normal strength, hyperactive deep tendon reflexes (++), arched posture, left finger-to-nose test unsteady with intention tremor, no pathological reflexes, no meningeal signs, sensory normal. Unified Dystonia Rating Scale (UDRS) score 38. Initial brain MRI (2018) showed no significant abnormalities. Follow-up brain MRI (2023) revealed symmetric short T2 signals in bilateral globus pallidus with central punctate hyperintensity on T2-weighted imaging (eye-of-the-tiger sign). Blood biochemical tests normal. Based on the clinical presentation and imaging, what is the most likely diagnosis?

aliases: neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) type 1
answer_key.diagnosis: Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN), atypical non-classic type
answer_key.etiology: Compound heterozygous mutations in PANK2 gene (c.1355A>G and c.1369G>C)
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for PANK2 mutations, consider deep brain stimulation (DBS) targeting globus pallidus internus (GPi) and/or subthalamic nucleus (STN) for drug-refractory dystonia

title: Case report: Asymmetric bilateral deep brain stimulation for the treatment of pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration in a patient: a unique case of atypical PKAN with a novel heterozygous PANK2 mutation

challenge_prompt: A 28-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus (diagnosed at age 22, treated with hydroxychloroquine) presents with a 2-week history of progressive headache, difficulty concentrating, mood swings, and a single generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Vital signs: temperature 38.1°C, BP 130/80, HR 88. Neurologic exam: no focal deficits, intact cranial nerves, normal gait. Laboratory findings: hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL (normocytic), leukocytes 3.2×10^9/L, lymphocytes 1.2×10^9/L, platelets 120×10^9/L; serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, proteinuria 1.5 g/24h; ANA positive (1:1280), anti-dsDNA 150 IU/mL (elevated), C3 45 mg/dL (low), C4 8 mg/dL (low); anti-SSA positive, anti-SSB negative; CRP 15 mg/L, ESR 80 mm/h; CSF analysis: WBC 10/μL (80% lymphocytes), protein 55 mg/dL, glucose 60 mg/dL, negative for infection. Brain MRI shows multiple confluent white matter hyperintensities on FLAIR, predominantly in frontal and parietal periventricular regions, without enhancement or microbleeds. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

challenge_prompt: A 52-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of hallucinations and delusions that spontaneously resolved 2 months ago, followed by progressive memory decline, difficulty with calculations, organizing tasks, and recognizing family members. Neurological examination reveals impairments in episodic memory, calculation, attention, executive function, and visuospatial ability, with otherwise normal findings. MMSE score is 17/30, MoCA score is 12/30. CSF analysis shows decreased Aβ1-42 and Aβ1-42/Aβ1-40 ratio, elevated P-tau and T-tau; inflammatory and autoimmune panels are normal. Brain MRI shows bilateral hippocampal atrophy (MTA grade 2) and mild white matter lesions (Fazekas grade 1). 18F-FDG-PET reveals reduced glucose metabolism in bilateral temporal, parietal, and frontal lobes. 11C-PiB-PET shows diffuse Aβ deposition in bilateral frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital lobes, especially in posterior cingulate and precuneus. There is no family history of dementia or psychiatric disorders.

challenge_prompt:
A 79-year-old woman presents after an unexplained fall with probable short loss of consciousness. She has a left sixth cranial nerve palsy present since childhood and an axonal distal-symmetric peripheral neuropathy of unknown etiology. On ultrasound, no stenosis of brain-supplying vessels; EEG shows no epileptic activity. MRI of brain and cervical spine shows a dorsal foramen magnum meningioma measuring 15×16×20 mm, and two small meningiomas at the right sphenoidal wing and adjacent to the right precentral cortex. There is moderate narrowing of the cervical spinal canal between C4 and C6.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next step?

challenge_prompt:
A 54-year-old male with severe Parkinson's disease (9-year history) on maximal dopamine agonist therapy (levodopa 1150 mg/day, opicapone, subcutaneous apomorphine pump 4 mg/h day, 3.5 mg/h night) was scheduled for elective deep brain stimulation (DBS). He had multiple comorbidities: ASA grade 4, severe pulmonary artery hypertension (PAH) on triple therapy (tadalafil, macitentan, selexipag), untreated sleep apnea, and disseminated lupus erythematosus. Preoperative transthoracic echocardiography showed right ventricular dysfunction with severe chamber dilation and moderate wall hypertrophy. Right heart catheterization revealed systolic pulmonary artery pressure (PAP) of 107 mmHg and mean PAP of 58 mmHg. Left ventricular ejection fraction was 65%. He had NYHA class 2 dyspnea.

Perioperatively, all antiparkinsonian medications were stopped the night before except the apomorphine pump which was stopped the morning of surgery. During the first electrode implantation (sedation with dexmedetomidine and propofol), the patient lost consciousness and had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. He received levetiracetam and lacosamide; CT head was negative. After a stable period, the second electrode was implanted uneventfully. In the step-down unit, he developed status epilepticus, refractory to anticonvulsants. MRI brain was negative for PRESS or cerebral edema. Reintroduction of subcutaneous apomorphine dramatically improved his neurological status, suggesting dopamine agonist withdrawal syndrome (DAWS).

After stabilization, he underwent the final stage of DBS (subcutaneous tunneling and device implantation) under general anesthesia with sevoflurane and remifentanil, while on full antiparkinsonian therapy. Despite dobutamine and noradrenaline support and adrenaline boluses, his hemodynamic status worsened with worsening cardiogenic shock, leading to cardiac arrest requiring chest compressions.

Based on this information, what is the most likely cause of the cardiac arrest?

Intraoperative transesophageal echocardiography revealed acute right ventricular failure, and pulmonary artery pressure monitoring showed a rapid increase to 150 mmHg, consistent with a pulmonary hypertensive crisis.

aliases: Right ventricular failure from PAH exacerbation
answer_key.diagnosis: Acute right heart failure due to pulmonary hypertensive crisis
answer_key.etiology: Perioperative exacerbation of severe pulmonary artery hypertension due to hypoxemia, hypercarbia, and/or adrenergic stimulation (e.g., adrenaline) in the setting of underlying PAH, untreated sleep apnea, and lupus
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate vasopressin to support systemic blood pressure without increasing pulmonary pressures, optimize oxygenation, avoid further adrenergic vasopressors, consider inhaled pulmonary vasodilators, and manage fluid status
optional_findings: MRI brain negative for PRESS or cerebral edema
Dopamine agonist withdrawal syndrome (DAWS) suggested by apomorphine response
Use of dobutamine, noradrenaline, and adrenaline for hemodynamic support

title: Cardiac arrest during deep brain stimulation: A case report

challenge_prompt:
A woman in her 60s, former nurse, with no family history of mood disorders or Parkinson disease (PD), had recurrent major depressive episodes over 16 years. She was on maintenance vortioxetine 20 mg and aripiprazole (6-9 mg). Two years after the fourth episode, she developed a fifth depressive episode triggered by chronic pain. Despite treatment, her condition deteriorated with new lower limb stiffness and gait instability, rendering her unable to live independently. On admission, psychiatric exam revealed depressed mood, tearfulness, guilt, anhedonia (including loss of interest in her dogs), profound psychomotor retardation (low voice volume, prolonged response latency, generalized slowness), insomnia, substantial weight loss, impaired concentration, and suicidal ideation. No manic episodes or use of alcohol/depressogenic medications. Labs: normal thyroid function, no systemic inflammation. Brain MRI: no atrophy or vascular lesions. Diagnosed with recurrent major depressive disorder with melancholic features (DSM-5-TR), CGI-S score 6. Neurologic exam: masked face, marked bradykinesia, right-hand rest tremor, left-dominant rigidity, and postural instability, satisfying MDS criteria for parkinsonism. Hoehn and Yahr (HY) stage 5, requiring wheelchair. She was continued on vortioxetine. To exclude drug-induced parkinsonism, aripiprazole was discontinued. After a 2-week washout, severe motor dysfunction (HY 5) and depression (CGI-S 6) persisted. A diagnostic levodopa trial was initiated at Week 4 (150 mg, increased to 300 mg by Week 6). By Week 8, parkinsonian features resolved completely (HY 0), and mood and pleasure fully recovered (CGI-S 1). She was discharged on vortioxetine 20 mg and levodopa 300 mg. At Week 78, a DaTscan showed normal striatal uptake.

Based on this information, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?

aliases: Geriatric depression with severe psychomotor retardation manifesting as pseudoparkinsonism
SWEDDs (scans without evidence of dopaminergic deficit) due to depression
answer_key.diagnosis: Pseudoparkinsonism secondary to geriatric depression
answer_key.etiology: Hypodopaminergic state without nigrostriatal neurodegeneration
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue vortioxetine and levodopa; consider psychiatric management for depression; monitor for recurrence

Normal thyroid function and inflammatory markers
Depressive and motor symptoms both fully responsive to levodopa

title: Geriatric Depression Mimicking Parkinson's Disease: A Case of Levodopa‐Responsive Pseudoparkinsonism With a 3‐Year Follow‐Up

challenge_prompt: A 55-year-old Black male presented with progressive loss of balance, gait abnormality, slurred speech, urinary urgency, nocturia, incontinence, loss of morning penile erection, and rigidity. Symptoms started 4 years ago; in the past 3 months, he developed choking episodes, intermittent hiccups, difficulty speaking at a regular pace, and worsening slurred speech. He had taken levodopa/carbidopa 250/50 mg twice a day without significant improvement and developed irritability, anger, and aggressiveness. Physical examination: BMI 23.5 kg/m2, blood pressure 130/80 mmHg in both positions, heart rate 92/min regular, respiratory rate 16/min. Neurologic examination: motor power 4+/5 in left upper extremity; exaggerated deep tendon reflexes with clonus at bilateral knees and ankles, upgoing plantar reflex; cogwheel-type rigidity in left upper extremity; intention tremor in both hands; cerebellar coordination abnormalities: unable to perform finger-to-nose, heel-to-shin, and tandem walk tests; cerebellar gait disturbance with impaired velocity, stride length, and slow swing speed. Cranial nerves and other systems unremarkable. Brain MRI showed marked pontocerebellar atrophy with a 'hot cross bun' sign. Laboratory investigations including complete blood count, renal function, liver enzymes, fasting blood glucose, serum electrolytes, vitamin B12, thyroid function tests, and serologies (HBsAg, HCV, VDRL, PICT) were normal.

challenge_prompt: A 69-year-old woman with secondary progressive multiple sclerosis (diagnosed at age 29) is brought to the emergency department in an agitated state. Two months ago, her visual acuity worsened, rendering her bedridden. Since then, she has been experiencing visual hallucinations of spiders of various sizes and shapes walking toward her. She recognizes these as unreal, but they cause extreme distress, leading to agitation and aggression. She has no history of psychiatric illness, substance use, or recent infection or trauma. Her medications include ocrelizumab (last dose one week before visual worsening) and solifenacin. On examination, she is alert, oriented, and vitals are normal. Motor strength is 4/5 in upper limbs and 1/5 in lower limbs, with hyperreflexia and Babinski signs bilaterally. MRI brain shows demyelinating plaques in the left temporal subcortical matter, left cerebellar hemisphere, and bilateral pontine regions. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.etiology: Visual pathway deafferentation due to MS-related optic neuritis and demyelinating plaques

challenge_prompt:
A 14-month-old boy, born at term to non-consanguineous parents, presented with global developmental delay and seizures. Developmental milestones were mildly delayed: head control at 4 months, independent sitting at 8 months, rolling over at 11 months, pulling to stand at 14 months. At 11 months, during a febrile illness (38–39°C), he had five to six generalized tonic-clonic seizures lasting 1–3 minutes, each occurring within 1 hour of temperature exceeding 38°C. Brain MRI was unremarkable. Interictal EEG showed mildly slowed occipital background (6–7 Hz theta with intermixed low-amplitude fast activity) during wakefulness and sparse sleep spindles with occasional left parietal–posterior temporal sharp-slow wave complexes during sleep. Routine labs, metabolic screening, cardiac ultrasound, and ECG were normal. Gesell Developmental Scale at 11 months revealed global developmental quotient of 73, with gross motor 60, fine motor 53, cognitive 70, language 73, social 70. He was diagnosed with complex febrile seizures and improved with symptomatic treatment.

One month later, he was re-admitted for six similar febrile seizures in one day (fever up to 39°C), again with seizures within 1 hour of temperature >38°C. No new etiology was identified.

Three months after the initial seizure, he began experiencing afebrile generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Given the evolution, whole-exome sequencing was performed. This revealed a de novo heterozygous nonsense variant in the NBEA gene (NM_001385012.1): c.4715C>A, leading to a premature stop codon [p.(Ser1572Ter)]. The variant was absent from population databases (1000 Genomes, gnomAD, ClinVar) and predicted to cause nonsense-mediated mRNA decay. No other pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants that could explain the phenotype were detected; additional findings included a VUS in BRPF1 and heterozygous variants in ALDOB and HBB (recessive disorders).

What is the most likely genetic etiology for this patient’s neurodevelopmental disorder and epilepsy, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: NBEA-related neurodevelopmental disorder with epilepsy
answer_key.etiology: de novo heterozygous nonsense variant in NBEA: c.4715C>A (p.Ser1572Ter)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate or optimize antiepileptic therapy, such as valproic acid, and consider genetic counseling.

challenge_prompt:
A 26-year-old woman presents for evaluation of seizures and headaches. She was born at term without perinatal complications. Family, medical, and surgical histories are unremarkable. Over the past 8 years, she has experienced both epileptic seizures and headaches. The seizures are generalized tonic-clonic. The headaches typically present with transient visual scintillations and a nearly half-hour decrease in left ear hearing followed by left frontotemporal distension pain, sensations of head heaviness, photophobia, phonophobia, nausea, and sweating, requiring rest for relief. These headache episodes last 2–72 hours and occur 4–5 times per month, meeting ICHD-3 criteria for migraine with aura. Notably, each epileptic seizure is preceded by a brief headache episode, separated by only decades of seconds, with the pre-seizure headaches mirroring her regular migraine in aura, pain characteristics, location, and accompanying symptoms. During the interictal period, she remains alert but experiences transient amnesia, with continuation of the headache symptoms for 48–72 hours. She has been treated solely with sodium valproate for epilepsy; no seizures occur during regular medication adherence, but she self-discontinued twice, each time resulting in seizure recurrence.

On examination, her height is 159 cm, weight 59 kg. There is no evidence of developmental delay. Neurological examination reveals decreased muscle strength (Grade 4/5) in the right limbs, with no other positive neurological signs. When she reports severe headaches, EEG shows no continuous epileptiform discharges but paroxysmal low- to medium-amplitude spike waves are observed in the left brain. No ictal events were captured during 3-day continuous EEG monitoring. Non-contrast cranial MRI confirmed an open lip schizencephaly in the left frontal lobe. Cranial Doppler ultrasound showed no significant abnormalities. Right heart echocardiography was normal, ruling out patent foramen ovale. Mini-Mental State Examination score exhibited no significant neurocognitive dysfunction.

Given these findings, what is the most likely pathophysiological relationship between her headaches and seizures, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Migraine-triggered epileptic seizures secondary to left frontal open-lip schizencephaly
answer_key.etiology: Open-lip schizencephaly (congenital malformation of cortical development)
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue sodium valproate; initiate levetiracetam for seizure control; add ibuprofen as needed for acute migraine attacks.

challenge_prompt:
A 47-year-old male with a 13-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus (complicated by peripheral neuropathy and bilateral stage 2 diabetic retinopathy), grade 3 hypertension, and obesity (BMI 32.1 kg/m²) presents with one week of fever, headache, and dizziness. One week prior to admission, he developed fever (maximum 39.3 °C), persistent right temporal headache aggravated by fever, cough with white sputum, fatigue, and decreased appetite. Self-administered NSAIDs provided poor antipyretic effect. On admission, temperature 38.5 °C; brain MRI showed lacunar infarction. On the 2nd day after admission, temperature rose to 38.4 °C and he developed urinary retention (reported difficulty urinating, thin stream). Pathogen detection: positive Mycoplasma pneumoniae IgM, positive parainfluenza virus (types 1–3) IgM; EBV VCA IgG 67.8 AU/mL (normal 0–2), EBV NA IgG 89.34 AU/mL (0–2), CMV IgG 4.55 AU/mL (normal <4.2) with negative IgM for all three. Treatment with moxifloxacin and oseltamivir had poor efficacy.

On the 4th day after admission, temperature 37.9 °C, he reported aggravated dizziness and headache, sudden inability to speak, and numbness in the right arm (resolved after 20 minutes). That night, slurred speech and slow response recurred; emergency brain MRI showed no abnormalities.

On the 5th day after admission, chills, fever (max 38.4 °C), restlessness, and inability to lie still were noted. Despite chlorpromazine, involuntary movements occurred. Emergency lumbar puncture: opening pressure >330 mm H2O (normal 70–180), yellowish CSF; protein 1.81 g/L (0–0.4), glucose 3.51 mmol/L (2.5–4.4); total cell count 218 × 10⁶/L (0–5) with 75% monocytes; IgG 167 mg/L (0–34), IgA 36.4 mg/L (0–5), IgM 2.4 mg/L (0–1.3); no tumor cells. Mannitol and acyclovir were started.

On the 6th day, consciousness improved but remained drowsy; neurological examination showed nuchal rigidity and positive bilateral Kernig sign. Over the next days, dizziness persisted.

On the 10th day after admission, temperature 37.8 °C, poor appetite. Inflammatory markers: CRP 11.34 mg/L (<10), monocyte percentage 12.8% (3–10%), SAA 13.41 mg/L (<10). Urethral secretions tested positive for N. gonorrhoeae DNA; ceftriaxone was administered. The results of CSF cell-based indirect immunofluorescence assay (collected on day 5) returned: positive GFAP-IgG antibody (titer 1:32; normal negative, titer <1:10), and negative for N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor, aquaporin 4, and leucine-rich glioma inactivated 1 antibodies. Repeat brain MRI showed no typical parenchymal or perivascular enhancement; only mild thickening and roughness of the bilateral tentorium cerebelli and dura mater. Lumbar MRI with contrast demonstrated linear enhancement on the surface of the cauda equina nerves, without abnormal signal or edema in the spinal cord parenchyma.

On the 13th day after admission, the patient had mild nuchal rigidity and positive Kernig sign. Repeat CSF: pressure >330 mm H2O, protein 1.82 g/L, cell count 150 × 10⁶/L (90% monocytes), glucose 5.3 mmol/L, chloride 114.1 mmol/L (120–130), and negative Mycobacterium tuberculosis antibody.

Question: Based on the above information, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) antibody-associated astrocytopathy (GFAP-A)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune: production of IgG antibodies targeting GFAP in astrocytes, likely triggered by preceding infections (Mycoplasma pneumoniae, parainfluenza virus) and facilitated by diabetes-induced blood-spinal cord barrier disruption.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate immunosuppressive therapy with high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3–5 days or dexamethasone 10 mg IV) while continuing anti-infective coverage. Consider empirical anti-tuberculous therapy given low CSF chloride, but prioritize immunosuppression for GFAP-A.

Meningeal signs (nuchal rigidity, bilateral Kernig sign)
Elevated serum SAA (13.41 mg/L) and CRP (11.34 mg/L)
Low CSF chloride (114.1 mmol/L) raising suspicion for tuberculous meningitis
History of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension
Urinary retention (possibly neurogenic or due to prostatic enlargement)
Lacunar infarction on admission brain MRI
required_findings: Positive CSF GFAP-IgG antibody with titer 1:32 (normal <1:10)
Negative testing for NMDAR, AQP4, LGI1 antibodies in CSF
CSF monocyte-predominant pleocytosis (75–90% monocytes)
Elevated CSF protein (1.81–1.82 g/L)
Linear contrast enhancement on the surface of cauda equina nerves on lumbar MRI, without spinal cord parenchymal abnormality
Brain MRI lacking typical parenchymal or perivascular enhancement, showing only mild dural/tentorial thickening
Absence of neoplastic cells and negative M. tuberculosis antibody in CSF
Presence of preceding and concurrent infections (M. pneumoniae, parainfluenza, N. gonorrhoeae) and diabetes as potential triggers

title: Glial fibrillary acidic protein antibody-associated astrocytopathy presents isolated spinal cord enhancement: A case report

challenge_prompt:
A 40-year-old Colombian male merchant with no prior medical conditions was admitted to the emergency room. He reported a history of self-administered subcutaneous Kambo frog venom exposure every six months as part of spiritual rituals over the past eight years. The most recent exposure occurred on the right shoulder four days prior to symptom onset. After self-application, he developed symmetrical progressive weakness in the upper limbs, which progressed over two days to involve all four limbs and neck flexor muscles. He denied any preceding gastrointestinal or respiratory illness or recent vaccinations. He reported an episode of drowsiness accompanied by nausea and vomiting. No bulbar symptoms were present.

Neurological examination revealed bilateral facial diparesis; Medical Research Council (MRC) muscle strength was 4/5 in neck flexors, 2/5 proximally and 1/5 distally in the upper limbs, 3/5 in hip flexors and knee extensors, and 1/5 in ankle dorsiflexors and plantar flexors. Deep tendon reflexes were absent in the upper limbs, with generalized hypopallesthesia and hypotonia.

Initial laboratory studies including blood count test, renal and hepatic function, thyroid function, creatine phosphokinase (CPK), and electrolytes were within normal limits except for hyponatremia (130 mmol/L; reference range: 135–145 mmol/L). Rheumatological profile was negative. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed elevated protein (88.9 mg/dL; reference range: 15–45 mg/dL) with normal cell count and negative microbiological studies. Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) testing was negative. Sensory nerve conduction studies were normal. Motor conduction studies demonstrated acute demyelinating features affecting multiple nerves, with evidence of conduction block. Electromyography was normal.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute polyneuropathy associated with Kambo frog venom exposure
answer_key.etiology: Immune-mediated reaction triggered by Phyllomedusa bicolor venom peptides
answer_key.next_management_step: Immunomodulatory therapy with intravenous immunoglobulin or plasmapheresis, and supportive care including monitoring for respiratory failure

Normal blood count, renal, hepatic, thyroid function, CPK
required_findings: History of repeated Kambo frog venom self-administration
Recent exposure 4 days before symptom onset
Acute progressive symmetrical motor weakness involving limbs and neck flexors
Absent deep tendon reflexes
CSF albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein 88.9 mg/dL, normal cells)
Motor NCS showing acute demyelinating features with conduction block
Hyponatremia (130 mmol/L)
Bilateral facial diparesis

title: Acute polyneuropathy associated with Kambo poisoning: An unusual case report

challenge_prompt:
A 37-year-old unmarried woman without children, with a 15-year history of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), presents with a 2-month history of numbness and hypoesthesia in the right knee and lower leg. She has no motor weakness, muscle atrophy, or reflex abnormalities. She was diagnosed with SLE in 2008 after facial erythema, arthralgia, low complement, positive ANA (1:320, granular and nucleolar pattern), anti-dsDNA antibody 319.8 IU/mL, anti-Sm positivity, and 3+ proteinuria. Renal biopsy at that time showed lupus nephritis types IV and V. She received two courses of cyclophosphamide (CYC): from August 2008 to August 2009 she received 1 g monthly (cumulative 6.2 g); after a flare in 2014, she resumed CYC every 2 weeks at 0.4–0.6 g per dose from March 2014 to January 2016 (cumulative 7 g). Total CYC exposure is 13.2 g. Current medications are prednisone 10 mg daily and mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 0.75 g in the morning and 0.5 g in the evening. Laboratory studies now show ESR 54 mm/h, hs-CRP 14.25 mg/L, C3 0.69 g/L (low), C4 0.09 g/L (low), positive c-ANCA, but PR3-ANCA ELISA negative. 24-hour urine protein is 179.8 mg (normal). Nerve conduction studies/EMG: 1. Segmental lesions in bilateral ulnar nerves at the elbow with motor fiber involvement and demyelinating changes; 2. Right superficial peroneal nerve involvement with sensory fiber axonal damage, and myogenic damage in the right anterior tibial and vastus medialis muscles.

What is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient's new peripheral neuropathy?

answer_key.diagnosis: SLE-associated peripheral neuropathy
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune-mediated peripheral nerve damage in SLE, likely immune complex deposition and B-cell dysregulation
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate telitacicept (BLyS/APRIL dual inhibitor) 160 mg subcutaneously weekly, given high cumulative cyclophosphamide exposure and risk of premature ovarian failure; continue mycophenolate mofetil and consider prednisone taper.

challenge_prompt: A 64-year-old man with a history of stage IV small-cell lung cancer (diagnosed March 2023; T4N3M1a) receiving tislelizumab plus etoposide and cisplatin (first cycle 16 March 2023, second cycle 12 April 2023) presented with a 3-week history of progressive cognitive decline. Three weeks after his second cycle, he developed memory loss, impaired calculation, and executive dysfunction (unable to recall recent actions, repeated tasks). He had no hypertension, diabetes, surgery, or trauma. Physical exam was otherwise unremarkable. Cognitive scales: MMSE 10/30 (orientation 5, memory 1, language 4); MoCA 8/30 (visuospatial 1, naming 1, attention 1, language 2, orientation 3). Self-rating scales normal (SDS 34, SAS 48). Routine bloods, urine, stool, liver/kidney/thyroid/glucose/lipids, and glycation normal. T-SPOT, EBV, respiratory viruses, SARS-CoV-2, and preoperative infection screens negative. Procalcitonin and CRP normal. Tumor markers negative. Anti-GBM, ENA profile, dsDNA, anticardiolipin, and immune panel negative. Residual urine, supine BP, ECG normal. Brain MRI plain+contrast: no disease-related changes; only small ischemic lesions in bilateral corona radiata and basal ganglia, deemed chronic infarcts. Chest CT: right hilar mass with metastases. EEG: moderately abnormal, widely diffusely distributed slow waves, no epileptiform discharges. CSF (left lateral position): pressure 130 mmH2O; WBC 5 ×10^6/L; protein 0.56 g/L; glucose normal, chloride normal. Bacterial and Cryptococcus cultures negative. Aβ1-40, Aβ1-42, P-Tau181, P-Tau normal. Exfoliative cytology negative for malignant cells. Serum and CSF paraneoplastic antibody panels negative. CSF viral metagenomic testing negative. Using a cytometric bead array (CBA) to detect autoantibodies of autoimmune encephalitis, CSF showed anti-glutamate receptor (AMPA2) antibody IgG(+) at 1:100. Serum anti-AMPA2 antibody IgG(+) at 1:100; NMDAR, GABABR, CASPR2 and 13 other antibodies negative. Oligoclonal bands positive on agarose gel electrophoresis with immunofixation.

challenge_prompt:
An eight-month-old male child, the first child of non-consanguineous parents, had an uneventful perinatal and neonatal history. He developed stereotyped episodes characterized by ocular deviation and sustained gaze, initially interpreted as focal epileptic seizures. On examination, mild facial dysmorphism was noted: hypertelorism, broad forehead, and thin lips. Neurodevelopmental assessment demonstrated severe global developmental delay with absent gross motor milestones, impaired visual fixation, and minimal social engagement. Cranial nerve examination identified convergent strabismus. Neurological exam revealed generalized muscle hypotonia, predominantly axial, significantly increased deep tendon reflexes with expanded reflexogenic zones, and positive Babinski and Rossolimo signs bilaterally. The clinical course was complicated by recurrent episodes of vomiting and poor appetite lasting 2–3 days, without infection, during which intravenous glucose was given. Between episodes, he was exclusively fed orally. A period of no weight gain and even weight loss was observed: at 8 months he weighed 8.7 kg, which decreased to 7.6 kg at 9 months and remained unchanged at 12 months. Electroencephalography showed bilateral high-amplitude slow wave activity in the occipital regions. Brain MRI demonstrated benign enlargement of the subarachnoid spaces (BESS), without other structural abnormalities. Initially, levetiracetam was started under a working diagnosis of epilepsy. However, video-EEG monitoring captured the episodes and showed no associated paroxysmal EEG activity, leading to reclassification as dystonic rather than epileptic. Levetiracetam was subsequently tapered.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

Genetic testing revealed a homozygous pathogenic variant in SLC39A8.

aliases: congenital disorder of glycosylation type II due to SLC39A8 mutation
answer_key.diagnosis: SLC39A8-congenital disorder of glycosylation (SLC39A8-CDG)
answer_key.etiology: Homozygous pathogenic variant in SLC39A8 (c.112G>C, p.Gly38Arg) leading to impaired manganese transport and defective glycosylation.
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform genetic testing (e.g., dystonia panel or whole exome sequencing) and simultaneously measure serum manganese and transferrin isoelectric focusing to confirm the diagnosis.
optional_findings: Bilateral high-amplitude slow wave activity on EEG
Recurrent vomiting and poor appetite requiring IV glucose
Initial misdiagnosis as focal epilepsy and treatment with levetiracetam
Weight at 12 months unchanged at 7.6 kg

Mild facial dysmorphism (hypertelorism, broad forehead, thin lips)

title: Early Diagnosis and Targeted Therapy in SLC39A8-Congenital Disorder of Glycosylation: A Case Report From Bulgaria

challenge_prompt:
A 51-year-old male presented with a three-week history of progressively worsening headache, intermittent confusion, and subtle left-sided clumsiness. There was no history of recent head trauma, anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, alcohol abuse, or known coagulopathy. On presentation, the patient was fully alert with a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 15. Neurological examination revealed mild left upper extremity weakness graded 4+/5 without aphasia, visual field disturbance, or cranial nerve deficit. Vital signs were stable.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain demonstrated a right-sided chronic subdural hematoma with mixed signal intensity and internal septations on coronal T2 FLAIR sequences, causing marked cortical compression and radiological features of subfalcine herniation. No diffusion restriction or parenchymal injury was seen.

Management options were discussed in a multidisciplinary conference. Given the marked mass effect and herniation, surgical evacuation was indicated. However, the patient remained fully alert with a mild, nonprogressive deficit. After detailed shared decision-making, a decision was made to pursue a less invasive strategy targeting the pathological vascular supply of the hematoma.

Angiography of the right external carotid artery demonstrated hypertrophy and pathological neovascular blush arising from the right middle meningeal artery consistent with vascularized subdural membranes. Superselective catheterization of the frontal and parietal branches was achieved, confirming the absence of dangerous anastomoses. Under continuous fluoroscopic guidance, a 20% mixture of N-butyl cyanoacrylate and lipiodol was injected into the distal dural branches supplying the hematoma membranes, achieving complete elimination of the pathological blush. No intraprocedural complications occurred.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what specific therapeutic procedure was performed?

answer_key.diagnosis: Right-sided chronic subdural hematoma with subfalcine herniation
answer_key.etiology: Spontaneous (no trauma, coagulopathy, or anticoagulant use), likely due to chronic vascularized outer membrane fed by the middle meningeal artery
answer_key.next_management_step: Middle meningeal artery embolization using N-butyl cyanoacrylate and lipiodol
optional_findings: Surgical evacuation was indicated but patient opted for less invasive approach
required_findings: Three-week history of progressive headache, intermittent confusion, left-sided clumsiness
No trauma, anticoagulant/antiplatelet use, alcohol abuse, or coagulopathy
Alert, GCS 15
Mild left upper extremity weakness 4+/5
MRI: right-sided chronic subdural hematoma with mixed signal, internal septations, subfalcine herniation, no diffusion restriction
Angiography: right middle meningeal artery hypertrophy with pathological blush
Injection of NBCA-lipiodol into MMA branches, achieving elimination of blush

title: Middle Meningeal Artery Embolization as Standalone Therapy for Chronic Subdural Hematoma with Radiological Herniation Features: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 51-year-old female with a history of hypertension presented with normal blood pressure at admission. Outpatient CT angiography revealed a left cerebral artery aneurysm at location A4. Neurologic examination on admission was normal. On the third day of admission, cerebral angiography confirmed a 5.5x4.0 mm aneurysm. Preoperatively, she received aspirin 100 mg/day and clopidogrel 75 mg/day for 3 days. During the procedure, a flow diversion technique was used with secure angiographic wall adherence. Intraoperative blood pressure was maintained at 120/80 mmHg. Four hours postoperatively, she experienced an acute generalized tonic-clonic seizure with altered consciousness and right gaze deviation. Diazepam 10 mg temporarily alleviated the seizure, and continuous sodium valproate was started. Thirty minutes later, the same symptoms recurred. Emergency head CT showed no evidence of hemorrhage, infarction, or new lesions. She continued to have involuntary shaking of the right limb, with complex partial seizures propagating to the whole body, consistent with status epilepticus. She was transferred to the ICU. Transcranial Doppler evaluations revealed reduced blood flow velocities in the bilateral middle cerebral arteries, internal carotid arteries, and vertebral arteries. Treatment with nimodipine and mannitol was started. On ICU day 5, brain MRI demonstrated predominantly vasogenic edema in the parietal lobes and bilateral corpus callosum, with symmetrically distributed hyperintense lesions on FLAIR imaging and patchy, limited diffuse lesions on diffusion-weighted sequences. Concurrent head CT showed new hypodensity in the parietal lobe near the midline and corpus callosum bilaterally. Brain CT perfusion demonstrated reduced perfusion in the regions affected by vasogenic edema. EEG showed persistent epileptiform wave patterns. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome (PRES) complicating interventional embolization of an unruptured aneurysm
answer_key.etiology: Cerebral vasospasm and contrast-induced endothelial injury following aneurysm coiling
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate edaravone (free radical scavenger) and methylprednisolone (corticosteroid) while continuing antiepileptics, nimodipine, and supportive care; add mannitol/furosemide for cerebral edema as needed

Seizure lateralized to right side with gaze deviation
CT perfusion showing reduced perfusion in edematous areas
Treatment with diazepam, sodium valproate, nimodipine, mannitol prior to diagnosis

Generalized seizure with altered consciousness 4 hours post-aneurysm coiling
Initial head CT negative for hemorrhage, infarction, or new lesions
Transcranial Doppler showing reduced blood flow velocities in bilateral anterior and posterior circulation
MRI with FLAIR hyperintensities in parietal lobes and corpus callosum consistent with vasogenic edema

CT showing new hypodensity in parietal lobe near midline and corpus callosum bilaterally

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

An 18-year-old previously healthy woman was admitted on 30 May 2025 with acute onset of neuropsychiatric symptoms. Five days prior, she developed malaise, diaphoresis, subjective fever, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. She then became confused, agitated, and behaviorally inappropriate, with severe insomnia, emotional lability, and paranoid ideation. Psychiatric evaluation noted hyperthymia, grandiose ideation, and paranoid thoughts. She was treated with haloperidol and antihistamines. She had no known comorbidities, denied illicit drug use, and had consumed a small amount of wine and used an electronic cigarette. On admission, she was febrile (37.5 °C), tachycardic (128 bpm), hypertensive (138/104 mmHg), and tachypneic (29/min). Neurological exam: disoriented, psychomotor agitation, absent speech, delayed command execution, bilateral palmomental reflexes and Hoffmann/Trömner signs, left Babinski sign, diffuse hyperreflexia with clonus in lower extremities and spastic hypertonia, no meningeal signs. She later had two generalized tonic-clonic seizures, controlled with valproate. Lumbar puncture on admission showed cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) pleocytosis with mildly elevated protein and normal glucose. CSF PCR was negative for a broad panel of pathogens including HSV-1, HHV-6, enteroviruses, parvovirus B19, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, and bacterial/fungal pathogens. Urine toxicology was negative. Brain MRI was normal. Autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel was sent on 2 June, with results pending. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis on 5 June revealed a 27 × 19 mm cystic lesion in the left ovary containing fat and calcifications, most consistent with a mature cystic teratoma.

challenge_prompt:
A 59-year-old male with a history of bilateral sequential lung transplantation 3 years prior for severe COPD, on maintenance immunosuppression with tacrolimus 3 mg twice daily, azathioprine 100 mg daily, and prednisolone 12.5 mg daily, was admitted after a high-speed motor vehicle accident with multiple injuries including traumatic brain injury. He underwent seven surgeries over 3 weeks in the ICU. His course was complicated by steroid-induced hyperglycemia, acute kidney injury, and deep tissue infection treated with piperacillin/tazobactam, azithromycin, and vancomycin. Tacrolimus levels were monitored; doses varied and levels were predominantly subtherapeutic (<5–15 µg/L). On day 25, he developed progressive hypertension hours before sudden bilateral cortical visual loss. On examination, NIH Stroke Scale score was 0, blood pressure 187/82 mmHg. Code stroke CT brain with perfusion revealed subcortical hypoattenuation in bilateral occipital lobes, with reduced mean transit time and increased cerebral blood volume. Follow-up MRI brain showed coronal T2 FLAIR hyperintensity and mild volume expansion within deep and subcortical white matter of both posterior occipital lobes. Shortly after, he developed encephalopathy, tonic-clonic seizures, worsening hypertension (208/125 mmHg), and tachycardia (134 bpm). Postictal labs: creatinine 185 µmol/L, eGFR 34 mL/min/1.73 m², lactic acid 14.8 mmol/L, potassium 2.9 mmol/L, magnesium 0.79 mmol/L. He had no history of hypertension, seizure disorder, or substance misuse.

What is the most likely diagnosis? What is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)
answer_key.etiology: Tacrolimus neurotoxicity with subtherapeutic levels, likely multifactorial including hypertension, sepsis, acute kidney injury, and corticosteroid use
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue tacrolimus, switch to an alternative immunosuppressant (e.g., cyclosporine), and aggressively control blood pressure

NIH Stroke Scale score 0 despite bilateral cortical visual loss
Deep tissue infection treated with antibiotics

challenge_prompt:
Clinical presentation:

A 35-year-old man, 167 cm tall and weighing 50 kg, with a history of congenital sensorineural hearing loss and two generalized tonic-clonic seizures (first at age 20, treated with carbamazepine; second at age 24) presents with progressive gait disturbance beginning at age 33 (now using a walking aid), cognitive decline noticeable since age 33 affecting memory, language, attention, and executive function, and spasticity since age 34 (now wheelchair-bound). His psychomotor development was normal; he completed primary and secondary school. Family history: a sister has sensorineural hearing loss successfully treated with a cochlear implant. His maternal and paternal grandfathers were first cousins. No other family members have seizures, hearing loss, cognitive impairment, muscle weakness, or spasticity.

Examination (at age 35): The patient is mute but alert, bradyphrenic, with hypoacusis. He performs simple tasks only irregularly. There is spontaneous nystagmus, irregular horizontal gaze impairment, and repeated clonus in the left corner of the mouth and on the cheek. Bilateral ptosis with right-sided emphasis. Muscle weakness in upper and lower extremities with right-sided emphasis, spastic tetraparesis with right-sided emphasis, and diffuse bilateral muscle atrophy. Non-fixed flexion contractures in the right arm. Finger-snapping reflex positive bilaterally. Patellar tendon reflex is subclonic on the right, normal on the left. No response to painful stimuli in any limb. He cannot stand or walk without support. Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score is 0.

Investigations:
- Brain MRI (at age 33, unchanged at 35): Extensive, inhomogeneous, confluent signal abnormalities of the deep cerebral white matter and infratentorially; mild pontine and cerebellar atrophy. Radiological appearance suggestive of leukodystrophy.
- Nerve conduction studies: Slight proximal axonal lesion of the left peroneal nerve; otherwise unremarkable.
- Needle electromyography: Myogenic, without abnormal spontaneous activity; no full interference pattern could be recorded.
- EEG (awake): Background alpha activity 9-10 Hz extending to frontal lobe. Repeated, generalized patterns of polymorphic, slow, and sharp waves (6/sec) lasting up to 1.5 seconds.
- Blood tests: Normal creatine kinase, lactate, pyruvate, and gonadotropins. Hypertriglyceridemia and iron deficiency. At age 35: mild vitamin D deficiency, normal HbA1c (4.4%), CK normal.
- ECG and echocardiography normal. No renal or endocrine involvement.

Question: Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis? What is the next most appropriate diagnostic step?

Whole exome sequencing revealed a homozygous pathogenic variant c.457A>C (p.Asn153His) in the LARS2 gene.

answer_key.diagnosis: Perrault Plus Syndrome due to homozygous LARS2 c.457A>C variant (p.Asn153His)
answer_key.etiology: Autosomal recessive, biallelic pathogenic variant in LARS2 (c.457A>C, p.Asn153His) encoding mitochondrial leucyl-tRNA synthetase.
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform genetic testing for LARS2 (Sanger sequencing or whole exome sequencing) to confirm diagnosis; initiate levetiracetam for seizure control based on EEG findings.
