challenge_prompt:
Case Presentation: A 9-year-10-month-old boy presented on December 20, 2024, with a 2-year history of progressively worsening bilateral lower limb pain (thighs and hips), evolving to intermittent claudication and nocturnal lumbar pain over the last 2 months. Twenty days before admission, he developed acute urinary retention and markedly limited mobility, without preceding trauma or fever.

Physical examination: Alert but lethargic, semi-reclined position. Indwelling urinary catheter with scant pale-yellow urine. Diffuse tenderness over lower extremities, hips, and lumbosacral region; mild midline lumbar spinous processes tenderness. Neurological: asymmetric lower extremity weakness (right 3/5, left 2/5, limited by pain and claudication), diminished left patellar reflex (1+), superficial and deep sensation preserved. Cardiorespiratory unremarkable.

Laboratory findings: Baseline CBC: WBC 8.36 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 116 g/L, platelet count 337 × 10^9/L. Coagulation: hypofibrinogenemia (0.93 g/L), D-dimer 16.97 μg/mL, fibrin/fibrinogen degradation products 57.80 μg/mL.

Imaging: CT and MRI revealed multiple osteolytic lesions with associated soft-tissue masses involving T10-L5 vertebrae and the pelvis, resulting in spinal canal stenosis and cord compression. PET/CT showed mild heterogeneous uptake in involved bones and reactive lymphadenopathy in cervical and axillary regions. ECG: normal sinus rhythm.

Bone marrow examination: Aspirate showed 95% hypergranular promyelocytes. Cytochemical stains: MPO/POX strongly positive (100%), PAS diffusely positive (100%). Flow cytometry: Abnormal myeloid population (88.1%) with CD45dim, CD33+, MPO+, CD13+, CD9+, CD123+, CD117partial+, CD64dim; CD34−, HLA-DR−; lymphoid markers CD7, CD19 negative. FISH using LSI PML/RARA dual-color dual-fusion probe: negative for PML::RARA fusion (0/200 nuclei, 0.00%, lab cutoff 7%).

Molecular studies: RNA sequencing (targeted 318-gene panel, total RNA from bone marrow, RIN 8.2) detected a high-abundance TTMV::RARA fusion (81,142 copies; TTMV::RARA/ABL1 ratio 28.33%). The viral-host junction was consistent with TTMV integration into RARA intron 2 upstream of exon 3. No pathogenic variants were identified in FLT3 (ITD/TKD), NPM1, NRAS, KRAS, WT1, CEBPA, RUNX1, ASXL1, TET2. Sanger sequencing confirmed the fusion junction. Paired tumor/normal whole-exome sequencing: no reportable pathogenic/likely pathogenic alterations; three germline VUS (ZBTB16 p.I264N, FCGR3A p.R36S, HYOU1 p.K816N).

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis (including etiology) and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute promyelocytic leukemia with TTMV::RARA fusion (TTMV::RARA-positive APL), complicated by extramedullary osseous/paravertebral involvement, spinal cord compression, and coagulopathy.
answer_key.etiology: TTMV (Torque teno mini virus) integration into RARA intron 2/exon 3, resulting in a pathogenic TTMV::RARA fusion oncoprotein.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate induction therapy with all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) 25 mg/m²/day and an oral arsenic-containing compound (Compound Huangdai Tablets 0.135 g/kg/day), along with supportive care for coagulopathy and close monitoring for differentiation syndrome.

title: Case report: TTMV::RARA-positive pediatric APL with spinal cord compression as initial presentation: unique clinical features and therapeutic outcomes revealed by a 12-case systematic cohort analysis

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 19-year-old female of nonconsanguineous Chinese descent presented with early-onset seizures. Perinatal history was unremarkable, and developmental milestones were appropriate. At 7 months of age, she developed seizures that were initially focal to bilateral tonic–clonic, triggered by fever. Initial interictal EEG recorded paroxysmal, asynchronous sharp-and-slow wave complexes involving the bilateral frontopolar, occipital, and regions. Despite treatment with ASMs, she developed recurrent cluster seizures monthly. Between the ages of 4 and 13 years, seizure frequency spontaneously declined to once every 2–3 years without ASMs treatment. After age 13, recurrent cluster seizures increased significantly, with a frequency of once every 2–3 months. Notably, at age 14, she developed cognitive regression and behavioral disturbances, leading to a diagnosis of DEE. Treatment with levetiracetam and valproate proved ineffective, and by age 17, seizure clusters occurred at a frequency of 6–20 episodes per month. At age 18, a neuropsychological assessment indicated severe cognitive impairment, with delayed responsiveness and deficits in comprehension and executive function. Video EEG confirmed multifocal epileptiform discharges in temporal, frontal, and occipital regions, while brain MRI was unremarkable, showing no evidence of atrophy or delayed myelination. No relevant family history of neurological disorders was identified.

Trio-based whole-exome sequencing identified a novel missense variant in the SCN8A gene.

answer_key.diagnosis: SCN8A-associated developmental and epileptic encephalopathy (SCN8A-DEE)
answer_key.etiology: De novo heterozygous missense variant in SCN8A (c.791T>C, p.Val264Ala)
answer_key.next_management_step: Order genetic testing (epilepsy gene panel or trio whole-exome sequencing) to confirm SCN8A variant.

Severe cognitive impairment on neuropsychological assessment
Seizure clusters 6-20 times per month at age 17

title: Implication of genetic-dependent stage in the development of SCN8A-associated epilepsy: a case report

challenge_prompt:
An 8-year-old boy presented with a 3-month history of progressive headaches and declining vision. Neurological examination revealed bilateral papilledema. Brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) demonstrated a large, heterogeneously enhancing solid-cystic mass measuring 20 × 33 × 45 mm, centered in the fourth ventricle. The lesion extended through the right foramen of Luschka into the cerebellopontine angle and was responsible for severe obstructive hydrocephalus.

The patient initially underwent an emergency endoscopic third ventriculostomy (ETV) to relieve hydrocephalus, which resulted in the rapid resolution of acute symptoms. He subsequently underwent a suboccipital craniotomy via a transvermian approach for tumor resection. Approximately 95% of the tumor volume was resected. Complete resection was intentionally avoided due to the firm adherence of the residual tumor to the floor of the fourth ventricle, where further dissection carried a high risk of brainstem injury. Intraoperative monitoring revealed transient cardiorespiratory rhythm disturbances, reinforcing the decision to limit resection.

Postoperative MRI confirmed successful tumor debulking, with a small residual lesion located in the right cerebellopontine angle.

Histopathological examination of the resected tumor revealed a biphasic architecture. The glial component resembled pilocytic astrocytoma and showed strong GFAP expression. The neurocytic component was composed of small, uniform cells forming neurocytic rosettes and perivascular pseudorosettes, with strong synaptophysin expression. The Ki-67/MIB-1 labeling index was approximately 10%. Molecular testing for BRAF or FGFR1 alterations was not available.

The case is to be discussed at a multidisciplinary tumor board. What is the most likely pathological diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate management strategy for the residual tumor?

answer_key.diagnosis: Rosette-forming glioneuronal tumor (RGNT)
answer_key.etiology: Unknown; possibly associated with BRAF or FGFR1 alterations (not tested in this case)
answer_key.next_management_step: Adjuvant radiotherapy (conventional cranial radiotherapy) for residual tumor given elevated Ki-67 and critical location
optional_findings: Transient cardiorespiratory rhythm disturbances during surgery
Solid-cystic heterogeneously enhancing mass on MRI
required_findings: Biphasic tumor with glial component resembling pilocytic astrocytoma
Strong GFAP expression in glial areas
Neurocytic component with uniform neurocytic rosettes
Perivascular pseudorosettes
Strong synaptophysin expression in neurocytic rosettes
Ki-67 labeling index approximately 10%
Tumor centered in fourth ventricle and extending through right foramen of Luschka
Residual tumor in right cerebellopontine angle after subtotal resection

title: Subtotal resection and adjuvant radiotherapy for a fourth ventricle rosette-forming glioneuronal tumor in a child: a case report and review of management strategies

challenge_prompt: In 2020, the parents of a 3.5-year-old girl present to the clinic because of developmental regression and seizures. The child was born at term following an uneventful pregnancy. Early development during the first year was reportedly normal. At approximately 12 months, she babbled and imitated animal sounds. At 18 months, the parents noticed head tremor; she was evaluated and diagnosed with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. By age 3 years, she began losing previously acquired skills, including purposeful hand use, and developed stereotypic hand movements (hand-wringing). Seizures started approximately 6 months before presentation, following an infectious illness. They occur several times a day, including nocturnal episodes, and are generalized. She also has episodes of apnea, behavioral disturbances with aggression and spontaneous screaming, and prolonged insomnia. There is no history of status epilepticus. She has been treated with carbamazepine without adequate seizure control. On examination, she appears calm. Visual fixation is preserved. Spontaneous movements of the upper limbs are present. Frequent stereotypic hand-wringing movements are noted. Muscle tone is normal. Deep tendon reflexes are moderately increased, slightly more on the right side. Pathological reflexes are not detected. Coordination testing is limited due to difficulty performing directed tasks. Cardiorespiratory parameters are stable. An EEG shows focal epileptiform discharges predominantly in the fronto-central and frontotemporal regions with background slowing. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Rett syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Clinical diagnosis of Rett syndrome, most commonly caused by loss-of-function mutations in the MECP2 gene (genetic testing was not performed in this case due to unavailability).
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform MECP2 genetic testing to confirm the diagnosis if feasible.
optional_findings: Onset of seizures after infectious illness

challenge_prompt: A 58-year-old male with a 30-year history of chronic hepatitis B initially presented in December 2020 with persistent right upper abdominal pain. Laboratory tests showed alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) of 8406 ng/mL, and imaging confirmed hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) at Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer (BCLC) stage C with metastasis to the hepatic hilum and retroperitoneal lymph nodes. The patient declined surgery and received transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) and multiple ablations (cryoablation/microwave ablation), but the disease progressed. In June 2021, he developed lower limb weakness and urinary retention. Imaging revealed multiple bone metastases in the thoracolumbar spine and a pathological fracture of the T11 vertebral body with spinal cord compression. After emergency spinal surgery, severe neurological deficits persisted: American Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) grade C paraplegia and lower extremity muscle strength of grade 0–2.

answer_key.diagnosis: Hepatocellular carcinoma with bone metastasis and spinal cord compression
answer_key.etiology: Chronic hepatitis B virus infection leading to HCC, with subsequent metastatic spread
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate systemic therapy with atezolizumab plus bevacizumab (or another immune checkpoint inhibitor + anti-angiogenic combination) for advanced HCC with progression after locoregional therapy

challenge_prompt:
A 52-year-old man was admitted to a neurorehabilitation unit in January 2018, 5 weeks after a cardiac arrest (November 2017) that resulted in hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy. He was in a vegetative/unresponsive wakefulness syndrome. On admission, Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) was 9/15, Nociception Coma Scale-Revised (NCS-r) 1/9, and Coma Recovery Scale-Revised (CRS-r) 3/23. Medical history included smoking (40 cigarettes/day), type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypertension. He had no prior neurological symptoms. Brain MRI showed bilateral damage to the cerebral hemispheres, internal capsule, and basal ganglia. Median nerve somatosensory evoked potentials revealed absent N20 and bilaterally prolonged N9. No seizures or epileptiform discharges were initially present. Examination showed decorticate rigidity with severe spasticity: Modified Ashworth Scale (MAS) for upper limbs = shoulders 4 bilaterally, elbows and wrists 3; lower limbs = hips, knees, ankles 3. He was on dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 100 mg/day and clopidogrel 75 mg/day). Intrathecal baclofen was contraindicated due to bleeding risk. Treatment included physical therapy, oral baclofen 100 mg/day and clonazepam 6 mg/day, and EMG-guided botulinum toxin-A injections (250 U each pectoral and biceps, 80 U each pronator quadratus bilaterally). After 2 months, lower limb spasticity improved (MAS hips and knees reduced to 2), but upper limbs remained severe: elbows flexed with only 10° passive extension, wrists flexed at 90°, MAS shoulders 4. Three months after admission, the patient had a seizure related to a febrile episode.

What is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Post-anoxic encephalopathy with unresponsive wakefulness syndrome and severe spasticity
answer_key.etiology: Hypoxic-ischemic brain injury due to cardiac arrest
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate levetiracetam for seizure control and potential improvement of upper limb spasticity
optional_findings: Improvement of lower limb spasticity with standard treatment

title: Levetiracetam Improves Upper Limb Spasticity in a Patient With Unresponsive Wakefulness Syndrome: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 52-year-old woman presents with intermittent headache for 5 months, worsening over 1 week, with vomiting for 2 days. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Physical exam is normal. Laboratory results: blood routine, CRP, ESR, serum tuberculosis antibody, toxoplasma antibody, and tumor markers are negative. Serologic tests: TPPA positive, TRUST positive with titer 1:64. HIV test negative. CSF not performed. Conventional brain MRI shows multiple nodules in the right temporal lobe with extensive perilesional edema. The nodules are hypointense on T1-weighted images, heterogeneously hyperintense on T2-weighted images, and mildly hyperintense on DWI. Post-contrast T1 images show significant enhancement of the nodules and adjacent meninges, including a 'dural tail'. The right ventricle is compressed with midline shift. MR spectroscopy (TE=35ms) of the lesion shows a slightly increased choline peak, slightly decreased creatine and NAA peaks, with Cho/Cr=1.24 and Cho/NAA=0.932, and a lipid/lactate peak. Perfusion MRI using arterial spin labeling shows that regional cerebral blood flow in the nodule area is lower than contralateral normal tissue, with highest relative CBF of 0.84 in the lesion. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Cerebral syphilitic gumma (gummatous neurosyphilis)
answer_key.etiology: Treponema pallidum
answer_key.next_management_step: Start intravenous penicillin G therapy (18-24 million units/day for 2 weeks) for neurosyphilis.

challenge_prompt:
A 55-year-old Caucasian male presents with a long-standing history of multisystem involvement including mental retardation, epilepsy since age 12, cataract surgery, hypoacusis, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with left ventricular hypertrabeculation/noncompaction, QT prolongation, left anterior hemiblock, anemia, sleep disorder, hepatopathy, hyperlipidemia, and myopathy. Since age 47, he developed camptocormia (bent spine syndrome). He also had deep venous thrombosis after a tibial fracture at age 48, and inguinal hernia repair at age 51. Seizures are controlled with two generalized tonic-clonic seizures per year and several focal seizures.

On examination: short stature (160 cm), hypertelorism, myopia, bilateral ptosis (left > right), bulb protrusion, upslanting palpebral fissures, positional nystagmus, cognitive impairment, hypoacusis, barrel thorax, gibbus, camptocormia, generally reduced tendon reflexes, diffuse wasting, dysdiadochokinesia, mild postural tremor, mild weakness for foot extension bilaterally (M5-), ataxia of lower limbs, genu valga, outwardly rotated feet, and flexion contractures of the knees with right-sided predominance.

Laboratory findings: Creatine kinase repeatedly elevated. Serum lactate 2.5 mmol/L (normal <2.2). Lactate stress test abnormal. Nerve conduction studies show axonal polyneuropathy. MRI of lumbar spine shows wedge vertebrae L3, vertebral stenosis L2/3, and lumbar osteochondrosis. Muscle biopsy from right gastrocnemius shows subsarcolemmal accumulation of mitochondria, ragged red fibers, tubular-shaped mitochondria, and bizarre cristae proliferation. Biochemical analysis of muscle homogenate reveals a combined complex-I and complex-IV defect.

His 78-year-old mother has a history of hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, cataract surgery bilaterally, carpal tunnel syndrome, and sensorimotor polyneuropathy. On examination, she has short stature (164 cm), alopecia, dysarthria, diffuse weakness of left upper limb, hypoesthesia, reduced tendon reflexes, dysdiadochokinesia, dysmetria, and thigh wasting. Labs show elevated HbA1c (7.3%), triglycerides (313 mg/dL), serum iron (187 mg/dL), CK (224 U/L), and mild lactic acidosis. Cerebral MRI shows gliotic spots bilaterally including basal ganglia and diffuse atrophy. Nerve conduction studies confirm sensorimotor polyneuropathy. She refused muscle biopsy.

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis? What is the most likely etiology?

answer_key.diagnosis: Mitochondrial disorder (mitochondrial myopathy with multisystem involvement)
answer_key.etiology: Maternally inherited mitochondrial DNA mutation (likely mtDNA point mutation or deletion; POLG1 mutation considered but not confirmed)
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic counseling, mitochondrial DNA sequencing, and supportive management (e.g., coenzyme Q10, L-carnitine, vitamin supplementation); avoid medications toxic to mitochondria.

challenge_prompt: A 25-year-old right-handed man with 13 years of schooling presents with daily partial seizures lasting about 1 minute. MRI reveals a T2 hyperintense, non-enhancing lesion involving the left fronto-temporo-insular cortex, consistent with a low-grade glioma. Presurgical neuropsychological assessment shows good performance in most domains except below-average scores on auditory and visual lexical decision tests. The patient is calm and not anxious. fMRI and DTI indicate the tumor is infiltrating functional tissue. The patient undergoes awake craniotomy with direct electrical stimulation (DES) mapping and real-time neuropsychological testing (RTNT). Initial DES during counting yields no response; DES during object naming elicits motor speech arrest with visible orbicularis oris contraction. Resection proceeds with continuous RTNT. The RTNT battery includes object naming, word and pseudoword reading, pseudoword repetition, short-term memory (digit span), working memory, phonological processing, lexical decision, and verbal comprehension (Token test). During the first runs, performance is high. Over subsequent runs, the patient shows slight decrements: pseudoword repetition errors (consonant substitutions like 'dilanciario' for 'bilanciario'), phonological processing errors (matching a word with wrong phonological target), short-term memory span drops from 6 to 3, and two errors on the Token test (out of 10 items). Lexical decision errors are consistent with presurgery. The patient appears increasingly tired and falls asleep toward the end of resection. Total resection is performed. Based on this intraoperative course, what is the most likely cause of the patient's declining performance?

answer_key.diagnosis: Intraoperative fatigue leading to reversible neuropsychological deficit
answer_key.etiology: Prolonged awake surgery with continuous cognitive testing
answer_key.next_management_step: Provide a short break and continue resection once performance recovers

Patient was calm and not anxious

challenge_prompt:
A 17-year-old right-handed female with drug-resistant epilepsy since 17 months of age presents for a second opinion. Seizures are characterized by right eye and facial twitching, salivation, gurgling sounds, spitting, and yelling out, occurring about four times per week. Prior evaluation at another epilepsy center suggested a frontal lobe focus, with iEEG showing bilateral ictal onset zones. MRI was reported as non-lesional. She has failed multiple antiseizure medications and the ketogenic diet. 

At our center, prolonged video-EEG captured nine focal-onset clinical seizures with maximal frontal onset (right frontal spikes noted). FDG-PET revealed a small region of hypometabolism at the right anterior superior temporal gyrus. Simultaneous MEG with conventional EEG (19-channel) and high-density EEG (256-channel) showed frequent interictal epileptiform discharges (IEDs) over right fronto-temporal regions. Source localization with dipole clustering consistently localized IEDs to the right anterior insula. High-frequency oscillations (HFOs) were localized near the right middle insula. MRI re-evaluation guided by these findings showed a mild hyperintense signal in the right frontal white matter extending between cortex and frontal horn, possibly representing gliosis or abnormal myelination.

Based on these presurgical evaluation results, what is the most likely location of the epileptogenic zone, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or therapeutic step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Right anterior insular epilepsy (epileptogenic zone in right anterior insula)
answer_key.etiology: Suspected focal cortical dysplasia of the right anterior insula
answer_key.next_management_step: Proceed with long-term intracranial EEG (iEEG) monitoring to confirm seizure onset zone, and if concordant, perform laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) ablation of the anterior right insula

challenge_prompt: A female patient presented with prolonged generalized tonic-clonic seizure 12 hours after first immunization at 2 months of age, and another prolonged GTCS after second immunization at 3 months. Shortly thereafter, habitual unprovoked seizures developed, including myoclonic jerks, unclassified unresponsive episodes (possibly absence or atypical absence seizures), and GTCS. Seizures were drug-resistant, with several episodes of status epilepticus. Early development was normal, but after a severe and prolonged GTCS at 4 years, developmental regression occurred with subsequent intellectual disability. During adolescence, food and fluid intake reduced progressively, leading to weight loss. Assessments including speech and language therapy, ear nose and throat evaluation, blood tests (antiseizure drug levels, ammonia), and barium swallow did not reveal a cause. Over 3 years, food intake fluctuated with prolonged refusal periods; seizure-free periods between GTCS clusters shortened. The patient has autism spectrum disorder and thoracolumbar scoliosis (surgically corrected at 13 years). Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

answer_key.diagnosis: Dravet syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Pathogenic variant in SCN1A gene
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for SCN1A variants
optional_findings: Shortening of seizure-free periods between GTCS clusters over time
Normal results from speech therapy, ENT, blood tests (including drug levels and ammonia), and barium swallow

challenge_prompt: A 22-year-old Asian woman, 7 days after an emergency cesarean section at 33-34 weeks due to premature rupture of membranes, presents with behavioral changes over the past 3 weeks. She had a 3-week history of intermittent headaches and mild fever. Two days after cesarean section, she lost consciousness, which was not explained by electrolyte or metabolic abnormalities. Physical examination: temperature 38.7°C, no other abnormal findings. Laboratory: hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL (reference 12.3-15.3), WBC 11 × 10^9/L (reference 4-11), platelets 308 × 10^9/L, normal calcium, glucose, BUN, creatinine. Neurological consultation 5 days after admission: GCS 14, positive meningeal sign, right facial paresthesia, tetra paresthesia with positive pathological reflexes. Non-contrast head CT: hypodense lesions at right subcortical occipital lobe, left parietooccipital lobe, and right cerebellum, with hydrocephalus. Contrast-enhanced head CT: overlying meningeal enhancement at sulci of right occipital lobe, bilateral temporal lobes, tentorium cerebelli, ambient cisternae, basalis cisternae, bilateral Sylvian fissures, anterior and posterior interhemispheric fissure, with hydrocephalus communicans. Abdominal ultrasound: hepatosplenomegaly and multiple paraaortic lymphadenopathies. Chest X-ray: active pulmonary tuberculosis and cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema. What is the most likely diagnosis and the next best diagnostic step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Intracranial tuberculoma with tuberculous meningitis
answer_key.etiology: Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis for acid-fast bacilli, culture, and molecular testing (e.g., GeneXpert)
required_findings: Head CT: hypodense lesions at right subcortical occipital, left parietooccipital, right cerebellum with hydrocephalus

challenge_prompt: A 29-year-old Caucasian female, single, higher-educated, employed as an accountant, presents with a history of major depressive disorder (MDD) since age 17. She has experienced a slow decline in mental wellbeing since 2018 without clear triggers. Her past treatments include psychotherapy (ineffective), multiple antidepressant trials (sertraline, mirtazapine up to 45 mg, venlafaxine up to 225 mg, bupropion up to 300 mg, quetiapine up to 150 mg, escitalopram up to 20 mg, vortioxetine up to 20 mg) without stable remission. She has had two inpatient hospitalizations after suicidal attempts and three day-hospital stays. One course of electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) with 8 sessions resulted in transient remission followed by relapse after 4 months. She declined maintenance ECT due to memory concerns. Subsequently, she was enrolled in a sham-controlled trial of transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), randomized to active intermittent theta-burst stimulation (iTBS). After 32 of 40 sessions, stimulation was stopped due to sudden exacerbation of depressive symptoms and intense suicidal ideation. She has comorbid avoidant personality disorder. Family history includes paternal alcohol dependence and grandfather suicide. She has a history of childhood neglect, sexual abuse, and self-harm. No significant somatic comorbidities. What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next therapeutic step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Treatment-resistant depression (TRD) in the context of major depressive disorder with avoidant personality traits
answer_key.etiology: Likely multifactorial including genetic predisposition, childhood trauma, and personality factors contributing to treatment resistance
answer_key.next_management_step: Referral for deep brain stimulation (DBS) targeting the medial forebrain bundle (MFB) combined with optimized pharmacotherapy

challenge_prompt: A 75-year-old woman presents with 2-year history of gradually progressive concentration difficulties, memory and retentiveness disorders, and a gait disorder. She has urinary and occasional fecal incontinence. Past medical history includes recurrent depressive disorder, chronic pain syndrome, arterial hypertension, obesity, and presbycusis. Neurological examination reveals extinguished right patellar reflex, bilaterally extinguished Achilles tendon reflexes, and mild right foot jack paresis. No rigor, tremor, or cogwheel phenomenon. Neuropsychological testing shows impaired semantic and phonematic fluency, reduced cognitive processing speed, faulty switching, pathological clock test, and verbal memory deficits with preserved confrontation recognition, action planning, working and figural memory, verbal discriminability, visuoconstructive skills, and visuospatial perception. MRI demonstrates Fazekas grade II microangiopathic changes and medial temporal lobe atrophy score of 2. 123I-FP-CIT SPECT shows no reduced dopaminergic uptake in the nigrostriatal system. CSF analysis reveals mild pleocytosis (6/μL, 93% lymphocytes), elevated phosphorylated tau 181 (54.8 pg/mL), total tau 418 pg/mL, normal amyloid beta 42/40 ratio. Serum is positive for anti-CARPVIII autoantibodies at 1:32 intensity, confirmed on repeat testing 2 months later. No evidence of intrathecal IgG synthesis. Based on this presentation and workup, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what is the next appropriate management step?

challenge_prompt: A 13-year-old female with no known past medical history presented with paresthesia in her right arm and chest area. Systemic and neurologic examinations were normal. Initial brain and spinal MRI showed a large brain tumor in the left occipital lobe with mass effect, as well as periventricular and juxtacortical demyelinating lesions. Two months after surgery, she experienced blurred vision in the right eye and pain on eye movement. Ophthalmologic examination was normal. Repeat brain MRI showed enhancing periventricular demyelinating plaques. Based on the clinical presentation and imaging, what is the most likely diagnosis for the new symptoms, and what is the most appropriate next treatment step?

challenge_prompt: A 48-year-old man was admitted to an acute care hospital with right-hand dyskinesia and dysarthria in July 2015. Cerebrospinal fluid examination showed a cell count of 102/dL, protein 54.7 mg/dL, glucose 59.0 mg/dL, and positive anti-NMDA receptor antibodies. FLAIR MRI of the head revealed a high-signal area in the left parietal cortex. After admission, the patient developed impaired consciousness, aspiration pneumonia, delusions, and abnormal behavior. Immunotherapy (steroids and cyclophosphamide) was administered, and neurological symptoms improved. The patient was transferred to a convalescent hospital in January 2016. Although consciousness and abnormal behaviors resolved, dysphagia persisted. Videoendoscopic examination showed poor swallowing reflexes, impaired laryngeal elevation, pharyngeal contraction, and lack of epiglottic movement. Cough and glottal closure reflexes were preserved, but the patient was unaware of pharyngeal residuals. The Hyodo score was: salivary pooling G2, glottal closure reflex G0, swallowing reflex initiation G3, pharyngeal clearance G3. Videofluoroscopy showed a delayed swallowing reflex (laryngeal elevation delay time 2.3 s), poor pharyngeal contraction, and inadequate laryngeal elevation. Residue was noted in the vallecula and pyriform sinuses, with a Penetration-Aspiration Scale score of 5. Blood tests were normal (white blood cells 5.6×10^3/μL, total protein 6.7 g/dL, albumin 4.1 g/dL, hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL, CRP 0.11 mg/dL). The patient had no limb or trunk muscle weakness, and functional independence measure was 125/126. He was self-sufficient except for tube feeding via percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy. What is the most likely cause of this patient's persistent dysphagia, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis with persistent neurogenic dysphagia
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune
answer_key.next_management_step: Outpatient swallowing rehabilitation including interferential current stimulation therapy and progressive resistance exercises

challenge_prompt: Two siblings, a 13-month-old boy and a 5.5-year-old girl, present with craniofacial abnormalities. Their parents have no similar features, no family history of craniofacial disorders, and no known teratogen exposure. The boy has a long, narrow head (dolichocephaly) with a wide and high forehead, a small nose with a low bridge, and midface hypoplasia. The girl has proptosis, a wide and high forehead, low nasal bridge, and midface hypoplasia. Both have normal neurological function, cognitive and motor development appropriate for age, no signs of increased intracranial pressure, and no limb, finger, toe, or other systemic anomalies. CT brain with 3D reconstruction shows complete closure and partial obliteration of multiple sutures in both. MRI brain reveals altered contour of the left cerebral hemisphere, optic nerve tortuosity with prominent perioptic CSF, posterior orbital sclera flattening bilaterally, and an empty sella, suggestive of increased intracranial pressure. There is no Chiari I malformation or ventriculomegaly. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next best management step?

challenge_prompt:
A male infant was born at 37 weeks gestation to a 23-year-old healthy mother via normal vaginal delivery. Birth weight was 2.5 kg and height 45 cm (both below 3rd percentile). Pregnancy was uncomplicated, no consanguinity. At 3 months, he presented with poor feeding and inadequate weight gain. Examination revealed: roundish facial profile, flat nasal bridge, widened nasal base, low-set ears, 5th finger clinodactyly, short stature (length 69 cm at 11 months, below 3rd percentile), stretched penile length 1.9 cm (micropenis), and testicular volume <1 mL. Neurologically, generalized hypotonia was noted. Global developmental delay was evident: gross motor at 9-month level, and fine motor, language, and social-adaptive at 6-month level at age 11 months. Echocardiogram showed a secundum atrial septal defect (ASD) of 6.5 mm with left-to-right shunt and mild tricuspid regurgitation. At 11 months, he presented with acute onset generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 30 minutes, managed with intravenous diazepam. CECT head showed mild hydrocephalus (Evans index 0.37) and widened anterior fontanelle. Brain MRI showed mild restricted diffusion in bilateral frontal and parietal subcortical white matter and globus pallidus, mild white matter volume loss in periventricular area, aplastic frontal and sphenoid paranasal sinuses, and aplasia of left transverse and sigmoid dural venous sinuses. He had no further seizures but developed hypertonia and developmental arrest. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what next diagnostic test would be most appropriate?

Cytogenetic analysis of peripheral blood revealed 49,XXXXY in all cells counted.

answer_key.diagnosis: 49,XXXXY syndrome (Fraccaro syndrome)
answer_key.etiology: Sex chromosome aneuploidy with three additional X chromosomes (49,XXXXY karyotype)
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform karyotype (chromosomal analysis) or chromosomal microarray

challenge_prompt: A 72-year-old man with a history of atrial fibrillation and abdominal aortic aneurysm repair presented with a wake-up stroke manifesting as right-sided weakness, facial drop, and aphasia. CT perfusion and angio-CT at 3.5 hours showed an infarct in the left middle cerebral artery (MCA) territory (mismatch volume 62 mL, ratio 2.3) with a left MCA thrombus. The ischemic core involved the left superior and middle temporal gyrus (excluding the pole), inferior parietal lobule and supramarginal gyrus, posterior insula, and entorhinal cortex. The penumbra extended to the inferior temporal gyrus including the pole, superior parietal lobe, and parahippocampal region. He underwent thrombectomy and intravenous tPA. On day 3, neurological examination revealed mild right upper extremity weakness, expressive and receptive aphasia, spatio-temporal disorientation, and right-sided hemispatial and body neglect. MRI on day 4 confirmed residual ischemic damage of the left inferior parietal lobule, temporal lobe (excluding the pole), and posterior insula. On the same day, resting-state awake high-density EEG (HD-EEG) was performed using 128 channels. Power spectrum analysis revealed significant asymmetry between hemispheres with predominant delta (1-4 Hz) and theta (4-8 Hz) activity (p<0.05) over the lesioned hemisphere. Delta activity was increased bilaterally in ventral regions (including temporal poles) and superior parietal lobules, and in the left post-central gyrus. Alpha activity (8-12 Hz) was absent over the occipital regions bilaterally (posterior-to-anterior shift). Delta-to-alpha ratio (DAR) and delta-theta-to-alpha-beta ratio (DTABR) were significantly higher in the lesioned hemisphere (p<0.05). Source reconstruction localized delta slowing beyond the ischemic focus, in perilesional and distant regions, including contralateral areas. Connectivity analysis showed delta connectivity in the lesioned hemisphere (measures: degree, strength, eigenvector centrality, local efficiency, clustering coefficient), though not statistically significant. Based on these findings, what is the most likely underlying neurophysiological process, and what would be an appropriate next step for rehabilitation?

answer_key.diagnosis: Perilesional and remote delta slowing with loss of posterior alpha and delta connectivity following acute ischemic stroke (consistent with electro-cortical diaschisis)
answer_key.etiology: Acute ischemic stroke in left MCA territory with residual infarct in left inferior parietal lobule, temporal lobe, and posterior insula
answer_key.next_management_step: Personalized neuromodulation targeting the left dorsal attention network (shown to be deactivated by HD-EEG) to address right-sided neglect

Penumbra in left inferior temporal gyrus, superior parietal lobe, parahippocampal region

challenge_prompt: A 66-year-old woman presented with a 1-year history of pain and numbness radiating from the right buttock to the posterior thigh, and intermittent claudication limiting walking to 10 minutes. Physical examination revealed no motor deficits, no neurogenic bladder or rectal dysfunction, and a negative straight leg raise test. MRI showed a 32 × 16 mm intradural mass at the L5 level with iso-intensity on both T1- and T2-weighted images and uniform contrast enhancement; the lesion had a well-defined border with the central canal. CT myelography demonstrated obstruction of CSF flow at the tumor level. The patient underwent lumbar laminectomy; intraoperatively, the tumor compressed the cauda equina but was not adherent to the dura mater. A single thinning nerve fiber connected the tumor to surrounding neural structures. The tumor was elastic-hard, smooth-surfaced, and yellowish. Histopathology revealed lobular proliferation of tumor cells with oval or round nuclei and clear cytoplasm arranged in vague whorls. Interstitial collagen fibers were highlighted by Masson-Trichrome stain. Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining showed PAS-positive, diastase-sensitive cytoplasmic granules. No tumor necrosis or psammoma bodies were identified. Immunohistochemistry was positive for epithelial membrane antigen (EMA), somatostatin type 2 receptor (SSTR2), progesterone receptor (PgR), and Vimentin; negative for glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP), S-100, and CD34. The Ki-67/MIB-1 labeling index was 5% at the hotspot. Short-axis sections of the tumor showed no dural collagen tissue and no evidence of dural attachment. Localized S-100 positivity suggested a potential neural origin. Based on the information provided, what is the most likely diagnosis and the next appropriate management step?

aliases: non-dural clear cell meningioma of lumbar spine
answer_key.diagnosis: Clear cell meningioma (WHO grade II)
answer_key.etiology: Unknown; possibly originating from the soft membrane of the caudal nerve
answer_key.next_management_step: Complete surgical resection achieved; no adjuvant radiotherapy indicated; close long-term follow-up with MRI surveillance

challenge_prompt:
A 20-year-old man presents with a two-year history of persistent urinary urgency and pollakiuria, voiding 15 to 20 times per day. Clinical examination is unremarkable. Paraclinical investigations: urine culture, pelvic ultrasound, and urinalysis are all normal. Urodynamic testing is normal. He is initially diagnosed with neurogenic bladder. challenge_prompt: A 20-year-old Hispanic male with newly diagnosed stage IIB osteosarcoma of the left distal femur presents with acute onset of slurred speech and right-sided hemiplegia 9 days after receiving his fourth cycle of methotrexate. He is receiving MAP therapy (methotrexate, doxorubicin, cisplatin) per AOST0331 protocol. MTX blood levels throughout his fourth cycle were within goal range; the level on the last day of cycle 4 was 0.07 μmol/L (goal <0.1 μmol/L). On the day of presentation, MTX blood level was undetectable (<0.05 μmol/L). He also received fosaprepitant during the fourth MTX cycle for nausea. Brain MRI with and without contrast shows three separate foci of restricted diffusion within the centrum semiovale without associated T2/FLAIR hyperintensity or enhancement. No other abnormalities are noted.

challenge_prompt: A 4-year-old girl presented with right upper limb weakness and inability to grip with her right hand for over 3 years. On physical examination, a heart murmur was detected. Echocardiogram revealed right atrial and right ventricular enlargement, a 7mm atrial septal defect with right-to-left shunt, and total anomalous pulmonary venous drainage into the left branch of the portal vein. On the night of admission, she suddenly cried out, became cyanotic, with oxygen saturation fluctuating around 20-30%, poor responsiveness, tachypnea, perioral cyanosis, and coarse breath sounds. A II/VI systolic murmur was heard at the left second intercostal space with fixed splitting of S2, and lower extremities were marbled and cool. After resuscitation, oxygen saturation improved to 80-84%. The next day, she underwent total anomalous pulmonary venous drainage repair, ligation of patent ductus arteriosus, and partial atrial septal defect repair under hypothermic cardiopulmonary bypass. Two days later, delayed chest closure was performed. Postoperatively, she exhibited lateral head shaking. Cranial CT showed brain atrophy, low-density lesions in the left parietal lobe, and high-density lesions in the surrounding cerebral cortex. She was followed for nearly 3 years. On outpatient examination at age 4, she was conscious, responsive, with head control. She had difficulty bearing weight and the right upper limb movement was less than the left. Right upper limb muscle strength was III+, while other limbs were normal. The right thumb was adducted and there was no active grasping. Gross Motor Function Measure indicated hand dysfunction and hand apraxia. Head MRI showed localized cerebral softening and atrophy in the left temporoparietal lobe, insula, and precentral gyrus; the lesion had enlarged compared to prior imaging; there was a small amount of fluid under the left parietal subdural membrane that had been absorbed; and bilateral frontal and temporal subarachnoid spaces were prominent. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what next management step is most appropriate?

answer_key.etiology: Cardiac embolism from atrial septal defect with right-to-left shunt and total anomalous pulmonary venous drainage

challenge_prompt: A 63-year-old woman of East Asian descent presented with acute left basal ganglia hemorrhage (CT: small-volume <10 mL). She had a prior history of cerebral infarction in 2003. In 2016, she experienced transient limb weakness and dysarthria. Digital subtraction angiography at that time revealed findings suggestive of Moyamoya disease. On admission, her peak systolic blood pressure was 150 mmHg; she had no other chronic medical conditions. A detailed family history identified 17 surviving members across three generations. Multiple relatives had suffered from cerebrovascular events: the proband's older brother had recurrent strokes, two sons of a sister had childhood-onset strokes, one sister had subarachnoid hemorrhage, and another sister had multiple ischemic strokes. Several other family members were asymptomatic. Based on the clustering of cerebrovascular events, genetic testing was recommended.

answer_key.etiology: Dual RNF213 variants (R4810K and T1727M) with possible modifier genes

challenge_prompt:
A 55-year-old woman with a 5-year history of Paget disease of bone (diagnosed in June 2019 based on multifocal osteolytic lesions, cortical hyperplasia, osteosclerosis, and alkaline phosphatase >800 U/L) treated with bisphosphonates presented in November 2024 with acute neurological symptoms: rapidly ascending numbness reaching the chest level within 24 hours, lower limb weakness (right grade 4, left grade 3), and urinary and bowel dysfunction. Imaging studies including CT, MRI, and PET-CT demonstrated multiple low-density bone destruction in the cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine; compression fractures of C6, T9, L3; spinal stenosis at T2; and increased glucose metabolism in multiple skeletal sites. Intraoperative vertebral biopsy confirmed osteosarcoma.

challenge_prompt: A 79-year-old man with no prior medical history was brought to a hospital after a witnessed generalized tonic–clonic seizure lasting 5–10 minutes, followed by postictal drowsiness for 30 minutes and right-sided weakness. Upon transfer to our facility, he was alert and oriented to time, person, and place. Neurological examination revealed normal extraocular movements, and visual field assessment confirmed no homonymous hemianopia. He had moderate dysarthria, flattening of the right nasolabial fold, and mild facial sensory loss in the distribution of V1–V3. Right upper limb power was 3/5, compared with 5/5 in the other limbs. Finger-to-nose testing was intact bilaterally. During observation, the patient exhibited involuntary but seemingly purposeful movements of the right upper limb, occasionally striking his own body or face, without conscious control. The patient repeatedly described the limb as 'moving on its own'. He reported being unable to restrain it when the abnormal movements occurred. These movements were witnessed by the neurology team and gradually resolved over two days without specific pharmacologic intervention. The initial brain CT scan revealed atrophic brain parenchyma and evidence of chronic microangiopathy but no hemorrhage, ischemic events, or brain mass. The laboratory workup was largely unremarkable. ECG showed normal sinus rhythm. Brain MRI was performed within 48 hours and revealed restricted diffusion and hyperintense FLAIR signals in the left medial temporal lobe, occipital lobe, and posterior thalamus. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate next management step?

aliases: Posterior alien hand syndrome due to PCA infarction
Sensory alien hand syndrome secondary to PCA stroke
answer_key.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke in the left posterior cerebral artery territory presenting as posterior alien hand syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Thromboembolic occlusion of the left posterior cerebral artery
answer_key.next_management_step: Antiplatelet therapy (e.g., aspirin), stroke secondary prevention (risk factor management), and rehabilitation for alien hand syndrome (e.g., mirror box therapy, distracting maneuvers)

title: Alien hand syndrome as the initial presentation of posterior cerebral artery infarction: a case report

challenge_prompt: A 56-year-old male construction worker with a long-standing history of alcohol use disorder was admitted after a witnessed syncopal episode. Family reported several months of progressive apathy, confusion, and weight loss due to reduced food intake. On examination, he had erythematous, scaly lesions on the dorsal hands and neck in photo-exposed areas. Neurological examination revealed mild disorientation in time, impaired attention, reduced processing speed, apathy, decreased verbal output, and slow speech. Intermittent myoclonus of the left upper limb was noted. Blood tests showed macrocytic anemia (Hb 12 g/dL, MCV 107 fL), thrombocytopenia (122 × 10^9/L), elevated gamma-glutamyl transferase (419 U/L). Serum vitamin B12 (553 pg/mL) and folic acid (3.1 ng/mL) were normal, but vitamin B3 (niacin) was markedly reduced at 9.3 µg/L. Lumbar puncture was unremarkable. EEG revealed diffuse slowing without epileptiform activity. Brain MRI showed marked atrophy of the corpus callosum, particularly the body and splenium, with hypointensity on T1 and hyperintensity on T2-weighted images. Mini-Mental State Examination score was 13/30. Neuropsychological testing demonstrated severe diffuse cognitive impairment with deficits in attention, memory, executive function, language with anomia and paraphasias, and visuoconstructive abilities. Dermatological examination confirmed a photosensitive dermatitis consistent with pellagra.

aliases: alcohol-related demyelinating disorder of corpus callosum
niacin deficiency dermatitis with neuropsychiatric symptoms
answer_key.diagnosis: Marchiafava-Bignami disease and pellagra
answer_key.etiology: Chronic alcohol use disorder leading to malnutrition, thiamine deficiency (contributing to MBD) and niacin deficiency (causing pellagra)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate intravenous B-complex vitamins including thiamine and niacin, ensure alcohol abstinence, and provide supportive care.

challenge_prompt: A 77-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presented with numbness in the left buttock and anterior left thigh in February 2025. Lumbar spine MRI showed mild spinal canal stenosis. Over several months, he developed progressive lower limb weakness, urinary dysfunction, left upper limb weakness, and neck discomfort. By mid-July, he was hospitalized. Brain diffusion-weighted MRI showed slight increased signal in both parietal cortices, possibly artifact. Thoracic spine MRI revealed high T2 signal at Th2-Th3. CSF showed elevated protein without pleocytosis. His condition deteriorated: he became bedridden, depressed, and reticent, then developed impaired consciousness and was transferred in mid-August. On transfer, GCS was E4V2M5, he was unable to speak but made incomprehensible sounds, and had myoclonus in limbs, especially right upper limb. Muscle strength was 4/5 left upper, 2/5 right upper and both lower limbs. No sensory level. Tendon reflexes absent, Babinski negative. Urinary and fecal incontinence. No neck stiffness. Blood tests showed hypernatremia (Na 179 mEq/L) and renal dysfunction. EEG showed only slow waves without epileptic discharges. Hypernatremia was corrected slowly but consciousness did not improve. Autoantibodies (antinuclear, anti-DNA, anti-GAD, anti-AQP4, onconeuronal) were negative. Second CSF: no cells, no oligoclonal bands, herpes PCR negative; IgG index elevated at 1.07, IL-6 elevated at 25.5 pg/mL. Follow-up brain DWI in late August showed clear hyperintensity in bilateral parietal cortices with expanded area. EEG in early September showed periodic sharp wave complexes at approximately 1 Hz. Based on this information, what is the most likely diagnosis?

answer_key.diagnosis: Sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (sCJD)
answer_key.etiology: sporadic prion disease
answer_key.next_management_step: CSF real-time quaking-induced conversion (RT-QuIC) assay and measurement of 14-3-3 and total tau proteins

periodic sharp wave complexes on EEG at ~1 Hz
cortical hyperintensity on brain DWI in bilateral parietal lobes

challenge_prompt: An 87-year-old woman with long-standing type 2 diabetes (T2D), hypertension, dyslipidaemia and chronic kidney disease stage 4 presented with continuous, irregular and non-patterned movements of her right upper limbs accompanied with random jerks of her lower limbs and twitching at the right corner of her mouth for 3 weeks. Her condition was associated with worsening abnormal movements involving the right arm, leg and face which were present throughout the day and disappeared during sleep. She denied fever, constitutional symptoms, osmotic symptoms, acute behavioural changes or cognitive decline. She had no personal or family history of movement disorders, and there had been no change in her regular medications, none of which were known to cause extra-pyramidal side effects. There was no preceding trauma, and she had otherwise been well except for worsening home blood glucose levels, which ranged around 14–16 mmol/L. Her blood pressure upon presentation was 144/69 mmHg, while her random capillary sugar level was 14.2 mmol/L. Electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus rhythm at a rate of 72 bpm with premature atrial complexes. Neurological examination confirmed right sided choreiform movements with dystonia and orofacial dyskinesia. Muscle tone was preserved, with normal power, reflexes, coordination, cranial nerve function and mental status. Blood parameters: random blood sugar 14.2 mmol/L, HbA1c 10.3%, HCO3 22.7 mmol/L, lactate 1.9 mmol/L, urine ketone negative, hemoglobin 10.0 g/dL, platelet 220 x10^9/L, total white cell 8.2 x10^9/L, urea 12.8 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 5.1 mmol/L, creatinine 184 mmol/L, calcium 2.39 mmol/L, magnesium 0.75 mmol/L, phosphate 1.33 mmol/L. She was referred to the emergency department for brain CT to exclude intracranial pathology. The non-contrast CT scan demonstrated subtle hyperdensity in the left striatum. This pattern helped exclude ischaemic or haemorrhagic stroke. Based on the above presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

answer_key.diagnosis: Diabetic striatopathy
answer_key.etiology: Poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus with non-ketotic hyperglycemia
answer_key.next_management_step: Optimize glycemic control (e.g., oral hypoglycemic agents) and consider short-term symptomatic treatment with a D2-receptor antagonist (e.g., risperidone or haloperidol) for disabling chorea.

Sinus rhythm with premature atrial complexes

Elevated random blood glucose (14.2 mmol/L) and HbA1c (10.3%)
Non-ketotic (urine ketone negative, HCO3 22.7 mmol/L)
CT head showing subtle hyperdensity in the left striatum

challenge_prompt: A 58-year-old male patient presents with left hemiparesis following a cerebral stroke. Baseline assessment shows a Fugl-Meyer Assessment for Upper Extremity (FMA-UE) score of 20 (moderate impairment). The patient undergoes a rehabilitation session using a P300-based brain-computer interface (BCI) system. During the session, the patient achieves a median online classification accuracy of 0.63 (chance level 0.2). Upon successful classification, a robotic orthosis moves the paretic left hand. EEG recorded during the passive movement shows a significant decrease in μ- (10-14 Hz) and β- (18-22 Hz) oscillatory activity, predominantly over the left sensorimotor areas (contralesional to the moving hand). The μ-ERD is more focused in the parietal area (Pz channel), and the β-ERD shows rapid onset and lasts approximately 6 seconds. Based on these findings, what is the most likely location of the stroke lesion, and what is the most appropriate next step in the rehabilitation approach described?

answer_key.diagnosis: Right hemisphere stroke (ipsilesional)
answer_key.etiology: Ischemic stroke (presumed)
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue P300-based BCI rehabilitation with visuomotor transformation paradigm

challenge_prompt: A 76-year-old man, still working as a bus driver, presents to the emergency department with an 18-day history of violent, involuntary limb movements and intense inner restlessness that prevents him from sitting, standing, or sleeping. The symptoms began abruptly one day after a diagnostic coronary angiogram performed for coronary artery disease assessment. Notably, he experienced a fleeting, much milder episode of 'over-mobility' in all four limbs immediately after electrical cardioversion for new-onset atrial fibrillation about six weeks earlier, which resolved within days. His past medical history includes hypertension, atrial fibrillation (on apixaban 5 mg twice daily since November 2022), and hyperlipidemia (rosuvastatin 20 mg nightly). He has no medical or family history of neurological disease and has never taken dopamine-blocking agents. On examination, he appears exhausted but alert and fully oriented. Speech is mildly dysarthric. Cranial nerves are normal. Motor examination shows normal bulk, strength, and tone without rigidity or spasticity. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk in the biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, and patellae; Achilles reflexes are appropriate, and plantar responses are flexor. Finger-nose testing discloses mild bilateral dysmetria, and rapid alternating movements are slowed. Throughout the examination, he exhibits frequent multifocal involuntary movements involving the arms, legs, neck, and jaw, consisting predominantly of irregular, flowing choreiform movements with intermittent larger amplitude, ballistic flinging components. Brief superimposed shock-like jerks are observed. The movements are approximately 20/min and reportedly persist during sleep. He describes pronounced inner restlessness with a persistent urge to move, resulting in inability to sit or stand still and severe sleep disturbance. Initial laboratory studies, including serum electrolytes, renal and liver panels, glucose, thyroid function, vitamin B12, folate, HbA1c, magnesium, parathyroid hormone, inflammatory markers, antinuclear and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, and viral serologies, are normal except for a weakly positive homogeneous ANA titer. Brain MRI with and without gadolinium demonstrates a small right subcortical frontal lacunar infarct and scattered supratentorial white matter hyperintensities but no diffusion restriction, edema, basal ganglia signal change, or contrast enhancement. The infarct is considered unlikely to be causative due to its anatomical location and temporal profile. Cerebrospinal fluid shows no cells, normal protein, glucose, and lactate, negative oligoclonal bands, and a mildly elevated total tau (528 ng/L; reference <409 ng/L); infectious, autoimmune, and paraneoplastic antibody panels are negative. Thoracic CT and whole-body screening reveal no malignancy. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

answer_key.diagnosis: Post-procedural choreo-ballism and akathisia due to transient basal ganglia dysfunction
answer_key.etiology: Transient basal ganglia dysfunction likely related to peri-procedural hypoxic-ischemic stress from coronary angiography (and possibly prior cardioversion)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate symptomatic treatment with a vesicular monoamine transporter type 2 (VMAT-2) inhibitor such as tetrabenazine after excluding alternative causes
optional_findings: Mild bilateral dysmetria on finger-nose testing
Brisk deep tendon reflexes in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellae
Oxygen saturation 93% during angiography (without O2 supplementation)
Mildly elevated CSF total tau (528 ng/L)

Prior milder transient hyperkinetic episode after electrical cardioversion
Normal serum electrolytes, glucose, thyroid function, vitamin B12, folate, HbA1c, magnesium, PTH, inflammatory markers, ANA (except weakly positive homogeneous), ANCA, viral serologies
Brain MRI: small right frontal subcortical lacunar infarct, no basal ganglia signal change or diffusion restriction
CSF: no cells, normal protein/glucose/lactate, negative oligoclonal bands, negative infectious/autoimmune/paraneoplastic panels
Negative malignancy screening on CT chest/whole-body
No prior neurological history or dopamine-blocking agent use

title: Tetrabenazine-Responsive Choreo-Ballism and Akathisia After Cardioversion and Coronary Angiography: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 22-year-old man presented with a history of epilepsy since age 4, when he experienced a generalized status epilepticus. He had three febrile seizures before age 4 and motor clumsiness with occasional falls since age 3. He was born full-term via C-section due to prolonged labor, with normal Apgar scores. He achieved motor and language milestones within normal limits. At age 1, he underwent orchidopexy for cryptorchism. His current medications include levetiracetam 500 mg/day. Family history includes consanguineous parents and a sister with fine motor disorder.

On examination at age 22, he had widely spaced eyes, dysarthria, deep voice, exaggerated masseter reflex, mild diffuse muscle atrophy in all limbs, mild tetraspasticity, exaggerated deep tendon reflexes with bilateral ankle clonus, mild tetraataxia, positive pyramidal signs, normal gait, and normal cognitive function (MMSE 29). Funduscopy and electrocardiogram were normal.

Brain MRI showed generalized, extensive, confluent subcortical T2 hyperintensities predominantly in the frontal and bilateral temporal lobes, with no contrast enhancement, no diffusion restriction, and no atrophy. EEG was normal. Urine organic acid analysis revealed markedly elevated L-2-hydroxyglutaric acid with normal D-2-hydroxyglutaric acid. Blood tests were normal except for mild vitamin D deficiency.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: L-2-hydroxyglutaric aciduria (L2HGA)
answer_key.etiology: Homozygous c.905C>T (p.Pro302Leu) variant in L2HGDH gene
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for L2HGDH mutation to confirm diagnosis and provide family counseling; initiate supportive care including antiseizure medication, physical therapy, and monitoring.

Mild tetraspasticity and tetraataxia

Widely spaced eyes

No cognitive impairment (MMSE 29, normal milestones)

challenge_prompt:
A 28-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of bipolar disorder was brought to the Emergency Department after being found unconscious at home. Prior to being found, she had exhibited abnormal behavior characterized by slurred speech, unsteady gait, and nystagmus. There were at least two episodes of generalized tonic-clonic seizures accompanied by bleeding from the mouth. On arrival, Glasgow Coma Scale score was 9/15. Vital signs: heart rate 122 bpm, blood pressure 122/33 mmHg, temperature 102.4°F. Physical examination revealed multiple superficial bruises over extremities. Urine pregnancy test negative. Her regular medications include lamotrigine 200 mg once daily and fluoxetine 20 mg once daily. Initially admitted to Neurology ward, she experienced rapid deterioration with two additional generalized tonic-clonic seizures with upward gaze deviation. She was administered IV midazolam and phenytoin sodium. Her neurological status worsened to E1V1M1, and she suffered an aspiration event, necessitating urgent intubation and transfer to CCU. On CCU admission, heart rate 155 bpm, febrile at 102°F, oxygen saturation 99% on mechanical ventilation. ECG demonstrated atrial fibrillation, treated with IV amiodarone. Initial arterial blood gas showed mixed acidosis (severe lactic acidosis and respiratory acidosis). Laboratory investigations revealed severe anemia, mild transaminitis, and raised C-reactive protein. Neuroimaging with MRI brain (with gadolinium) and cerebrospinal fluid analysis were unremarkable. Echocardiogram showed LVEF 60%; abdominal ultrasound normal. Despite midazolam infusion and phenytoin, she continued to have seizure episodes, leading to addition of IV lacosamide. Seizures persisted on Day 3, requiring addition of IV levetiracetam. Based on the presentation and available information, what is the most likely etiology of this patient's condition, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

It was later learned that she had ingested approximately 30 tablets of lamotrigine (6000 mg) and fluoxetine (600 mg).

answer_key.diagnosis: Lamotrigine and fluoxetine overdose
answer_key.etiology: Ingestion of supratherapeutic doses of lamotrigine and fluoxetine
answer_key.next_management_step: Aggressive supportive care including airway protection, multi-agent antiepileptic therapy (e.g., benzodiazepines, lacosamide, levetiracetam), cardiac monitoring, and treatment of complications (aspiration pneumonia, atrial fibrillation). Obtain detailed collateral history to confirm suspected overdose.

Generalized tonic-clonic seizures
Atrial fibrillation on ECG
Nystagmus
History of lamotrigine and fluoxetine use
Refractory seizures despite midazolam and phenytoin
Normal MRI brain and CSF analysis
Severe lactic acidosis on ABG

title: Lamotrigine and Fluoxetine Overdose Presenting With Severe Neuro-Cardiotoxicity: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 17-month-old female with no significant past medical history presented with multiple seizure types including epileptic spasms, tonic, and atonic seizures. She had met all developmental milestones but showed regression days before presentation. Video EEG showed hypsarrhythmia (BASED score 5). Brain MRI showed a small area of gliosis and hemosiderin staining in the left periventricular white matter. Genetic testing revealed a mutation in SCN1B associated with epileptic encephalopathy, and she was diagnosed with SCN1B-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy (DEE). She was initially treated with high-dose steroids and levetiracetam with partial improvement; clobazam was added due to worsening tonic seizures. At 20 months of age, due to persistent frequent seizures, she was started on fenfluramine and titrated to 0.7 mg/kg/day, with a marked reduction in seizure frequency (>50%). Baseline echocardiogram (ECHO) prior to fenfluramine was normal. Follow-up ECHO after 4 months of therapy was normal. ECHO at 11 months of therapy showed new findings: elevated right ventricular systolic pressure (RVSP) estimate of 45.2 mmHg, with no valvular disease. She was asymptomatic. What is the most likely cause of these new echocardiographic findings, and what is the next best management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Fenfluramine-associated pulmonary arterial hypertension
answer_key.etiology: Fenfluramine therapy for SCN1B-related DEE
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue fenfluramine and perform serial echocardiographic monitoring until resolution of PAH.

challenge_prompt:
A 14-day-old female infant, born at term via uncomplicated vaginal delivery, birth weight 2.85 kg, no family history of congenital heart disease or arrhythmias, passed newborn screening. At DOL 14, presented with vomiting and diarrhea for 2 days. Over 4 visits to PCP for similar concerns, vital signs stable, physical exam unremarkable. On DOL 18, brought back for persistent feeding difficulties, new weight loss (weight 2.72 kg from 3.37 kg), and increased work of breathing. Sent to ED.

ED: Afebrile, BP high 60s, HR 263 bpm, RR 60/min, O2 sat 92%. Physical exam: mottled, weak cry, poor perfusion to lower extremities, increased work of breathing, unable to tolerate feeds. ABG: mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis. Initial ECG: wide complex tachycardia at 262 bpm, concerning for SVT with aberrant conduction or VT. Vagal maneuvers (ice packs) unsuccessful. Adenosine 1 mg/kg and then 2 mg/kg IV no change in HR. Required cardioversion twice with 2 J/kg, returning to sinus rhythm at ~180 bpm. Echocardiogram ordered.

PICU: Required further cardioversion en route. ABG on arrival: mixed respiratory/metabolic acidosis, lactic acid 7.1. Given D10 bolus, high-flow nasal cannula. ECG now shows sinus rhythm with ventricular pre-excitation and delta waves. Recurrent episodes of SVT; responds to adenosine but requires escalation of esmolol drip. Weaned off esmolol, transitioned to oral propranolol 4 mg/kg/day. Initial echocardiogram shows depressed ventricular function. Propranolol decreased to 2 mg/kg/day, oral amiodarone 5 mg/kg/day started, magnesium carbonate 10 mg/kg three times daily added. Stabilized with no further SVT episodes on this antiarrhythmic regimen and cleared for discharge.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

answer_key.diagnosis: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome with supraventricular tachycardia
answer_key.etiology: Accessory pathway (bundle of Kent) leading to ventricular pre-excitation and re-entrant tachycardia
answer_key.next_management_step: Chronic oral antiarrhythmic therapy (e.g., propranolol and amiodarone) and follow-up with pediatric cardiology/electrophysiology
optional_findings: Weight loss from 3.37 kg to 2.72 kg
No family history of arrhythmias or sudden death
Normal newborn screening including congenital heart disease screen

Failed vagal maneuvers and adenosine

Post-cardioversion ECG showing sinus rhythm with ventricular pre-excitation and delta waves
Recurrent SVT episodes requiring esmolol drip
Depressed ventricular function on initial echocardiogram
Stabilization on propranolol, amiodarone, and magnesium

title: When Fussiness Is Not Just Fussiness: A Case Report of Early Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome Recognized in Primary Care

challenge_prompt:
A male in his seventh decade with obesity, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cirrhosis, and chronic back pain presents with acute onset bilateral lower extremity paralysis upon awakening. He had worsening back pain and radiculopathy for 3 weeks prior. On examination: thoracic spine tenderness, diminished sensation from nipple line down, absent perineal sensation, areflexia in legs, loss of rectal tone, 0/5 strength in legs, 5/5 strength in arms. challenge_prompt:
A 32-year-old Chinese woman presents with sudden-onset dizziness and blurred vision for 2 days. The symptoms began upon waking, characterized by persistent non-rotatory dizziness and bilateral visual blurring, particularly when looking to the left. She reports no associated nausea, vomiting, hearing disturbance, dysphagia, dysarthria, headache, or dyspnoea. She has no significant past medical history, including no hypertension, diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, or smoking. She denies oral contraceptive use, illicit drug use, or ergot derivative exposure. Her father had a history of aortic dissection and underwent surgical repair at another hospital. Physical examination reveals blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 72 beats/min. Craniofacial features include hypertelorism and mild malar flattening. No bifid uvula, joint hypermobility (Beighton score 2/9), arachnodactyly, pectus deformity, skin translucency, or striae are present. Neurological examination shows left homonymous hemianopia and mild decreased sensation on the left side. NIHSS score is 3. Laboratory investigations: LDL-cholesterol 2.22 mmol/L. Transthoracic echocardiography shows normal cardiac structure and aortic root dimensions (28 mm); bubble contrast study is negative for patent foramen ovale; 24-hour ECG monitoring shows sinus rhythm without atrial fibrillation. Brain MRI with DWI confirms acute ischemic infarction in the right occipital lobe and thalamus. SWI reveals co-localised microbleeds in the right occipital lobe. CT angiography demonstrates bilateral vertebral artery stenosis with post-stenotic dilatation and an aberrant right subclavian artery (arteria lusoria) with aneurysmal dilatation (maximum diameter 15 mm) at its origin. Digital subtraction angiography confirms bilateral multisegmental vertebral arteriopathy: the right vertebral artery shows alternating aneurysmal dilatation and stenosis across V1–V4 (V2 severe stenosis, V1 aneurysmal dilatation, V3–V4 moderate stenosis); the left vertebral artery shows multifocal segmental severe stenosis at V2. Thoracoabdominal CTA shows normal aortic dimensions.

Based on the above findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next best diagnostic step?

Genetic testing revealed a pathogenic SMAD3 variant (c.859C>T, p.Arg287Trp).

answer_key.diagnosis: Loeys-Dietz syndrome type 3 (LDS3) due to SMAD3 pathogenic variant
answer_key.etiology: Heterozygous pathogenic variant in SMAD3 (c.859C>T; p.Arg287Trp) causing TGF-β pathway dysfunction
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform whole-exome sequencing or targeted genetic testing for SMAD3 or hereditary connective tissue disorder panel
optional_findings: SWI microbleeds co-localized in right occipital lobe
Left homonymous hemianopia and mild left-sided sensory loss
Normal cardiac structure and aortic root diameter (28 mm)
Negative bubble contrast study for PFO
No joint hypermobility, bifid uvula, or other syndromic features

challenge_prompt:
A 30-year-old woman with a history of three early miscarriages presents 45 days postpartum with severe right-sided headaches, blurred vision, and ipsilateral cervical fullness. Ophthalmologic evaluation reveals decreased visual acuity (5/10 right, 6/10 left), right ptosis, impaired abduction of the left eye, and bilateral grade-2 papilledema. Laboratory studies show markedly elevated prolactin (1092 ng/mL), normal cortisol, thyroid function, and growth hormone. CRP is 78 mg/dL and D-dimer is 25 mg/L. Brain MRI with MR venography demonstrates thrombosis of the right lateral sinus extending into the ipsilateral internal jugular vein, and an invasive intra-sellar mass compressing the optic chiasm, filling the sphenoid sinus, and eroding the sellar floor. Testing for antiphospholipid antibodies is positive for anticardiolipin and anti-β2-glycoprotein I antibodies. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis (or diagnoses) and what is the next best step in management?

Histopathological examination confirmed a prolactin-producing pituitary carcinoma, with diffuse prolactin expression on immunohistochemistry, a high Ki-67 index, and p53 overexpression.

challenge_prompt: A 31-year-old man presents with a single episode of spontaneous hemothorax. Imaging studies reveal a mass involving the left sixth rib with extension toward the costovertebral junction and adjacent vertebral body. Percutaneous needle biopsy establishes a diagnosis of grade 1 chondrosarcoma. The patient undergoes surgical resection of the affected rib through left posterior thoracotomy. Histopathological examination confirms low-grade chondrosarcoma; however, the medial surgical margin is positive, with residual disease identified at the level of the rib head. challenge_prompt:
A 27-year-old male presents following a road traffic accident (two-wheeler collision) with head, face, and chest injuries, loss of consciousness, and altered sensorium for two hours. No significant comorbidities. On examination, swelling and abrasion over right forehead and temporal region. Neurological assessment: GCS E1VTM4. Intubated and ventilated.

Initial non-contrast CT brain (day 0): Acute hemorrhagic contusions in right frontal and basifrontal lobes with intracranial air foci/pneumocephalus. Subarachnoid, extradural, and subdural hemorrhages. Midline shift ~4 mm to the left. Extensive comminuted skull base fractures involving paranasal sinuses with associated hemosinus.

Early follow-up CT (day 0, 10 hours): Mild increase in hemorrhagic contusions and extradural hemorrhage; persistent intracranial air foci.

Day 2 CT: Interval reduction in hemorrhagic components; complete resolution of intracranial air foci. Persistent right hemosinus.

Day 14: Patient develops low-grade fever and fluctuating sensorium (GCS E3V3M5). CT shows partial resolution of right frontal hemosinus; new small intracranial air collection in right basifrontal region measuring ~2.8 × 1.7 cm, consistent with delayed onset pneumocephalus.

Day 40: Marked neurological deterioration (GCS E1VTM3) with features of raised intracranial pressure for 8 hours. CT reveals complete resolution of right frontal hemosinus; a large, well-defined intracranial air collection in right basifrontal region measuring ~7.9 × 4.8 cm, exerting significant mass effect with effacement of surrounding cortical sulci, compression of frontal horn of lateral ventricle, midline shift ~5 mm to the left, and subfalcine herniation. Hemorrhagic contusions have largely resolved.

Based on the presentation and imaging progression, what is the most likely cause of this patient's deterioration, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Delayed expanding intracerebral pneumatocele
answer_key.etiology: Persistent communication between intracranial compartment and frontal sinus due to skull base fracture, initially tamponaded by hemosinus, which resolved allowing progressive air entry
answer_key.next_management_step: Urgent neurosurgical intervention: anterior cranial fossa base repair with dural defect closure and cranioplasty

Resolution of initial pneumocephalus by day 2
Extensive skull base fractures involving paranasal sinuses

Absence of new hemorrhage or rebleeding

challenge_prompt: A 65-year-old right-handed woman (13 years of education, teacher) presented 61 days after a stroke involving the right temporal and parietal lobes, extending to the insula and subcortical structures. Neurological examination: naming and comprehension within normal range; no difficulty in verbal or visuospatial memory tests; abstract reasoning intact; no signs of perceptual or representational neglect on standard battery (Letter Cancellation, Line Cancellation, Wundt-Jastrow Area Illusion Test, Sentence Reading, Familiar Squares Description Test, O'Clock Test). She underwent two planning tasks: the Mine Field Task (MFT), a navigational planning task requiring participants to remember the locations of obstacles on a chessboard and plan a shortest route from start to end while avoiding obstacles; and the Tower of London (ToL-16) task, a visuospatial planning task involving moving beads to match a goal configuration. Results: On the MFT, her performance was superior to controls (t(1,9)=2.5, one-tailed p=0.01); on the ToL-16, she showed a deficit (t(1,9)=−1.8, one-tailed p=0.05). The Bayesian Standardized Difference Test (BSDT) indicated a probability of 0.00226 (one-tailed) that the standardized difference for a control would be greater than her difference (Z-DCC = −3.715, 95% CI −5.704 to −2.042). Based on this presentation, what is the most likely cognitive dissociation and its underlying lesion? Also, what next diagnostic or management step is most appropriate?

aliases: double dissociation between visuospatial and travel planning
right temporoparietal stroke causing VP deficit
answer_key.diagnosis: Selective impairment of visuospatial planning (VP) with preserved travel planning (TP), consistent with a classical dissociation due to right temporoparietal lesion.
answer_key.etiology: Right temporoparietal ischemic stroke (involving temporal and parietal lobes, insula, and subcortical structures)
answer_key.next_management_step: Further neuropsychological assessment to specify cognitive deficits and guide rehabilitation; consider cognitive training for visuospatial planning.

normal memory and abstract reasoning
no neglect
deficit on ToL-16 (visuospatial planning task)
