challenge_prompt: A 39-year-old woman with a history of multiple sclerosis (diagnosed in 2008 after onset of visual acuity disturbance in 2005) presents for evaluation. She was initially treated with natalizumab (stopped after 30 infusions due to JCV positivity), then dimethyl fumarate (8 months, stopped due to disease activity), then fingolimod (partial response, worsening EDSS), and finally alemtuzumab (started 2016, second cycle 2017). challenge_prompt:
A 52-year-old female with a past medical history of spinocerebellar ataxia, hyperthyroidism, and depression presents with 2–3 weeks of hallucinations. She has been taking amantadine 300 mg daily for 2.5 years. Vital signs: heart rate 104 bpm. On exam, she is alert and answering questions appropriately. Admission labs: BUN 76 mg/dL, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (baseline 0.8 mg/dL), COVID-19 positive. Complete blood count, urinalysis, thyroid stimulating hormone, serum ethanol level, and head CT are unremarkable. Serum amantadine level is 1505 ng/dL (therapeutic range 200-1000 ng/mL; toxicity >2000 ng/mL). Neurology suspects amantadine toxicity and recommends a 3-day taper, which is shortened to 2 days by the primary team. The patient continues to have hallucinations with concurrent agitation. EEG shows background slowing consistent with encephalopathy and no epileptiform activity. Lumbar puncture studies are normal. After 3 days, hallucinations resolve but the patient becomes increasingly somnolent and disoriented. Five days into hospitalization, she is no longer consistently speaking. Two weeks into hospitalization, she requires assistance with feeding and is not reliably following commands. The possibility of COVID-19 encephalopathy is raised, and a repeat lumbar puncture is considered. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely etiology of the patient's current encephalopathy, and what is the next most appropriate management step?

Prior to this, the patient was resumed on 200 mg of oral amantadine. Within 2 days she was alert, speaking, and able to follow commands.

answer_key.diagnosis: Amantadine withdrawal syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Rapid withdrawal of amantadine after prolonged use
answer_key.next_management_step: Resume amantadine (e.g., 200 mg orally daily) and monitor for clinical improvement

challenge_prompt:
A 65-year-old woman presents with a 3-year history of slowly progressive speech difficulties (word-finding, comprehension), memory dysfunction, and concentration deficits. She reports feeling stressed when speaking, stagnant speech, and mild depressive symptoms (BDI-II score 17). Her husband notes social withdrawal, reduced drive, and rarely aggressive behavior. Neurological examination is normal. Neuropsychological testing shows impairments in verbal fluency, processing speed, cognitive flexibility, working memory span, and verbal memory; deficits are consistent with mild dementia affecting multiple domains, but reported speech difficulties are not objectively confirmed on testing. Brain MRI reveals enlarged lateral ventricles without clear frontotemporal atrophy, T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the basal ganglia (small vascular lesions), and left periventricular cerebral microangiopathy. CSF analysis shows no pleocytosis, elevated phosphorylated tau protein 181 (ptau181: 65 pg/ml, pathological >61 pg/ml), and an elevated herpes simplex virus antibody index (2.6, pathological >1.5) indicating prior infection. No viral or bacterial infections (including lues and borreliosis) are detected. Serum autoantibody testing (immunoblot, immunofluorescence, cell-based assays) is negative for paraneoplastic antibodies (GAD65, Zic4, TR, SOX1, Ma1, Ma2, Amphiphysin, CV2, Ri, Yo, HuD) and for neural autoantibodies (NMDAR, LGI1, AMPAR1, GABAB1/2, AMPAR2, DPPX, CASPR2, Aquaporin 4), but positive for anti-flotillin 1/2 autoantibodies at a titer of 1:32. No CSF anti-flotillin antibodies are detected. Vitamin deficiencies and metabolic disorders are ruled out.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis (etiology) and the most appropriate next management step?

challenge_prompt:
A 51-year-old man with a 16-year history of Parkinson's disease (PD) presented with severe motor fluctuations and off symptoms for more than 2 hours per day despite medication. He was on levodopa/carbidopa 800 mg 8 times daily, pramipexole 1.5 mg, zonisamide 25 mg, and istradefylline 20 mg. Cognitive function was normal (MOCA 26, FAB 17) with no psychiatric symptoms (NPI 10, BDI 29). MDS-UPDRS motor score was 38 in the off-state (12-hour drug withdrawal) and 4 in the on-state (1 hour after 1.5 times usual levodopa dose). He underwent STN-DBS implantation. Four days postoperatively, bipolar LFP recordings from contacts 1-3 and 9-11 in the STN showed a beta oscillation at 16.6 Hz (amplitude 1.07 μVp) in the off-state. Conventional DBS was initiated with final settings: 2(-) C(+), 130 Hz, 60 μs, 2.2 mA bilaterally. His levodopa equivalent daily dose was reduced from 950 to 550 mg. One month post-surgery, re-evaluation showed: MDS-UPDRS motor score 27 in med-off/stim-off state; LFP intensity 1.20 μVp (left) and 1.72 μVp (right) in med-off/stim-off; and MDS-UPDRS motor score 22 in med-off/stim-on state with LFP intensity 1.24 μVp (left) and 1.00 μVp (right). The patient still experienced motor fluctuations (score 3 points during conventional DBS outside hospital). Beta band oscillations in the STN are known to correlate with motor symptom severity.

Based on the clinical and neurophysiological data, what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Parkinson's disease with motor fluctuations
answer_key.etiology: Idiopathic Parkinson's disease
answer_key.next_management_step: Implementation of adaptive deep brain stimulation (aDBS) with real-time beta band LFP monitoring to adjust stimulation amplitude

challenge_prompt: A 63-year-old woman presents with coma, fever, headache, vomiting, and seizures. On examination, she is comatose (GCS 6), febrile, hypotensive (BP 94 mmHg), tachypneic (RR 24/min), and has cold limbs and dry skin. Her qSOFA score is 3. She has a history of subarachnoid hemorrhage with a ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt installed over 20 years ago, which has been non-functional for at least 10 years due to tube breakage. She is on antihypertensives. Initial labs show: WBC 24,800/μL, CRP 12.5 mg/dL, total bilirubin 1.6 mg/dL, creatinine 4.8 mg/dL, potassium 6.3 mEq/L, glucose 551 mg/dL, platelets 255×10^3/μL, PT ratio 1.1, antithrombin 102%. Arterial blood gas (on O2 6L/min): PaO2 78.4 mmHg, PaO2/FiO2 156.8, lactate 1.1 mmol/L. CSF analysis shows elevated total protein. Chest X-ray shows mild pulmonary edema but no pneumonia. Brain CT shows mild hydrocephalus and a broken VP shunt, no hemorrhage or mass. Brain MRI shows old microinfarctions, no acute infarction or inflammation. Blood and CSF cultures grow Enterococcus faecalis; sputum and urine cultures are negative. She is started on IV crystalloid, norepinephrine, oxygen, ceftriaxone, and levetiracetam. Seizures stop after antiepileptic treatment. CSF orexin level measured 2 days after admission is 187.3 pg/mL (normal range 250-500 pg/mL). Blood orexin is detectable at very low levels (below 40 pg/mL) on admission and during ICU stay, but becomes undetectable after recovery. The focus of infection remains unknown.

answer_key.etiology: Systemic inflammation leading to blood-brain barrier dysfunction and reduced CSF orexin levels
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider experimental orexin replacement therapy or further study of orexin dynamics in sepsis-associated encephalopathy

challenge_prompt:
A 27-year-old male presents with a chronic motor and sensory complete C8 spinal cord injury (SCI) sustained from a fall 7 years ago. He has persistent orthostatic hypotension and lower extremity spasticity. As part of an experimental clinical trial, he underwent percutaneous implantation of two eight-electrode spinal cord epidural stimulation (SCES) leads positioned between T11 and L1 to cover the lumbosacral enlargement. Six months after permanent implantation, SCES mapping identified a configuration (cathode −0, anode +3, frequency 25 Hz, pulse duration 250 μs, amplitude 3 mA) that produced rhythmic locomotor-like electromyographic activity in supine. This configuration was subsequently used during exoskeleton-assisted walking (EAW) training sessions (3×/week) along with task-specific exercises. After 4 weeks of EAW training, the patient self-reported reduced symptoms of low blood pressure and decreased spasticity when using the SCES configuration outside of training. An interim assessment was performed to evaluate these effects.

At the first assessment (15 weeks before the second), a 45-degree head-up tilt test was conducted. Without SCES, supine systolic blood pressure (SBP) averaged 101.8 mmHg and dropped rapidly upon tilt, requiring early termination. With SCES at 3 mA, supine SBP increased to ~122 mmHg in minute one but still dropped with tilt; increasing to 5 mA stabilized SBP at ~111.5 mmHg and yielded the highest low-frequency (LF) and high-frequency (HF) heart rate variability (HRV) components. At the second assessment (15 weeks later), supine SBP without SCES averaged 98.9 mmHg. With SCES at 3 mA, supine SBP rose to ~140 mmHg in minute one, prompting a reduction to 2 mA. At 2 mA, SBP decreased over 4 minutes of tilt; at 3 mA, SBP stabilized at ~93.2 mmHg. Tonic, non-rhythmic lower extremity muscle activity was observed at stimulation amplitudes ≥3 mA. Spasticity was quantified using an isokinetic dynamometer: passive torque-time integral at knee flexors and extensors across multiple angular velocities decreased with SCES on (3 mA) compared to off—knee flexor by 5.53% and knee extensor by 4.95%.

Based on the clinical presentation and test results, what is the most likely explanation for the patient's improvement in blood pressure and spasticity, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Chronic motor and sensory complete C8 spinal cord injury with orthostatic hypotension and spasticity
answer_key.etiology: Beneficial off-target effects of a spinal cord epidural stimulation (SCES) configuration originally intended for exoskeleton-assisted walking on cardiovascular autonomic function and spasticity
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue using the same SCES configuration (cathode −0, anode +3, frequency 25 Hz, pulse duration 250 μs, amplitude 3 mA) during exoskeleton-assisted walking training and daily life to maintain improvements in blood pressure and spasticity; no new intervention is indicated.

LF and HF HRV changes during tilt
No other interventions for hypotension or spasticity mentioned
Patient's perspective: stimulator helps with spasms and blood pressure

Head-up tilt: SCES off caused rapid SBP drop; SCES on (3-5 mA) increased supine SBP and improved tilt stability
At second assessment, SCES 3 mA produced larger supine SBP increase and stabilized tilt SBP
Isokinetic dynamometer: passive torque decreased with SCES on (knee flexor −5.53%, extensor −4.95%)

challenge_prompt: A 71-year-old right-handed Japanese male presents 9 months after a left middle cerebral artery stroke. He was treated conservatively (thrombectomy/thrombolysis not available) and started speech-language-hearing therapy (ST) 7 days post-onset, which continued on an outpatient basis after discharge at 2 months. However, there has been little improvement in speech and language function. He now has good awareness and no paralysis. Cognitive FIM score is 31/35 (communication comprehension 6, expression 4; social interaction 7, problem solving 7, memory 7). Brain MRI (T1-weighted) shows low signal regions from the left temporal lobe to supramarginal gyrus and inferior parietal lobule. Standard Language Test of Aphasia (SLTA) reveals greater language loss in speaking and writing compared with aural and reading comprehension. His utterance is fluent in short sentences; no anarthria, but phonemic paraphasia and neologisms are present. He has phonological spelling errors in writing. Raven’s Colored Progressive Matrices score is 26/36 (average for his age 26.9). He has had little improvement with prior ST. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate management step?

challenge_prompt:
A 31-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS) presents with dizziness, headache, and recent memory decline for one month. She was diagnosed with clinically isolated syndrome (CIS) in January 2022 after optic neuritis, and subsequently developed RRMS in June 2022. She received pulse steroids for the initial episodes but has not taken any disease-modifying therapy (DMT). In December 2022, she had a confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection with fever, headache, fatigue, and myalgia. On admission in February 2023, neurological exam shows reduced recent memory, MMSE 27/30. Lab results: peripheral blood B cell to lymphocyte ratio 31% (reference 5-18%); T cell and NK cell ratios normal; routine labs unremarkable; serum SARS-CoV-2 antibody positive (102.63 S/CO, IgM-). CSF analysis: positive oligoclonal bands (pattern II), elevated QAlb (6.26 x 10^-3) indicating blood-brain barrier damage, CSF SARS-CoV-2 IgG positive, IgM negative; CSF NGS (DNA+RNA) negative; CSF and serum negative for AQP4, MOG, GFAP, and MBP antibodies. MRI brain with contrast: multiple speckled, mass-like nodular lesions in right cerebellar hemisphere, left temporal lobe, left basal ganglia, periventricular white matter, right frontal lobe, and corpus callosum (genu and splenium); lesions show ring-like, nodular, and open-ring enhancement. Spinal cord MRI shows patchy lesions at C3-4 and C5-6. Imaging findings are consistent with demyelinating lesions but also raise concern for tumefactive lesions. The patient refuses brain biopsy.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Tumefactive multiple sclerosis (TMS) / tumefactive demyelinating lesions (TDLs) in a patient with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis, triggered by SARS-CoV-2 infection

challenge_prompt: A previously healthy 4-year-old boy presents with fever (39°C) that was difficult to treat. On day 4 of fever, he develops generalized tonic-clonic seizures that do not respond to diazepam. On admission, Glasgow Coma Scale score is 8, with eyeball floating and increased muscle tone. He is transferred to the intensive care unit. Lumbar puncture shows aseptic meningitis (18 cells/μL). Brain MRI shows acquired periventricular white matter changes in the frontal lobes. He is treated with valproic acid, midazolam, fentanyl, methylprednisolone 500 mg, and intravenous immunoglobulin 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days due to suspicion of autoimmune encephalitis. Despite these interventions, seizures continue, requiring intubation, barbiturate coma, and hypothermic therapy. Extensive workup for autoimmune, oncological, and genetic causes (including trio whole-exome sequencing) is negative. Electroencephalogram shows background slowing, prominent slow wave activity in the right temporal region, and multifocal epileptiform activity predominantly in both temporal regions. Brain MR spectroscopy indicates prior hypoxia. On day 16, rituximab (260 mg) is added. What is the most likely diagnosis?

answer_key.diagnosis: Febrile infection-related epilepsy syndrome (FIRES)
answer_key.etiology: Immune dysregulation triggered by febrile infection (unknown pathogen)
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider initiation of anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist) or ketogenic diet for seizure control

challenge_prompt: A 24-year-old Japanese man with Dravet syndrome (diagnosed at age 1.5 years) presents with aspiration pneumonia and fever (39.9°C). He has generalized onset clonic seizures triggered by hyperthermia. His birth history is unremarkable, and there is no family history of epilepsy. Current antiseizure medications (ASMs) include valproate 1800 mg/day, sodium bromide 3.8 g/day, and zonisamide 200 mg/day, which have been unchanged for >5 years. Developmental delay is present (limited speech, crawling, assisted feeding). Neurological exam shows motor aphasia, poor soft palate elevation, minimal tongue movement, but voluntary extremity movement; no tetraplegia, normal reflexes, no sensory deficits or cerebellar ataxia; difficulty maintaining sitting position. Laboratory findings: WBC 5200/mm³, CRP 0.76 mg/dL, valproic acid level 80.8 μg/mL. Chest CT confirms left lower lobe pneumonia. Sputum culture grows Haemophilus influenzae. Brain MRI normal. After admission, his temperature often ranges from low to high 37°C. Despite antibiotic treatment, generalized clonic seizures occur during fever. After fever resolves, seizure frequency returns to baseline (about once monthly). Over the next year, recurrent infections (pneumonia, urinary tract infections, skin infections) trigger seizures, and he develops status epilepticus (SE) requiring high-dose phenobarbital, midazolam, or thiopental. His body temperature frequently rises to ≥39°C for ~3 days with infections. Seizure frequency increases to once every 2–3 days, sometimes daily. Interictal EEG shows a mixture of sharp slow-wave complexes and sharp waves. Clobazam and levetiracetam are added but ineffective. Genetic testing reveals a frameshift mutation in SCN1A (c.2176+1G>A). His temperature is persistently in the 37°C range. The family does not agree to discontinue zonisamide. He continues to have SE each time hyperthermia occurs. Given this presentation, what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Dravet syndrome with recurrent status epilepticus triggered by hyperthermia
answer_key.etiology: SCN1A loss-of-function mutation causing NaV1.1 haploinsufficiency
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue zonisamide and initiate perampanel

challenge_prompt: A 15-year-old male presents with communication difficulties, speaking in single words or short sentences, and frequently repeating the last word spoken. He often reaches out to touch objects, stands up and wanders during conversations, and requires one-to-one support to remain on task. He does not initiate activities or conversations and is described as very passive and easy-going. He has no friends and spends time only with family. challenge_prompt: A 65-year-old woman presented with walking instability for 1 year, worsening over the past 5 months. She had unsteady walking without obvious triggers, weakness of both lower limbs (shuffling gait, difficulty lifting legs, falling easily), slurred speech, and frequent urination at night. MRI of the cranium showed hot cross bun sign and cerebellar atrophy. Neurological examination: clear consciousness, slightly vague speech, cranial nerves otherwise normal. Upper limb strength 5/5, lower limb strength 4+. Normal muscle tone, symmetrical active tendon reflexes, no pathological signs. Left-sided finger-nose test unstable and inaccurate; right side normal. Heel-knee-tibia test normal. Romberg sign positive. Poor straight-line walking. Proprioceptive and superficial sensation normal. Kernig's sign negative. She had hypertension for 1 year (on amlodipine, BP 110/80) and aortic sclerosis with cerebral ischemia for 5 months (on atorvastatin). She also took buspirone for ataxia without improvement and symptoms worsened. Previous pharmacologic treatments did not improve her symptoms.

challenge_prompt:
A 73-year-old female with an 8-year history of Parkinson's disease presents with worsening non-motor symptoms. Initially, she had bradykinesia in both lower limbs and tremors in the right upper limb, which were untreated. Over the years, she developed significant bradykinesia, hyposmia, and short-term memory impairment starting 4 years ago. She was prescribed entacapone and pramipexole with improvement. Two years ago, she began experiencing visual hallucinations, often talking to herself, occurring 3-4 times per week, which partially responded to medication adjustment. In the past 3 days, her symptoms worsened: resting tremors in both upper limbs and right lower limb, difficulty turning over at night, constipation, short-term memory impairment, depression, and anxiety. Her medication (levodopa/benserazide 1/4 tablet four times daily, entacapone 1 tablet daily, clozapine 1/4 tablet twice daily) provides relief lasting only three hours. She has lost confidence in medication efficacy. Head MRI shows increased iron deposition in the pallidum and putamen bilaterally. Plasma phosphorylated tau-181 and beta-amyloid 1-42 are normal. Her Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score is 16, Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD-24) is 14, Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale (HAMA) is 16, SCOPA-AUT score is 21, and PD Sleep Scale (PDSS) is 104. Resting-state EEG shows power spectral density fluctuation around 10 Hz. P300 latency is 352 ms and amplitude 4.1 μV.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and the next most appropriate management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Parkinson's disease with prominent non-motor symptoms (cognitive impairment, mood disorder, autonomic dysfunction, and psychosis)
answer_key.etiology: Neurodegeneration of dopaminergic neurons with cortical and limbic involvement

challenge_prompt:
A 56-year-old Vietnamese man with hypertension presented with moderate to severe headache, low-grade fever, dizziness, and postprandial vomiting for 5 days. He was initially diagnosed with sinusitis and treated with cefpodoxime, but symptoms worsened. On admission, he had neck stiffness, no focal neurological deficits, and was alert. Initial MRI showed maxillary sinus mucosal thickening with no hemorrhage. Labs: WBC 12.3 K/μL (82% neutrophils), CRP 12.3 mg/L, procalcitonin 0.02 ng/mL. CSF: cloudy, 1615 cells/μL (65% neutrophils, 30% lymphocytes), protein 1.54 g/L, glucose 1.49 mmol/L (blood glucose 5.91 mmol/L), lactate 5.62 mmol/L. CSF GeneXpert Ultra for M. tuberculosis and Gram stain negative. Chest X-ray normal. He was started on ceftriaxone 2 g BID, vancomycin 1 g BID, and dexamethasone. On day 9, WBC 20 K/μL (92.6% neutrophils), repeat LP: 521 cells/μL (lymphocyte predominant), GeneXpert Ultra negative, CXR normal. Dexamethasone stopped after 4 days, then restarted due to cognitive decline. On day 12, WBC 27.5 K/μL (83.7% neutrophils), meropenem 2 g TID added. Repeat MRI showed hydrocephalus and lacunar infarct at left caudate. He was transferred to a tertiary hospital on day 14. On admission, GCS 14, temperature 37.5°C, BP 160/90, oral thrush noted. Labs: WBC 26.44 K/μL (92.3% neutrophils), sodium 106 mmol/L. LP: cloudy CSF, 22,518 cells/μL (81% neutrophils, 19% lymphocytes), RBC 2000/μL, protein 1.72 g/L, glucose 0.85 mmol/L (blood 6.35 mmol/L), lactate 5.28 mmol/L. CSF Gram stain, India ink, Cryptococcal antigen, bacterial and fungal cultures negative. He continued meropenem and hypertonic saline. On day 16, WBC 20.87 K/μL (85.5% neutrophils), sodium 120 mmol/L. Fourth LP: 2929 cells/μL (77% neutrophils, 23% lymphocytes), protein 2.21 g/L, glucose 1.91 mmol/L (blood 5.82 mmol/L), lactate 5.28 mmol/L. CSF Ziehl-Neelsen smear showed 1 acid-fast bacilli per 100 fields, but GeneXpert Ultra was negative. HIV serology negative. Blood cultures negative. What is the most likely diagnosis and the next best diagnostic step?

CSF culture grew Mycobacterium avium complex after 6 weeks.

challenge_prompt:
A 57-year-old male presented with low back pain for one week without apparent cause. Pain worsened with prolonged sitting, and he had difficulty turning over. He experienced numbness and weakness in both lower limbs and sensory numbness in the saddle area. Physical examination revealed deep tenderness at the L3-L4 and L4-L5 interspinous spaces, with radiating pain to both lower limbs (VAS 6). Both lower limbs tested positive for straight leg raise and strengthening test at 30°. There was sensory numbness in the posterior lateral aspects of both legs and the lateral aspect of the foot. Muscle strength of bilateral tibialis anterior, thumb dorsiflexor, and toe flexor was grade 0/5. Lumbar MRI showed bulging and central protrusion of the L3-L4 intervertebral disc, spinal stenosis, cauda equina nerve compression, and similar changes at L2-L3, L4-L5, and L5-S1. Decompression and nucleus pulposus removal at L3-L4 and L4-L5 were performed. One week after surgery, the patient's symptoms did not improve.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis? What next management step is most appropriate?

challenge_prompt:
A 12-year-old girl with a history of epilepsy for 12 years, intellectual disability for 10 years, and autism for 5 years is scheduled for scoliosis surgery. She has been on a ketogenic diet (KD) for 10 years (daily intake 220 g, total energy 1430 kcal, high fat, low carbohydrate). On examination, she has a right thoracic scoliosis with Cobb angle 55° (severe). Preoperative laboratory tests are normal. A multidisciplinary team is involved. The patient's mother is trained for intraoperative wake-up test. Fasting blood ketone level is 0.1 mmol/L before bedtime the day before surgery and 0.7 mmol/L at 6 am on the day of surgery (normal 0.03–0.30). Oral KD is taken at 6 am on the day of surgery. The surgery requires intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring (IONM). Propofol is a common total intravenous anesthetic for IONM but contains a large amount of carbohydrates, which may disrupt ketosis and lower seizure threshold. The goal is to maintain ketosis and avoid perioperative seizures.

What is the most appropriate anesthetic management for this patient?

We used remimazolam, a novel ultra-short-acting benzodiazepine, for induction of anesthesia to reduce the risk of seizures.

challenge_prompt: A 50-year-old male was transferred after a motor vehicle accident. On admission, he had impaired consciousness, incoherent responses, active hemorrhage from the right external auditory canal, nausea, and vomiting. GCS was 13-14. Emergency CT showed: right temporal bone fracture with ipsilateral small epidural hematoma; left frontotemporal subdural hematoma; adjacent small left frontal intraparenchymal hematoma; subarachnoid hemorrhage; minimal intracranial pneumocephalus. No surgical indication, so managed conservatively with intensive neuro-monitoring. Serial CT over 5 days showed persistent compression of the left lateral ventricle, midline shift to the right, and bilateral frontotemporal soft tissue swelling. GCS remained stable at 13. On the morning of day 6, repeat CT showed stable intracranial pathology. Pupils were reactive bilaterally. At 2:40 AM on day 7, acute deterioration occurred: sudden loss of consciousness (GCS 6), anisocoria (left pupil 5 mm, right 4 mm, bilateral light reflex absent), and inability to respond to motor commands. Intravenous mannitol was given, and emergency CT was performed. The CT showed compression of the left lateral ventricle with rightward midline shift, effacement of the suprasellar and perimesencephalic cisterns, and marked cerebral edema. Preoperative consultation was completed at 3:11 AM.

answer_key.etiology: Delayed progression of traumatic intracranial hemorrhages and cerebral edema

challenge_prompt: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 17-month history of progressive gait instability and dysarthria. Symptoms began with sudden right lower limb weakness, gait stiffness, and unsteadiness, later involving upper limbs with intention tremor and scanning speech. Initial brain MRI showed bilateral periventricular hyperintensities. CSF analysis revealed mild pleocytosis and positive oligoclonal bands. Transient positivity for HSV-1 and CMV was detected, but no clinical signs of encephalitis. Extensive workup including viral panels, paraneoplastic antibodies, autoimmune encephalitis antibodies, EEG, and EMG was unremarkable. Autoimmune panel, metabolic screening, and genetic testing were normal. She received IVIG with no benefit, then high-dose methylprednisolone pulse therapy tapered to oral prednisone with acyclovir, resulting in partial symptom relief. Follow-up MRIs showed bilateral pyramidal tract and centrum semiovale lesions and progressive cerebellar atrophy. She was started on mycophenolate mofetil and prednisone, which stabilized her condition but did not restore independent ambulation. Neurological exam at that time showed persistent ataxia: SARA score 20 (gait 6/8, stance 4/6, sitting 2/4, speech 3/6, finger chase 1/4, nose-finger 1/4, fast alternating movements 1/4, heel-shin 2/4). Based on the presentation and workup, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next best management step?

challenge_prompt: A 46-year-old woman suddenly experienced dizziness during sleep. The next day, she developed weakness in her right limb, slurred speech, and difficulty swallowing. She was taken to a hospital where a head CT scan revealed an infarction in the left basal ganglia. She has a history of hypertension. challenge_prompt:
A 47-year-old woman with a 2-year history of Meige syndrome (blepharospasms, oromandibular and neck dystonia) underwent bilateral subthalamic nucleus deep brain stimulation (STN-DBS). Preoperatively, she had no response to oral medications (benzhexol, clonazepam) and only a partial response to botulinum toxin injections. The Burke–Fahn–Marsden Dystonia Rating Scale (BFMDRS) total score was 29 (motor subscore 26, disability subscore 3). One month after surgery, initial programming used monopolar stimulation of contacts 3 (left STN) and 7 (right STN) at 1.3 V, pulse width 90 μs, frequency 130 Hz. Within half an hour, she developed slight stimulation-induced dyskinesia (SID) in the right arm. Over the next month, blepharospasms improved significantly and oromandibular dystonia partially, but SID progressed to rapid jerks in the right arm, right leg, left leg, and trunk. Stimulation was switched to monopolar stimulation of more dorsal contacts 4 (left) and 8 (right) at 3.6 V. This led to complete resolution of symptoms for 1 week, after which symptoms (blepharospasms, oromandibular and cervical dystonia) reappeared. Voltage was increased to 3.8 V, which improved blepharospasms but only slightly improved oromandibular and cervical dystonia; no SID was observed. Returning to contacts 3 and 7 at 1.3 V, with a gradual increase to 1.5 V over 2 months, produced severe SID and less symptom improvement than before. Interleaved stimulation caused unbearable dizziness and gait disability. Bipolar and double monopolar stimulation failed to achieve a balance between symptom control and SID. The patient disagreed with a plan to start at a voltage without SID and slowly increase, because she experienced minimal symptom improvement at sub-SID levels. Twenty-five months after surgery, the patient still experiences SID when using ventral contacts (3 and 7) and has inadequate symptom control with dorsal contacts (4 and 8) alone.

What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Stimulation-induced dyskinesia (SID) secondary to STN-DBS
answer_key.etiology: Subthalamic nucleus deep brain stimulation
answer_key.next_management_step: Timed stimulation programming with alternating ventral (contacts 3 and 7) and dorsal (contacts 4 and 8) contacts, gradually transitioning to full-time ventral contact stimulation
optional_findings: No response to benzhexol or clonazepam
SID progressed to all limbs and trunk

challenge_prompt: A 33-year-old male presents with a history of motor tics starting at age 4, followed by phonic tics. Tics peaked between ages 10-15, then subsided until age 23, when they recurred and have been continuously disabling despite multiple medication trials. Current medications include haloperidol 0.5 mg twice daily, biperiden 2 mg twice daily, zolpidem 10 mg as needed, diazepam 5 mg as needed, and vortioxetine 10 mg daily. Tics manifest as fast, dystonic twisting of neck and abdominal muscles, gagging, coughing, and have caused a rib fracture. He can suppress tics for at most 20 seconds. Previous trials: risperidone (weight gain), tetrabenazine (nausea), paroxetine, mirtazapine. He has undergone Habit Reversal Training with no benefit. Preoperative Yale Global Tic Severity Scale (YGTSS) score is 71 (motor 17, phonic 14, impairment 40). What is the most likely diagnosis and the next most appropriate management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Gilles de la Tourette syndrome (GTS) with severe refractory tics
answer_key.etiology: Neurodevelopmental disorder with disturbed associative/limbic basal ganglia circuits
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider deep brain stimulation (DBS) targeting the anteromedial globus pallidus internus (amGPi)

challenge_prompt: A 16-year-old boy (height 148 cm, weight 24 kg) with a history of Leigh syndrome (diagnosed at 7 months after presenting with poor head control and failure to roll over) is scheduled for posterior spinal fusion from T3 to the pelvis for severe neuromuscular scoliosis. His regular medications include levocarnitine, ubidecarenone, triclofos sodium, clonazepam, perampanel hydrate, levetiracetam, and vitamins. Preoperative evaluation shows an ASA physical status III, fluctuating consciousness but able to communicate with finger movements, contractures of left elbow and right knee, and no paralysis. Preoperative chest radiograph shows severe thoracic deformity and reduced thoracic volume. Cobb angle is 76° and pelvic tilt is 38°. There is no family history of malignant hyperthermia. Routine preoperative laboratory studies are unremarkable. Intraoperative motor evoked potential (MEP) monitoring is planned. Considering the risks of volatile anesthetics (potential malignant hyperthermia and lactic acidosis in mitochondrial disorders) and propofol (risk of propofol infusion syndrome in mitochondrial disease), and the need to preserve MEP signals while avoiding delayed emergence due to limited respiratory reserve, what is the most appropriate intravenous anesthetic agent for induction and maintenance of anesthesia in this patient?

answer_key.diagnosis: Leigh syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Mitochondrial disorder (complex I/IV deficiency)
answer_key.next_management_step: Remimazolam-based anesthesia (with flumazenil reversal available)

challenge_prompt: A 53-year-old male with a history of uremia secondary to diabetic nephropathy on maintenance hemodialysis presents with gait ataxia, dysarthria, and mild memory impairment. He has had significant weight loss (>10 kg over 3 months) and decreased food intake. He has no history of alcohol use. MRI brain shows bilateral symmetrical hyperintensities in the basal ganglia on T2/FLAIR sequences, forming a lentiform fork sign. Laboratory studies show serum thiamin level 0.97 ng/mL (reference 1.7-10 ng/mL). Blood glucose is well-controlled (HbA1c 5.9%). He is not taking metformin. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Wernicke encephalopathy
answer_key.etiology: Thiamin deficiency
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose thiamin supplementation (e.g., 500 mg intravenously three times daily for 2 days, followed by 250 mg daily), and continue hemodialysis as scheduled.

challenge_prompt: A 63-year-old woman with cryptogenic cirrhosis complicated by hepatocellular carcinoma, CMV viremia, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, Sjogren's syndrome, and sarcoidosis presents with several months of intermittent confusion, generalized weakness, and progressive dysphagia. She has been on azathioprine 50 mg daily and prednisone 20-40 mg daily for approximately 15 years for sarcoidosis and Sjogren's syndrome. On examination, she is afebrile, hemodynamically stable, oriented only to self. Suprapubic tenderness is noted. Labs: MELD score 20. Urinalysis shows 3+ bacteria. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis shows cirrhosis with stable 4.1 cm hepatic lesion, mild compression deformities of T11-L1. EGD shows grade I esophageal varices, portal hypertensive gastropathy, and CMV-negative esophageal ulcers. Initially, hepatic encephalopathy due to cystitis is suspected, but she fails to improve after treatment with ceftriaxone followed by doxycycline for VRE in urine, and after lactulose/rifaximin. Repeat neurologic exam reveals left upper extremity neglect and 1/5 grip strength. MRI brain shows confluent white matter lesions on FLAIR throughout hemispheres, more focal in right frontal region, with bright signal on DWI without restricted diffusion, and no intracranial enhancement. Cervical spine MRI is normal. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and the next best diagnostic step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML)
answer_key.etiology: JC virus infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Lumbar puncture for CSF JC virus PCR

challenge_prompt: A 57-year-old female with a BMI of 27 kg/m² and a history of glaucoma and epilepsy presented for elective dental surgery under general anesthesia. Physical examination revealed a port-wine stain covering >75% of the face, large oral angiomas involving the lips and buccal mucosa with severe edema and lip protrusion, macroglossia, and facial hypertrophy. Preoperative vitals and oxygen saturation were normal. There were no signs or history of increased intracranial pressure. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what are the key anesthetic considerations for this patient?

answer_key.diagnosis: Sturge-Weber syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Sporadic mutation in GNAQ gene (less commonly GNA11)

Continue antiepileptics perioperatively, monitor for seizures, and manage intracranial and intraocular pressures.

challenge_prompt:
A 45-year-old female with a history of advanced cerebellar atrophy and ataxia due to involvement of the spinocerebellar arteries was brought to the institute after a fall at home. She is dependent on a walker for ambulation. Examination reveals a right femoral shaft fracture. The fracture is internally fixed with a plate. Postoperatively, the patient is placed on a nonweight-bearing status for 3 months to allow fracture healing. Early rehabilitation is started within 2 weeks of surgery with knee range-of-motion exercises. The rehabilitation is carefully monitored because of ataxia and balance problems.

Based on this presentation and initial management, what is the most appropriate next step in this patient's postoperative management?

challenge_prompt: A 30-week gestational age fetus was evaluated with prenatal ultrasound and non-contrast fetal MRI. Imaging revealed a large omphalocele measuring approximately 5.5 × 4 × 6.6 cm containing the liver, multiple bowel loops, and possibly the gallbladder. The urinary bladder and external genitalia were not visualized. There was severe pelvic dysplasia. A large covered myelomeningocele extending from L5 to S5 was noted, involving neural tissue with splaying of posterior elements. Bilateral clubfeet were present. The brain appeared normal without Chiari II malformation. The right kidney measured 3.7 cm; the left kidney was not clearly identified. Fetal genetic testing (SNP chromosomal microarray) showed no copy number abnormalities. The infant was delivered via cesarean at 37 weeks 1 day with Apgar scores of 4 at 1 minute and 9 at 5 minutes, requiring intubation for respiratory distress. Birth weight was 3290 g. Postnatal examination confirmed a large omphalocele with exposed liver and bowel, bifid bladder plates (bladder exstrophy), imperforate anus, a lumbosacral cystic mass (myelomeningocele), and bilateral talipes equinovarus. Echocardiography showed a tiny mid-muscular ventricular septal defect and a moderate patent ductus arteriosus with bidirectional shunting, both hemodynamically insignificant. MRI of the spine confirmed the myelomeningocele without intracranial anomalies. Genetic testing reaffirmed normal findings. Based only on the presentation above, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Cloacal exstrophy variant (OEIS complex: Omphalocele, Exstrophy of the Cloaca, Imperforate Anus, and Spinal Defects)
answer_key.etiology: Congenital malformation sequence of unknown cause, likely related to early embryonic developmental field defect.
answer_key.next_management_step: Staged multidisciplinary reconstructive surgery: initial neonatal stabilization, omphalocele repair with reduction of viscera and creation of colostomy, followed by sequential myelomeningocele repair, pelvic osteotomies, and definitive bladder and abdominal wall closure.

Solitary right kidney (left kidney not clearly identified)
Normal fetal SNP microarray

title: Multistage Reconstruction of a Severe Cloacal Exstrophy Variant With Associated Myelomeningocele and Multisystem Involvement: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 10-year-old female, born full-term to non-consanguineous parents after an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery, presented with a history of global developmental delay. She rolled at 6 months, sat at 8 months, walked independently at 14 months with a waddling, wide-based gait, and climbed stairs with support at 18 months; she never ran. First words appeared at 27 months, first sentences at 36 months, and asking questions at 42 months. At age 17 months, she was evaluated for waddling gait and delayed language; coarse facial features, short neck, wide rib cage, umbilical hernia, and subtle hepatomegaly were noted. Muscle tone and strength were normal initially. Subsequently, she experienced neurodevelopmental regression with worsening ataxia and decreased muscle tone and strength. By age 5, ambulation required hand support, and by age 6, she lost the ability to walk. Severe kyphoscoliosis developed, requiring surgical correction at age 8. At age 9, she lost the ability to point, speak, sit independently, and roll. She had recurrent respiratory infections. On readmission at age 9, she was undernourished (weight 14 kg, BMI <3rd centile), requiring gastrostomy. Chronic respiratory failure and apneic episodes led to tracheostomy and intermittent ventilatory support. Examination revealed kyphoscoliosis, multiple contractures, dystrophic teeth, generalized muscle hypotrophy with no active movements, limited passive ankle mobility, generalized deep tendon hypo-areflexia, negative Babinski and Rossolimo signs, left exotropia, and a hyperactive oculocephalic reflex. Brain MRI at age 6 showed cerebellar atrophy. Blood gas analysis showed hypoxia (pO2 55-77 mmHg) with respiratory alkalosis or acidosis; lactate was infrequently mildly elevated (2.2-6 mmol/L) only during acute episodes. Cardiac and hepatic function were normal. A Mito Stress Test on peripheral blood mononuclear cells at age 9 revealed severely impaired mitochondrial function: maximal respiration 28.77 pmol/min (healthy controls 52.36, p=0.02), spare respiratory capacity 107% (controls 261%), and reduced extracellular acidification rate. What is the most likely diagnosis?

Genetic testing revealed a de novo heterozygous CTBP1 mutation (c.991C>T).

answer_key.diagnosis: Hypotonia, Ataxia, Developmental Delay, Tooth enamel defect Syndrome (HADDTS) caused by CTBP1 mutation
answer_key.etiology: CTBP1 c.991C>T (p.Arg331Trp) de novo heterozygous mutation
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate supportive care and consider mitochondrial-targeted nutritional supplementation (vitamin C, riboflavin, vitamin E, coenzyme Q10) and genetic counseling
required_findings: dystrophic teeth
cerebellar atrophy on MRI
global developmental delay and regression
hypotonia
ataxia
fine motor and speech delays
wide-based gait
progressive loss of skills

title: A Rare CTBP1-Related Neurodevelopmental Disorder Is Associated with Impaired Mitochondrial Bioenergetics: A Functional Case Report

challenge_prompt: A female in her early 50s with a five-year history of palmoplantar pustulosis (PPP) presents with persistent pustules, thick scaling, and fissures localized to the right plantar surface. Pain and discomfort significantly impair daily activities. No other areas are affected. Prior treatments include topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone, clobetasol), crisaborole, ruxolitinib cream, halobetasol/tazarotene, apremilast, acitretin (discontinued due to worsening joint pain and multiple sclerosis [MS] symptoms), narrowband UVB phototherapy, and biologics: secukinumab, certolizumab pegol, ixekizumab, and dupilumab. Medical history is notable for MS well-controlled on ofatumumab, tinea pedis (treated with terbinafine), alcohol use (<1 drink/day), active daily tobacco use, and amoxicillin allergy. Examination reveals thick hyperkeratotic plaques and subcutaneous purulent collections on the right plantar surface; no hand or other involvement. Body surface area (BSA) estimated at 2-3%; Psoriasis Physician Global Assessment (PGA) score 3.0. Laboratory studies (CBC, CMP, QuantiFERON Gold) are within normal limits. Given the refractory nature of the disease and prior treatment failures, what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.etiology: Chronic inflammatory dermatosis with dysregulation of IL-17 pathway, specifically IL-17A and IL-17F

challenge_prompt: A 21-year-old male professional rugby player (flank position, 190 cm, 104.5 kg) with a history of four confirmed concussions sustained over 3 years. The most recent concussion occurred 13 months prior to evaluation from a direct knee-to-head contact to the right temporal region during a tackle. After the injury, he was advised to refrain from contact sport for approximately 6 months and underwent cognitive and physical rehabilitation, including memory and balance training. He has since returned to competition but expresses concerns about future health and is considering ending his rugby career. At testing, he reports mild depressive symptoms: little interest or pleasure in doing things, feeling down, trouble sleeping, fatigue, and feeling bad about himself (several days over 2 weeks). He also reports mild anxiety: feeling nervous, not being able to stop worrying, worrying too much, and becoming easily annoyed (several days). The Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score is normal. The Balance Error Scoring System (BESS) was performed three times with six trials each (double, single, and tandem stances on firm and foam surfaces, eyes closed). Both subjective error scores and objective center of pressure measurements (via lumbar accelerometer) were within normal ranges compared to normative data. During the BESS, a 64-lead electroencephalogram (EEG) was recorded. After independent component analysis (ICA) and dipole source localization, significant dipoles were identified in the following brain regions: left inferior parietal (dipole 1), right posterior cingulate (dipole 2), left cerebellum (dipole 3), left precentral (dipole 4), right precentral (dipole 5), left supramarginal (dipole 6), left superior frontal (dipole 7), left occipital (dipole 8), right occipital (dipole 9), and left middle frontal (dipole 10). No structural imaging was performed.

answer_key.diagnosis: Persisting symptoms after concussion (PSaC) / Post-concussion syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Multiple sports-related concussions (repetitive head impacts)
answer_key.next_management_step: Comprehensive multidisciplinary management including graded exercise therapy, cognitive rehabilitation, vestibular and balance therapy, and screening/treatment for mood disorders (e.g., cognitive-behavioral therapy, pharmacotherapy if indicated). Serial monitoring of symptoms and functional outcomes.
optional_findings: Return to contact sport after rehabilitation
Concern about future health and potential retirement from rugby

Normal BESS subjective and objective balance scores compared to normative data

title: Brainwave Activity Localization, Mood Symptoms, and Balance Impairment in a Male South African Rugby Player With Persisting Symptoms After Concussion: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 47-year-old male Army and Navy veteran presents for evaluation. He has a history of multiple traumatic brain injuries (TBIs), with the most significant occurring in 2017 from a motor vehicle accident where his car collided with a semi-truck. He sustained a moderate-to-severe TBI (right frontal intraparenchymal hemorrhage, possible diffuse axonal injury, cerebral edema), open basal skull fracture with extension into the carotid canal, temporal bone, and right vertebral foramen, multiple facial fractures, rib fracture, mild anterior wedge deformities at C5-C6, bilateral lung contusions, and right hand injury. He was intubated, underwent surgical repair of facial fractures and tendon injury, and was hospitalized for 10 days. At discharge, he was awake, alert, oriented x4, ambulating, tolerating oral diet, and voiding. No inpatient rehabilitation. He was unable to return to work in IT.

Prior outpatient treatments: 5 sessions of speech-language pathology (SLP) for cognitive rehabilitation; 6 sessions of vestibular physical therapy (PT); 7 sessions of occupational therapy (OT) for hand pain, neck/upper back pain, and binocular vision dysfunction. He was treated with botulinum toxin injections for headaches (worsened), triptans (helpful but not refilled), duloxetine (not helpful), sertraline, buspirone, and monthly counseling. MRI brain (September 2021): few nonspecific tiny bifrontal white matter T2 hyperintensities. MRI cervical spine: mild spinal canal stenosis at C6/C7, moderate right neural foraminal stenosis at C6/C7.

Current chief complaints (as of evaluation in 2024): chronic daily headaches; severe migraines at least twice per week; insomnia; problems with attention, memory, processing, and executive function; severe depression with frequent passive suicidal ideation; post-traumatic stress; impaired balance; chronic musculoskeletal pain. He uses a single-point cane. Physical exam: tenderness to palpation over bilateral greater occipital, lesser occipital, auriculotemporal, supratrochlear, and supraorbital nerves; active range of motion of neck and lumbar spine limited by pain in all planes; tenderness over spinous processes of lower cervical and thoracic vertebrae; diffuse paraspinal muscle tenderness in cervical and thoracic spine.

What is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

answer_key.etiology: Traumatic brain injury from motor vehicle accident (2017)

MRI brain showing nonspecific tiny bifrontal white matter T2 hyperintensities
MRI cervical spine showing mild spinal canal stenosis at C6/C7

challenge_prompt: A 60-year-old man presents for consultation regarding further rehabilitation treatment. challenge_prompt:
A 59-year-old Chinese male with a 40-year smoking history presented with cough, productive sputum, and hemoptysis for 1 year. Chest and abdominal CT revealed a large right hilar and mediastinal mass with obstructive atelectasis, invasion of right pulmonary vessels with tumor thrombus, pericardial and pleural involvement, and extensive lymph node metastases (bilateral cervical, mediastinal, right hilar, right cardiophrenic, hepatogastric). Metastases were also noted in the right adrenal gland and liver. Brain MRI showed metastatic lesions in the right frontal lobe and posterior horn of the lateral ventricle. Bone scan showed left iliac crest metastasis. Bronchoscopic biopsy confirmed small cell lung cancer (SCLC) with immunohistochemistry: TIF(-), Ki-67 ~95%, Syn(+), CgA(+). The patient was diagnosed with extensive-stage SCLC and received 4 cycles of a PD-1 inhibitor combined with etoposide and carboplatin, achieving a partial response. After the fifth cycle, the patient developed dizziness, progressively worsening headaches, and bilateral lower limb weakness. Brain MRI showed slight enlargement of the right frontal lesion compared to prior scans. Given the small size of parenchymal lesions and the severity of symptoms disproportionate to MRI findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

Pathological examination of the CSF confirmed small cell carcinoma.

answer_key.diagnosis: Leptomeningeal metastasis (LM) from small cell lung cancer
answer_key.etiology: Small cell lung cancer with spread to leptomeninges
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF cytology and next-generation sequencing (NGS) of CSF to confirm leptomeningeal metastasis and assess genetic alterations

Multiple metastases (lymph nodes, adrenal, liver, bone)

challenge_prompt:
A 25-year-old woman presents with a history of chronic seizures only partially managed by antiepileptic drugs. Over the past few years, she has experienced progressive cognitive decline and insidious onset of right-sided weakness. No previous neuroimaging history is available. On examination, she has hyperreflexia, elevated muscular tone, and right-sided hemiparesis. Gait analysis shows a spastic pattern, and the right hand and foot have minor contractures. She has mild cognitive impairment with specific deficits in complex problem-solving and memory.

An MRI of the brain reveals left-sided cerebral hemiatrophy with ex-vacuo dilatation of the ipsilateral lateral ventricle. There is dilatation of the frontal and sphenoid sinuses, thickening of the ipsilateral calvaria, and hyperpneumatization of the mastoid air cells. The cerebellum, internal capsule, and right basal ganglia appear intact. There is no aberrant post-contrast enhancement, ruling out active inflammatory or neoplastic processes.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate management step?

answer_key.etiology: Likely congenital or acquired due to early-life brain insult (e.g., intrauterine vascular insult, trauma, infection, ischemic stroke).
answer_key.next_management_step: Multidisciplinary management: neurological follow-up, physiotherapy, adjustment of antiepileptic medication, and long-term surveillance.

deficits in complex problem-solving and memory
intact cerebellum, internal capsule, and right basal ganglia

challenge_prompt: A 65-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presents with fever, chills, fatigue, and altered mental status progressing over two days. Vital signs: temperature 101.8°F, other vitals normal. Exam: acutely ill, obtunded, noncommunicative, unable to follow commands, nuchal rigidity, no focal deficits. Labs: WBC 9100/μL (14% bands), platelets 78,000/μL, lactate 5.4 mmol/L, mild transaminitis, normal INR/fibrinogen. CT head: normal. Chest X-ray: normal. CSF: neutrophilic pleocytosis, hypoglycorrhachia, Gram-positive cocci in pairs and chains. Blood and CSF cultures grow pan-susceptible Streptococcus pneumoniae. Treated with IV dexamethasone and empiric ampicillin, vancomycin, cefepime, acyclovir, then de-escalated to ceftriaxone. Sepsis resolves after 48 hours, but mental status minimally improves. vEEG shows left frontal focal seizures with frequent left central sharp waves; levetiracetam given, seizures resolve but mental status unchanged. Repeat lumbar puncture on day 3: opening pressure 27.5 cmH2O, protein 208 mg/dL, glucose 41 mg/dL. MRI brain on day 5: diffuse areas of white matter contrast enhancement, vasogenic edema, and diffusion restriction, suggestive of a cerebral vasculitis within parietal and frontal lobes. CT angiogram of head/neck: diffuse white matter hypodensities, not sensitive for medium-to-small vessel changes. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Presumed cerebral vasculitis secondary to pneumococcal meningitis
answer_key.etiology: Streptococcus pneumoniae infection leading to immune-mediated vasculitis
answer_key.next_management_step: Administer high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g every 24 hours) and consider prolonged corticosteroid taper

challenge_prompt:
A 36-year-old female presented with 1 day of vague pain in the left eye. No significant past medical history. Initial examination: visual acuity right eye 1.2, left eye 1.0; intraocular pressures normal; fundoscopy showed left optic disc margin blurring. She was treated with topical levofloxacin. Two days later, she returned with left eye pain and vision loss for 2 days. Examination: visual acuity right eye 1.2, left eye 0.2; intraocular pressures normal; fundoscopy revealed left optic disc edema. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) showed left optic nerve edema; retinal and macular structure normal bilaterally. Visual evoked potentials (VEP) showed prolonged P100 latency and reduced amplitude in both eyes (right: 112 ms, 4.3 μV; left: 153 ms, 4.0 μV). Visual field testing showed left eye concentric constriction; right eye normal. Fundus fluorescein angiography (FFA) of the left eye showed intense optic nerve hyperfluorescence. Enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the orbits revealed patchy and striated enhancement in the left optic nerve and striated enhancement in the right optic nerve.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what confirmatory test should be ordered?

Serum aquaporin-4 (AQP4) IgG antibody testing was positive.

answer_key.diagnosis: Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD, AQP4-IgG positive neuromyelitis optica)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune, anti-aquaporin-4 antibody mediated
answer_key.next_management_step: Serum AQP4-IgG antibody testing via cell-based assay

challenge_prompt: A 55-year-old woman with chronic neuropathic pain is prescribed pregabalin 150 mg twice daily. She also takes oxycodone for breakthrough pain and diazepam for anxiety. After 6 months, she reports craving, withdrawal symptoms when doses are missed, and paranoid ideation. Pharmacovigilance data from Australia (2016 pregabalin case reports) show the most common adverse events are toxicity to various agents (10%), suicidal ideation (7%), and overdose (7%). Compared to all other drugs, pregabalin has reporting odds ratios (ROR) of 13.53 (95% CI 12.12-15.10) for drug abuse/dependence, 6.76 (95% CI 4.99-9.15) for drug withdrawal, 12.40 (95% CI 10.71-14.36) for suicide/self-injury, and 5.81 (95% CI 4.99-6.76) for psychosis/psychotic disorders. Compared to other neuropathic pain drugs (gabapentin, duloxetine, amitriptyline), pregabalin shows increased reporting for dependence (ROR 2.37, 95% CI 1.76-3.19 in single-suspected-medicine cases) and psychosis (ROR 1.44, 95% CI 1.10-1.90 in single-suspected-medicine cases), but not for withdrawal or suicidality. Based on these data, which adverse drug reaction pattern is most strongly associated with pregabalin, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Pregabalin-associated adverse drug reactions: drug dependence and psychosis
answer_key.etiology: Pregabalin use in a patient on concomitant opioids and benzodiazepines
answer_key.next_management_step: Gradually taper pregabalin to minimize withdrawal, discontinue or reduce opioids/benzodiazepines if possible, monitor for psychosis and suicidal ideation, consider switching to an alternative neuropathic pain agent (e.g., gabapentin, duloxetine, amitriptyline) with lower dependence risk, and refer to addiction or psychiatric services as needed.

challenge_prompt: A 45-year-old male presents with left eye ptosis, diplopia, and limited eye movement after a motor vehicle accident 1 month ago. He lost consciousness at the scene and was admitted to a hospital. Initial CT head showed bilateral temporal and frontal lobe contusions, subarachnoid hemorrhage, bilateral occipital and parietal subdural hematomas, pneumocephalus, left occipitoparietal bone fracture, and widening of the left temporo-occipital suture. He received supportive care and gradually regained consciousness, but upon awakening noted left eye ptosis and diplopia. On transfer for rehabilitation, examination reveals: left upper eyelid ptosis with 3 mm reduction in palpebral fissure height; left exotropia; left anisocoria (1-2 mm larger than right) with delayed light reflex; limited adduction, upward, and downward gaze of the left eye; diplopia in most directions; horizontal gaze is full. Right eye exam is normal. He denies history of diabetes, autoimmune disease, or other neurological conditions. Follow-up CT head (from initial hospital) also showed left sphenoid fracture with sphenoid sinus effusion. No aneurysm or vascular lesion noted on imaging. What is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Traumatic oculomotor nerve palsy (left third cranial nerve palsy)
answer_key.etiology: Traumatic brain injury with left sphenoid fracture causing compression of the oculomotor nerve
answer_key.next_management_step: Multimodal therapy including Uyghur medicine (herbal formulations with anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects), acupuncture, and rehabilitation training (eye movement exercises, eyelid strengthening, visual rehabilitation, pupillary reflex training)

challenge_prompt:
A 40-year-old man with a history of early T-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia treated with induction chemotherapy (cyclophosphamide, adriamycin, dexamethasone, vincristine, methotrexate, cytarabine) and subsequent allogeneic stem cell transplant (conditioning: busulfan, fludarabine, thiotepa) presents with severe chronic insomnia. Post-transplant, he had ESBL Klebsiella pneumoniae bacteremia treated with IV meropenem for one month. Over the next two years, he experienced recurrent bacterial sinusitis requiring at least 15 courses of antibiotics (amoxicillin/clavulanic acid, doxycycline alternating), each ~10 days. Three years post-transplant, he developed insomnia with difficulty initiating and maintaining sleep. Initial evaluation: PHQ-9 score 3, GAD-7 score 3, Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) 26/28 (severe insomnia). Labs (CBC, CMP, vitamin B12, iron studies, thyroid profile, vitamin D) normal. Actigraphy showed prolonged sleep latency (~90 min), total sleep time <4 hours, sleep efficiency 50-55%. Failed treatments: mirtazapine, melatonin, CBT-I, zolpidem, lemborexant, acupuncture, Ashwagandha, valerian. Integrative medicine evaluation ordered a cellular micronutrient assay (CMA) and GI microbial assay. CMA results: multiple deficiencies including vitamin K2 isoforms, riboflavin, pyridoxine, phenylalanine, methionine, taurine, choline, glutathione, omega-9; borderline deficiencies in thiamine, vitamin D, tyrosine, chromium, iodine, lithium, selenium, zinc, omega-3 DHA, carnitine, coenzyme Q10. GI Microbial Map: elevated Bacteroides fragilis, Enterococcus, Enterobacter; reduced Faecalibacterium prausnitzii; overgrowth of Streptococcus, Klebsiella, Fusobacterium, Prevotella; Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes both markedly increased; Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes ratio 0.20; intestinal health markers: elastase-1 >750 μg/g, calprotectin 16 μg/g, secretory IgA 1400 μg/g.

After a regimen of probiotics, prebiotics, and targeted antibiotics, the patient's insomnia resolved completely.

aliases: Gut dysbiosis secondary to recurrent antibiotic exposure
answer_key.diagnosis: Antibiotic-induced gut dysbiosis causing chronic insomnia
answer_key.etiology: Prolonged and recurrent antibiotic therapy leading to gut dysbiosis and functional micronutrient deficiencies, disrupting the gut-brain axis and neurotransmitter synthesis
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate microbiome restoration with multistrain probiotics, fermented foods (kefir, kombucha, sauerkraut), high-fiber plant-based diet, and targeted supplementation for deficient nutrients (e.g., omega-3/9, amino acids, vitamins)

Intestinal health markers normal (elastase-1 >750, calprotectin 16, secretory IgA 1400)

challenge_prompt:
A 20-year-old male sustained a cervical spinal cord injury at the C7 level from a motor vehicle accident, treated with a cervical interbody cage. At discharge, he was classified as AIS-D with predominant distal motor impairment in both hands and feet. Twelve months post-injury, he continued to require a cane for ambulation and had not used any spasticity medications. Examination at that time (baseline before a planned intervention) revealed: upper extremity motor score (UEMS) 40, lower extremity motor score (LEMS) 43; light touch sensation score 86, pinprick sensation score 85; spasm frequency score 4 (spontaneous spasms >10 per hour); Modified Ashworth Scale (MAS) showed spasticity limited to hands—left hand finger extension 1, thumb flexion 4; right hand finger flexion 1, finger extension 1. Handgrip strength: right 32 kg, left 8 kg. Box and Block Test: left 26 blocks/min, right 30 blocks/43 s. 9-Hole Peg Test: left 2.43 min, right 20 s. Gait: 6-Minute Walk Test 224.4 m, 3-Meter Walk Test 7.96 s. Surface EMG during gait showed irregular, poorly defined activation with limited intermuscular coordination. Autonomic function was normal.

What is the most likely spinal cord injury syndrome, and what is the next appropriate management step?

He exhibited disproportionately greater motor impairment in the upper extremities compared to the lower extremities, consistent with central cord syndrome.

answer_key.diagnosis: Central cord syndrome (CCS)
answer_key.etiology: Traumatic hyperextension injury (motor vehicle accident)
answer_key.next_management_step: Transcutaneous spinal cord stimulation combined with physical therapy (tSCS+PT)

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 64-year-old female with a university degree was hospitalized because of a year-long progression of walking difficulties, which had intensified over the past 6 months. She was fully conscious, communicated effectively, exhibited no speech impairment, and could walk independently. She presented with a slightly wide-based gait and impaired mobility alongside a 10-year history of auditory impairment; tinnitus had gradually developed 3 years prior. She had suffered a traumatic lumbar fracture in her mid-30s, underwent surgical intervention. The patient was admitted for additional diagnostic evaluation.

Neurological examination: Hyperactive tendon reflexes in all four limbs. Bilateral alternating movement, heel-knee-tibia, and finger-nose tests were inaccurate. Romberg’s sign was positive. Maintaining a straight line while walking was difficult; broad-based gait observed. Hoffmann’s sign was positive, whereas Oppenheim’s, Gordon’s, Chaddock’s, and bilateral Babinski signs were negative. Upper-limb postural tremors were observed.

Cognitive assessment: Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score 26. Laboratory analyses revealed normal results.

Pure tone audiometry: profound sensorineural hearing impairment in the right ear; left ear normal. Video head impulse testing: positive for the left horizontal canal without evidence of otolith involvement. Electromyography: damage to the left ulnar nerve at the elbow level and right median nerve at the wrist suggestive of mild carpal tunnel syndrome; decreased sensory nerve action potential amplitude in the right ulnar nerve. No evidence of polyneuropathy.

Cervical and craniocerebral MRI: extensive hemosiderin deposition, highlighting involvement of the left temporal lobe, brainstem, and cervical pulp. Hemosiderin deposits were visible on standard T2-weighted MRI of the cervical spinal cord as linear hypointensities. Susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) enhanced visibility of these signals across the left temporal lobe, bilateral insula, temporal cortex sulci, and the brainstem. CT angiography of the head and neck showed no anomalies. Chest, abdominal, and pelvic CT showed no abnormalities.

The patient refused all invasive procedures such as lumbar puncture, CT myelography, or lumbar vascular digital subtraction angiography.

answer_key.diagnosis: Central nervous system superficial siderosis (classical type)
answer_key.etiology: Chronic subarachnoid hemorrhage (exact source undetermined)
answer_key.next_management_step: Attempt to locate the bleeding source (e.g., digital subtraction angiography) and consider surgical repair; if patient declines or source not found, initiate medical therapy (iron chelation and supportive care).

Left ear normal hearing on pure tone audiometry
Normal chest, abdominal, and pelvic CT
Patient refusal of invasive diagnostic procedures
Positive video head impulse test for left horizontal canal without otolith involvement

Chronic progressive sensorineural hearing loss (profound right ear, 10-year history)
Pyramidal signs: hyperactive tendon reflexes in all limbs and positive Hoffmann sign

title: Central nervous system superficial siderosis: A case report and literature review

challenge_prompt:
A 56-year-old male with multiple comorbidities (severe ischemic heart disease, ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy, congestive heart failure NYHA III, poorly controlled insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes mellitus with neuropathy, hypertension, grade III obesity, generalized anxiety disorder, active tobacco use) was admitted for elective right lower limb femoro-popliteal bypass surgery for occlusive peripheral arterial disease. There was no history of upper respiratory tract infections or gastroenteritis in the 4 weeks preceding admission; screening for influenza and COVID-19 were negative. Surgery was performed under epidural anesthesia but complicated by intraoperative venous graft thrombosis requiring reconstruction, extending duration to 8 hours.

Postoperatively, he developed delirium despite preventive measures, with agitation leading to epidural catheter and drain displacement, necessitating continuous light intravenous sedation. Sedation was titrated to a RASS score of 0 for 48 hours. Mild muscular weakness was noted but attributed to pre-existing diabetic and vascular peripheral neuropathies.

On postoperative day 3, the patient had an apnea episode shortly after a RASS score of −1, followed by cardiac arrest (asystole). ACLS was initiated per guidelines, achieving ROSC after 10 minutes. Workup for the cause of arrest: ECG and cardiac enzymes showed no evidence of malignant arrhythmias or myocardial infarction; therapeutic anticoagulation for the graft ruled out pulmonary embolism. CT head showed no ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke but revealed findings consistent with probable aspiration pneumonia.

Twenty-four hours post-arrest, with sedation stopped, neurological exam using the FOUR scale yielded a score of 5; pupils and corneal reflexes were normal. The patient had flaccid quadriplegia with diminished deep tendon reflexes, no cranial nerve involvement, and was comatose. Continuous eight-channel EEG initiated shortly after arrest and over the following week showed no epileptiform activity. Mechanical ventilation maintained stable oxygenation and CO2 clearance. Hemodynamically, he exhibited persistent sinus tachycardia and arterial hypertension. Low-grade fever was present from ICU admission, modestly responsive to antipyretics.

Cerebral MRI with contrast, performed 72 hours post-arrest due to logistical delays, showed no pathological changes. Laboratory studies for metabolic causes of coma showed mild hypothyroidism (TSH elevated, free T4 normal), moderate hypernatremia, normal thiamine. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome was excluded; benzodiazepine challenge did not produce a Lazarus response, ruling out catatonia/akinetic mutism. Cerebrospinal MRI with contrast also showed no macroscopic abnormalities.

Lumbar puncture (performed after 24-hour anticoagulation hold) revealed albuminocytologic dissociation: CSF protein 63.2 mg/dL (reference 15–60 mg/dL), white cell count < 50 × 10^9/L. Tests for NMDA receptor antibodies, neuronal IgG antibodies, antiganglioside antibodies, and systemic vasculitis were negative. Blood and CSF cultures obtained after antibiotic suspension and coagulation correction were negative.

Electromyography (EMG) performed during the third week of ICU care demonstrated the following: sural sparing sensory phenomenon (normal sural sensory nerve action potential [SNAP] with reduced amplitude upper limb SNAPs, including an abnormal median SNAP); severe, acute, symmetric motor axonal polyneuropathy affecting both proximal and distal segments.

Based on the available data, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

aliases: acute sensory and motor axonal neuropathy
answer_key.diagnosis: Guillain-Barré syndrome (hyperacute/fulminant form, likely acute sensory and motor axonal neuropathy [ASMAN] variant)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune, potentially triggered by recent surgery and prolonged intraoperative inflammatory response
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy (0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days) or consider plasma exchange if not contraindicated
optional_findings: History of prolonged surgery (8 hours) with intraoperative complication
No preceding infectious illness; negative influenza/COVID screening
Mild hypothyroidism and moderate hypernatremia without other metabolic derangement
Absence of epileptiform activity on continuous EEG
No response to benzodiazepine challenge (ruling out catatonia)
Normal cardiac workup (ECG, enzymes) excluding cardiac cause of arrest
CT evidence of probable aspiration pneumonia but no brain pathology
required_findings: Flaccid quadriplegia with diminished deep tendon reflexes post-cardiac arrest
Normal cerebral MRI and cerebrospinal MRI ruling out structural brain/brainstem lesions

EMG showing sural sparing sensory phenomenon (normal sural SNAP with reduced upper limb SNAP amplitudes, including abnormal median SNAP)

title: The Differential Diagnosis of Coma in the ICU: Hyperacute Postoperative Guillain‐Barré Syndrome—A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 60-year-old man presented with left-sided limb weakness and slurred speech for over 7 hours. He had a history of ischemic stroke in 2016 resulting in mild right-sided hemiparesis, hypertension diagnosed 6 months ago (on amlodipine 5 mg daily), and cholecystectomy in 2017. He had not been on secondary stroke prevention. Approximately 7 hours prior to admission, he developed slurred speech, pseudobulbar affect (inappropriate laughing/crying), left-sided limb weakness, difficulty gripping objects and maintaining balance, sensory loss in the left limb, and dizziness. Head CT showed subtle hypodensity in the left lateral paraventricular region and right centrum semiovale, suggestive of acute cerebral infarction.

On admission, vital signs: T 36.4°C, P 96 bpm, RR 19/min, BP 150/100 mmHg. Neurological exam: alert, oriented, fluent, mild dysarthria. Left upper motor neuron facial weakness. Left-sided hemiparesis (4/5), right-sided strength 5/5 with slightly increased tone in right lower limb. Diminished light touch and pinprick on left side. No pathological reflexes. Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) 27/30. Standardized Swallowing Assessment (SSA) score 23/46, indicating grade 1 (light) aspiration risk.

Initial brain MRI revealed a punctate acute infarct in the center of the right frontal and parietal lobes, along with scattered ischemic areas and a remote infarction in the left lateral paraventricular region. MRA showed occlusion of the lower trunk of the right MCA M2 segment and sparse distal branches. Follow-up MRI at 9 days showed progression of the lesion size.

During the first month after the stroke, the patient developed an expressionless face with oral drooling and inability to maintain voluntary eyelid closure for more than 1–3 seconds; he would manually close his eyelids, but they reopened immediately upon release. However, he retained the ability to blink spontaneously and maintained complete eyelid closure during sleep. He also exhibited voluntary paralysis of the facial, lingual, and pharyngeal muscles, while automatic and emotional movements (spontaneous blinking, laughing, crying) remained intact. Dysphagia for solids and liquids was prominent.

A blink reflex test was performed. Following supraorbital nerve stimulation, the ipsilateral R1 and R2 responses in the orbicularis oculi muscles were normal, but the contralateral R2' response was absent. This indicated impaired cortical-to-brainstem signaling with preserved brainstem pathways.

Dynamic laryngoscopy revealed dysphagia during the oropharyngeal phase. There was localized hyperemia in the hypopharynx; bilateral vocal cords had smooth edges, but the left vocal fold appeared loose with an arcuate closure pattern. Residual material was identified in the piriform sinuses and epiglottic valleculae. Delayed coughing occurred after ingesting 3 mL and 5 mL of low-consistency food. Secretions around the laryngeal vestibule were graded as level 1 on the Murray Secretion Severity Scale. The YPR-SRS scale for residues scored 3 at the epiglottic valleculae and 5 at the epiglottic margins/aryepiglottic folds. Penetration-Aspiration Scale (PAS) was level 6, indicating laryngeal penetration below the vocal folds with aspiration, which could be completely cleared.

answer_key.diagnosis: Foix-Chavany-Marie syndrome (bilateral anterior opercular syndrome)
answer_key.etiology: Bilateral cerebral infarctions (acute right frontoparietal and remote left lateral paraventricular) causing disruption of bilateral corticonuclear (corticobulbar) tracts
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate nasogastric tube feeding for nutrition and aspiration prevention, and begin speech and swallowing therapy as part of a multidisciplinary rehabilitation program
optional_findings: Standardized Swallowing Assessment score 23/46 (grade 1 light aspiration risk)
Follow-up MRI at 9 days showed progression of lesion size

Slightly increased muscle tone in right lower limb from prior stroke
CT head: subtle hypodensities left paraventricular and right centrum semiovale suggestive of acute infarction
required_findings: Voluntary paralysis of facial, lingual, and pharyngeal muscles with preserved automatic and emotional movements (e.g., spontaneous blinking, laughing, crying)
Apraxia of eyelid closure with motor impersistence: unable to maintain voluntary eyelid closure >1–3 seconds, but normal spontaneous blinking and complete eyelid closure during sleep
Blink reflex: normal R1 and ipsilateral R2 responses, but absent contralateral R2'

challenge_prompt: A 45-year-old right-handed male insurance maker with 12 years of education and unremarkable medical history presented with somnolence and disorientation with regard to time and location, without lateralizing signs at the neurological examination. Two weeks after admittance he improved in orientation. MRI was performed one day after admission and revealed an infarction in the left paramedian thalamus (dorsomedial nucleus). Based on the presentation and imaging findings, what is the most likely diagnosis? What is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Left paramedian thalamic ischemic stroke (lacunar infarct)
