answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate antiplatelet therapy (e.g., aspirin) and evaluate stroke etiology with vascular risk factor assessment, echocardiography, cardiac rhythm monitoring, and imaging of cervical/cerebral arteries.
optional_findings: Improvement in orientation over two weeks
required_findings: MRI revealed infarction in the left paramedian thalamus (dorsomedial nucleus)
Acute onset somnolence and disorientation
No lateralizing signs on neurological exam

challenge_prompt: A 5-month-old male infant, delivered at term by cesarean section to a healthy mother, had been followed since a prenatal ultrasound at 19 weeks of gestation revealed cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, borderline ventriculomegaly (9 mm bilaterally), and absence of the cerebellar vermis. The family history was negative for congenital anomalies, neurological disorders, or genetic syndromes. At delivery, the infant weighed 3.3 kg, presented with a weak cry and signs of respiratory distress, and had Apgar scores of 7 at 1 minute and 8 at 5 minutes. He was admitted to the NICU for further evaluation and management. Physical examination revealed a head circumference above the 95th percentile, an anterior fontanelle approximately four fingers wide, tachypnea with mild subcostal retractions, generalized hypotonia, a weak Moro reflex, diminished sucking reflex, recurrent hiccups, and occasional suspected seizures. Cardiovascular examination was initially unremarkable, but a mild systolic murmur was later detected. The infant was placed on 2 L/min of nasal cannula oxygen to maintain SpO₂ ≥ 92%, and nasogastric feeding was initiated. A comprehensive laboratory panel, including a full septic workup and serial labs, yielded unremarkable results. Cardiac evaluation identified a small muscular ventricular septal defect with a left-to-right shunt and a patent foramen ovale, both hemodynamically insignificant. Renal ultrasound was normal. A non-contrast brain CT demonstrated cystic dilation of the posterior fossa, absence of the cerebellar vermis, enlargement of the fourth ventricle, and positioning of the straight sinus and torcula superior to the lambdoid suture. There was moderate dilation of the right lateral ventricle without transependymal CSF permeation. No intracranial hemorrhage, midline shift, or white-gray matter differentiation abnormalities were observed. Intracranial pressure remained persistently elevated. Based on the presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Dandy-Walker Malformation
answer_key.etiology: Congenital malformation of the posterior fossa with cerebellar vermis hypoplasia/agenesis, cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, and enlarged posterior fossa; often sporadic, can be associated with genetic syndromes or chromosomal anomalies.
answer_key.next_management_step: Placement of a ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt to relieve hydrocephalus and elevated intracranial pressure.

challenge_prompt:
A 39-year-old female patient was admitted to our hospital complaining of headaches, pain, and paralysis in the left hemifacial region, along with diplopia. The patient had previously experienced pain and diplopia and was admitted to another healthcare facility where treatment with corticosteroids resulted in partial clinical improvement. Following discharge, the patient reported a recurrence of symptoms after tapering off the corticosteroids, albeit with a milder presentation. 3 days prior to her most recent admission, the patient's symptoms worsened, prompting her to seek medical attention at our institution. Notably, the patient denied experiencing any sensory or motor dysfunction or symptoms related to infectious diseases. Her headaches were persistent throughout the day and intensified with positional changes. On physical examination, the patient exhibited no dysarthria, and her pupils were normal in size, with a normal reaction to light. Additionally, her eye movements were regular in all directions, with no observed ptosis, lid lag, or nystagmus. She suffered from left-sided facial plegia. No evidence of meningeal irritation was observed, and other clinical examinations were unremarkable.

Initial screening tests, including complete blood count, renal and liver function tests, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, fasting blood sugar, arterial blood gas, urine analysis, and fasting blood sugar, were performed and reported within the normal range. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain, thyroid, and abdominopelvic sonography and toxicology tests were also conducted and showed no pathological findings. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis was also performed and reported within the normal range. Additionally, the patient's CSF sample was tested for oligoclonal bands, which was negative. However, thyroid peroxidase antibody (TPO-Ab) levels were significantly elevated, and a positron emission tomography scan (PET-Scan) revealed diffuse increased metabolic activity in the thyroid gland due to autoimmune thyroiditis.

Focal neurological manifestation of Hashimoto's thyroiditis

challenge_prompt:
A 9-year-old child with a history of celiac disease and dermatitis herpetiformis presented to the emergency room with a 2-month history of vomiting and loose stools, and a recent seizure lasting 20 minutes. On examination, the child appeared pale, emaciated, and drowsy, with a generalized rash. Glasgow Coma Scale was 10/15. Increased muscle tone and heightened reflexes were noted. The patient was tachycardic and hypertensive, with oxygen saturation of 65%. Supplemental oxygen was started, and intravenous ceftriaxone, acyclovir, and hydralazine were administered. The patient was transferred to the pediatric intensive care unit, intubated, and a CT brain showed intraparenchymal and subarachnoid hemorrhages. Subsequent MRI of the brain demonstrated multifocal areas of parenchymal hemorrhages in the supratentorial region, predominantly at the gray-white matter junctions in the bilateral parieto-occipital and parasagittal frontal regions, with subarachnoid hemorrhage components. MRA and MRV were unremarkable, with no significant vessel stenosis or filling defects.

Based on the presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome (PRES)
answer_key.etiology: Multifactorial, likely precipitated by acute hypertension in the setting of underlying celiac disease and pro-inflammatory state, leading to endothelial dysfunction and vasogenic edema.
answer_key.next_management_step: Aggressive blood pressure control (reduce mean arterial pressure by 25% from baseline) and seizure management with anticonvulsants, while addressing underlying triggers (e.g., infection, autoimmune activity).

challenge_prompt: A 56-year-old woman presents with worsening headaches over 2 years, acute bilateral visual loss and projectile vomiting one week ago. She had blurred vision for 3 years, for which glasses were prescribed. One year ago, a head CT scan for worsening headaches revealed a pituitary adenoma measuring 3 cm by 2 cm. She was referred to neurosurgery but did not attend due to fear. She now reports complete visual loss in the left eye and reduced vision in the right. On examination: conscious, vital signs stable (BP 121/80 mmHg, heart rate 80 bpm, temperature 36.7°C, respiratory rate 18 bpm). Confrontation visual field testing reveals bitemporal hemianopia. Extraocular movements are normal. Laboratory investigations: prolactin 2 ng/L (normal 4.79–23.3 pmol/L), free T4 7.1 pmol/L (12.0–22.0), free T3 <1.7 pmol/L (3.1–6.8), serum cortisol 669 nmol/L (140–690), ACTH 1.0 pg/mL (2.8–64.6), glycated hemoglobin 5.9% (<5.7%). A repeat CT head (skull base to vertex) shows a cystic mass forming a figure of eight with an enhancing rim, measuring 4.6 x 2.7 cm, located in the sella turcica, with no hemorrhage, and compression of the optic chiasm. Paranasal sinuses are clear. Humphrey visual field: no light perception left eye, right temporal hemianopia; fundoscopy normal. Optical coherence tomography shows thinning of the optic nerve in both eyes, with neuroretinal rim loss inferiorly, nasally, and superiorly.

answer_key.diagnosis: Pituitary apoplexy
answer_key.etiology: Hemorrhagic infarction of a pituitary adenoma
answer_key.next_management_step: Start high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone 60 mg daily) and arrange urgent neurosurgical consultation for possible transsphenoidal resection.

challenge_prompt:
An 86-year-old Asian woman with a 2-year history of Alzheimer's disease (baseline MMSE score 23) presented with acute right-sided weakness upon waking, accompanied by drowsiness, drooling, and impaired swallowing. On examination, vital signs were stable; GCS was E4V3M6. She had right facial weakness, absent gag reflex, right hemiparesis, wet voice, increased drooling. Oral motor examination showed symmetrical but impaired motor function of lips, tongue, soft palate, mandible, and dentition. Delayed and infrequent voluntary swallow initiation was noted. Voluntary cough, throat clearing, and gag reflex were absent; only reflexive coughing was observed. Swallowing was effortful with occasional choking on saliva. MMSE score was 2, indicating severe cognitive impairment. Brunnstrom stage was III in the right limbs. Laboratory tests were normal. Emergent brain MRI revealed acute infarctions in the left frontal and parietal watershed zones and the left putamen. The patient was diagnosed with acute ischemic stroke causing right hemiplegia, post-stroke cognitive impairment, and post-stroke dysphagia. NIHSS score was 21. A nasogastric tube was placed. Conventional rehabilitation (30-min sessions, 3 times per week) including swallowing therapy (thermal-tactile stimulation, oral motor exercises) and speech therapy was initiated.

After three months of standardized therapy, motor function improved: Brunnstrom stage V in the right limbs, GCS E4V5M6. However, swallowing function remained poor, with a Functional Oral Intake Scale (FOIS) score of 1. The patient still had severe cognitive impairment. A videofluoroscopic swallowing study (VFSS) demonstrated prolonged oral transit time and severely impaired bolus formation and control across all tested volumes and consistencies. No pharyngeal swallow was triggered; therefore, all pharyngeal-phase parameters were scored as maximally impaired, yielding a Functional Dysphagia Scale (FDS) score of 95 (oral-phase predominant).

Based on the information provided, what is the most likely etiology of this patient's persistent dysphagia, and what is the most appropriate next step in management to address it?

aliases: Oral-phase dysphagia due to cognitive dysfunction
answer_key.diagnosis: Post-stroke oral-phase dysphagia secondary to cognitive impairment
answer_key.etiology: Left frontal/parietal/putamen infarcts causing severe cognitive impairment disrupting voluntary oral phase control
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) targeting the bilateral dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) for cognitive enhancement, using a protocol of high-frequency (10 Hz) stimulation at 50% of resting motor threshold, 3000 pulses per session.

challenge_prompt: A 5-year-old boy with no prior medical history and normal development was struck by a car while walking. He lost consciousness immediately, experienced several brief convulsive episodes each lasting seconds, then regained consciousness but was irritable and crying. On arrival, his Glasgow Coma Scale was 11 (E4 V2 M5). Pupils were equal and reactive to light. The left upper and lower limbs moved less and were hypotonic and hyporeflexic; cranial nerves were intact. Blood investigations were normal. Initial non-contrast head CT revealed a small hemorrhagic contusion involving the left superior cerebellar peduncle and dentate nucleus. The remainder of the brain was unremarkable. MRI of the brain a few days later confirmed the same lesion without additional pathology. The patient was admitted to the pediatrics high dependency unit for observation. Over the next week, he remained mute but was otherwise stable. On day 8 of admission, he began to obey commands and spoke single words. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next step in management?

A 31-year-old previously healthy female presented to a community health center in the Napu Valley, Central Sulawesi, Indonesia, with tonic-clonic convulsions. answer_key.etiology: Schistosoma japonicum

challenge_prompt:
A 19-year-old female presents with a 3-month history of persistent cough, 6 weeks of progressive dyspnea, and right-sided chest pain for 1 month. No fever, night sweats, autoimmune or genetic disorders; no smoking history; no relevant occupational exposure. Empirical antibiotics and painkillers gave no response. Laboratory studies show hypochromia with anisocytosis, elevated ESR, mildly elevated CRP. Sputum GeneXpert negative. Chest X-ray: inhomogeneous opacity in right upper lung zone. Contrast-enhanced CT chest: large heterogeneous mass in the right upper lobe with internal septations and solid peripheral components, invading T3-T4 vertebrae, with extension into spinal canal causing spinal cord compression; displacement of right pulmonary hilum and main bronchus; a similarly appearing mass in the medial basal segment of the left lung. Bone and soft tissue windows show vertebral erosion. Core needle biopsy of the posterior mediastinal mass: gray-white to dark brown tissue fragments, histopathology shows sheets of spindle and epithelioid cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, eccentric nuclei (rhabdoid morphology), high mitotic activity and necrosis. Immunohistochemistry: tumor cells show focal positivity for Myogenin and MyoD1, diffuse positivity for Desmin; negative for SOX10, CD68, SALL4. What is the most likely diagnosis? challenge_prompt:
A 41-year-old female with a history of polysubstance use (nicotine, buprenorphine/naloxone, methamphetamine, amphetamine/dextroamphetamine), alcohol use disorder, bipolar disorder, ADHD, iron-deficiency anemia, and prior thoracic epidural abscess was found unresponsive in her bathroom after ingesting six pregabalin tablets of unknown strength with approximately 7–8 shots of vodka. She had used intranasal buprenorphine/naloxone two days prior. On arrival, she was febrile (101°F) and reported profound quadriparesis and neck pain. Neurological exam demonstrated near-complete distal paralysis of all four extremities, preserved cranial nerves, intact sensation above the neck, diminished deep tendon reflexes, and inability to lift arms against gravity.

Urine toxicology: positive for amphetamine, methamphetamine, buprenorphine; negative for methadone, cannabis, opiates, oxycodone, PCP, benzodiazepines, tricyclic antidepressants. Initial labs: CPK 16,093 U/L, AST 349 U/L, ALT 158 U/L, Na 129 mmol/L (mild hyponatremia, normalized during hospitalization). CSF: glucose 69 mg/dL, protein 50 mg/dL, 3 WBCs, negative oligoclonal bands. EEG showed no seizure activity. Transthoracic echocardiogram normal. CT head and CT cervical/thoracic/lumbar spine without acute abnormalities. Chest X-ray normal. MRI brain with and without contrast normal. MRI cervical spine without contrast: longitudinal T2-hyperintense lesion extending from C2 to T3 with mild cord expansion and extensive posterior paraspinal muscle edema involving the multifidus and semispinalis muscles. Infectious/autoimmune workup: HIV negative, hepatitis serologies negative, ANA negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis/etiology, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

aliases: Venous hypertensive myelopathy from deep neck muscle rhabdomyolysis
Cervical myelopathy associated with paraspinal rhabdomyolysis

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most appropriate next step in management?

In March 2023, a 1-year-old Chinese male infant was admitted due to frequent seizures and developmental delay. Since birth, he had gradual developmental delay. At age 1 year, he started experiencing two types of seizures. One type: limb stiffness, unresponsiveness, staring, cyanosis, without limb tremors or drooling; each episode ~20 seconds, about twice daily. Second type: nodding or cessation of movement in clusters, each cluster 10 or more episodes, lasting seconds to minutes, up to 7–8 times per day. He could sit alone but not crawl, walk, or speak words. Gesell Developmental Scale showed moderate delays in adaptive behavior, language, personal-social, gross motor, fine motor. Physical exam: mild hypotonia in extremities. Lab tests: blood glucose, plasma ammonia, lactic acid, electrolytes, blood and urine metabolic screening all normal. Cranial MRI: reduced medullary volume, fewer gyri, focal cortical thickening in both hemispheres, consistent with malformations of cortical development. EEG: interictal discharges in right parietal, occipital, meso-posterior temporal regions; recorded epileptic spasms and focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures. Seizure types classified as epileptic spasms and focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures; epilepsy syndrome diagnosed as infantile epileptic spasms syndrome (IESS). He was treated with topiramate, ACTH, levetiracetam, and sodium valproate sequentially, but seizures remained refractory, with persistent daily clusters. He is now 2 years old and continues to have frequent seizures.

Genetic testing revealed a novel heterozygous missense variant in the DYNC1H1 gene.

challenge_prompt:
A 61-year-old male with a history of gastric sleeve surgery, major depressive disorder, and PTSD presented with persistent neurological symptoms 4 months after an episode of serotonin syndrome. Four months prior, he was treated for a UTI with linezolid while continuing fluoxetine 40 mg and buspirone 30 mg. Within 48 hours, he developed acute confusion, diaphoresis, tremor, hyperreflexia, clonus, agitation, hypertonia, and hyperthermia, meeting Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria. All serotonergic agents were discontinued, and the acute autonomic instability resolved within days, but he did not return to his neurological baseline.

Over the following months, he experienced a range of episodic symptoms: right-sided facial droop, gait disturbance, episodes resembling seizures, brain zaps, internal tremors, severe anxiety, panic attacks, intrusive thoughts, depression, tinnitus, and complex parasomnias (sleepwalking). The facial droop was documented on examination; it fluctuated in severity across visits, was not always present, and did not follow a fixed upper or lower motor neuron pattern. Notably, the asymmetry diminished when the patient was distracted and intensified during emotional distress and close observation.

Investigations:
- Brain MRI: no acute intracranial pathology, restricted diffusion, or structural lesions.
- Lumbar puncture was not performed; the treating team deemed it unnecessary given the clinical trajectory and absence of progressive encephalopathic features.
- Porphyria screen: urine analysis showed elevated coproporphyrin I, heptacarboxyl, pentacarboxyl, and uroporphyrins. Genetic testing for acute intermittent porphyria was negative.
- Liver function tests: mildly elevated transaminases; albumin low-normal. Renal function (BUN, creatinine) and inflammatory markers (CRP, CBC) normal.
- Pharmacogenomic panel: SLC6A4 homozygous short promoter polymorphism (reduced serotonin transporter expression), MTHFR heterozygous C677T, ADRA2A homozygous C allele.
- A trial of hemin infusions yielded only subjective, inconsistent improvement.

The patient was started on vilazodone 5-10 mg with caution but developed severe depression (PHQ-9 25, GAD-7 21), electric shock sensations, and parasomnias.

answer_key.next_management_step: refer to a multidisciplinary FND clinic for integrated physical, occupational, and speech therapy; cautious pharmacological management of psychiatric symptoms

An 8-year-old boy presented with 5 days of abnormal mood, primarily irritability, followed by 2 days of speech disorder and involuntary movements. He had a fall and vomiting 5 days prior. He developed incoherent speech, slurred articulation, involuntary head shaking, flailing limbs, impaired recognition of individuals, confusion, and sleep disturbances. title: Severe anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in a pediatric patient presenting with status dystonicus as the primary clinical manifestation: A case report (CARE)

challenge_prompt:
A 32-year-old Han Chinese male farmer presents with a 6-year history of progressive neurological and psychiatric symptoms. At age 26, he developed resting tremor and bradykinesia in the right limb. One year later, the tremor affected the left limb, and levodopa was started with good motor control. By age 28, gait instability and mild cognitive decline appeared. Brain MRI at that time showed mild cortical atrophy (Global Cortical Atrophy [GCA] score 1 bilaterally in frontal and temporal lobes). At age 29, he developed depression, anxiety, obsessive thoughts, severe psychomotor agitation, and violent behaviors. He was treated with levodopa/benserazide and rasagiline, but benefit was only temporary. By age 32, cognitive deterioration progressed, gait instability worsened with frequent falls, and he required bed rest or wheelchair. He also developed dysarthria, urinary incontinence, and myoclonus. There was no orthostatic hypotension, hyposmia, or rapid eye movement sleep behavior disorder. He is the second child of non-consanguineous healthy parents; his older sister is healthy, and there is no family history of neurological disorders.

Neurological examination: severe dysarthria; impaired orientation, memory, calculation, and comprehension; limited upward vertical gaze; rigidity in all limbs and trunk; normal muscle strength; inability to perform bilateral heel-knee-shin test; resting tremor and myoclonus in both limbs; unsteady, wide-based, slow gait; positive pull test; brisk tendon reflexes in upper limbs, active in lower limbs; bilateral Babinski sign negative; meningeal irritation signs negative. MDS-UPDRS score: 89. MMSE: 7/30.

Laboratory tests: normal complete blood count, urinalysis, stool routine, biochemical profile, homocysteine, ceruloplasmin, hepatitis B/C, syphilis, HIV, coagulation, and residual urine volume. Serum superoxide dismutase-1 (SOD1) was 236 U/mL (normal 110–215 U/mL). Interleukin-6 was 1.8 pg/mL (normal 0–7 pg/mL). Brain MRI at age 32: generalized brain atrophy, especially bilateral frontal, temporal, and parietal lobes, cortex, and hippocampus. GCA score was 2 bilaterally in frontal lobes and 3 bilaterally in temporal lobes. Susceptibility-weighted imaging was normal. Electroencephalography showed a moderately diffuse slow wave. Lumbar puncture: normal cell counts, protein, and negative autoimmune encephalitis antibodies.

Whole-exome sequencing identified compound heterozygous variants in the VPS13C gene: a nonsense variant c.1699C>T (p.Arg567*) on chromosome 15, and a large deletion (chr15:62156504–62352664) encompassing exons 13, 14, and 35. Both variants were novel and not found in population databases. The mother was an asymptomatic carrier of the nonsense variant, and the father was an asymptomatic carrier of the deletion, confirmed by Sanger sequencing and quantitative PCR, respectively.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: VPS13C-related early-onset Parkinson disease (autosomal recessive)
answer_key.etiology: Loss-of-function mutations in VPS13C leading to defective mitochondrial function and lysosomal lipid transport
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate symptomatic pharmacotherapy including levodopa/benserazide, rasagiline, donepezil, and quetiapine, and provide genetic counseling.
optional_findings: Brisk tendon reflexes in upper limbs
required_findings: Resting tremor and bradykinesia onset at age 26
Rapid progression with cognitive decline, psychiatric symptoms, myoclonus, and loss of motor benefit from levodopa
Brain MRI showing progressive cortical atrophy (GCA score 2 frontal, 3 temporal)
MMSE score 7/30
Serum SOD1 elevated at 236 U/mL (normal 110–215)
Whole-exome sequencing identified compound heterozygous VPS13C variants: c.1699C>T (p.Arg567*) and a large deletion (chr15:62156504–62352664)
Family history negative and parents non-consanguineous
Normal ceruloplasmin, negative autoimmune encephalitis antibodies, ruling out Wilson disease and immune-mediated encephalopathy

title: Two novel variants of VPS13C gene related Parkinsonism: A case report and literature review

challenge_prompt:
A 9-year-old Bangladeshi boy presents with a few months of blurry distant vision in both eyes. His father reports that he has watched television from a close distance and has had a history of convulsions several times since age 4, with unknown frequency. He has irregular follow-up and no documented medical history. Prenatal, perinatal, and vaccination histories are unremarkable. He has no developmental delay but has limited, disconnected speech, indicative of intellectual handicap, and is unable to attend school. He is the only child of non-consanguineous parents, with no family history of neurological or skin disorders.

On examination, the boy is anxious and irritable. There are numerous facial angiomas (adenoma sebaceum) over the face and forehead. Further skin examination reveals an ash leaf spot over the right hypochondriac region and multiple Shagreen patches on the back.

Ophthalmic evaluation reveals best-corrected visual acuity of 6/6 in both eyes with mild myopic correction, intraocular pressure of 14 mmHg, and no anterior segment abnormalities. Dilated fundus examination shows a retinal astrocytic hamartoma approximately 2 mm × 2.5 mm above the optic disc in the left eye.

Neurological examination shows normal tone and reflexes, with bilateral negative Babinski sign. Other systems are unremarkable.

Laboratory investigations including complete blood count, serum electrolytes, renal and liver function tests are within normal limits. Echocardiography and abdominal ultrasonography show no abnormalities.

Axial MRI of the brain shows multiple well-circumscribed cortical lesions that are hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2 and FLAIR, consistent with cortical tubers. There are multiple subependymal nodules (SENs) along the ventricular surfaces of the lateral ventricles, which are hyperintense on T1 and iso- to hyperintense on T2 and FLAIR. FLAIR images also demonstrate small, cyst-like, well-demarcated hyperintense lesions near the lateral ventricles, suggestive of white matter lesions (WMLs), which are hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2. Additionally, there are thin, straight bands of radial linear WMLs, hyperintense on T2 and iso- to hypointense on T1.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

answer_key.diagnosis: Tuberous sclerosis complex
answer_key.etiology: Autosomal dominant mutation in TSC1 or TSC2 genes
answer_key.next_management_step: Referral to neurology for seizure management and multidisciplinary care, including genetic counseling
optional_findings: Blurry distant vision in both eyes
Normal laboratory investigations (complete blood count, electrolytes, renal and liver function)
Normal neurological tone and reflexes with negative Babinski
No family history of neurological or skin disorders
required_findings: Facial angiomas (adenoma sebaceum) all over face and forehead
Ash leaf spot over the right hypochondriac region
Multiple Shagreen patches on the back
Retinal astrocytic hamartoma in left eye
Brain MRI: multiple cortical tubers (well-circumscribed cortical lesions hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2/FLAIR)
Brain MRI: multiple subependymal nodules (hyperintense on T1, iso- to hyperintense on T2/FLAIR) along lateral ventricles
Brain MRI: white matter lesions (cyst-like hyperintense on FLAIR, hypointense on T1) and radial linear WMLs

title: Neurocutaneous features of tuberous sclerosis complex: A case report from Bangladesh

challenge_prompt: A 36-year-old male with a history of past excessive alcohol use (quit 2 years ago) presented with one week of double vision and gait instability, and two days of confusion and forgetfulness, immediately after completing a 40-day religious water-only fast (no food or supplements). He denied headaches, dizziness, numbness, dysphagia, fever, loss of consciousness, seizures, or trauma. He reported decreased urine output. Vital signs normal. Neurologic exam: disorientation, confusion, bitemporal hemianopia, bilateral 6th cranial nerve palsy, facial twitching, difficulty following commands, ataxic gait. Normal muscle tone, reflexes, sensation. Labs: Hb 12.3 g/dL (13–17), albumin 32 g/L (35–50), HCO3 31 mmol/L (22–29). CT head: incidental right parietal venous developmental anomaly, no acute findings. Brain MRI: T2 hyperintensities in bilateral medial thalami and periaqueductal region with mild diffusion restriction. What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next most appropriate management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Wernicke encephalopathy (non-alcoholic)
answer_key.etiology: Thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency due to prolonged fasting
answer_key.next_management_step: Administer high-dose intravenous thiamine (500 mg IV three times daily)
optional_findings: incidental right parietal venous developmental anomaly on CT
required_findings: 40-day water-only fast
confusion and disorientation
bilateral 6th cranial nerve palsy
ataxic gait
MRI T2 hyperintensities in bilateral medial thalami
MRI T2 hyperintensities in periaqueductal region
mild diffusion restriction on MRI
history of past alcohol use (thiamine store depletion risk)

title: Wernicke's Encephalopathy Following 40 Days of Water‐Only Fasting: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 48-year-old male with a history of chronic low back pain with left lower extremity radiculopathy, and chronic abdominal pain from chronic pancreatitis, gastritis, esophagitis, and splenic venous thrombosis, and prior opioid addiction, underwent a spinal cord stimulation (SCS) trial and subsequent permanent implant. The two percutaneous leads were placed with the 0-position at the superior endplate of T5. Intraoperative findings included thoracic rotational scoliosis. Post-implant, he reported over 50% reduction in both abdominal and low back/leg pain, with some asymmetry in stimulation. Seven months later, after moving out of state, he experienced an abrupt loss of pain relief, with return of severe symptoms. Outside imaging studies showed interval caudal migration of both SCS leads down two vertebral levels, now at T7, and rightward displacement. Attempts at reprogramming by the manufacturer team found high impedance on the left-sided lead at contact positions 5-7 and 13-15. Reprogramming could not recapture the pain relief.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Spinal cord stimulator lead migration with resultant device failure
answer_key.etiology: Mechanical complication of spinal cord stimulator due to lead migration, possibly exacerbated by thoracic rotational scoliosis
answer_key.next_management_step: Refer for surgical revision with paddle leads (or neurosurgeon consultation for lead replacement/repositioning)
optional_findings: History of initial >50% pain reduction post-implant
History of opioid addiction and failed conservative treatments
required_findings: Abrupt loss of SCS therapeutic efficacy 7 months after permanent implant
Imaging shows interval caudal migration of both leads two vertebral levels (from T5 to T7) with rightward displacement
High impedance on left lead at contact positions 5-7 and 13-15 on reprogramming attempt
Unsuccessful reprogramming attempts

title: Spinal Cord Stimulation as a Potential Therapeutic Modality for Managing Concurrent Chronic Low Back and Abdominal Pain Complicated by Device Migration: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 32-year-old Chinese male presented with a 2-month history of progressive bilateral upper and lower limb muscle weakness. The weakness initially involved the proximal lower limbs, causing difficulty squatting, and progressed to difficulty standing up and lifting his arms. He also reported inflammatory joint pain over the fingers and wrists, a rash over the bilateral lower limbs, loss of appetite, and weight loss. He denied trauma, back pain, prolonged fever, facial rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, alopecia, chronic cough, hemoptysis, or urinary/bowel incontinence. He had no significant past medical or family history. He was a cigarette smoker and worked as a delivery driver.

Physical examination revealed multiple flat, nontender, dark brown hyperpigmented rashes with distinct borders (1 cm × 2 cm) on the forearms and lower legs. There was no facial rash, photosensitivity, or oral ulcers. Cardiovascular, respiratory, and abdominal examinations were normal. Neurological examination showed no muscle fasciculations. Tone and reflexes were normal. Muscle strength testing revealed proximal myopathy: bilateral shoulder power 3/5, elbow power 3/5, wrist power 5/5, hand grip 5/5; bilateral hip power 3/5, knee power 5/5, ankle plantar flexion and dorsiflexion 5/5. Sensation was normal in all dermatomes. Babinski signs were downgoing, proprioception normal, and cerebellar signs negative.

Initial blood investigations showed anemia (hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL), leukopenia (total white cell count 2.96 × 10³/µL), and lymphopenia (0.92 × 10³/µL); platelet count 148 × 10³/µL. Renal profile showed sodium 130 mmol/L, potassium 3.5 mmol/L, urea 2.70 mmol/L, and creatinine 45 µmol/L (normal 62–106). Creatine phosphokinase was normal at 47 U/L (normal 39–308). Liver function tests, serum cortisol (512 nmol/L, normal 133–537), and thyroid function (TSH 2.550 mIU/L, free T4 18.80 pmol/L) were normal. Complement levels were low: C3 0.32 g/L (normal 0.90–1.80) and C4 0.07 g/L (normal 0.10–0.40). Antinuclear antibody (ANA) was positive at >200 CU. Anti-dsDNA antibody was positive at 50.6 IU/mL (negative <27). Extractable nuclear antibody panel showed anti-Smith (anti-Sm) 125.7 CU (<20), anti-RNP 21.5 CU (<20), anti-Ro-60 21.7 CU (<20), and anti-ribosomal P >362 CU (<20). These were performed by chemiluminescent immunoassay. Nerve conduction studies and electromyography demonstrated axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy and irritative myopathy. The patient declined a lumbar puncture.

A connective tissue disease was considered, and he was started on high-dose corticosteroids and hydroxychloroquine. Two days after admission, he developed urinary incontinence. Examination revealed weak anal tone and reduced perianal sensation. Cauda equina syndrome was suspected. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine with contrast showed leptomeningeal enhancement at the cauda equina region, with no cord compression and no features of transverse myelitis or multiple sclerosis. There was no evidence of malignancy.

What is the most likely underlying diagnosis, and what is the next best management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) with neuropsychiatric manifestations: polyneuropathy and noncompressive cauda equina syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune (systemic lupus erythematosus)
answer_key.next_management_step: Pulse intravenous methylprednisolone, intravenous immunoglobulin, and intravenous cyclophosphamide
optional_findings: Inflammatory joint pain of fingers and wrists
Constitutional symptoms (loss of appetite, weight loss)
Urinary incontinence with weak anal tone and reduced perianal sensation
Positive anti-RNP, anti-Ro-60, anti-ribosomal P antibodies
required_findings: ANA >200 CU (positive)
Anti-dsDNA 50.6 IU/mL (positive)
Anti-Sm 125.7 CU (positive)
Low C3 0.32 g/L and C4 0.07 g/L
Leukopenia 2.96 × 10³/µL and lymphopenia 0.92 × 10³/µL
Nerve conduction studies: axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy
MRI: leptomeningeal enhancement at cauda equina without cord compression
Proximal muscle weakness pattern (3/5) with normal sensation and reflexes

title: Polyneuropathy and noncompressive cauda equina syndrome as manifestation of systemic lupus erythematosus: a case report

challenge_prompt:
An 8-month-old girl was admitted to the Department of Pediatric Audiology for suspected hearing loss. She had failed otoacoustic emission (OAE) and automated auditory brainstem response (AABR) tests, and a history of generalized mild motor developmental delay. At age 7 months, she was treated for pneumococcal meningitis and recovered well.

Initial examination showed well-ventilated middle ears with Type A tympanograms. Behavioral audiometry revealed moderate to severe hearing loss and missing transient evoked otoacoustic emissions (TEOAE) and distortion product otoacoustic emissions (DPOAE) bilaterally.

Under general anesthesia, microscopic examination of the right ear revealed middle ear effusion; a myringotomy was performed to drain it. Auditory brainstem response (ABR) and auditory steady-state response (ASSR) testing was then performed. Click-evoked ABR showed no detectable threshold below 100 dB for air conduction and 45 dB for bone conduction in both ears. Frequency-specific ABR using narrow-band chirps showed no responses in the right ear at any frequency up to 100 dB. In the left ear, no response at 1 kHz, but responses at 2 kHz (threshold 70 dB) and 4 kHz (80 dB). ASSR showed no responses in the right ear at maximal stimulation (90 dB) across 500 Hz-4 kHz; left ear responses were obtained at 70 dB (500 Hz), 80 dB (1 kHz), 70 dB (2 kHz), and 70 dB (4 kHz).

High-resolution MRI of the inner ear, performed under the same anesthesia, revealed malformations in both inner ears. The right ear showed a common cavity deformity (CCD); the left ear had a malformation at the border between incomplete partition type I (IP-I) and CCD. The 8th cranial nerves on both sides were clearly separated into cochlear and vestibular branches.

A couple of days after the myringotomy, constant clear otorrhea developed from the right ear.

What is the most likely underlying diagnosis or complication? What is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Perilymphatic fistula (right ear)
answer_key.etiology: Congenital inner ear malformation (right common cavity deformity) with abnormal communication between the subarachnoid space and the middle ear via the oval window
answer_key.next_management_step: Obtain fluid sample from the right ear for beta-2 transferrin testing to confirm CSF leak; if positive, proceed with urgent surgical exploration and closure of the fistula, with possible simultaneous cochlear implantation.
optional_findings: Moderate to severe hearing loss on behavioral audiometry
Click ABR no threshold below 100 dB air conduction both ears
Frequency-specific ABR: left ear responses at 2 kHz 70 dB, 4 kHz 80 dB
ASSR no responses right ear at 90 dB
Clear separation of cochlear and vestibular branches of 8th nerve on MRI
Well-ventilated middle ears with Type A tympanograms
required_findings: 8-month-old girl
History of failed OAE and AABR tests
Treated for pneumococcal meningitis at age 7 months
Right middle ear effusion drained by myringotomy
Constant clear otorrhea from right ear post-myringotomy
MRI: right common cavity deformity (CCD)
Left ear malformation at border of IP-I and CCD
No ABR responses in right ear at 100 dB

title: Simultaneous closure of a perilymphatic fistula and placement of cochlear implant in a case of complex inner ear malformation

challenge_prompt:
A 35-year-old male with a history of chronic insomnia, fatigue, and emotional stress presented with a one-week history of severe headaches, generalized arthralgias, and myalgias. He was initially diagnosed with an upper respiratory tract infection and treated with IV fluids, which provided temporary relief. He later developed a severe, throbbing headache with visual disturbances, photophobia, and phonophobia; neurological examination was unremarkable, and a brain MRI showed nonspecific findings. He was diagnosed with complex migraine and discharged with temporary improvement.

However, his symptoms recurred and progressively worsened over the following days, with new onset of difficulty sleeping, tongue heaviness, hypoactivity, numbness in the right hand, bluish discoloration of the lips, speech difficulties, and episodes of disorientation. On the day of admission, he had a fever (38.2°C), tachycardia (104 bpm), blood pressure 133/80 mmHg, oxygen saturation 95%. He was confused, hypoactive, and poorly responsive, with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 10/15. Physical examination revealed swelling in the right eye, tachycardia, and decreased breath sounds. Neck motion elicited pain, suggesting meningeal irritation.

An initial brain CT scan and limited brain MRI did not reveal acute ischemia or hemorrhage. A follow-up brain MRI showed newly developed areas of high T2-weighted fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) signal intensities in the left hemisphere, right parietal, and temporal lobes, as well as mild leptomeningeal enhancement.

Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis revealed elevated protein levels and lymphocytic pleocytosis. PCR testing for viral pathogens, including herpes simplex virus, was negative. CSF cultures showed no bacterial growth. A pathergy test and eye examination were normal.

Based on the clinical presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM)
answer_key.etiology: Post-viral immune-mediated demyelination (suspected viral trigger)
answer_key.next_management_step: High-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (e.g., 20–30 mg/kg/day, max 1 g/day, for 3–5 days)
optional_findings: history of chronic insomnia, fatigue, and emotional stress
severe headaches, generalized arthralgias, and myalgias
complex migraine diagnosis with temporary improvement

Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis revealed elevated protein levels and lymphocytic pleocytosis
PCR testing for viral pathogens, including herpes simplex virus, was negative

title: Adult-onset acute disseminated encephalomyelitis mimicking complex migraine: A CARE-compliant case report

challenge_prompt:
A 12-year-old male presented with a 2-day history of high-grade fever and severe polyarticular joint pain involving the wrists, knees, and ankles. Over the subsequent 4 days, he developed progressive decline in consciousness, becoming lethargic and disoriented. He had been engaging in outdoor activities during the evening in a tropical region, with potential mosquito exposure. He had a history of seasonal allergies and mild asthma. On admission, temperature was 38.7°C, pulse 92/min, respiratory rate 18/min, blood pressure normal. He was lethargic, with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 12 (E3V3M6). Neurologic examination revealed no focal deficits, and muscle strength and reflexes were normal. Musculoskeletal exam showed tenderness and swelling in the wrists, knees, and ankles, without erythema or effusion. Skin exam showed no rash. Cardiovascular and respiratory exams were unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed leukopenia and thrombocytopenia. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis demonstrated lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain with contrast revealed scattered bilateral white matter lesions with periventricular diffusion-restricted spots on diffusion-weighted imaging; there was no involvement of the posterior fossa, cortical, or deep gray matter, and no contrast enhancement. T2-weighted, FLAIR, and susceptibility-weighted images were unremarkable.

Serology for chikungunya virus was positive for IgM antibodies.

answer_key.diagnosis: Chikungunya encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Chikungunya virus (CHIKV)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy and supportive care; send serum and CSF for CHIKV RT-PCR.
optional_findings: No involvement of posterior fossa, cortical, or deep gray matter
History of seasonal allergies and mild asthma
required_findings: Fever and polyarthralgia involving wrists, knees, ankles
Progressive decline in consciousness with GCS 12
Exposure to mosquitoes in a tropical region
Leukopenia and thrombocytopenia
CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein
MRI showing scattered bilateral white matter lesions with periventricular restricted diffusion
No contrast enhancement on MRI
Absence of skin rash

title: A rare case of chikungunya encephalitis and its management: A case report and literature review

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

On January 28, 2024, a 46-year-old Chinese woman with no prior history of mental illness presented with abnormal behavior, cognitive impairment, altered consciousness, and epileptic seizures. Thirteen days prior to admission, she exhibited unexplained crying, nonsensical speech, irritability, and physical aggression. She was treated at a psychiatric facility with oral medication, but there was no improvement. Seven days prior to admission, she developed fever and unresponsiveness, progressing to impaired consciousness, accompanied by facial twitching, a rightward gaze, stiff limbs, and intermittent involuntary shaking of the upper limbs. On examination, she had increased muscle tone in the limbs, positive bilateral Babinski signs, and did not cooperate with the remainder of the exam.

Initial complete blood count showed elevated white blood cell counts. CSF analysis revealed a significant increase in WBC count, predominantly lymphocytes. Rheumatologic and immunological markers, as well as tumor markers, were normal. High-throughput sequencing of CSF for pathogenic microorganisms detected Epstein-Barr virus (sequence number 31, confidence 99%) and herpes simplex virus type 1 (sequence number 3, confidence 70%). Tests for HIV, SARS-CoV-2, influenza A/B, Treponema pallidum, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Brucella, Aspergillus, and other pathogens were negative.

Initial autoimmune encephalitis antibodies and paraneoplastic antibody tests (performed on January 30, 2024) were negative. EEG showed moderate abnormalities, with background beta wave changes during coma and frequent partial seizures originating from the left frontal pole and anterior temporal region. Electroneurography revealed abnormal H-reflex responses in both tibial nerves and abnormal F-wave patterns in bilateral median and tibial nerves. Cranial MRI (performed twice) showed no abnormalities. CT of the chest and abdomen and ultrasound of the reproductive system showed no tumors.

Based on the symptoms, signs, and auxiliary examinations, a diagnosis of viral encephalitis was suspected. The patient was started on ganciclovir for antiviral therapy, and a combination of sodium valproate and levetiracetam for seizure management, along with antipsychotic medications. After initiating treatment, she had no further epileptic seizures, and follow-up EEGs showed no epileptic activity. However, her consciousness did not improve, involuntary movements increased, and she developed autonomic dysfunction.

Given the lack of improvement with antiviral therapy and the new symptoms, the clinical team suspected a possible alternative diagnosis and considered further workup.

Repeat autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel on February 10, 2024, revealed positive NMDAR antibodies in CSF.

aliases: autoimmune encephalitis associated with NMDAR antibodies
answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (NMDAR) encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Triggered by Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) and herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) co-infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Repeat cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and serum testing for anti-NMDAR antibodies; initiate first-line immunotherapy with intravenous methylprednisolone and/or human immunoglobulin.
optional_findings: 46-year-old Chinese woman with no prior psychiatric history
Prodromal psychiatric symptoms: unexplained crying, nonsensical speech, irritability, physical aggression
Fever and unresponsiveness 7 days before admission
Facial twitching, rightward gaze, stiff limbs, intermittent shaking of upper limbs
Increased muscle tone in limbs and positive bilateral Babinski signs
Electroneurography: abnormal H-reflexes in both tibial nerves, abnormal F-waves in median and tibial nerves
No further seizures after starting valproate and levetiracetam
required_findings: CSF analysis: significant increase in WBC count, predominantly lymphocytes
High-throughput sequencing of CSF detected Epstein-Barr virus (sequence number 31, confidence 99%) and herpes simplex virus type 1 (sequence number 3, confidence 70%)
Initial autoimmune encephalitis antibodies and paraneoplastic antibody tests (January 30, 2024) were negative
Cranial MRI (performed twice) showed no abnormalities
After antiviral treatment, consciousness did not improve, involuntary movements increased, and autonomic dysfunction developed
EEG showed moderate abnormalities with frequent partial seizures from left frontal pole and anterior temporal region

title: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis triggered by EBV and HSV-1: A case report and literature review

challenge_prompt: A 77-year-old woman presented with a one-year history of progressive forgetfulness, confusion, difficulty recalling the names of objects (anomia), and an inability to navigate familiar places (e.g., locating the restroom). On examination, she was alert (GCS 15). Cognitive assessment revealed an MMSE score of 4/30 and a Nishimura Dementia Scale score of 25 (severe impairment). Specific deficits included acalculia, agraphia, finger agnosia, and mild left-right disorientation, consistent with incomplete Gerstmann syndrome. Brain CT showed ventricular enlargement with an Evans index of 0.33, widening of the Sylvian fissures, pronounced left posterior dominant ventricular dilation, and bilateral hippocampal atrophy. Additionally, a positive disproportionately enlarged subarachnoid space hydrocephalus (DESH) sign and a callosal angle of less than 90 degrees were noted. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Idiopathic normal pressure hydrocephalus (iNPH) with incomplete Gerstmann syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Idiopathic impairment of cerebrospinal fluid absorption leading to ventricular enlargement and compression of adjacent brain structures, particularly the left parietal lobe
answer_key.next_management_step: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt surgery

title: Effect of shunt surgery on idiopathic normal pressure hydrocephalus with incomplete Gerstmann syndrome: A CARE-compliant case report

challenge_prompt: A 32-year-old previously healthy male from Sweden developed bilateral lower limb numbness and weakness 30 minutes after surfacing from a recreational dive at Unawatuna, Galle, Sri Lanka. He had completed 18 self-reported diving sessions over the previous week, including two dives the day before the incident. The patient did not have adequate surface intervals between dives. On the day of the incident, he completed a 26 m dive lasting 25 minutes, diving with an instructor and using a wrist-mounted diving computer. Notably, he had used different dive computers from various centers during the week. His ascent to surface was guided by the diving computer. At the diving center, oxygen was administered via face mask, with only slight symptomatic improvement without resolution of neurological deficits. He was transferred to the hospital, arriving approximately 8 hours after the diving session. Upon admission, he was conscious and rational with stable vital signs and normal oxygen saturation. He denied pain but reported bilateral lower limb weakness and numbness. Neurological examination revealed increased tone bilaterally in the lower limbs, exaggerated knee and ankle reflexes, mild lower limb weakness, and a sensory level at T10 with impaired pain and touch sensations. There was no spinal tenderness. Bowel and bladder functions were fully preserved with no urinary retention or incontinence. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Type II Decompression Sickness (DCS)
answer_key.etiology: Nitrogen gas bubble formation from rapid ascent after repetitive diving with inadequate surface intervals and loss of cumulative dive history due to use of different dive computers, leading to spinal cord involvement.
answer_key.next_management_step: Arrange immediate transfer for hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT) using US Navy Treatment Table 6 or equivalent recompression protocol.
required_findings: History of repetitive diving (18 sessions in one week) with inadequate surface intervals
Use of different dive computers resulting in loss of cumulative dive history
Onset of bilateral lower limb numbness and weakness 30 minutes after surfacing from a 26 m dive
Neurological examination: increased tone bilaterally in lower limbs
Neurological examination: exaggerated knee and ankle reflexes
Sensory level at T10 with impaired pain and touch sensations
Normal oxygen saturation and no respiratory distress
Slight improvement with oxygen but no resolution of major neurological deficits

title: Managing Decompression Sickness in a Low‐Resource Setting, a Clarion Call to Improve Systems: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 32-year-old female patient presented with feverish feelings and joint pain, followed by a sudden onset of unclear speech and unsteady gait. Initial management by a physician did not lead to improvement. On examination, she appeared fatigued, with a low-grade fever (temperature 99.5°F / 37.5°C, heart rate 90 bpm, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 16 bpm). Neurological examination revealed an unsteady gait with mild ataxia (wide-based stance, slight swaying, difficulty maintaining balance) and dysarthria (slurred and unclear speech). No focal weakness or sensory deficits were noted; deep tendon reflexes were symmetric and normal. Joint examination revealed mild tenderness on palpation in both knees without swelling, redness, effusion, or synovitis; range of motion was normal. The remainder of the physical exam (cardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal, skin) was unremarkable. Laboratory investigations were negative. MRI of the brain demonstrated lesions in the left frontal lobe. On T2-weighted/STIR images, the lesions were hyperintense. Diffusion-weighted imaging showed vivid diffusion restriction. T1-weighted images revealed multiple well-defined lesions that were hypointense, with the largest measuring 21 × 20 mm and exhibiting concentric rings. What is the most likely diagnosis?

answer_key.diagnosis: Balo's concentric sclerosis (Baló's concentric sclerosis, Balo disease)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune demyelinating disorder, considered a rare variant of multiple sclerosis.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone) to reduce inflammation and mitigate disease progression.

challenge_prompt:
A 75-year-old male patient had recurrent erythematous macules and papules with itching mainly on the flexed side of the body for more than 2 years. Combined history of asthma and allergic rhinitis for more than 20 years. ALS was diagnosed according to the revised El Escorial criteria 3 years prior to presentation; he was currently on riluzole (50 mg), administered twice daily. The pharyngeal reflex was weakened, and the muscle strength of the extremities was 0. The body was covered with dry skin, with a few visible fine white scales; red spots of varying sizes were scattered on the back, and millet grain to mung bean-sized papules were distributed. The erythema on the instep is eroded with yellow exudate and crusts. Laboratory tests revealed that the blood C-reactive protein was 81.92 mg/L (normal: 0–5 mg/L), immunoglobulin E was 258 IU/mL (normal: 0–100 IU/mL), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate was 65 mm/h (normal: 0–15 mm/h). Routine blood tests revealed no abnormalities in the liver and kidney functions, and blood lipids.

Based only on the presentation above, what is the most likely diagnosis for the skin condition, and what is the next best step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Moderate atopic dermatitis
answer_key.etiology: Chronic inflammatory skin disease associated with skin barrier dysfunction and type 2 immune activation, in a patient with a personal history of atopy.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate dupilumab therapy (600 mg loading dose, then 300 mg subcutaneously every 2 weeks) with topical corticosteroids as needed for flares.

challenge_prompt:
A 47-year-old Caucasian female presented with a 6-day history of persistent throbbing frontal headache, photophobia exacerbated by bright lights and loud noises, low-grade fever (37.6°C), a single episode of nausea and vomiting on the day of admission, dizziness for 1 day described as feeling of losing balance, intermittent ringing sensation in both ears, and fatigue for the past 2 days. She denied sick contacts, recent travel, confusion, memory loss, hearing or vision loss, personality changes, tremors, or seizures. She reported no rash prior to admission. Past medical history: hypertension.

On admission, vital signs: blood pressure 161/79 mmHg, otherwise normal. Physical exam: alert but in mild distress due to pain and photophobia. Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal, round, reactive to light; extraocular movements intact; no nystagmus. Tympanic membranes normal. Painful vesicular rash observed at the right T4–T6 dermatome region near the right breast. Cranial nerves II–XII intact. Rinne test: air conduction > bone conduction; Weber test: equal lateralization. Motor and sensory exam normal (no deficits to light touch, pain, position sense, vibration; muscle strength 5/5 all four extremities; no tremors). Reflexes: biceps, knee tendon, and Babinski sign normal. Nuchal rigidity present; Kernig and Brudzinski signs negative.

Initial laboratory work-up unremarkable. Lumbar puncture performed. CSF: colorless, clear; WBC 648/μL (92% lymphocytes); RBC 47/μL; protein 396 mg/dL; glucose 41 mg/dL; sodium 151 mmol/L. Concurrent serum glucose: 56 mg/dL. CSF bacterial antigens (group B streptococcus, Haemophilus influenzae, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis) negative. HIV screening negative. CSF coccidioides antibody negative. CSF HSV PCR negative. CSF West Nile virus IgM and IgG negative.

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis? What is the next best diagnostic step?

CSF VZV PCR was positive.

title: An Immunocompetent Patient with a Vesicular Rash and Neurological Symptomatology

challenge_prompt:
A 45-year-old para 2 woman, heavy smoker, with a family history of a sister diagnosed with breast cancer at age 37, presented with bloating. Pelvic ultrasonography showed bilateral complex adnexal masses. Pelvic MRI revealed bilateral suspicious ovarian masses measuring 12 × 8 cm on the right and 12 × 6 cm on the left. CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no evidence of distant metastases or peritoneal carcinomatosis. CA-125 level was 3997 U/mL. A diagnostic laparoscopy confirmed the presence of a peritoneal nodule in the right iliac fossa and was consistent with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer. The patient underwent primary debulking surgery including hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, pelvic peritonectomy, ileo-cecal resection en bloc, infragastric omentectomy, and para-aortic and pelvic lymphadenectomy. Complete cytoreduction was achieved. Histopathological examination of the surgical specimen revealed high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma infiltrating the meso-colon, meso-appendix, and uterine serosa; two of 70 para-aortic lymph nodes were positive, corresponding to FIGO stage IIIA.

On postoperative day 6, the patient developed headache and focal neurological deficits including paresthesia, weakness, and clonic movements of the left limbs. Brain MRI with gadolinium showed two ring-enhancing lesions: a 17-mm lesion in the right fronto-parietal lobe and a 6-mm lesion in the right parietal lobe, both associated with significant surrounding vasogenic edema.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis for the brain lesions, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or therapeutic step?

aliases: Cerebral metastases from epithelial ovarian cancer
answer_key.diagnosis: Brain metastases from high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma
answer_key.etiology: Hematogenous dissemination of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (with underlying germline BRCA1 mutation as a predisposing factor)
answer_key.next_management_step: Stereotactic biopsy for histologic confirmation, followed by stereotactic radiosurgery/radiotherapy and systemic chemotherapy with carboplatin, paclitaxel, and bevacizumab, then maintenance PARP inhibitor (olaparib).

challenge_prompt: An 69-year-old male presented with sudden onset of right-sided foot drop, not related to any recent direct trauma, accompanied by numbness and paresthesia over the lateral leg and foot, without significant pain. He reported a 2-week history of frequent right ankle sprains and limping. Physical examination revealed muscle strength of BMRC grade 1 in dorsiflexion and toe extension, grade 2 in eversion, preserved ankle plantar flexion, and full active range of motion at the knee. Sensory examination showed reduced sensation over the lateral leg and dorsum of the foot. Tinel sign was positive at the fibular head. Lumbar MRI demonstrated a mild herniated intervertebral disc and degenerative disc changes at the L3–4 and L4–5 levels, without significant radicular compression. challenge_prompt: A 55-year-old nonsmoker, nonalcoholic male with type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic vertigo, and a prior mid‑brain ischemic stroke 6 years ago (residual left‑side weakness) presented to the emergency room with a 2‑day history of visual disturbance in the left eye, burning sensation in the left hemi‑body, occipital headache, polyuria, severe dry mouth, vomiting, productive yellow cough, fever, and abdominal pain. On admission: temperature 38.0°C, blood pressure 121/74 mm Hg, pulse 110 bpm regular, respiratory rate 25/min, oxygen saturation 70% on room air, BMI 34.39. Physical exam: confused, fruity odor on breath, coarse crackles at both lung bases. Neurologic exam: GCS 14/15, upper and lower limb weakness, diplopia, left homonymous hemianopsia; pupils equal and reactive, right‑side reflexes +3, left‑side +1, meningeal signs and Babinski negative. Initial laboratory tests were consistent with diabetic ketoacidosis (markedly elevated blood glucose, ketonemia, metabolic acidosis). Chest X‑ray showed mild basilar right opacities suggestive of aspiration pneumonia. Electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus tachycardia. Non‑contrast brain CT at admission revealed global sulci widening with infarctions and foci in the right posterior and middle cerebral artery territories (temporal and occipital lobes), and bilateral calcifications along both vertebral arteries. Magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) disclosed stenosis of the right posterior cerebral artery and an anomalous artery connecting the two vertebral arteries at the level of the medulla. Prior MRI from 6 years earlier confirmed an old mid‑brain infarct. Based on the above, what is the most likely underlying etiology for the recurrent posterior circulation strokes, and what is the next appropriate management step?

aliases: Congenital vascular malformation of the vertebro‑basilar system
answer_key.diagnosis: Recurrent posterior circulation ischemic strokes due to a rare congenital vertebral artery anomaly (V4 segment connecting artery) and decompensated diabetic ketoacidosis
answer_key.etiology: Hemodynamically significant anomalous artery connecting both vertebral arteries at the medullary level leads to turbulent flow, accelerated atherosclerosis, and recurrent embolic/thrombotic events in the posterior circulation, compounded by diabetic ketoacidosis as an acute precipitant.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate aspirin 81 mg daily and atorvastatin 40 mg daily, manage diabetic ketoacidosis with intravenous insulin and fluids, treat aspiration pneumonia with appropriate antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone), and consider long‑term anticoagulation (later rivaroxaban); obtain vascular surgery consultation for the anomalous vessel.

challenge_prompt:
A 46-year-old previously healthy male electrical technician presented with an insidious onset of high-grade continuous fever, chills, rigors, productive cough, headache, and arthralgia. He denied abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, cellulitis, seizures, or loss of appetite. After 1 week of unsuccessful outpatient treatment, he was admitted to a hospital.

On first admission, he was conscious (GCS 15/15), with blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, pulse 96/min, and had neck stiffness with positive Kernig’s and Brudzinski’s signs. Respiratory exam revealed bilateral rhonchi; cardiovascular and abdominal exams were unremarkable. Initial laboratory investigations: white cell count 22.54 × 10⁹/L with neutrophil predominance (18.62 × 10⁹/L), CRP 32.87 mg/L, ESR 124 mm/h, serum creatinine 0.84 mg/dL, sodium 153 mmol/L. Lumbar puncture showed CSF with granulocytes 250 cells/mm³, lymphocytes 12 cells/mm³, protein 132 mg/dL, glucose 54 mg/dL (random blood sugar 130 mg/dL). CT brain (noncontrast and contrast-enhanced) was unremarkable. Blood, urine, and CSF cultures yielded no growth. A presumptive diagnosis of acute pyogenic meningitis was made, and he received intravenous ceftriaxone, vancomycin, and meropenem. During the hospitalization, he developed limb weakness, hypertonia, and exaggerated tendon reflexes. He was discharged with residual neurological deficits and advised physiotherapy.

Two months later, he was readmitted with 2-week history of recurrent fever, generalized body aches, intermittent altered consciousness, involuntary movements of bilateral upper limbs, severe imbalance, and difficulty ambulating. Examination revealed marked hypertonia in all limbs, myoclonic jerks in bilateral upper limbs, exaggerated deep tendon reflexes in all four limbs, and positive jaw jerk. Cranial nerve exam was normal. Repeat investigations: white cell count 7.45 × 10⁹/L (neutrophils 60.0%, lymphocytes 25.1%), ESR 94 mm/h, CRP 7.0 mg/dL. CSF was clear, with 7 lymphocytes/mm³, no polymorphs, and protein 134 mg/dL. EEG was normal. MRI spine showed infective spondylitis involving thoracic vertebrae T2 through T6, with vertebral body marrow edema, preserved intervertebral disc spaces, paraspinal inflammatory changes, spinal canal narrowing at T5 causing cord compression with myelopathic signal changes and early syrinx formation. MRI brain demonstrated basal meningeal enhancement. Chest X-ray was unremarkable. Additional workup: HIV serology negative; no history of animal contact to suggest brucellosis; normal serum calcium (sarcoidosis unlikely); peripheral blood smear and full blood count unremarkable without lymphadenopathy; no clinical features or positive serology for autoimmune disease or vasculitis.

Based on the presentation and investigations, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Spinal tuberculosis (Pott's disease) with secondary meningoencephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Mycobacterium tuberculosis
answer_key.next_management_step: Start anti-tubercular therapy (fixed-dose combination HRZE) with adjunctive corticosteroids, and refer for neurosurgical/orthopedic evaluation for possible decompression if neurological deficits progress.

A 69-year-old woman with a history of total thyroidectomy for benign nodules in 1992, grade III hypertension, dyslipidemia, and obesity presented with a two-month history of intermittent headache and tinnitus. She denied head trauma. In March 2025, she developed conjunctival hyperemia and progressive bilateral exophthalmos, more pronounced in the right eye. Neurologic and ophthalmologic evaluations at that time did not reveal a definitive etiology. Endocrinology workup in April 2025 showed normal TSH and free T4, and negative thyroid autoantibodies (anti-TPO and TRAb). Cardiology evaluation confirmed grade III hypertension, dyslipidemia, and obesity; carotid Doppler ultrasonography revealed no significant abnormalities. In early May 2025, a neurological re-evaluation documented a left sixth cranial nerve palsy with abduction deficit. Brain MRI demonstrated a small meningioma of the right greater wing of the sphenoid, punctate demyelinating lesions consistent with a microangiopathic process, and normal-appearing orbits, internal and external carotid arteries, optic nerves, tracts, and chiasm. Time-of-flight (TOF) sequences showed no abnormalities of major arteriovenous structures. In late May 2025, she presented to the ophthalmology emergency unit with worsening ocular pain and diplopia. On examination: best-corrected visual acuity was 0.9 right eye and 1.0 left eye; intraocular pressure was 25 mmHg right and 26 mmHg left; ocular motility was full in the right eye but showed marked abduction deficit in the left eye. Anterior segment examination revealed bilateral episcleral venous congestion, diffuse conjunctival chemosis, and marked bilateral proptosis with orbital fat prolapse. Fundus examination and optic nerve optical coherence tomography excluded optic neuropathy. Topical brinzolamide-timolol was initiated for elevated intraocular pressure.
