CT head revealed a hyperdense crescentic extra-axial collection over the right cerebral hemisphere, consistent with acute subdural hematoma.

aliases: Intracranial subdural hemorrhage following spinal anesthesia
answer_key.diagnosis: Acute intracranial subdural hematoma secondary to dural puncture
answer_key.etiology: CSF leakage from multiple dural punctures causing intracranial hypotension, leading to brain sag and rupture of bridging veins, resulting in subdural hemorrhage.
answer_key.next_management_step: Non-contrast brain CT to confirm, followed by urgent burr hole evacuation if subdural hematoma is present.
required_findings: Post-cesarean delivery with spinal anesthesia requiring multiple dural punctures
Persistent headache for 3-4 weeks
Initial postural component to headache
Worsening over last 2-3 days to constant, non-postural headache
Vomiting
No history of trauma
No coagulation disorders or anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy

title: Intracranial Acute Subdural Hematoma Following Spinal Anesthesia for Cesarean Delivery

challenge_prompt:
A 46-year-old woman presented with a sudden-onset fall accompanied by forced rightward gaze deviation, nausea, and vertigo. She had no history of hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, smoking, alcohol, or liver disease. NIHSS was 3 (gaze deviation 2, mild ataxia 1). Oculocephalic maneuver overcame the gaze preference, confirming cortical origin. MRI brain revealed multiple acute infarcts: diffusion restriction in bilateral cerebellar hemispheres, left parieto-occipital, and right parieto-frontal regions, involving anterior and posterior circulations. CT angiography showed only moderate stenosis (<50%) in the V4 segment of the vertebral artery, deemed insufficient to explain the multifocal infarcts. Transthoracic echocardiogram was unremarkable. A 30-day outpatient cardiac event monitor detected no paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. Transesophageal echocardiogram with bubble study was negative for intracardiac thrombus, vegetation, shunt, or patent foramen ovale. Laboratory studies: D-dimer 2.5 mg/L FEU (reference <0.5), AST 120 U/L, ALT 145 U/L, ALP 220 U/L, total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 1.5 mg/dL. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated multiple hypoechoic foci with interspersed hyperechoic areas and poorly defined borders in the right hepatic lobe, the largest measuring 50 × 45 mm. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT showed mass-like lesions with arterial phase hyperenhancement and washout on venous phase.

Based on the above presentation and workup, what is the most likely diagnosis?

answer_key.diagnosis: Hepatocellular carcinoma (occult malignancy presenting as embolic stroke of undetermined source)
answer_key.etiology: Cancer-associated hypercoagulability from hepatocellular carcinoma leading to multifocal embolic strokes
answer_key.next_management_step: Referral to hepatobiliary surgery/oncology for staging and treatment planning of hepatocellular carcinoma.
optional_findings: Age 46 without traditional vascular risk factors
NIHSS 3 with conjugate gaze deviation and mild ataxia
Fall described as sudden loss of postural control (drop attack)
Oculocephalic maneuver confirming cortical origin of gaze preference
FLAIR hyperintensity and ADC hypointensity corresponding to DWI lesions
Bubble study negative for right-to-left shunt
No headache or focal weakness on presentation
required_findings: Multifocal acute infarcts in multiple vascular territories without a large-vessel or cardioembolic source (ESUS)
D-dimer markedly elevated at 5× upper limit of normal (2.5 mg/L FEU)
Elevated liver enzymes (AST 120, ALT 145, ALP 220) and hyperbilirubinemia (total 2.1, direct 1.5)
Abdominal ultrasound showing multiple hypoechoic foci with interspersed hyperechoic areas in right hepatic lobe, largest 50×45 mm
Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrating mass-like lesions with arterial hyperenhancement and washout
Absence of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on 30-day monitor, negative TEE for thrombus/vegetation/shunt
CT angiography with only moderate vertebral artery stenosis (<50%), insufficient to cause multifocal infarcts
Non-contrast MRI showing diffusion-restricted lesions in bilateral cerebellar and cerebral hemispheres

title: Stroke as an Initial Presentation of Hepatocellular Carcinoma: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 17-year-old male with known, uncorrected Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) and a history of secondary polycythaemia presented to the emergency department with recurrent headaches, dizziness, and exertional shortness of breath. His chronic medications included furosemide, spironolactone, metoprolol, and sodium valproate for a seizure disorder. He had been receiving intermittent therapeutic phlebotomy for symptomatic erythrocytosis. Definitive surgical correction had not been performed because of limited access to specialized cardiac surgical services.

On arrival, his vital signs were blood pressure 88–90/60–68 mmHg, pulse 90–96 beats/min, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 85% on room air. A therapeutic phlebotomy was performed, and he was discharged. Five days later, he returned with recurrence of the same symptoms and was kept in the emergency unit for evaluation and planned symptomatic phlebotomy pending laboratory results. While awaiting investigations, he suddenly developed right-sided weakness and facial deviation to the left, without loss of consciousness, convulsions, fever, or bleeding tendencies.

On examination, he was alert and oriented (GCS 15/15). There was right-sided supranuclear facial palsy and expressive aphasia, with preserved comprehension but impaired fluency, naming, reading, and writing. Muscle strength was 0/5 in the right upper limb and 2/5 in the right lower limb, with increased tone and brisk deep tendon reflexes on the right side (patellar reflex +3), compared to +2 on the left. The right plantar response was extensor, and visual fields were grossly intact.

Laboratory investigations revealed a hematocrit of 62.3%, red blood cell count of 7.79 × 10^6/μL, mean corpuscular volume of 80 fL, and red cell distribution width of 26.5%. Iron studies, renal function, and electrolytes were within normal limits. Lipid profile was unremarkable except for low HDL cholesterol (34 mg/dL). Non-contrast brain CT showed no acute hemorrhage but subtle hypodensity in the left basal ganglia and insular region. Subsequent MRI of the brain demonstrated acute ischemic changes involving the left corpus striatum, insular region, and adjacent temporal and parietal cortices, showing hyperintensity on diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and corresponding low signal on the apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) map. MR venography excluded cerebral venous thrombosis. Arterial vascular imaging (MRA) was not performed. An echocardiogram performed during admission demonstrated the typical TOF anatomy without any new abnormalities, intracardiac thrombus, or changes in shunt physiology.

Based only on the information above, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

aliases: Polycythemic stroke in cyanotic heart disease
answer_key.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke due to hyperviscosity from secondary polycythemia in unrepaired Tetralogy of Fallot
answer_key.etiology: Severe secondary erythrocytosis (hematocrit 62.3%) from chronic hypoxemia in uncorrected TOF, leading to hyperviscosity and in situ cerebral thrombosis; recent therapeutic phlebotomy may have contributed by causing volume depletion and microcytosis (MCV 80 fL) with increased thrombotic risk.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 81 mg daily) and atorvastatin 40 mg daily, administer subcutaneous unfractionated heparin 5000 IU twice daily, provide hydration and oxygen, hold further phlebotomy, and plan for definitive surgical repair of TOF.
optional_findings: Iron studies, renal function, electrolytes within normal limits
Lipid profile: low HDL cholesterol 34 mg/dL
Chronic medications: furosemide, spironolactone, metoprolol, sodium valproate
Absence of fever, convulsions, bleeding tendencies
required_findings: Known uncorrected Tetralogy of Fallot with secondary polycythemia
Hematocrit 62.3%, red blood cell count 7.79 × 10^6/μL
Mean corpuscular volume 80 fL, red cell distribution width 26.5%
Acute onset right-sided weakness (right upper limb 0/5, right lower limb 2/5) with right supranuclear facial palsy and expressive aphasia
Non-contrast brain CT: no acute hemorrhage, subtle hypodensity left basal ganglia and insula
MRI brain: acute infarction left corpus striatum, insular, temporal, parietal cortices with DWI hyperintensity and ADC low signal
MR venography: no cerebral venous thrombosis
Echocardiogram: typical TOF anatomy, no new abnormalities, no intracardiac thrombus

title: Hyperviscosity‐Related Ischemic Stroke in an Adolescent With Unrepaired Tetralogy of Fallot: A Case Report From a Resource‐Limited Setting

challenge_prompt:
A 37-year-old man presented with a 6-month history of sudden onset weakness of right wrist dorsiflexion and finger extension. He initially had no pain, but developed pain in the extensor side of the upper arm after 1 month. On examination, right wrist volar-flexion was 5/5, dorsiflexion was 2/5 with radial deviation. Metacarpophalangeal joint flexion was 5/5, extension was 0/5. Right forearm supination was 4/5. Tendon reflexes were normal at the biceps brachii but decreased at the right triceps brachii. Sensory examination revealed hypoesthesia over the dorsum of the radial side of the right hand. Nerve conduction studies showed that sensory nerve action potential and compound muscle action potential of the right radial nerve were not evoked. Needle electromyography revealed spontaneous denervation potentials and neuropathic motor unit action potentials (MUAPs) on volition in the right brachioradialis, extensor carpi radialis longus, and supinator. Denervation potentials were seen in the right extensor digitorum communis, extensor indicis proprius, and extensor carpi ulnaris; no volitional MUAPs were noted in these muscles. The triceps muscle showed normal MUAPs without denervation potentials. MRI of the right arm demonstrated a well-defined, intra-neural, round mass measuring about 0.8 cm × 0.5 cm within the radial nerve. The mass exhibited iso-intensity on T1-weighted images and slight hyper-intensity on T2-weighted images compared to adjacent muscles, with homogeneous signal intensity on both. On T1-weighted images with fat saturation and contrast enhancement, the mass showed homogeneous mild enhancement. A well-demarcated hypointense rim surrounded the mass on both T1- and T2-weighted images.

Histopathological examination of the excised mass revealed a well-demarcated intraneural leiomyoma, composed of smooth muscle cells without atypia, and immunohistochemistry was positive for smooth muscle actin and desmin.

answer_key.diagnosis: Intraneural leiomyoma of the radial nerve
answer_key.etiology: Benign smooth muscle tumor arising from vascular smooth muscle within the nerve
answer_key.next_management_step: Surgical exploration with complete excision and nerve grafting as necessary
optional_findings: Pain developed after 1 month in extensor side of upper arm
Radial nerve mass size 0.8 x 0.5 cm
Location at middle to distal humerus level
required_findings: Sudden onset weakness of right wrist dorsiflexion and finger extension
Radial deviation on wrist dorsiflexion
Decreased right triceps reflex
Hypoesthesia over dorsum of radial hand
Non-evoked radial nerve on NCS
Denervation in radial-innervated muscles except triceps on EMG
Intraneural mass with homogeneous iso-to-hyperintensity and hypointense rim on MRI

title: Radial neuropathy caused by intraneural leiomyoma

challenge_prompt: A 48-year-old male with a history of untreated hypertension and 30 pack-year tobacco use presented with 48 hours of fluctuating right-sided weakness and speech difficulty. He took no medications and had no prior history of hypercholesterolemia, coronary artery disease, or surgeries; he had not seen a primary care physician in three years. In the emergency department, the patient had right-sided weakness, aphasia, and right homonymous hemianopsia. Vital signs: blood pressure 140/85 mmHg, pulse 80 bpm, respirations 16/min, temperature 37.1°C, oxygen saturation 97%. Neurologic examination in the sitting position revealed right facial droop, expressive aphasia, 1/5 muscle strength in the right upper and lower extremities, 1/4 brachioradial, biceps, and patellar reflexes, diminished pinprick sensation on the right, downgoing right toe, and right homonymous hemianopsia. NIHSS score was 15. Laboratory results: white blood cell count 7400/mm³, hemoglobin 15.7 g/dL, hematocrit 47%, platelet count 360,000/mm³, BUN 17 mg/dL, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, sodium 138 mEq/L, chloride 97 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, PTT 27 sec, PT 11.4 sec, INR 1.1, D-dimer 1.4 ng/mL, fibrinogen 227 mg/dL. Lipid panel: total cholesterol 200 mg/dL, LDL-C 170 mg/dL, HDL-C 22 mg/dL, triglycerides 160 mg/dL. Albumin, ALT, AST, alkaline phosphatase, and urinalysis were normal. While in the CT scanner, the patient noted complete resolution of symptoms upon assuming a supine position; symptoms returned when he sat up. CT brain without contrast showed no acute hemorrhage. CT angiography revealed a cervical left internal carotid artery occlusion with retrograde flow through the left ophthalmic artery into the internal carotid terminus where it joins the left middle cerebral artery. Additional findings included severe atherosclerotic narrowing of the right vertebral artery, a congenitally narrow right anterior cerebral artery, left A1 segment dominant, and right A1 segment hypoplastic. CT cerebral perfusion demonstrated a large penumbra of at-risk brain involving the entire left cerebrum (mean transit time 12–14 seconds on the left vs normal on the right), affecting the left middle cerebral artery territory and bilateral anterior cerebral artery territories.

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke due to left cervical internal carotid artery occlusion (large vessel occlusion) with positional cerebral hypoperfusion.
answer_key.etiology: Atherosclerotic plaque rupture/thrombosis leading to occlusion of the left internal carotid artery.
answer_key.next_management_step: Carotid artery stenting to restore antegrade flow and resolve the flow-limiting lesion.
optional_findings: History of untreated hypertension and 30 pack-year smoking.
No prior primary care in 3 years.
Normal complete blood count and metabolic panel.
Right toe downgoing on plantar stimulation.
Diminished pinprick sensation on right side.
required_findings: Complete resolution of neurologic symptoms when lying flat (supine) and recurrence upon sitting.
Right-sided weakness, aphasia, and right homonymous hemianopsia with NIHSS 15.
CT angiography showing cervical left internal carotid artery occlusion with retrograde flow through the ophthalmic artery.
CT perfusion demonstrating a large left hemispheric penumbra with prolonged mean transit time (12–14 seconds).
Absence of acute intracranial hemorrhage on non-contrast CT.
Left A1 segment dominance and right A1 hypoplasia.
Severe atherosclerotic narrowing of the right vertebral artery.
Congenitally narrow right anterior cerebral artery.

title: Heads Down for Acute Stroke: Supine Position for Acute Large Vessel Occlusion in Ischemic Stroke

challenge_prompt:
A 73-year-old man with a history of rectal cancer (diagnosed May 2020, treated with radical resection and 6 cycles of XELOX: oxaliplatin 150 mg plus capecitabine) presented to the Acupuncture Department in April 2023 with a 1-month history of persistent facial numbness. He had developed lung metastases in January 2023, underwent thoracoscopic lobectomy in February 2023, and then started salvage chemotherapy with XELOX (oxaliplatin 150 mg plus capecitabine every 3 weeks) on March 7, 2023. After that first salvage cycle, he developed recurrent hand and foot numbness (also experienced since 2020 but previously mild) along with new-onset facial numbness. The facial numbness involved the periorbital, lateral nasal, preauricular, and perioral regions bilaterally. He reported a mild restriction of facial movements and a tightening sensation. The symptoms persisted and severely impaired his daily activities, including tooth brushing and eating, and caused insomnia. After the second cycle on March 30, 2023, the symptoms worsened.

On examination, voluntary facial movements (eye closure, eyebrow elevation, nose twitching, cheek puffing) were intact, though the patient reported mild subjective restriction. Tactile and pain sensations were preserved on facial sensory testing. Limb muscle strength and tone were normal. Head CT was unremarkable. The patient declined facial nerve electromyography. Hand and foot numbness were more severe in the lower extremities, consistent with a glove-and-stocking distribution. Using Levi's Oxaliplatin-Specific Sensory Neurotoxicity Scale, his symptoms were graded as 3.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy presenting as facial numbness
answer_key.etiology: Neurotoxic effect of oxaliplatin
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate electroacupuncture therapy
optional_findings: Worsening after second cycle on March 30, 2023
Insomnia and functional impairment with daily activities
Lung metastases and thoracoscopic lobectomy in February 2023
required_findings: 73-year-old man with rectal cancer on XELOX chemotherapy (oxaliplatin and capecitabine)
New-onset facial numbness after salvage chemotherapy
Facial numbness in periorbital, lateral nasal, preauricular, perioral regions
Intact voluntary facial movements with subjective restriction
Preserved tactile and pain sensation on facial exam
Unremarkable head CT
Hand and foot numbness with glove-and-stocking distribution
Levi's scale grade 3 neurotoxicity

title: Electroacupuncture for oxaliplatin-induced facial numbness: A CARE-compliant case report

challenge_prompt:
A 28-year-old female patient presented with unilateral facial numbness and tenderness involving the left midface and oral region. She reported that a miniscrew-assisted rapid palatal expansion (MARPE) appliance had been placed at an external dental clinic approximately 6 weeks before her initial visit and activated at 2 turns (approximately 0.27 mm) per day. About 5 weeks after appliance placement, she developed sensory disturbances in the left infraorbital region. The direction of activation was reversed at the same rate approximately 1 week later, leading to gradual symptom improvement. She noted partial relief in the palate and upper lip, but hypesthesia persisted in the left philtrum and zygomatic area, accompanied by palpation-induced pain in the left buccal mucosa and lower lip. Total expansion amount and screw specifications were not available.

At the initial visit, she reported numbness in the left zygomatic region, upper lip, philtrum, and palate, with palpation-induced pain in the left buccal mucosa and lower lip. The discomfort was dull and throbbing, triggered by touch but not thermal stimuli, rated 2–3 on the visual analog scale. She denied spontaneous pain, trauma, nocturnal awakening, or relief from NSAIDs. Medical, family, and psychosocial histories were unremarkable.

Clinical examination revealed normal oral function with painless maximum mouth opening of 55 mm. Palpation of temporomandibular joints and masticatory muscles was non-tender bilaterally, and no facial asymmetry or muscle weakness was noted.

Standard panoramic and Waters’ view radiographs showed no bony abnormalities or asymmetry; no cone-beam CT was performed. Quantitative sensory testing (QST) was conducted using a standardized orofacial neurosensory protocol, including contact threshold (CTH), direction discrimination (DIR), 2-point discrimination (2PD), pin-prick (PP), and cold perception. Compared with the contralateral side, the patient had marked sensory asymmetry in the left infraorbital nerve distribution: DIR accuracy was 30% (6/20) on the left vs 100% (20/20) on the right; 2PD was abnormal on the left (a single point was perceived as two points, while the right side had a normal threshold of 10 mm); PP threshold was elevated on the left (60 g) compared to the right (30 g); cold perception was reduced on the left; CTH showed no significant asymmetry.

Other possible causes of facial numbness were excluded: no history of recent local anesthesia near the infraorbital region, no systemic metabolic disorders, no evidence of infection or sinus pathology on examination or imaging, and no temporomandibular joint or masticatory muscle dysfunction.

Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis/etiology, and what is the next best management step?

aliases: Mechanical compressive neuropathy of infraorbital nerve
answer_key.diagnosis: Infraorbital nerve neuropathy (trigeminal neuropathy) secondary to MARPE
answer_key.etiology: Mechanical stress or compression of the infraorbital nerve from rapid palatal expansion forces
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain (e.g., gabapentin 300 mg once daily, titrated to three times daily, with or without a short course of corticosteroids such as prednisolone 20 mg/day for 5 days), and schedule close follow-up to monitor symptom progression. Consider adding low-dose amitriptyline if inadequate response.
optional_findings: Bilateral non-tender TMJ and masticatory muscles
No spontaneous pain or thermal trigger
Reversal of activation direction 1 week after symptom onset
required_findings: 28-year-old female with MARPE appliance placed 6 weeks prior
Activation at 2 turns/day; onset of left infraorbital sensory disturbances 5 weeks after placement
Reversal of activation led to partial improvement but persistent hypesthesia and pain
Numbness in left zygomatic, upper lip, philtrum, palate; dull throbbing pain, VAS 2-3
Normal panoramic and Waters’ view radiographs (no bony abnormalities)
QST: marked asymmetry, DIR 30% on left vs 100% on right, 2PD abnormal (single point felt as two), PP threshold elevated 60g vs 30g, cold perception reduced, CTH symmetric
No other identifiable cause: no trauma, infection, metabolic disease, TMD, or local anesthetic injury
Symptoms localized to distribution of infraorbital nerve (maxillary branch of trigeminal nerve)

title: Neuropathy of the infraorbital area following rapid palatal expansion: A case report

challenge_prompt: A 68-year-old male Macau resident with a known history of ankylosing spondylitis (AS) diagnosed 10 years ago, not on disease-modifying therapy, presented to the emergency department with neck pain and bilateral lower extremity weakness that began 4 hours after a fall. Physical examination revealed loss of cervical lordosis, restricted cervical range of motion, and tenderness over the cervicoscapular region. Sensory exam showed hypesthesia below the T2/T3 dermatomal level and perianal anesthesia (S3–S5 distribution). Motor testing: right wrist extensors, biceps, brachioradialis, triceps, and wrist flexors were grade 4/5; other right upper extremity muscles were grade 5/5; left upper extremity was grade 5/5 throughout; bilateral lower extremities were grade 1+/5. Muscle tone was normal in all limbs. Hoffmann signs were negative bilaterally; cremasteric and anal reflexes were absent; neurogenic bladder and bowel dysfunction were noted. Past medical history: seropositive AS, essential hypertension; no diabetes, cardiovascular disease, prior spinal issues, or surgeries. Laboratory studies: HLA-B27 positive; C-reactive protein 11.99 mg/L; D-dimer >20.00 μg/mL. Cervical CT scan: comminuted C7 vertebral body fracture; multi-level posterior element fractures from C5 to C7; hyperextension deformity; C6 segmental instability; syndesmophyte formation and facet joint ankylosis consistent with advanced AS. Cervical MRI: longitudinal posterior epidural hematoma extending from cervical to upper thoracic regions, causing significant spinal cord compression with T2 signal changes indicative of contusion and edema at C6–C7 levels. Cardiac workup (ECG, echocardiogram, troponins) and head CT were unremarkable. The patient’s motor function rapidly declined after admission, with bilateral lower extremity strength progressing to grade 0/5. Based on the presentation and available data, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate management step?

aliases: AS with cervical fracture and SEH
Cervical fracture-dislocation in AS with epidural hematoma
answer_key.diagnosis: Ankylosing spondylitis complicated by traumatic cervical spine fracture (C5–C7) and cervicothoracic spinal epidural hematoma causing incomplete spinal cord injury
answer_key.etiology: Minor trauma in a patient with ankylosing spondylitis leading to unstable three-column cervical fractures and epidural hematoma formation due to altered spinal biomechanics and vascular fragility.
answer_key.next_management_step: Emergent combined anterior cervical corpectomy/fusion and posterior laminectomy with evacuation of the epidural hematoma and instrumented stabilization.
optional_findings: Neck pain and bilateral leg weakness after a fall
Syndesmophytes and facet joint ankylosis on CT
Right upper extremity motor deficits (grade 4 in some muscles)
required_findings: Known history of ankylosing spondylitis for 10 years, untreated with DMARDs
HLA-B27 positive
Cervical CT: comminuted C7 vertebral body fracture with C5–C7 posterior element fractures and C6 segmental instability
MRI: longitudinal posterior epidural hematoma from cervical to upper thoracic levels with cord compression and signal change at C6–C7
Motor exam: bilateral lower extremities grade 1+/5, progressing to 0/5
Sensory level: hypesthesia below T2/T3 and perianal anesthesia
Absent cremasteric and anal reflexes with neurogenic bladder/bowel dysfunction
Rapid neurological deterioration after admission

title: Ankylosing spondylitis complicated by cervical spine fracture and cervicothoracic epidural hematoma: A case report

challenge_prompt:
A 5-year-old girl with consanguineous parents presented with a syndromic neurocutaneous syndrome of pharmacoresistant seizures, developmental regression, and intractable xerosis from infancy. She was born at term with normal birth weight and no perinatal complications. Family history: a brother with seizures and global developmental delay, and two other brothers who died in early infancy with ichthyotic skin changes. Parents are first-degree cousins.

Physical examination: dry scaly skin on trunk and extremities, most marked on extensor surfaces; scales fine to coarse, adherent, non-inflammatory. Hyperlinearity of palms and mild plantar keratoderma. Neurological examination: microcephaly, axial hypotonia with peripheral hypertonia, brisk deep tendon reflexes, poor visual tracking; fundoscopy unremarkable.

Developmental history: never developed expressive speech; unable to control head at 4 months, sat at 11 months. By age 3, loss of social interaction and increasing aggressive conduct. Remains non-verbal and unable to walk independently.

Seizure history: first seizure at 13 months; generalized tonic–clonic seizures; multiple antiepileptic drugs (clobazam, levetiracetam, valproate) failed to provide long-lasting seizure control.

EEG: multifocal epileptiform discharges with frontal-central preponderance. Brain MRI: high signal intensity adjacent to posterior horns of lateral ventricles; brainstem and cerebellum appeared normal, with no evidence of Molar Tooth Sign or hindbrain malformations.

Whole exome sequencing identified:
- Homozygous missense variant in CC2D2A: c.1268G>A (p.Arg423Gln)
- Heterozygous variant in ABCA12: c.5779-1G>A
- Heterozygous variant in DOCK6: c.2520dupT
- A 1 Mbp heterozygous interstitial deletion at 14q31.3–q32.11 encompassing FOXG1, BCL11B, and other genes.

Based on the above, what is the most likely primary diagnosis?

answer_key.diagnosis: CC2D2A-related Joubert syndrome (Joubert syndrome type 9)
answer_key.etiology: Homozygous CC2D2A c.1268G>A (p.Arg423Gln) variant, with modifying heterozygous variants in ABCA12 and DOCK6, and a 14q31.3–q32.11 deletion.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate multidisciplinary supportive care (seizure management, skin care, developmental therapies) and provide genetic counseling.
optional_findings: Hyperlinearity of palms and mild plantar keratoderma
Axial hypotonia with peripheral hypertonia and brisk reflexes
Poor visual tracking with normal fundoscopy
1 Mbp deletion at 14q31.3–q32.11 encompassing FOXG1, BCL11B
required_findings: Consanguineous parents (first-degree cousins)
Ichthyosis (intractable xerosis from infancy with dry scaly skin)
Pharmacoresistant generalized tonic–clonic seizures starting at 13 months
Severe global developmental delay (non-verbal, unable to walk, microcephaly)
Brain MRI: high signal adjacent to posterior horns, normal brainstem/cerebellum, no Molar Tooth Sign
EEG: multifocal epileptiform discharges with frontal-central preponderance
Family history of sibling with seizures/developmental delay and two infant deaths with ichthyosis
WES: homozygous CC2D2A c.1268G>A variant

title: Multilocus Genetic Variants in a Child With Neuro‐Ichthyosis: A Case of Pharmacoresistant Epilepsy and Developmental Delay Associated With CC2D2A, ABCA12, DOCK6 Variants, and a 14q31.3–q32.11 Deletion

challenge_prompt:
A 45-year-old woman presented with a 2-month history of tingling pain in the left middle and ring fingers. Symptoms were aggravated at and after work (assembly line packing 5-6 kg boxes) and partially relieved by rest. Initially intermittent, the symptoms became constant, interfering with sleep. She had two steroid injections at the carpal tunnel with partial and temporary relief lasting less than a week.

Examination: positive Phalen sign on the left, no Tinel sign at the wrist, mild paresthesia at the volar tips of the left index, middle, and ring fingers. Sensory and motor function of ulnar and radial nerves intact. No atrophy or weakness. During manipulation of the arm to position for nerve conduction study, she experienced shooting pain from the medial arm through the anterior forearm to the middle finger. Additional history revealed mild left medial arm pain just proximal to the elbow over several months. Palpation revealed an intense tender point with a positive Tinel sign over the median nerve at the left medial distal arm.

Electrodiagnostic testing: Distal motor and sensory nerve conduction studies of the median, ulnar, and radial nerves were normal, including sensitive tests for carpal tunnel syndrome. Needle electromyography of the pronator teres, flexor pollicis longus, pronator quadratus, and abductor pollicis brevis showed no abnormalities. An inching study of the median nerve with recording at the abductor pollicis brevis was performed in 2-cm segments along the distal arm. A segment located 3–5 cm proximal to the elbow crease demonstrated a decreased conduction velocity of 27 m/s, while adjacent segments had a conduction velocity of 64 m/s.

A quick ultrasonographic evaluation at the site revealed an echogenic structure superficial to the median nerve and brachial artery.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?

aliases: Median nerve entrapment by supracondylar process
answer_key.diagnosis: Supracondylar process syndrome (median nerve compression by supracondylar process of the humerus)
answer_key.etiology: Compression of the median nerve by a bony supracondylar process (anatomic variant) of the distal humerus, possibly with an associated ligament of Struthers.
answer_key.next_management_step: Obtain plain radiographs of the left humerus/elbow to visualize the supracondylar process and confirm the diagnosis.
optional_findings: History of work-related repetitive manual labor (packing boxes).
Temporary partial relief from carpal tunnel steroid injections.
Shooting pain provoked by arm manipulation.
Mild chronic medial arm pain proximal to the elbow.
No Tinel sign at the wrist.
No sensory or motor deficits in ulnar/radial nerves.
required_findings: Tingling pain in the left middle and ring fingers (median nerve distribution).
Positive Phalen sign.
Tender point and positive Tinel sign at the left medial distal arm.
Normal distal median motor and sensory nerve conduction studies and negative sensitive tests for carpal tunnel syndrome.
Normal needle electromyography in median-innervated forearm muscles including pronator teres.
Inching study reveals focal conduction slowing (27 m/s) in a 2-cm segment 3–5 cm proximal to the elbow crease, with adjacent normal conduction (64 m/s).
Ultrasonography shows an echogenic structure superficial to the median nerve and brachial artery at that level.

title: The utility of electrodiagnostic inching study and conservative treatment in supracondylar process syndrome

challenge_prompt:
A 44-year-old female with a 9-year history of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) previously managed with methotrexate, sulfasalazine, prednisolone, and hydroxychloroquine was switched to infliximab (no washout period) due to inadequate disease control. She received nine doses of infliximab over one year. Two months before presentation, she developed headache and neck pain, followed by numbness and weakness in the right upper limb. A nerve conduction study suggested carpal tunnel syndrome, and she underwent carpal tunnel surgery. Post-operatively, her symptoms persisted and extended to her right lower limb. Brain MRI without and with contrast was performed. The initial MRI revealed a left frontoparietal periventricular white matter lobulated cystic lesion measuring 22 × 16 × 16 mm, with peripheral T2/FLAIR hyperintensity and diffusion restriction in the wall and septa. Post-contrast sequences demonstrated both closed ring and open ring enhancement, with the open side of the ring facing gray matter, and an enhancing prominent medullary vein (central vein sign). There was mild vasogenic edema without mass effect. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed no oligoclonal bands. Serum and CSF were negative for myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein IgG (MOG-IgG) and aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibodies. Toxoplasma serology: IgM negative, IgG positive. Interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) was negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate management step?

answer_key.diagnosis: Tumefactive demyelinating lesion (TDL) induced by infliximab
answer_key.etiology: TNF-alpha inhibitor (infliximab) therapy
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue infliximab and initiate high-dose intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 5 days)

title: Tumefactive Demyelinating Lesion Induced by Infliximab in a Patient With Rheumatoid Arthritis: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 38-year-old man with no significant past medical history presented after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Recovery revealed a 10-day history of generalized headache. He denied weakness, numbness, speech or swallowing difficulties. On arrival, blood pressure was 170/101 mm Hg, otherwise hemodynamically stable. Neurological examination showed no cranial nerve or cerebellar deficits, no focal deficits, and normal symmetrical reflexes. General physical exam was unremarkable.

CT brain was normal. He was admitted to neurology intensive care. MRI brain revealed a right cerebellar small acute ischemic infarct. MR angiography of the brain and neck showed filling defects causing focal narrowing at the origins of both internal carotid arteries. Routine EEG showed no epileptiform activity.

He was started on aspirin, atorvastatin, and carbamazepine. A stroke-in-young workup was initiated. Laboratory studies revealed thrombocytopenia (50,000/μL). Transthoracic echocardiography showed a pedunculated, lobular left atrial mass measuring approximately 1 × 1.5 cm, freely mobile across the mitral valve. Transesophageal echocardiography confirmed the mass; the leading differential was atrial myxoma. Cardiothoracic surgery was consulted and planned for mass resection.

Thrombophilia and connective tissue disease tests returned. Anti-cardiolipin IgG was positive in high titer at >120 U/mL. Anti-β2-glycoprotein I IgG was 90 U/mL. Lupus anticoagulant studies: LA1 screen 109.30 sec, aPTT LA 153.8 sec, hexagonal phase neutralizing timing 25 sec, LA1 mixing time 88 sec, LA2 confirmation timing 38.46 sec (LA1/LA2 ratio 2.84). The dRVVT screen and aPTT were prolonged; mixing studies remained prolonged, consistent with an inhibitor. Hexagonal phase and dRVVT ratio were elevated, consistent with phospholipid dependence, indicating a strong lupus anticoagulant. Anti-nuclear antibody was negative, and ESR and CRP were within normal limits.

Based on the available data, what is the most likely unifying diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Primary antiphospholipid syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune disorder characterized by persistent antiphospholipid antibodies leading to arterial and/or venous thrombosis.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate anticoagulation with warfarin (bridged with heparin) and plan repeat antiphospholipid antibody testing in 12 weeks to confirm persistence.
optional_findings: MRA showing bilateral internal carotid artery narrowing
required_findings: Young patient (38 years) with ischemic stroke (MRI showing cerebellar infarct)
Thrombocytopenia (50,000/μL)
Left atrial mobile mass on echocardiography
Prolonged aPTT and dRVVT with mixing study showing inhibitor, phospholipid dependence
Elevated anti-cardiolipin IgG (>120 U/mL)
Elevated anti-β2-glycoprotein I IgG (90 U/mL)
Negative ANA, normal ESR and CRP

title: Atypical Presentation of Anti-Phospholipid Antibody Syndrome with Seizure and Atrial Mass

challenge_prompt:
A 74-year-old woman with a history of left breast neoplasia and hypokinetic dilated cardiomyopathy (left ventricular ejection fraction 30% by Teicholz, pulmonary artery pressure elevated to 55 mmHg) presents acutely with proportional left hemiparesis and speech impairment. Blood pressure 110/76 mmHg, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 105 bpm, atrial and left ventricular hypertrophy. Initial head CT reveals a small hypodensity in the posterior arm of the right internal capsule and diffuse hypodensity of the semioval centers and periventricular white matter, consistent with ischemic stroke in the right anterior choroidal territory with cortico-subcortical atrophy. She is started on enoxaparin, aspirin, and fluoxetine. On day 3, she deteriorates, developing proportional left hemiplegia and dysarthria. Repeat CT brain shows a large hypodensity (24 Hounsfield units) in the left parieto-temporo-occipital and right capsulo-lenticulo-thalamic regions, with mass effect causing partial collapse of the lateral ventricle and a 4 mm midline shift; no intra- or extra-axial hemorrhage. Doppler ultrasound of the supra-aortic trunks demonstrates total thrombotic occlusion of the right internal bulbar and post-bulbar carotid arteries. Angioscan of the supra-aortic trunks shows right carotid atherosclerosis with an occlusive thrombus beginning at the bulb, extending to the internal carotid artery and supra-cavernous portion, with no vascular malformation.

Based on the available information, what is the most likely etiology of her stroke, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Ischemic stroke due to total thrombotic occlusion of the right internal carotid artery secondary to atherosclerosis
answer_key.etiology: Atherosclerosis (atherothrombosis)
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue parenteral anticoagulation with enoxaparin (or low molecular weight heparin) for 10 days, then transition to aspirin and high-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin); consider carotid endarterectomy if surgical candidacy and resources allow.
optional_findings: Initial CT: small hypodensity in right internal capsule and periventricular white matter
ECG: sinus tachycardia 105 bpm, atrial and left ventricular hypertrophy
Initial treatment with enoxaparin, aspirin, fluoxetine
required_findings: Total thrombotic occlusion of right internal carotid artery on Doppler ultrasound
Angioscan showing right carotid atherosclerosis with occlusive thrombus from bulb to supra-cavernous ICA
No vascular malformation on angioscan
Absence of intra- or extra-axial hemorrhage on CT
Large hypodense lesions in capsulolenticulothalamic and parieto-temporo-occipital regions
Proportional left hemiplegia and dysarthria
Hypokinetic dilated cardiomyopathy with LVEF 30%
History of left breast neoplasia

title: Unusual Etiology of an Ischemic Stroke: Total Thrombosis of the Right Internal Carotid Artery in a Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 31-year-old right-handed male sustained a headfirst impact during an electric bicycle accident, with immediate loss of consciousness and unresponsiveness to verbal stimuli. Upon hospital admission, he had right-sided hemiplegia and recurrent projectile vomiting. Neurological examination showed altered mental status: eye opening to verbal commands but no meaningful verbal response, right-sided flaccid paralysis (Medical Research Council Grade 0), left-sided motor strength MRC Grade IV, and Glasgow Coma Scale score of 10. Initial cranial computed tomography revealed a large left frontal intracerebral hematoma (57 × 36 mm in axial dimensions) with concomitant left frontal subdural hemorrhage. Additional punctate hemorrhagic foci were present in the right frontal and bilateral parietal lobes, radiologically suggestive of diffuse axonal injury. Based on the presentation and imaging, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

aliases: Acute traumatic brain injury with mass effect
answer_key.diagnosis: Traumatic brain injury with large left frontal intracerebral hematoma, left frontal subdural hemorrhage, and diffuse axonal injury (multiple punctate hemorrhagic foci), causing right hemiplegia.
answer_key.etiology: Headfirst impact from electric bicycle accident.
answer_key.next_management_step: Emergency craniotomy for evacuation of the left frontal intracerebral hematoma.
optional_findings: Left-sided motor strength MRC Grade IV
Eye opening to verbal commands but no meaningful verbal response
CT evidence of diffuse axonal injury pattern

Right-sided hemiplegia
Recurrent projectile vomiting (indicating raised intracranial pressure)
Altered mental status: eye opening to verbal commands, no meaningful verbal response, GCS 10
Right flaccid paralysis MRC Grade 0
CT shows large left frontal intracerebral hematoma 57 × 36 mm
CT shows left frontal subdural hemorrhage
CT shows additional punctate hemorrhagic foci in right frontal and bilateral parietal lobes

title: Structural‐Functional Dissociation in TBI Hemiplegia: Meridian‐Sinew Therapy Promotes Motor Recovery Despite Corticospinal Tract Damage—A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 43-year-old male with a history of resistant hypertension, medication noncompliance, cystitis cystica, and smoking presented with severe bitemporal headache, blurring of vision, and dizziness for 3 days. He stopped his antihypertensives 3 months ago. Previous workup for secondary hypertension (including aldosterone, renin ratio, and genetic panel for Liddle syndrome) was negative. Family history: father with hypertension. On examination: BP 225/141 mm Hg, HR 77 bpm, O2 97% on room air. Fundoscopy showed Grade IV hypertensive retinopathy with papilledema. Neurological exam: no focal deficit, GCS 15, normal power. Labs: creatinine 132 μmol/L, potassium 3.2 mmol/L, magnesium 0.6 mmol/L, glucose 6.1 mmol/L; CBC, other electrolytes, troponin, liver function normal. CT head showed chronic white matter microvascular ischemic changes, no acute bleeding or infarct. He was given oral nifedipine and valsartan, but BP remained 219/148, so IV hydralazine and oral potassium/magnesium supplements were added. BP still high (201/134), labetalol infusion started. On the second day, he had two generalized tonic-clonic seizures within 15 min without regaining full consciousness (status epilepticus), preceded by systolic BP >200. Lorazepam and levetiracetam were given. Mental status declined: GCS 13, confusion. MRI brain: T2/FLAIR showed extensive patches of increased signal intensity in the left cerebellar hemisphere, bilateral periventricular and subcortical regions, predominantly posteriorly. DWI/ADC: no restricted diffusion. Postcontrast T1: left parieto-occipital leptomeningeal enhancement. MRA: no vasospasm. Lumbar puncture: CSF analysis normal (glucose, protein, cell count within normal limits). What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)
answer_key.etiology: Malignant hypertension
answer_key.next_management_step: Aggressive blood pressure control (gradual reduction with IV agents like labetalol), continuation of antiepileptic therapy (levetiracetam), and correction of electrolyte abnormalities (especially magnesium).
optional_findings: Previous negative workup for secondary hypertension
CT head: chronic white matter microvascular ischemic changes
Postcontrast T1: left parieto-occipital leptomeningeal enhancement
required_findings: BP 225/141 mm Hg
Grade IV hypertensive retinopathy with papilledema
Generalized tonic-clonic seizures (status epilepticus)
MRI: T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in bilateral periventricular and subcortical regions, predominantly posteriorly
MRI: no restricted diffusion on DWI/ADC
MRA: no vasospasm
Normal CSF analysis
Hypomagnesemia (0.6 mmol/L)

title: Malignant Hypertension Complicated by Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome (PRES)

challenge_prompt: An 8-year-old girl with known Seckel syndrome (diagnosed at age 2) presented to the emergency department with facial asymmetry, swallowing difficulty, and left-sided hemiplegia. Her parents reported a recent history of headache, dragging foot, and falling about 6 days earlier. She had previously visited another hospital where a brain CT scan was inconclusive, and she was discharged with recommendation for follow-up at a tertiary center. On examination, she had severe dwarfism (weight 13 kg, height 80 cm), psychomotor and mental retardation, speech disability, and dysmorphic features including protuberant beaked nose, low-set ears, prominent eyes, micrognathia, and sparse brittle hair. Neurologic exam revealed decreased muscle power on the left side in both upper and lower extremities, left upper motor neuron facial palsy, and no nystagmus. She was admitted to the ICU with impression of stroke. Brain MRI showed a bright area on diffusion-weighted images (DWI) with low signals on apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) in the territory of the right middle cerebral artery, consistent with acute infarction. Reconstruction images of brain MRA showed extreme narrowing of bilateral long segments of the intracranial internal carotid arteries (ICAs), obliteration of the right lacerum segment, and bilateral horizontal segment of the ICAs with formation of distal non-moyamoya collateral vessels.

answer_key.diagnosis: Moyamoya-like vasculopathy (non-moyamoya cerebral vasculopathy) associated with Seckel syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Genetic predisposition in Seckel syndrome leading to progressive stenosis/obliteration of large cerebral arteries with distal non-moyamoya collaterals
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate antiplatelet therapy with aspirin (5 mg/kg/day) and consider cerebral revascularization options if feasible
optional_findings: Severe dwarfism (13 kg, 80 cm at age 8)
Protuberant beaked nose, low-set ears, prominent eyes, micrognathia
History of headache and gait difficulty prior to stroke
required_findings: Known diagnosis of Seckel syndrome
Acute right MCA territorial infarction on DWI/ADC
Extreme narrowing of bilateral long segments of intracranial ICAs
Obliteration of the right lacerum segment of ICA
Bilateral horizontal segment ICA obliteration
Formation of distal non-moyamoya collateral vessels on MRA
Left-sided hemiplegia and facial palsy

title: Arterial stroke in a child with Seckel syndrome with a pattern of non‐moyamoya vasculopathy
